title: FalconApp: Rapid iPhone Deployment of End-to-End Perception via Automatically Labeled Synthetic Data
summary: Reliable perception for robotics depends on large-scale labeled data, yet real-world datasets rely on heavy manual annotation and are time-consuming to produce. We present FalconApp, an iPhone app with an end-to-end frontend-backend pipeline that turns a short handheld capture of a rigid object into a perception module for mask detection and 6-DoF pose estimation. Our core contribution is a rapid mobile deployment pipeline paired with a photorealistic auto-labeling workflow: from a user-captured video of an object, FalconApp reconstructs an editable GSplat asset, composites it with diverse photorealistic backgrounds, renders synthetic images with ground-truth masks and poses, trains the perception module, and deploys it back to the iPhone frontend. Experiments across five rigid objects with diverse geometry and appearance show that FalconApp produces usable perception models with about 20 minutes of synthetic-data generation and training per object on average, around 30 ms end-to-end on-device latency on iPhone, and better overall pose accuracy than a PnP baseline on 4 / 5 objects in both simulation and real-world evaluation.

AI.tldr: FalconApp provides an end-to-end iPhone deployment pipeline for fast synthetic-data generation and on-device perception models for 6-DoF pose estimation of rigid objects, achieving ~20 min per object, ~30 ms latency, and improved pose accuracy over a PnP baseline on most tested objects.
AI.motivation: Reduce data labeling bottleneck in robotic perception by enabling rapid mobile deployment and photorealistic auto-labeling from user-captured video, displacing heavy manual annotation.
AI.method: From a user video, reconstruct an editable GSplat asset of the object, composite with diverse photorealistic backgrounds, render synthetic images with ground-truth masks and poses, train a perception module, and deploy it back to the iPhone frontend. The pipeline is end-to-end on-device to enable low-latency inference.
AI.result: Applied to five rigid objects with diverse geometry; about 20 minutes of synthetic data generation and training per object on average; ~30 ms end-to-end on-device latency; better overall pose accuracy than a PnP baseline on 4/5 objects in both simulation and real-world evaluation.
AI.conclusion: A rapid mobile deployment pipeline coupled with a photorealistic auto-labeling workflow can produce usable perception models efficiently, enabling practical, on-device 3D object perception with competitive accuracy.

title: A Scaled Three-Vehicle Platooning Platform
summary: Vehicle platooning has attracted increasing attention as a promising approach to improve traffic efficiency, energy consumption, and roadway safety through coordinated multi-vehicle operation. A key challenge in platooning lies in maintaining stable and accurate path tracking during dynamic maneuvers such as lane changes, where lateral deviations and heading disturbances generated by the lead vehicle may propagate downstream to following vehicles. Robust longitudinal and lateral control systems are therefore essential not only for individual vehicle tracking performance, but also for overall platoon stability. For experimental studies, the Intelligent Mobility and Robotics Lab (IMRL) develops a scaled multi-vehicle platform for autonomous platooning research, with a particular emphasis on cooperative control and human-in-the-loop autonomy. This platform consists of one human-operable lead vehicle and two autonomous followers, enabling controlled and repeatable experiments on leader-follower coordination. Compared with full-scale field testing, this scaled platform offers a safer, lower-cost, and more flexible environment for rapid prototyping, controller validation, and multi-agent autonomy studies, while providing stronger physical realism than purely simulation-based evaluations.

AI.tldr: Scaled 3-vehicle platooning testbed enabling leader–follower coordination with a human-operable lead and two autonomous followers; offers safer, cheaper, and more realistic experimentation versus full-scale tests or pure simulations.
AI.motivation: Address stability and disturbance propagation in platoons during dynamic maneuvers; justify need for an experimental platform to validate robust longitudinal and lateral control and cooperative/human-in-the-loop autonomy.
AI.method: Developed a scaled platform consisting of one human-driven lead vehicle and two autonomous followers; emphasizes cooperative control and leader–follower coordination; enables repeatable experiments and rapid prototyping; positioned as an alternative to field tests and simulations.
AI.result: Provides safer, lower-cost, and more flexible environment for rapid prototyping, controller validation, and multi-agent autonomy studies; offers stronger physical realism than simulations (no explicit numerical results reported).
AI.conclusion: Scaled platooning platform complements simulations and field tests, facilitating controller validation, human-in-the-loop experiments, and multi-agent autonomy research with improved realism and repeatability.

title: Multi-Periodogram Velocity Estimation with Irregular Reference Signals for Robot-Aided ISAC
summary: This paper addresses velocity estimation within robot-aided integrated sensing and communications (ISAC), where mobile robots act as sensing nodes but can only opportunistically reuse irregular 5G/6G reference signals (RSs). We show that the velocity profile induced by such irregular time-domain patterns can be decomposed into a periodic-peak component and an amplitude-shaping (weighting) component. Leveraging this structure, we propose a multi-periodogram velocity estimation algorithm that is standard-compliant and does not require new sensing-dedicated RSs or 3GPP modifications. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with conventional periodogram processing, the proposed method improves low-SNR robustness by achieving a 3 dB SNR gain at the 10% missed-detection rate and reducing false alarms by 51%.

AI.tldr: A multi-periodogram velocity estimation method for robot-aided ISAC using opportunistic 5G/6G RSs. It models the velocity signal as a sum of a periodic-peak component and an amplitude-shaping weight, enabling a standard-compliant algorithm that requires no new RSs or 3GPP changes. It outperforms conventional periodograms in low-SNR regimes by improving 3 dB SNR at 10% missed detections and reducing false alarms by 51%.
AI.motivation: In ISAC with mobile robots, sensing relies on irregular reference signals (RSs) from 5G/6G; there is a need to accurately estimate velocity without extra sensing-specific RSs or modifications, even under low SNR.
AI.method: Decompose the velocity profile induced by irregular RS patterns into a periodic-peak component and an amplitude-shaping (weighting) component. Develop a multi-periodogram velocity estimation algorithm that is standard-compliant and does not require additional RSs or 3GPP changes.
AI.result: Simulation results show improved low-SNR robustness over conventional periodogram processing: a 3 dB SNR gain at a 10% missed-detection rate and a 51% reduction in false alarms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a practical, standard-compliant velocity estimation solution for robot-aided ISAC using opportunistic RSs, with notable robustness gains in challenging noise conditions.

title: FlowS: One-Step Motion Prediction via Local Transport Conditioning
summary: Generative motion prediction must satisfy three simultaneous requirements for real-world autonomy: high accuracy, diverse multimodal futures, and strictly bounded latency. Diffusion models meet the first two but violate the third, requiring tens to hundreds of denoising steps. We identify a conditioning strategy that resolves this tension: \textit{single-step integration is accurate when the underlying transport problem is local}. A model that must both discover the correct behavioral mode and traverse a long displacement in one step accumulates large discretization errors; conditioning the base distribution to lie near plausible futures reduces the problem to short-range refinement, the regime where a single Euler step suffices. We instantiate this \emph{local transport conditioning} in FlowS, a conditional flow matching framework with two mechanisms. First, an online, scene-conditioned learned prior emits $K$ calibrated anchor trajectories per agent, each already near a plausible future, converting mode discovery into local correction. Second, a step-consistent displacement field enforces semigroup self-consistency, guaranteeing that a single step inherits multi-step accuracy. Crucially, anchoring this field at learned priors along straight-line paths yields a {stable, low-variance} training target, unlike prior self-consistency methods that suffer from {high-variance bootstrap} signals on curved diffusion paths. On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, FlowS achieves state-of-the-art Soft mAP {(0.4804) and mAP (0.4703) with ensemble at 75\,FPS} with single-step inference, demonstrating that local transport conditioning makes one-step generative motion prediction practical for safety-critical autonomy. Code and pretrained models will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Local transport conditioning enables single-step diffusion-based motion prediction by anchoring to plausible futures via learned priors and enforcing semigroup self-consistency, achieving state-of-the-art one-step results on Waymo Open Motion with 75 FPS.
AI.motivation: Real-world autonomous motion prediction requires high accuracy, diverse multimodal futures, and low latency. Diffusion models excel at accuracy and diversity but are slow due to many denoising steps; conditioning the transport to be local enables accurate one-step refinement.
AI.method: FlowS framework with two mechanisms: (1) online scene-conditioned learned prior that emits K calibrated anchor trajectories per agent, placing futures near plausible modes; (2) a step-consistent displacement field enforcing semigroup self-consistency so a single step inherits multi-step accuracy. Anchors along straight-line paths yield stable, low-variance training targets. This converts mode discovery into local correction and enables single-step Euler refinement.
AI.result: On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, FlowS achieves state-of-the-art Soft mAP 0.4804 and mAP 0.4703 with ensemble at 75 FPS, using single-step inference.
AI.conclusion: Local transport conditioning makes one-step generative motion prediction practical for safety-critical autonomy. Code and pretrained models will be released upon acceptance.

title: A Multimodal and Explainable Machine Learning Approach to Diagnosing Multi-Class Ejection Fraction from Electrocardiograms
summary: Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) assessment depends on echocardiography, limiting access in primary care and resource-constrained settings. We developed a multimodal machine-learning framework that combines engineered 12-lead ECG timeseries features with structured EHR variables to classify LVEF into four clinically used strata: normal (>50%), mildly reduced (40-50%), moderately reduced (30-40%), and severely reduced (<30%). To support model explainability, we identified the most influential ECG and EHR features via SHAP attributions. Using retrospective data from Hartford HealthCare, we trained XGBoost models on 36,784 ECG-echocardiogram pairs from 30,952 outpatients and evaluated temporal generalizability on 19,966 ECGs from a subsequent period. The multimodal model achieved one-vs-rest AUROCs of 0.95 (severe), 0.92 (moderate), 0.82 (mild), and 0.91 (normal), outperforming ECG-only and EHR-only baselines, and maintained performance under temporal validation. This work supports ECG-based, multimodal LVEF stratification as a practical screening and triage aid to prioritize confirmatory imaging where resources are limited.

AI.tldr: Multimodal ML using ECG features plus structured EHR to classify LVEF into four strata; high AUROC and temporal generalization; suitable for screening in resource-limited settings.
AI.motivation: LVEF assessment relies on echocardiography, which limits access in primary care and resource-constrained settings; a noninvasive, scalable screening/triage method is needed.
AI.method: Retrospective study using Hartford HealthCare data; XGBoost models trained on 36,784 ECG–echocardiogram pairs from 30,952 outpatients; multimodal features combine engineered 12-lead ECG timeseries with structured EHR data; SHAP used for feature attribution; temporal generalizability evaluated on 19,966 ECGs from a subsequent period; compared against ECG-only and EHR-only baselines.
AI.result: One-vs-rest AUROCs: severe 0.95, moderate 0.92, mild 0.82, normal 0.91; multimodal model outperformed ECG-only and EHR-only baselines; performance preserved under temporal validation.
AI.conclusion: ECG-based multimodal LVEF stratification can serve as a practical screening/triage tool to prioritize confirmatory echocardiography in settings with limited resources.

title: Generalized Disguise Makeup Presentation Attack Detection Using an Attention-Guided Patch-Based Framework
summary: Despite significant advances in facial recognition systems, they remain vulnerable to face presentation attacks. Among them, disguise makeup attacks are particularly challenging, as they use advanced cosmetics, prosthetic components, and artificial materials to realistically alter facial appearance, often making detection difficult even for humans. Despite their importance, this problem remains underexplored, and publicly available datasets are limited. To address this, we propose a generalized disguise makeup presentation attack detection framework. The method adopts a two-phase design in which a style-invariant full-face model, trained with metric learning and enhanced by a whitening transformation, extracts region attention scores via Grad-CAM. These scores guide a patch-based phase that performs localized analysis using region-specific subnetworks trained with metric learning for fine-grained discrimination. We also construct a new, diverse dataset of live and disguise makeup faces collected under real-world conditions, covering variations in subjects, environments, and disguise materials. Experimental results demonstrate strong generalization across both the collected dataset and SIW-Mv2, achieving 8.97% ACER and 9.76% EER on the collected dataset, and 0% ACER on Obfuscation and Impersonation and 1.34% on Cosmetics attacks of SIW-Mv2. The proposed method consistently outperforms prior works while maintaining robust performance across other spoof types.

AI.tldr: A two-phase, metric-learning driven framework for disguise makeup presentation attack detection that combines a style-invariant full-face model with Grad-CAM guided patch-based analysis; accompanied by a newly collected dataset and strong cross-dataset generalization results (SIW-Mv2 and collected data).
AI.motivation: Faces recognition systems are vulnerable to presentation attacks, with disguise makeup being particularly challenging due to realistic cosmetics and prosthetics; public datasets are limited, hindering generalizable detection.
AI.method: Phase 1: train a style-invariant full-face model using metric learning and whitening to produce region attention via Grad-CAM. Phase 2: a patch-based analysis using region-specific subnetworks, trained with metric learning for fine-grained discrimination, guided by region attention scores. Dataset: a new diverse live vs. disguise makeup dataset collected under real-world conditions.
AI.result: On the collected dataset: 8.97% ACER and 9.76% EER. On SIW-Mv2: 0% ACER for Obfuscation and Impersonation, 1.34% ACER for Cosmetics attacks. The method outperforms prior works and shows robustness across other spoof types.
AI.conclusion: A generalizable disguise makeup PAD framework with two-phase design and region-aware, metric-learning-based discrimination, validated by a new dataset and strong cross-dataset performance, indicating effective handling of diverse disguise materials and real-world conditions.

title: Operating-Layer Controls for Onchain Language-Model Agents Under Real Capital
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures. Public onchain dashboard and supporting documentation linked in paper
summary: We study reliability in autonomous language-model agents that translate user mandates into validated tool actions under real capital. The setting is DX Terminal Pro, a 21-day deployment in which 3,505 user-funded agents traded real ETH in a bounded onchain market. Users configured vaults through structured controls and natural-language strategies, but only agents could choose normal buy/sell trades. The system produced 7.5M agent invocations, roughly 300K onchain actions, about $20M in volume, more than 5,000 ETH deployed, roughly 70B inference tokens, and 99.9% settlement success for policy-valid submitted transactions. Long-running agents accumulated thousands of sequential decisions, including 6,000+ prompt-state-action cycles for continuously active agents, yielding a large-scale trace from user mandate to rendered prompt, reasoning, validation, portfolio state, and settlement. Reliability did not come from the base model alone; it emerged from the operating layer around the model: prompt compilation, typed controls, policy validation, execution guards, memory design, and trace-level observability. Pre-launch testing exposed failures that text-only benchmarks rarely measure, including fabricated trading rules, fee paralysis, numeric anchoring, cadence trading, and misread tokenomics. Targeted harness changes reduced fabricated sell rules from 57% to 3%, reduced fee-led observations from 32.5% to below 10%, and increased capital deployment from 42.9% to 78.0% in an affected test population. We show that capital-managing agents should be evaluated across the full path from user mandate to prompt, validated action, and settlement.

AI.tldr: Reliability in autonomous language-model agents for real capital trading emerges from the surrounding operating layer (prompting, controls, validation, guards, memory, observability), as shown by a 21‑day live deployment with 3,505 agents performing real ETH trades—huge activity and high settlement success—improved by targeted harness fixes.
AI.motivation: Understand end-to-end reliability of capital-managing AI agents that convert user mandates into validated on-chain actions and settlements, addressing gaps not captured by text-only benchmarks and emphasizing the full lifecycle from mandate to settlement.
AI.method: Large-scale live deployment (DX Terminal Pro) over 21 days with 3,505 user-funded agents trading real ETH in a bounded on-chain market. Users configure vaults via structured controls and natural-language strategies; only agents perform normal buy/sell trades. Collected metrics include 7.5M agent invocations, ~300K on-chain actions, ~$20M volume, >5k ETH deployed, ~70B inference tokens, and 99.9% settlement success for policy-valid transactions. Long-running agents produced 6k+ prompt-state-action cycles, yielding traces from mandate to prompt, reasoning, portfolio state, and settlement. The analysis foregrounds the operating layer around the model: prompt compilation, typed controls, policy validation, execution guards, memory design, and trace observability. Pre-launch testing surfaced failures not captured by text benchmarks, such as fabricated trading rules and numeric/tokenomics issues; targeted harness changes reduced fabricated sell rules from 57% to 3%, reduced fee-led observations from 32.5% to <10%, and increased capital deployment from 42.9% to 78.0%.
AI.result: Deployment yielded 7.5M invocations, ~300K on-chain actions, ~$20M in volume, >5k ETH deployed, ~70B inference tokens, and 99.9% settlement success for policy-valid transactions. Long-running agents executed thousands of steps (6k+ cycles). Harness changes materially improved behavior: fabricated sell rules declined 57%→3%, fee-led observations 32.5%→<10%, capital deployment 42.9%→78.0%.
AI.conclusion: Reliability of capital-managing agents stems from the surrounding operating layer rather than the base model alone. Effective evaluation must trace the full path from user mandate to validated action to settlement; end-to-end end-user deployment, gap-filling testing, and targeted harness improvements can yield substantial reliability gains.

title: Calibrated Persistent Homology Tests for High-dimensional Collapse Detection
summary: We study detection of collapse in high-dimensional point clouds, where mass concentrates near a lower-dimensional set relative to a non-collapsed geometry. We propose persistent homology-based test statistics under two well-studied filtrations, with cutoffs calibrated under a broad set of non-collapsed reference models. We benchmark power across three alternative collapse mechanisms (linear/spectral, nonlinear-support, and contamination/heterogeneity) and distill the results into a mechanism map guiding the choice of filtration and statistic.

AI.tldr: Persistent-homology-based detection of collapse in high-dimensional point clouds using two filtrations with calibrated null cutoffs; power-benchmarked across three collapse mechanisms and summarized in a mechanism map for filtration/statistic choice.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional data can exhibit concentration of mass near lower-dimensional sets (collapse). Traditional methods may lack robustness across mechanisms; a filtration-aware, PH-based approach with calibrated nulls aims to robustly detect collapse and guide method selection.
AI.method: Develop PH-based test statistics for two standard filtrations. Calibrate cutoffs under broad non-collapsed reference models. Evaluate power under three collapse mechanisms (linear/spectral, nonlinear-support, contamination/heterogeneity) and synthesize results into a mechanism map that prescribes filtration and statistic choice.
AI.result: The study provides power benchmarks across collapse mechanisms and delivers a mechanism map that guides practitioners in selecting the filtration and statistic to maximize detection power under different collapse scenarios.
AI.conclusion: PH-based collapse detection with calibrated nulls is effective in high dimensions and yields a practical decision tool (mechanism map) for choosing the appropriate filtration and statistic based on the suspected collapse mechanism.

title: Lights Out: A Nighttime UAV Localization Framework Using Thermal Imagery and Semantic 3D Maps
summary: Reliable backup localization for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) operating in GNSS-denied nighttime conditions remains an open challenge due to the severe modality gap between daytime RGB maps and nighttime thermal imagery. This work presents a semantic reprojection framework for map-relative nighttime UAV localization by aligning segmented thermal observations with a globally referenced, semantically labeled 3D map constructed from daytime RGB data. Rather than relying on appearance-based correspondence, localization is formulated in a shared semantic domain and solved via a symmetric bidirectional reprojection objective with confusion-aware weighting to improve robustness under segmentation uncertainty. The approach is evaluated offline across 6.5 km of nighttime, real-world UAV flight trajectories in urban and semi-structured environments. Relative to RTK GNSS ground truth, the system achieves a bias-corrected RMSE2D of 2.18 m and a median RMSE2D of 1.52 m. Results show that localization performance is strongly correlated with the availability of semantic edge evidence and that large-error events are spatially localized to semantically ambiguous areas rather than uniformly distributed. These findings indicate that semantic reprojection offers a promising pathway toward globally referenced nighttime UAV localization using thermal imagery alone.

AI.tldr: Semantic reprojection for GNSS-denied nighttime UAV localization by aligning thermal observations to a daytime RGB-derived 3D semantic map. Uses a symmetric bidirectional reprojection with confusion-aware weighting, avoiding appearance-based matches. Offline evaluation on 6.5 km of real nighttime flight yields bias-corrected 2D RMSE 2.18 m and median RMSE 1.52 m; errors cluster in semantically ambiguous areas, showing robustness of semantic guidance.
AI.motivation: Overcome the modality gap between daytime RGB maps and nighttime thermal imagery for map-relative UAV localization when GNSS is unavailable. Traditional appearance-based methods struggle under night conditions; semantic guidance offers a stable representation across modalities.
AI.method: Build a globally referenced 3D map with semantic labels from daytime RGB data. Segment thermal observations at night. Perform localization in a shared semantic domain using a symmetric bidirectional reprojection objective with confusion-aware weighting to account for segmentation uncertainty.
AI.result: Offline evaluation over 6.5 km of nighttime UAV trajectories in urban/semi-structured environments. Relative to RTK GNSS ground truth: bias-corrected 2D RMSE = 2.18 m; median 2D RMSE = 1.52 m. Localization performance improves with semantic edge evidence; large errors are localized to semantically ambiguous regions.
AI.conclusion: Semantic reprojection is a promising approach for globally referenced nighttime UAV localization using thermal imagery alone, robust to segmentation uncertainty and capable of leveraging semantic structure to bridge modality gaps.

title: A Randomized PDE Energy driven Iterative Framework for Efficient and Stable PDE Solutions
summary: Efficient and stable solution of partial differential equations (PDEs) is central to scientific and engineering applications, yet existing numerical solvers rely heavily on matrix based discretizations, while learning based methods require costly training and often suffer from limited generalization. In this work, we proposes a PDE energy driven framework that solves PDEs through physically constrained diffusion iterations, without relying on classical matrix based finite element assembly or data driven neural network training. The proposed method evolves arbitrary random initial fields through PDE energy driven implicit iterations combined with Gaussian smoothing, while strictly enforcing boundary conditions at each iteration. The proposed formulation is applied to representative one dimensional Poisson, Heat, and viscous Burgers equations, covering both steady state and transient problems. Numerical results demonstrate stable convergence to the unique physical solution from random initializations, with accurate resolution of sharp gradients and controlled Mean Squared Error (MSE) across a wide range of discretization parameters. Detailed comparisons with analytical solutions indicate that the framework achieves competitive accuracy and stability. Overall, the proposed framework provides a fast, flexible, and physically consistent alternative to traditional numerical solvers, offering a potential pathway for scalable PDE solutions in both research and engineering applications.

AI.tldr: Energy-driven diffusion iterations for PDEs provide a matrix-free, physics-constrained solver that evolves random fields to the PDE solution with Gaussian smoothing and explicit boundary enforcement, validated on 1D Poisson, heat, and viscous Burgers equations.
AI.motivation: Limitations of traditional matrix-based discretizations and data-driven solvers: high cost, limited generalization; need for a physics-consistent, fast solver that can handle sharp gradients and various discretizations.
AI.method: PDE energy driven implicit iterations with Gaussian smoothing; enforce boundary conditions at each step; no FEM assembly or neural nets; applies to 1D Poisson, Heat, Burgers; works for steady-state and transient; demonstrate convergence from random initialization.
AI.result: Stable convergence to unique physical solution; accurate gradient resolution; controlled MSE across discretization; competitive accuracy vs analytical solutions; efficient and flexible solver.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a fast, flexible, physically consistent alternative to classical solvers and may enable scalable PDE solutions without matrix assembly or training.

title: Report of the 5th PVUW Challenge: Towards More Diverse Modalities in Pixel-Level Understanding
comment: Official Report of the 5th PVUW Challenge on CVPR 2026
summary: This report summarizes the objectives, datasets, and top-performing methodologies of the 2026 Pixel-level Video Understanding in the Wild (PVUW) Challenge, hosted at CVPR 2026, which evaluates state-of-the-art models under highly unconstrained conditions. To provide a comprehensive assessment, the 2026 edition features three specialized tracks: the MOSE track for tracking objects within densely cluttered and severely occluded scenarios; the MeViS-Text track for localizing targets via motion-focused linguistic expressions; and the newly inaugurated MeViS-Audio track, which pioneers acoustic-driven object segmentation. By introducing previously unreleased challenging data and analyzing the cutting-edge, multimodal solutions submitted by participants, this report highlights the community's latest technical advancements and charts promising future directions for robust video scene comprehension.

AI.tldr: Overview of PVUW 2026 Challenge: objectives, datasets, and top-performing, multimodal methods across three tracks—MOSE (dense occlusion tracking), MeViS-Text (motion-guided linguistic localization), MeViS-Audio (acoustic-driven segmentation)—with new challenging data and analysis of submissions, highlighting progress and future directions for robust video understanding.
AI.motivation: Address the difficulty of video understanding in unconstrained environments and assess state-of-the-art models using diversified, multimodal tracks to push robustness and generalization.
AI.method: Report structure includes description of objectives, datasets, and top-performing methodologies across three tracks; analysis of submitted approaches, introduction of novel data; cross-track evaluation to identify trends and limitations in multimodal video understanding research.
AI.result: Identification of leading approaches per track, demonstration of multimodal solutions, and synthesis of current progress; establishment of directions for future research in robust video scene understanding.
AI.conclusion: PVUW 2026 illustrates rapid advancements in multimodal video understanding and provides a roadmap for future work to enhance robustness under unconstrained conditions.

title: Distill-Belief: Closed-Loop Inverse Source Localization and Characterization in Physical Fields
summary: {Closed-loop inverse source localization and characterization (ISLC) requires a mobile agent to select measurements that localize sources and infer latent field parameters under strict time constraints.} {The core challenge lies in the belief-space objective: valid uncertainty estimation requires expensive Bayesian inference, whereas using fast learned belief model leads to reward hacking, in which the policy exploits approximation errors rather than actually reducing uncertainty.} {We propose \textbf{Distill-Belief}, a teacher--student framework that decouples correctness from efficiency. A Bayes-correct particle-filter teacher maintains the posterior and supplies a dense information-gain signal, while a compact student distills the posterior into belief statistics for control and an uncertainty certificate for stopping. At deployment, only the student is used, yielding constant per-step cost.} {Experiments on seven field modalities and two stress tests show that Distill-Belief consistently reduces sensing cost and improves success, posterior contraction, and estimation accuracy over baselines, while mitigating reward hacking.}

AI.tldr: Distill-Belief decouples correctness from efficiency in closed-loop inverse source localization via a teacher-student framework, enabling low-cost, reliable sensing with mitigated reward hacking.
AI.motivation: Accurate belief-space uncertainty estimation requires expensive Bayesian inference; fast learned beliefs risk reward hacking that exploits approximation errors instead of reducing uncertainty under strict time constraints.
AI.method: Introduce a Bayes-correct particle-filter teacher that preserves the true posterior and provides a dense information-gain signal, and a compact student that distills posterior statistics for control and an uncertainty certificate for stopping; at deployment only the student is used for constant per-step cost.
AI.result: Across seven field modalities and two stress tests, Distill-Belief reduces sensing cost, improves success rates, tightens posterior contraction, enhances estimation accuracy, and mitigates reward hacking relative to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The teacher-student distillation approach effectively decouples correctness from efficiency in ISLC, yielding robust, low-cost, real-time performance with reliable uncertainty handling.

title: The Nesting Bird Box Problem is ER-complete: Sharp Hardness Results for the Hidden Set Problem
summary:
In the (Nesting) Bird Box Problem we are given a polygonal domain P and a number k and we want to know if there is a set B of k points inside P such that no two points in B can see each other. The underlying idea is that each point represents a birdhouse and many birds only use a birdhouse if there is no other occupied birdhouse in its vicinity. We say two points a,b see each other if the open segment ab intersects neither the exterior of P nor any vertex of P.
  We show that the Nesting Bird Box problem is ER-complete. The complexity class ER can be defined by the set of problems that are polynomial time equivalent to finding a solution to the equation $p(x) = 0$, with $x\in R^n$ and $p\in $Z[X_1,...,X_n]$. The proof builds on the techniques developed in the original ER-completeness proof of the Art Gallery problem. However our proof is significantly shorter for two reasons. First, we can use recently developed tools that were not available at the time. Second, we consider polygonal domains with holes instead of simple polygons.

AI.tldr: Nestling Bird Box problem is ER-complete for polygonal domains (with holes); the proof adapts the ER framework from the Art Gallery problem and is shortened by new tools and allowing holes.
AI.motivation: Determine the exact complexity of placing k non-visibility points inside a polygonal domain, relating geometric configuration problems to the existential theory of the reals (ER) and to real algebraic geometry.
AI.method: Construct polynomial-size reductions from ER instances to instances of the nesting bird box problem using visibility gadgets; extend the reduction to polygonal domains with holes; leverage modern ER techniques and previously developed gadgets to encode polynomial equations into non-visibility constraints.
AI.result: ER-completeness established for the Nesting Bird Box problem; the proof is notably shorter than prior ER-based demonstrations because of new tools and the allowance of holes in polygons.
AI.conclusion: The Nesting Bird Box problem sits squarely in ER, generalizes prior Art Gallery-based reductions to polygonal domains with holes, and demonstrates how advances in the ER framework enable more concise hardness proofs for geometric problems.

title: 2D and 3D Grasp Planners for the GET Asymmetrical Gripper
summary: In this paper, we introduce GET-2D-1.0, a fast grasp planner for the GET asymmetrical gripper that operates from a single-view RGB-D image, using the Ferrari-Canny metric and a novel sampling strategy, and GET-3D-1.0, a mesh-based method using a 3D gripper model and ray-tracing. We evaluate both grasp planners against baselines with physical experiments, which suggest that GET-2D-1.0 can improve over a bounding box baseline by over 40% in lift success, shake survival, and force resistance. Experiments with GET-3D-1.0 suggest slight improvement compared to GET-2D-1.0 on lift success and shake survival, but are more computationally expensive, averaging 17 seconds of planning compared to 683 ms for GET-2D-1.0.

AI.tldr: GET-2D-1.0 provides fast 2D grasp planning for the GET gripper from a single RGB-D view, while GET-3D-1.0 offers a slower, mesh-based 3D approach. Both surpass baselines in physical tests, with GET-2D-1.0 outperforming a bounding-box baseline by >40% in lift, shake, and force resistance, and GET-3D-1.0 delivering modest gains over GET-2D-1.0 but at higher computational cost (≈17 s vs ≈0.68 s).
AI.motivation: Improve robust grasp planning for GET asymmetrical grippers from limited sensory input, balancing speed and geometric accuracy to enable practical manipulation from perception.
AI.method: GET-2D-1.0: fast 2D grasp planning from a single-view RGB-D image using the Ferrari-Canny metric and a novel sampling strategy. GET-3D-1.0: a mesh-based 3D grasp planner employing a 3D gripper model and ray-tracing to evaluate grasps.
AI.result: GET-2D-1.0 achieves >40% improvements over a bounding-box baseline in lift success, shake survival, and force resistance. GET-3D-1.0 shows slight improvements over GET-2D-1.0 in lift success and shake survival but incurs substantially higher planning time (avg 17 s vs 0.683 s).
AI.conclusion: 2D planning delivers a strong speed-accuracy trade-off suitable for real-time grasping, while 3D planning provides incremental accuracy at a significant computational cost; choice depends on available time budget and task requirements.

title: A Survey of Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning with Graph Neural Network-Based Communication
summary: In multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), the integration of a communication mechanism, allowing agents to better learn to coordinate their actions and converge on their objectives by sharing information. Based on an interaction graph, a subclass of methods employs graph neural networks (GNNs) to learn the communication, enabling agents to improve their internal representations by enriching them with information exchanged. With growing research, we note a lack of explicit structure and framework to distinguish and classify MARL approaches with communication based on GNNs. Thus, this paper surveys recent works in this field. We propose a generalized GNN-based communication process with the goal of making the underlying concepts behind the methods more obvious and accessible.

AI.tldr: Survey of GNN-based communication in MARL with a generalized framework to clarify taxonomy and concepts.
AI.motivation: Rapid growth of GNN-based communication in MARL lacks a unified structure to distinguish and classify approaches.
AI.method: Review recent MARL work employing GNNs for communication and propose a generalized GNN-based communication process to unify concepts.
AI.result: Proposes a structural taxonomy/framework to make underlying concepts explicit and accessible.
AI.conclusion: The generalized process aims to help researchers compare methods and understand design choices in GNN-based MARL communication.

title: Evaluating the Alignment Between GeoAI Explanations and Domain Knowledge in Satellite-Based Flood Mapping
summary: The increasing number of satellites has improved the temporal resolution of Earth observation, making satellite-based flood mapping a promising approach for operational flood monitoring. Deep learning-based approaches for flood mapping using satellite imagery, an important application within Geospatial Artificial Intelligence (GeoAI), have shown improved predictive performance by learning complex spatial and spectral patterns from large volumes of remote sensing data. However, the opaque decision-making processes of deep learning models remain a major barrier to their integration into critical scientific and operational workflows. This highlights the need for a systematic assessment of whether model explanations align with established domain knowledge in remote sensing. To address this research gap, this study introduces the ADAGE (Alignment between Domain Knowledge And GeoAI Explanation Evaluation) framework. The proposed framework is designed to systematically evaluate how well explanations of deep learning models align with established remote sensing knowledge, particularly regarding the distinctive spectral properties of the Earth's surface. The ADAGE framework employs Channel-Group SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) method to estimate the contributions of grouped input channels to pixel-level predictions. Experiments on two satellite-based flood mapping tasks demonstrate that the ADAGE framework can (1) quantitatively assess the alignment between model explanations and reference explanations derived from domain knowledge and (2) help domain experts identify misaligned explanations through alignment scores. This study contributes to bridging the gap between explainability and domain knowledge in GeoAI for Earth observation, enhancing the applicability of GeoAI models in scientific and operational workflows.

AI.tldr: ADAGE aligns deep learning explanations with remote-sensing domain knowledge for satellite flood mapping, using Channel-Group SHAP to quantify channel contributions and assess alignment with domain-derived explanations, yielding alignment scores and identifying misaligned explanations.
AI.motivation: To address opacity of deep learning models in GeoAI and improve trust and usability by validating that explanations align with established remote-sensing spectral knowledge.
AI.method: Introduce ADAGE framework; apply Channel-Group SHAP to estimate contributions of grouped input channels to pixel predictions; define alignment scores by comparing model explanations to reference explanations based on domain knowledge about spectral properties; evaluate on two satellite-based flood mapping tasks.
AI.result: ADAGE yields quantitative alignment measures and can identify misaligned explanations; demonstrates that model explanations can be evaluated against domain knowledge and used to guide model interpretation in GeoAI flood mapping.
AI.conclusion: ADAGE bridges explainability and domain knowledge in GeoAI for Earth observation, enhancing adoption in scientific and operational workflows by enabling systematic evaluation of explanations.

title: A proof of Jordan curve theorem based on the sweepline algorithm for trapezoidal decomposition of a polygon
summary: We prove the Jordan curve theorem by generalizing the sweepline algorithm for trapezoidal decomposition of a polygon. Our proof uses Zorn's lemma (or, equivalently the axiom of choice). Though several proofs have been given for the Jordan curve theorem by various authors, ours is the {\bf first algorithmic proof} of Jordan curve theorem using computational geometry. Further, with some preparation, the proof can be taught as part of an undergraduate discrete mathematics course, where till now the proof of this theorem was considered inaccessible.

AI.tldr: First algorithmic proof of Jordan curve theorem via generalized sweepline for trapezoidal decomposition; uses Zorn's lemma (Axiom of Choice); novel in computational geometry and suitable for undergraduate teaching.
AI.motivation: Bridge computational geometry and topology by providing an algorithmic perspective on a classical theorem; enhance accessibility and pedagogy in discrete mathematics.
AI.method: Generalize the sweepline method to produce a trapezoidal decomposition, and use Zorn's lemma to guarantee a maximal, consistent construction; derive that a simple closed curve separates the plane into interior and exterior, yielding an algorithmic proof within a geometric framework.
AI.result: Establishes the Jordan curve theorem with an algorithmic flavor in computational geometry; shows a constructive-like procedure built on a non-constructive existence principle.
AI.conclusion: Presents a novel, teachable approach to the Jordan curve theorem, enabling undergraduate-ready presentation, while noting the reliance on the axiom of choice and the non-constructive aspects inherent in Zorn's lemma.

title: Split over $n$ resource sharing problem: Are fewer capable agents better than many simpler ones?
comment: Short paper presented at the 15th International Conference on Swarm Intelligence (ANTS 2026)
summary: In multi-agent systems, should limited resources be concentrated into a few capable agents or distributed among many simpler ones? This work formulates the split over $n$ resource sharing problem where a group of $n$ agents equally shares a common resource (e.g., monetary budget, computational resources, physical size). We present a case study in multi-agent coverage where the area of the disk-shaped footprint of agents scales as $1/n$. A formal analysis reveals that the initial coverage rate grows with $n$. However, if the speed of agents decreases proportionally with their radii, groups of all sizes perform equally well, whereas if it decreases proportionally with their footprints, a single agent performs best. We also present computer simulations in which resource splitting increases the failure rates of individual agents. The models and findings help identify optimal distributiveness levels and inform the design of multi-agent systems under resource constraints.

AI.tldr: Resource sharing among n agents: equal split of a common resource leads to footprint scaling as 1/n; initial coverage improves with n, but speed scaling governs performance such that if speed scales with radius, all sizes equal; if speed scales with footprint, single agent is best; simulations show increased failure with splitting.
AI.motivation: Understand whether distributing a shared resource among many simple agents yields better multi-agent coverage or if concentrating resources on few capable agents is advantageous, and identify optimal distributiveness under resource constraints.
AI.method: Formal analysis of a split-over-n resource sharing model with disk-shaped footprints of radius scaling as 1/n; analysis of coverage rate and speed scaling; computer simulations evaluating performance and failure rates under different scaling assumptions.
AI.result: Coverage rate initially grows with the number of agents; however, depending on how agent speed scales with size, the advantage of having more agents may vanish or reverse. If speed decreases proportionally with radii, all group sizes perform equivalently; if speed decreases with footprints, a single agent outperforms others. Resource splitting tends to increase individual failure rates in simulations.
AI.conclusion: The formal model helps identify optimal distributiveness levels and informs the design of multi-agent systems operating under resource constraints, highlighting the critical role of how speed scales with agent size.

title: Rethinking KV Cache Eviction via a Unified Information-Theoretic Objective
summary: Key-value (KV) caching is essential for large language model inference, yet its memory overhead poses a critical bottleneck for long-context generation. Existing eviction policies predominantly rely on empirical heuristics, lacking a rigorous theoretical foundation. This work rethinks KV cache eviction through the lens of the Information Bottleneck principle. Under a linear-Gaussian surrogate of attention, we derive a closed-form mutual information objective that characterizes the effective information capacity of a retained KV cache subset. This formulation reveals that a wide range of existing eviction strategies can be interpreted as different approximations of the same capacity-maximization principle. Guided by this insight, we introduce CapKV, a capacity-aware eviction method that directly targets information preservation via a log-determinant approximation using statistical leverage scores. This approach replaces heuristic selection with a theoretically grounded mechanism that preserves the maximum predictive signal. Extensive experiments across multiple models and long-context benchmarks show that CapKV consistently outperforms prior methods, achieving a better trade-off between memory efficiency and generational fidelity.

AI.tldr: CapKV: a capacity-aware KV eviction policy for LLM caches based on Information Bottleneck, using a log-determinant leverage-score approximation to preserve predictive information; outperforms heuristic eviction methods in memory-fidelity trade-offs.
AI.motivation: KV caching for long-context LLMs incurs high memory overhead. Existing eviction policies are heuristic and lack a formal theory. A principled approach can better preserve information relevant to predictions while reducing memory.
AI.method: Model the KV cache within a linear-Gaussian attention surrogate. Derive a closed-form mutual information objective that captures the information capacity of retained KV subsets. Show that many existing policies are approximations to this capacity-maximization. Propose CapKV, which uses a log-determinant approximation with statistical leverage scores to select KV keys that maximize preserved information.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple models and long-context benchmarks shows CapKV consistently achieves better memory efficiency with higher generative fidelity than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: A principled, information-theoretic eviction strategy can outperform heuristic methods for KV caches in LLMs, providing a formal foundation and practical gains in memory–fidelity trade-offs.

title: RADIO-ViPE: Online Tightly Coupled Multi-Modal Fusion for Open-Vocabulary Semantic SLAM in Dynamic Environments
summary: We present RADIO-ViPE (Reduce All Domains Into One -- Video Pose Engine), an online semantic SLAM system that enables geometry-aware open-vocabulary grounding, associating arbitrary natural language queries with localized 3D regions and objects in dynamic environments. Unlike existing approaches that require calibrated, posed RGB-D input, RADIO-ViPE operates directly on raw monocular RGB video streams, requiring no prior camera intrinsics, depth sensors, or pose initialization. The system tightly couples multi-modal embeddings -- spanning vision and language -- derived from agglomerative foundation models (e.g., RADIO) with geometric scene information. This coupling takes place in initialization, optimization and factor graph connections to improve the consistency of the map from multiple modalities. The optimization is wrapped within adaptive robust kernels, designed to handle both actively moving objects and agent-displaced scene elements (e.g., furniture rearranged during ego-centric session). Experiments demonstrate that RADIO-ViPE achieves state-of-the-art results on the dynamic TUM-RGBD benchmark while maintaining competitive performance against offline open-vocabulary methods that rely on calibrated data and static scene assumptions. RADIO-ViPE bridges a critical gap in real-world deployment, enabling robust open-vocabulary semantic grounding for autonomous robotics and unconstrained in-the-wild video streams. Project page: https://be2rlab.github.io/radio_vipe

AI.tldr: An online monocular SLAM with open-vocabulary grounding that fuses vision-language embeddings (from RADIO) with geometry via initialization, optimization, and factor graphs, enabling dynamic-scene semantic mapping without calibrated sensors, achieving SOTA on dynamic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To enable robust, geometry-aware open-vocabulary semantic grounding in real-world robotic perception using raw monocular RGB streams, without relying on camera intrinsics, depth sensors, or pose initialization.
AI.method: Jointly leveraging vision-language embeddings from agglomerative foundation models (e.g., RADIO) and geometric scene information. Integration occurs at initialization, optimization, and via factor-graph connections. The system uses adaptive robust kernels to handle dynamic elements and ego-centric rearrangements, and operates directly on monocular video without depth or pose priors.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on the dynamic TUM-RGBD benchmark and remains competitive with offline open-vocabulary methods that assume calibrated data and static scenes.
AI.conclusion: RADIO-ViPE closes the gap for real-world deployment by providing robust open-vocabulary semantic grounding in unconstrained video streams, suitable for autonomous robotics in dynamic environments.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Persona Induction from User Behavioral Logs: Learning Evidence-Grounded and Truthful Personas
summary: Behavioral logs provide rich signals for user modeling, but are noisy and interleaved across diverse intents. Recent work uses LLMs to generate interpretable natural-language personas from user logs, yet evaluation often emphasizes downstream utility, providing limited assurance of persona quality itself. We propose a hierarchical framework that aggregates user actions into intent memories and induces multiple evidence-grounded personas by clustering and labeling these memories. We formulate persona induction as an optimization problem over persona quality-captured by cluster cohesion, persona-evidence alignment, and persona truthfulness-and train the persona model using a groupwise extension of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Experiments on a large-scale service log and two public datasets show that our method induces more coherent, evidence-grounded, and trustworthy personas, while also improving future interaction prediction.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical persona induction from user logs via intent-memory aggregation, constrained clustering, and groupwise DPO to yield coherent, evidence-grounded, and trustworthy personas that improve future interaction prediction.
AI.motivation: User logs are noisy and multi-intent; need interpretable, reliable user personas beyond downstream utility.
AI.method: Build intent memories from actions; cluster and label memories to form multiple personas; optimize for cluster cohesion, evidence alignment, and truthfulness; train using a groupwise extension of Direct Preference Optimization.
AI.result: Experiments on a large-scale service log and two public datasets show improved persona coherence, evidence grounding, and trustworthiness, along with better future interaction prediction.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that structured, evidence-grounded persona induction can enhance interpretability and predictive performance in user modeling.

title: Exact Dynamic Programming for Solow--Polasky Diversity Subset Selection on Lines and Staircases
summary:
We study exact fixed-cardinality Solow--Polasky diversity subset selection on ordered finite $\ell_1$ sets, with monotone biobjective Pareto fronts and their higher-dimensional staircase analogues as central applications. Solow--Polasky diversity was introduced in biodiversity conservation, whereas the same inverse-matrix expression appears in metric geometry as magnitude: for a finite metric space $(X,d)$ with exponential similarity matrix $Z_{ij}=e^{-q d(x_i,x_j)}$, the quantity $\1^\top Z^{-1}\1$ is the magnitude of the scaled finite metric space $(X,qd)$ whenever the weighting is defined by the inverse matrix. Thus, in this finite exponential-kernel setting, Solow--Polasky diversity and magnitude are mathematically the same object viewed through different motivations. Building on the linear-chain magnitude formula of Leinster and Willerton, we provide a detailed proof of the scaled consecutive-gap identity $ \SP(X)=1+\sum_r \tanh(qg_r/2), $ where the $g_r$ are the gaps between consecutive selected points. We then prove an exact Bellman-recursion theorem for maximizing this value over all subsets of a prescribed cardinality, yielding an $O(kn^2)$ dynamic program for an ordered $n$-point candidate set and subset size $k$. Finally, we prove ordered $\ell_1$ reductions showing that the same algorithm applies to monotone biobjective Pareto-front approximations and, more generally, to finite coordinatewise monotone $\ell_1$ staircases in $\R^d$. These are precisely the ordered $\ell_1$ chains for which the Manhattan metric becomes a line metric along the chosen order, so the one-dimensional dynamic program applies without modification.
  Keywords: Dynamic Programming, Solow--Polasky Diversity, Complexity Theory, Multiobjective Optimization, Pareto front, Magnitude

AI.tldr: Unifies Solow-Polasky diversity with magnitude for finite metric spaces using an exponential kernel; derives a closed-form SP(X) via consecutive gaps, and presents an exact O(k n^2) dynamic program for fixed-cardinality subset selection. Further extends to monotone biobjective Pareto fronts and finite coordinatewise monotone l1 staircases along ordered chains, enabling 1D DP in higher dimensions where Manhattan metric aligns with the order.
AI.motivation: Bridge Solow-Polasky diversity and magnitude theories, and provide an efficient exact algorithm for fixed-cardinality subset selection on ordered sets. Extend the framework to multiobjective Pareto-front approximations via ordered l1 staircases.
AI.method: Model uses exponential similarity Z_ij = e^{-q d(x_i,x_j)}; shows SP(X) equals the magnitude-based quantity 1^T Z^{-1} 1 for the scaled metric space (X, qd). Builds on Leinster–Willerton magnitude formula for linear chains; proves the scaled consecutive-gap identity SP(X)=1+sum_r tanh(q g_r/2) where g_r are gaps between consecutive selected points. Develops an exact Bellman-recursion for maximizing SP(X) under a cardinality constraint, yielding an O(k n^2) DP for an ordered n-point set. Proves ordered l1 reductions so the same DP applies to monotone biobjective Pareto-front approximations and finite coordinatewise monotone l1 staircases in R^d (the l1 chains where Manhattan distance is a line metric).
AI.result: An exact dynamic-programming framework with O(k n^2) time complexity for fixed-cardinality Solow–Polasky diversity maximization on ordered sets; a closed-form SP(X) in terms of gaps; and a demonstrated reduction of higher-dimensional monotone l1 staircase problems to the same 1D DP, unifying diversity and magnitude under the exponential kernel.
AI.conclusion: The work unifies Solow–Polasky diversity with magnitude in finite exponential-kernel spaces and delivers an efficient exact optimization method via Bellman recursion. It extends to monotone biobjective Pareto fronts and finite coordinatewise monotone l1 staircases, enabling scalable dynamic-programming solutions in higher dimensions along ordered chains.

title: Reactive Motion Generation via Phase-varying Neural Potential Functions
comment: Accepted by IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RAL)
summary: Dynamical systems (DS) methods for Learning-from-Demonstration (LfD) provide stable, continuous policies from few demonstrations. First-order dynamical systems (DS) are effective for many point-to-point and periodic tasks, as long as a unique velocity is defined for each state. For tasks with intersections (e.g., drawing an "8"), extensions such as second-order dynamics or phase variables are often used. However, by incorporating velocity, second-order models become sensitive to disturbances near intersections, as velocity is used to disambiguate motion direction. Moreover, this disambiguation may fail when nearly identical position-velocity pairs correspond to different onward motions. In contrast, phase-based methods rely on open-loop time or phase variables, which limit their ability to recover after perturbations. We introduce Phase-varying Neural Potential Functions (PNPF), an LfD framework that conditions a potential function on a phase variable which is estimated directly from state progression, rather than on open-loop temporal inputs. This phase variable allows the system to handle state revisits, while the learned potential function generates local vector fields for reactive and stable control. PNPF generalizes effectively across point-to-point, periodic, and full 6D motion tasks, outperforms existing baselines on trajectories with intersections, and demonstrates robust performance in real-time robotic manipulation under external disturbances.

AI.tldr: PNPF introduces phase-conditioned neural potential fields for LfD, enabling robust, generalizable control across point-to-point, periodic, and 6D motions, including intersections and disturbances.
AI.motivation: DS-based LfD faces interventions: first-order DS require a unique velocity per state; second-order DS rely on velocity to disambiguate directions near intersections, which can fail when position-velocity pairs are ambiguous. Phase-based methods that rely on open-loop time cannot recover after perturbations. A phase derived from state progression could handle revisits and perturbations.
AI.method: Phase-varying Neural Potential Functions (PNPF) condition a potential function on a phase variable estimated from state progression (not open-loop time). The phase variable enables handling state revisits, while the learned potential function provides local vector fields for reactive and stable control.
AI.result: PNPF generalizes across point-to-point, periodic, and full 6D motion tasks; it outperforms baselines on trajectories with intersections; demonstrates robust performance in real-time robotic manipulation under external disturbances.
AI.conclusion: PNPF offers a robust LfD framework that handles state revisits, intersections, and disturbances by conditioning potential fields on an inferred phase, with broad applicability across task types.

title: Mini-Batch Class Composition Bias in Link Prediction
comment: Accepted at GCLR 2026: the 5th Workshop on Graphs and more Complex Structures For Learning and Reasoning, colocated with AAAI 2026
summary: Prior work on node classification has shown that Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) can learn representations that transfer across graphs, when underlying graph properties are shared. For a fixed graph, one would then expect GNNs trained for link prediction to learn a representation consistent with that learnt for node classification. We show this intuition does not hold in the general case. Instead, we find popular link prediction models can learn a trivial mini-batch dependent heuristic, enabled by batch-normalisation layers, to solve the edge classification task. When correcting for this, we observe increased alignment of the network representation with node-class relevant features, suggesting the network has learnt a graph representation that better aligns with the underlying graph's properties. Our findings suggest that standard link prediction training may be leading us to overestimate link predictors' ability to learn a generalised representation of a graph that is consistent across tasks.

AI.tldr: Link prediction models may rely on batch-norm shortcuts to solve edge classification; removing these biases reveals representations more aligned with node-class features, challenging the idea of cross-task transferability.
AI.motivation: Question whether GNNs trained for link prediction learn representations that generalize to node classification across graphs; test the assumption that shared graph properties enable transfer.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of GNN-based link prediction with batch-normalization; identify minibatch-dependent heuristics enabling edge classification; correct for BN shortcuts and re-evaluate representation alignment with node-class features.
AI.result: BN shortcuts inflate edge-classification performance; once corrected, the learned representations align better with node-class relevant features, indicating a graph representation more consistent with underlying properties rather than minibatch artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Standard link prediction training can overestimate cross-task generalization; accounting for batch-norm effects reveals more faithful representations aligned with node-class properties and graph structure.

title: FruitProM-V2: Robust Probabilistic Maturity Estimation and Detection of Fruits and Vegetables
summary: Accurate fruit maturity identification is essential for determining harvest timing, as incorrect assessment directly affects yield and post-harvest quality. Although ripening is a continuous biological process, vision-based maturity estimation is typically formulated as a multi-class classification task, which imposes sharp boundaries between visually similar stages. To examine this limitation, we perform an annotation reliability study with two independent annotators on a held-out tomato dataset and observe disagreement concentrated near adjacent maturity stages. Motivated by this observation, we model maturity as a latent continuous variable and predict it probabilistically using a distributional detection head, converting the distribution into class probabilities through the cumulative distribution function (CDF). The proposed formulation maintains comparable performance to a standard detector under clean labels while better representing uncertainty. Furthermore, when controlled label noise is introduced during training, the probabilistic model demonstrates improved robustness relative to the baseline, indicating that explicitly modeling maturity uncertainty leads to more reliable visual maturity estimation.

AI.tldr: Treat tomato maturity as a latent continuous variable using a distributional detection head; map to class scores with the CDF, yielding competitive accuracy and better uncertainty representation, with improved robustness to label noise.
AI.motivation: Annotator disagreement is concentrated near adjacent maturity stages, suggesting maturity is a continuous attribute rather than a set of discrete classes.
AI.method: Introduce a distributional detection head that predicts a probability distribution over continuous maturity values; convert this distribution to class probabilities via the CDF; train with standard detection losses and assess on clean and noisy labels; compare to a conventional detector.
AI.result: Maintains comparable performance to a standard detector under clean labels; better calibrated uncertainty; shows improved robustness to label noise during training.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling maturity uncertainty as a continuous latent variable improves reliability of visual maturity estimation, particularly under label noise.

title: OMEGA: Optimizing Machine Learning by Evaluating Generated Algorithms
comment: ICLR 2026: Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement
summary: In order to automate AI research we introduce a full, end-to-end framework, OMEGA: Optimizing Machine learning by Evaluating Generated Algorithms, that starts at idea generation and ends with executable code. Our system combines structured meta-prompt engineering with executable code generation to create new ML classifiers. The OMEGA framework has been utilized to generate several novel algorithms that outperform scikit-learn baselines across a robust selection of 20 benchmark datasets (infinity-bench). You can access models discussed in this paper and more in the python package: pip install omega-models.

AI.tldr: End-to-end automated ML research framework (OMEGA) that converts ideas into executable ML classifiers via structured meta-prompting; reports novel algorithms outperforming scikit-learn baselines on 20 datasets; models available via omega-models.
AI.motivation: Automate ML research to accelerate discovery and deployment, reducing manual design bottlenecks and enabling systematic exploration of algorithm space.
AI.method: End-to-end framework from idea generation to executable code; uses meta-prompt engineering and code generation to produce ML classifiers; evaluation against scikit-learn baselines on 20 datasets (infinity-bench); release via python package omega-models.
AI.result: Generated several novel algorithms that outperform scikit-learn baselines across 20 benchmark datasets; models and code accessible via pip package.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of automated ML research pipelines; potential to accelerate ML algorithm discovery and deployment; further work needed on generalization, reproducibility, and broader benchmarking.

title: Alter-Art: Exploring Embodied Artistic Creation through a Robot Avatar
summary: As with every emerging technology, new tools in the hands of artists reshape the nature of artwork creation. Current frameworks for robotics in arts deploy the robot as an autonomous creator or a collaborator, thus leaving a certain gap between the human artist and the machine. Now, we stand at the dawn of an era where artists can escape physical limitations and reshape their creative identity by inhabiting an alternative body. This new paradigm allows artists not only to command a robot remotely, but also to {\it be} a robot, to see and feel through it, experiencing a new embodied reality. Unlike virtual reality, where art is created in a digital dimension, in this case art creation is still firmly grounded in the material world: clay molded by mechanical hands, paint swept across a canvas or gestures performed on a physical stage alongside human actors. Through the robot avatar Alter-Ego, we explore the Alter-Art paradigm in dance, theater, and painting; it integrates immersive teleoperation and compliant actuation to enable a first-person creative experience. Analyzing qualitative artistic feedback, we investigate how embodiment shapes creative agency, identity and interaction with the environment. Our findings suggest that artists rapidly develop a sense of presence within the robotic body. The robot's physical constraints influence the creative process, manifesting differently across artistic domains. We highlight embodiment as a central design principle, contributing to social robotics and expanding the possibilities for telepresence and accessible artistic expression.

AI.tldr: Introduces Alter-Ego, a robot avatar that lets artists inhabit an alternative body to create art via immersive teleoperation and compliant actuation, exploring embodiment, presence, and creative agency across dance, theater, and painting; positions embodiment as a central design principle expanding telepresence and accessible artistic expression.
AI.motivation: Current robotics-in-arts frameworks treat robots as autonomous creators or collaborators, leaving a gap between human artist and machine. The work argues for an embodied, first-person creative experience to overcome physical limits and reshape artistic identity through a robotic body.
AI.method: Utilizes the Alter-Ego robot avatar to enable immersive teleoperation and compliant actuation; applies the paradigm to dance, theater, and painting; gathers qualitative artistic feedback to examine how embodiment affects presence, agency, and interaction with the environment.
AI.result: Artists rapidly report a sense of presence within the robotic body; the robot’s physical constraints guide the creative process, with variations across artistic domains; embodiment emerges as a central facet of design, influencing social robotics, telepresence, and accessible artistic expression.
AI.conclusion: Embodiment is a central design principle for robot-mediated art, enabling a first-person creative experience, shaping agency and interaction, and broadening possibilities for telepresence, performance, and cross-domain artistic practice.

title: Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management
summary: Frontier AI both amplifies existing risks and introduces qualitatively novel challenges. Not only is there a notable lack of stable scientific consensus resulting from the rapid pace of technological change, but emerging frontier AI safety practices are often misaligned with, or may undermine, established risk management frameworks. To address these challenges, we systematically surface open problems in frontier AI risk management. Adopting a problem-oriented approach, we examine each stage of the risk management process - risk planning, identification, analysis, evaluation, and mitigation - through a structured review of the literature, identifying unresolved challenges and the actors best positioned to address them. Recognising that different types of open problems call for different responses, we classify open problems according to whether they reflect (a) a lack of scientific or technical consensus, (b) misalignment with, or challenges to, established risk management frameworks, or (c) shortcomings in implementation despite apparent consensus and alignment. By mapping these open problems and identifying the actors best positioned to address them - including developers, deployers, regulators, standards bodies, researchers, and third-party evaluators - this work aims to clarify where progress is needed to enable robust and meaningful consensus on frontier AI risk management.The paper does not propose specific solutions; instead, it provides a problem-oriented, agenda-setting reference document, complemented by a living online repository, intended to support coordination, reduce duplication, and guide future research and governance efforts.

AI.tldr: A problem-oriented, agenda-setting analysis of frontier AI risk management that catalogs open challenges across risk-management stages, classifies them by consensus/misalignment/implementation, identifies key actors, and provides a living repository to guide governance without proposing solutions.
AI.motivation: Frontier AI accelerates capabilities and risks; rapid development hampers stable scientific consensus and alignment with established risk-management frameworks. There is a need to surface open problems to coordinate governance and minimize duplicated effort.
AI.method: Structured literature review across risk management stages (planning, identification, analysis, evaluation, mitigation); classify open problems into three types (lack of scientific/technical consensus, misalignment with established risk-management frameworks, or implementation shortcomings); map actors (developers, deployers, regulators, standards bodies, researchers, evaluators); produce an agenda-setting reference document and a living online repository.
AI.result: A mapped compilation of open problems and actor roles, serving as an agenda-setting reference without prescribing solutions; complemented by a living online repository to guide future research and governance efforts.
AI.conclusion: Provides a framework to prioritize future work and coordinate governance in frontier AI risk management, targeting multiple stakeholders and facilitating robust consensus and effective risk mitigation; emphasizes problem-clarification over immediate solutions.

title: Sample Selection Using Multi-Task Autoencoders in Federated Learning with Non-IID Data
comment: Published in Engineering Science and Technology, an International Journal, 61 (2025), 101920. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jestch.2024.101920 and Codes: https://github.com/eardic/FL_DPQS
summary: Federated learning is a machine learning paradigm in which multiple devices collaboratively train a model under the supervision of a central server while ensuring data privacy. However, its performance is often hindered by redundant, malicious, or abnormal samples, leading to model degradation and inefficiency. To overcome these issues, we propose novel sample selection methods for image classification, employing a multitask autoencoder to estimate sample contributions through loss and feature analysis. Our approach incorporates unsupervised outlier detection, using one-class support vector machine (OCSVM), isolation forest (IF), and adaptive loss threshold (AT) methods managed by a central server to filter noisy samples on clients. We also propose a multi-class deep support vector data description (SVDD) loss controlled by a central server to enhance feature-based sample selection. We validate our methods on CIFAR10 and MNIST datasets across varying numbers of clients, non-IID distributions, and noise levels up to 40%. The results show significant accuracy improvements with loss-based sample selection, achieving gains of up to 7.02% on CIFAR10 with OCSVM and 1.83% on MNIST with AT. Additionally, our federated SVDD loss further improves feature-based sample selection, yielding accuracy gains of up to 0.99% on CIFAR10 with OCSVM. These results show the effectiveness of our methods in improving model accuracy across various client counts and noise conditions.

AI.tldr: Federated learning with multitask autoencoder-based sample contribution estimation plus centralized outlier detection (OCSVM, Isolation Forest, adaptive loss) and SVDD-based feature selection improves image classification accuracy under noisy, non-IID client data, validated on CIFAR-10 and MNIST with varying client counts and noise levels up to 40%.
AI.motivation: Address performance degradation in Federated Learning caused by redundant, malicious, or abnormal samples by improving sample selection and filtering to maintain model accuracy and efficiency.
AI.method: Employ a multitask autoencoder to estimate sample contributions via reconstruction loss and feature analysis. Central server orchestrates unsupervised outlier detectors (OCSVM, Isolation Forest, adaptive loss threshold) to filter noisy samples on clients. Introduce a multi-class deep SVDD loss controlled by the server to enhance feature-based sample selection. Evaluate on CIFAR-10 and MNIST across different numbers of clients, non-IID distributions, and noise levels up to 40%.
AI.result: Loss-based sample selection yields significant accuracy gains (up to 7.02 percentage points on CIFAR-10 with OCSVM; up to 1.83 pp on MNIST with AT). Feature-based selection via federated SVDD loss provides additional gains (up to 0.99 pp on CIFAR-10 with OCSVM). These improvements persist across varying client counts and noise conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed combination of loss-based and feature-based sample selection methods effectively mitigates the adverse impact of noisy and non-IID data in federated image classification, enhancing model accuracy.

title: Persuadability and LLMs as Legal Decision Tools
comment: Accepted to 21st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Law (ICAIL 2026)
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are proposed as legal decision assistants, and even first-instance decision-makers, across a range of judicial and administrative contexts, it becomes essential to explore how they answer legal questions, and in particular the factors that lead them to decide difficult questions in one way or another. A specific feature of legal decisions is the need to respond to arguments advanced by contending parties. A legal decision-maker must be able to engage with, and respond to, including through being potentially persuaded by, arguments advanced by the parties. Conversely, they should not be unduly persuadable, influenced by a particularly compelling advocate to decide cases based on the skills of the advocates, rather than the merits of the case. We explore how frontier open- and closed-weights LLMs respond to legal arguments, reporting original experimental results examining how the quality of the advocate making those arguments affects the likelihood that a model will agree with a particular legal point of view, and exploring the factors driving these results. Our results have implications for the feasibility of adopting LLMs across legal and administrative settings.
