AI.tldr: LLMs' response to legal arguments is influenced by advocate quality; experiments show susceptibility to persuasion; implications for deploying LLMs in legal decisions.
AI.motivation: Assess how frontier LLMs handle legal arguments and whether they can resist undue persuasion; examine open- vs closed-weights models; inform deployment decisions.
AI.method: Original experiments exposing models to arguments from varying advocate quality; measure likelihood of agreeing with specific legal viewpoints; compare across model types; analyze driving factors.
AI.result: Advocate quality modulates model's agreement with legal points; results differ by model type; factors include model weights and prompting; potential undue influence risk identified.
AI.conclusion: LLMs have potential as decision aids but require safeguards to mitigate persuasive manipulation; policy and methodological considerations for adoption in legal contexts.

title: HiPAN: Hierarchical Posture-Adaptive Navigation for Quadruped Robots in Unstructured 3D Environments
summary: Navigating quadruped robots in unstructured 3D environments poses significant challenges, requiring goal-directed motion, effective exploration to escape from local minima, and posture adaptation to traverse narrow, height-constrained spaces. Conventional approaches employ a sequential mapping-planning pipeline but suffer from accumulated perception errors and high computational overhead, restricting their applicability on resource-constrained platforms. To address these challenges, we propose Hierarchical Posture-Adaptive Navigation (HiPAN), a framework that operates directly on onboard depth images at deployment. HiPAN adopts a hierarchical design: a high-level policy generates strategic navigation commands (planar velocity and body posture), which are executed by a low-level, posture-adaptive locomotion controller. To mitigate myopic behaviors and facilitate long-horizon navigation, we introduce Path-Guided Curriculum Learning, which progressively extends the navigation horizon from reactive obstacle avoidance to strategic navigation. In simulation, HiPAN achieves higher navigation success rates and greater path efficiency than classical reactive planners and end-to-end baselines, while real-world experiments further validate its applicability across diverse, unstructured 3D environments.

AI.tldr: HiPAN introduces a hierarchical, onboard-depth-image navigation framework for quadrupeds, featuring a high-level planner that outputs plan velocity and body posture and a posture-adaptive low-level controller, augmented by Path-Guided Curriculum Learning to enable long-horizon goals; it shows superior simulation performance and real-world feasibility over reactive and end-to-end baselines.
AI.motivation: Overcome the shortcomings of conventional sequential mapping-planning pipelines in 3D unstructured environments, which suffer from perception errors and high computational load and are unsuitable for resource-constrained platforms. Enable goal-directed, robust locomotion using onboard depth sensors and posture adaptation.
AI.method: Hierarchical architecture with a high-level policy producing planar velocity and body posture commands, executed by a low-level posture-adaptive locomotion controller; deployment operates directly on onboard depth images. Introduces Path-Guided Curriculum Learning to progressively extend navigation horizon from reactive obstacle avoidance to strategic navigation.
AI.result: In simulation, HiPAN achieves higher navigation success rates and greater path efficiency than classical reactive planners and end-to-end baselines. Real-world experiments validate applicability across diverse, unstructured 3D environments.
AI.conclusion: HiPAN demonstrates effective onboard-depth-image-based navigation for quadrupeds in unstructured 3D spaces, with improved performance and real-world feasibility. The curriculum learning enables long-horizon planning while maintaining computational efficiency on resource-constrained platforms.

title: Correcting Performance Estimation Bias in Imbalanced Classification with Minority Subconcepts
summary: Class-level evaluation can conceal substantial performance disparities across subconcepts within the same class, causing models that perform well on average to fail on specific subpopulations. Prior work has shown that common evaluation measures for imbalanced classification are biased toward larger minority subconcepts and that utility-based reweighting using true subconcept labels can mitigate this bias; however, such labels are rarely available at test time. We introduce a practical utility-weighted evaluation that replaces unavailable subconcept labels with predicted posterior probabilities from a multiclass subconcept model. Evaluation weights are defined as the expected utility under this posterior, yielding a soft, uncertainty-aware metric we call predicted-weighted balanced accuracy (pBA). Experiments on tabular benchmarks as well as medical-imaging and text datasets show that unweighted scores can be misleading under within-class heterogeneity, while pBA provides more stable and interpretable assessments when subconcept distributions are uneven but not pathological. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/correcting-bias-imbalance-9C6C/.

AI.tldr: Introduces predicted-weighted balanced accuracy (pBA), a practical, uncertainty-aware evaluation that uses predicted subconcept posteriors to weight accuracy, addressing within-class heterogeneity without true subconcept labels.
AI.motivation: Class-level evaluation hides performance disparities across subconcepts; existing imbalanced metrics biased toward larger subconcepts; true-subconcept labels help but are unavailable at test time. Need a soft, posterior-based weighting.
AI.method: Define pBA as the expected utility under the predicted posterior over subconcepts from a multiclass subconcept model; compute weights from posterior, yielding a soft weighting of class accuracy; evaluate on tabular, medical imaging, and text datasets.
AI.result: Unweighted metrics can mislead under within-class heterogeneity; pBA provides more stable, interpretable assessments when subconcept distributions are uneven but not pathological; code available.
AI.conclusion: pBA offers a practical, utility-based, uncertainty-aware evaluation that balances within-class subpopulation performance without true subconcept labels, suitable for imbalanced, heterogeneous data.

title: MixerCA: An Efficient and Accurate Model for High-Performance Hyperspectral Image Classification
comment: Preprint accepted for publication in "Remote Sensing Applications: Society and Environment" Journal
summary: Over the past decade, hyperspectral image (HSI) classification has drawn considerable interest due to HSIs' ability to effectively distinguish terrestrial objects by capturing detailed, continuous spectral information. The strong performance of recent deep learning techniques in tasks like image classification and semantic segmentation has led to their growing use in HSI classification, due to their ability to capture complex spatial and spectral features more effectively than traditional methods. This paper presents MixerCA, a novel lightweight model for HSI classification that leverages depthwise convolution and a self-attention mechanism. MixerCA integrates depth-wise convolutions, token and channel mixing, and coordinate attention into a unified structure to decouple spatial and channel interactions, maintain consistent resolution throughout the network, and directly process HSI patches. Extensive experiments on four hyperspectral benchmark datasets reveal MixerCA's clear advantages over several competing algorithms, including 2D-CNN, 3D-CNN, Tri-CNN, HybridSN, ViT, and Swin Transformer. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/mqalkhatib/MixerCA.

AI.tldr: MixerCA is a lightweight hyperspectral image classifier that combines depthwise convolution, token and channel mixing, and coordinate attention to decouple spatial and channel interactions, while preserving resolution. It outperforms several baselines on four HSI datasets, and its source code is publicly available.
AI.motivation: HSI data provide rich spectral and spatial information, but effective deep-learning models often incur high computational cost. There is a need for lightweight architectures that efficiently capture spatial–spectral features and maintain the original resolution. A unified mixer–attention approach aims to reduce parameter count while enhancing feature interaction.
AI.method: A unified architecture (MixerCA) that integrates depthwise convolutions, token mixing, channel mixing, and coordinate attention to decouple spatial and channel interactions. The model processes HSI patches directly and maintains constant resolution throughout, avoiding aggressive downsampling. It leverages depthwise conv for local spatial processing and a self-attention-like mechanism (coordinate attention) for long-range dependencies and channel-spatial coupling.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four hyperspectral benchmark datasets show MixerCA achieving clear advantages over multiple baselines, including 2D-CNN, 3D-CNN, Tri-CNN, HybridSN, ViT, and Swin Transformer.
AI.conclusion: MixerCA provides a compact, effective HSI classifier that preserves resolution while efficiently modeling spatial and spectral interactions, outperforming established baselines. The approach is open-source (code at the provided GitHub URL).

title: Apriori-based Analysis of Learned Helplessness in Mathematics Tutoring: Behavioral Patterns by Level, Intervention, and Outcome
summary: This study applied the Apriori algorithm to analyze behavioral interaction patterns associated with learned helplessness (LH) in mathematics tutoring system logs. Interaction data were examined across three dimensions: LH level (low vs. high), system-based intervention (with vs. without), and problem-solving outcomes (solved vs. unsolved). The analysis of the complete dataset showed that skipping problems without using hints was the most frequent pattern linked to unsolved outcomes, while persistence behaviors such as not skipping were less dominant overall. Comparisons by LH level showed that low-LH students had stronger links between problem solving and not skipping, as well as positive associations between hint use and solved outcomes. High-LH students showed more avoidance patterns, with skipping strongly tied to unsolved outcomes. In the comparison of system-based intervention conditions, students without intervention had the highest lift for persistence-success links, while the with-intervention group had stronger patterns involving skipping behaviors leading to unsolved outcomes. Outcome-specific analysis showed that not skipping was consistently associated with solved problems across all groups, while skipping without hints predicted unsolved outcomes. Practical implications and recommendations are discussed.

AI.tldr: Apriori mining of tutoring logs reveals distinct interaction patterns linked to learned helplessness (LH) and system intervention; skipping without hints is most associated with unsolved problems, with low-LH showing stronger problem-solving links to not skipping and hint use, while high-LH shows more avoidance (skipping) linked to failures. Intervention reduces persistence-success associations but increases skipping-unsolved patterns; not skipping consistently correlates with solved problems across groups.
AI.motivation: Identify frequent behavioral interaction patterns associated with LH in a math tutoring system and determine how system-based interventions influence these patterns and outcomes.
AI.method: Apply the Apriori algorithm to logs partitioned by LH level (low vs. high), intervention condition (with vs. without), and problem outcome (solved vs. unsolved). Examine frequent patterns and lift for links among actions (e.g., skipping, not skipping, hint use) and outcomes.
AI.result: Across the full dataset, skipping problems without hints was the most frequent pattern linked to unsolved outcomes; persistence behaviors (not skipping) were less dominant. Low-LH: stronger links between solving and not skipping; positive association between hint use and solving. High-LH: more avoidance patterns; skipping tightly linked to unsolved. No-intervention condition showed higher lift for persistence->solved; with-intervention showed stronger patterns where skipping led to unsolved. Outcome-specific: not skipping consistently associated with solved problems; skipping without hints predicted unsolved.
AI.conclusion: Implications emphasize reducing skipping and promoting productive hint use; tailor interventions by LH level; design tutoring systems to support persistence and strategic hint utilization; acknowledge limitations of observational conditioning and call for further research to generalize and refine interventions.

title: 3D Generation for Embodied AI and Robotic Simulation: A Survey
comment: 26 pages, 11 figures, 8 tables. Project Page: https://3dgen4robot.github.io
summary: Embodied AI and robotic systems increasingly depend on scalable, diverse, and physically grounded 3D content for simulation-based training and real-world deployment. While 3D generative modeling has advanced rapidly, embodied applications impose requirements far beyond visual realism: generated objects must carry kinematic structure and material properties, scenes must support interaction and task execution, and the resulting content must bridge the gap between simulation and reality. This survey presents the first survey of 3D generation for embodied AI and organizes the literature around three roles that 3D generation plays in embodied systems. In \emph{Data Generator}, 3D generation produces simulation-ready objects and assets, including articulated, physically grounded, and deformable content for downstream interaction; in \emph{Simulation Environments}, it constructs interactive and task-oriented worlds, spanning structure-aware, controllable, and agentic scene generation; and in \emph{Sim2Real Bridge}, it supports digital twin reconstruction, data augmentation, and synthetic demonstrations for downstream robot learning and real-world transfer. We also show that the field is shifting from visual realism toward interaction readiness, and we identify the main bottlenecks, including limited physical annotations, the gap between geometric quality and physical validity, fragmented evaluation, and the persistent sim-to-real divide, that must be addressed for 3D generation to become a dependable foundation for embodied intelligence. Our project page is at https://3dgen4robot.github.io.

AI.tldr: A survey of 3D generation for embodied AI, organizing literature into three roles—Data Generator, Simulation Environments, and Sim2Real Bridge—highlighting a shift from visual realism to interaction readiness and outlining bottlenecks hindering deployability.
AI.motivation: Embodied AI requires scalable, diverse, and physically grounded 3D content that supports kinematics, materials, interaction, and real-world transfer, not just visual fidelity.
AI.method: Literature survey that categorizes 3D generation work by its role in embodied systems, analyzes current capabilities, gaps, and evaluation practices, and synthesizes a roadmap for data, environments, and real-to-sim transfer; includes a project page.
AI.result: Proposes a three-role taxonomy for 3D generation in embodied AI; documents a trend toward interaction-ready content; identifies bottlenecks (limited physical annotations, mismatch between geometry and physics, fragmented evaluation, sim-to-real gap); outlines challenges and directions for improving 3D generation as a foundation for embodied intelligence.
AI.conclusion: Advancing 3D generation for embodied AI requires addressing physical annotation scarcity, ensuring physical validity alongside geometric quality, standardizing evaluations, and bridging sim-to-real gaps to make generated 3D content a dependable asset for real-world embodied systems.

title: RaMP: Runtime-Aware Megakernel Polymorphism for Mixture-of-Experts
summary: The optimal kernel configuration for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) inference depends on both batch size and the expert routing distribution, yet production systems dispatch from batch size alone, leaving 10-70% of kernel throughput unrealized. We present RaMP, a routing-aware dispatch framework. A performance-region analysis derives, from hardware constants alone, when each optimization helps, correctly predicting all 8 tested architectures, including 3 unseen. A four-parameter wave cost model selects the fastest configuration from the runtime expert histogram, achieving 0.93% mean regret versus exhaustive search, fitted from just 10-24 minutes of one-time profiling per model. Because the model depends only on CTA grid geometry, it is kernel-agnostic: applied to Alpha-MoE, it delivers 1.14x with no source modification. Paired with a co-designed CuTe DSL kernel exposing 134-268 polymorphic configurations, RaMP delivers 1.22x kernel speedup over static dispatch and 1.30x end-to-end speedup in vLLM serving over Triton, 1.41x over DeepGEMM, and 1.13x over FlashInfer CUTLASS.

AI.tldr: RaMP provides routing-aware dispatch for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) inference by combining a performance-region analysis with a four-parameter wave cost model and a runtime expert histogram, enabling near-optimal kernel configurations with minimal profiling and delivering substantial speedups across backends.
AI.motivation: In production MoE inference, kernel optimization is driven by batch size alone, ignoring the expert routing distribution; this leaves 10-70% of potential kernel throughput unrealized. Routing-aware optimization is needed to exploit throughput across varying routing patterns.
AI.method: Derive a performance-region analysis from hardware constants to predict when each optimization helps across 8 architectures (including 3 unseen). Develop a four-parameter wave cost model that selects the fastest kernel configuration from the runtime expert histogram, with profiling of 10–24 minutes per model. Make the model kernel-agnostic by relying on CTA grid geometry. Co-design CuTe DSL kernel exposing 134–268 polymorphic configurations. Evaluate on Alpha-MoE and multiple backends (vLLM with Triton, DeepGEMM, FlashInfer CUTLASS).
AI.result: The framework correctly predicts optimization usefulness across all tested architectures (8 total, 3 unseen). It achieves a mean regret of 0.93% versus exhaustive search, using only 10–24 minutes of one-time profiling. With kernel-agnostic modeling and CuTe DSL, RaMP delivers 1.14× end-to-end speedup on Alpha-MoE without source changes, 1.22× kernel speedup over static dispatch, and 1.30× end-to-end speedup in vLLM serving over Triton, 1.41× over DeepGEMM, and 1.13× over FlashInfer CUTLASS.
AI.conclusion: Routing-aware dispatch guided by a simple, hardware-grounded model plus a co-designed DSL kernel yields near-optimal configurations and substantial practical speedups for MoE inference, with modest profiling overhead and kernel-agnostic applicability.

title: A Data-Centric Framework for Intraoperative Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging for Glioma Surgical Guidance
summary: Accurate intraoperative assessment of glioma infiltration is essential for maximizing tumor resection while preserving functional brain tissue. Fluorescence lifetime imaging (FLIm) offers real-time, label-free biochemical contrast, but its clinical utility is challenged by biological heterogeneity, class imbalance, and variability in histopathological labeling. We present a data-centric AI (DC-AI) framework that integrates confident learning (CL), class refinement, and targeted label evaluation to develop a robust multi-class FLIm classifier for glioblastoma (GBM) resection margins. FLIm data were collected from 192 tissue margins across 31 newly diagnosed IDH-wildtype GBM patients and initially labeled into seven tumor cellularity classes by an expert neuropathologist. CL was applied to quantify FLIm point-level confidence, identify label inconsistencies, and guide iterative class merging into a three-class scheme ("low", "moderate", "high"). The resulting high-fidelity dataset enabled training a model that achieved 96% accuracy in the three-class task. SHAP analysis revealed class-specific FLIm feature importance, highlighting distinct optical signatures across the infiltration spectrum. Targeted FLIm analysis further identified biological (e.g., gray matter composition) and acquisition-related (e.g., blood contamination) contributors to low-confidence predictions. Blinded re-evaluation of margins flagged by CL demonstrated intra-pathologist variability, underscoring the value of selective relabeling rather than exhaustive review. Together, these findings demonstrate that a DC-AI framework can systematically improve data reliability, enhance model robustness, and refine biological interpretation of FLIm signals, supporting the development of clinically actionable optical tools for real-time glioma margin assessment.

AI.tldr: A data-centric AI framework using confident learning, class refinement, and targeted label evaluation improves FLIm-based multiclass glioma margin classification, achieving 96% accuracy on a three-class scheme and revealing label reliability issues and interpretable feature signatures.
AI.motivation: Intraoperative glioma margin assessment requires robust, real-time, label-free biophysical contrast. FLIm offers potential, but data label noise, class imbalance, and histopath labeling variability hinder clinical translation. A data-centric approach aims to stabilize labels, refine classes, and improve model robustness.
AI.method: Collect FLIm data from 192 tissue margins across 31 IDH-wildtype GBM patients. Initial seven tumor cellularity classes labeled by an expert pathologist. Apply confident learning (CL) to quantify point-level confidence, detect label inconsistencies, and guide iterative class merging into a three-class scheme (low/moderate/high). Train a classifier on the high-fidelity data. Use SHAP to identify class-specific feature importance. Conduct targeted analysis of low-confidence cases to identify biological and acquisition contributors. Blind re-evaluation of CL-flagged margins to assess intra-pathologist variability and the value of selective relabeling.
AI.result: A robust three-class FLIm classifier achieving 96% accuracy. SHAP revealed distinct optical signatures per infiltration level. Identified factors contributing to low-confidence predictions (e.g., gray matter, blood contamination). Blind re-evaluation showed intra-pathologist variability, supporting selective relabeling over exhaustive review.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric AI framework can systematically improve data reliability and model robustness for FLIm-based glioma margin assessment, enabling clinically actionable optical tools for real-time surgical guidance.

title: DreamProver: Evolving Transferable Lemma Libraries via a Wake-Sleep Theorem-Proving Agent
summary: We introduce DreamProver, an agentic framework that leverages a "wake-sleep" program induction paradigm to discover reusable lemmas for formal theorem proving. Existing approaches either rely on fixed lemma libraries, which limit adaptability, or synthesize highly specific intermediate lemmas tailored to individual theorems, thereby lacking generality. DreamProver addresses this gap through an iterative two-stage process. In the wake stage, DreamProver attempts to prove theorems from a training set using the current lemma library while proposing new candidate lemmas. In the "sleep" stage, it abstracts, refines, and consolidates these candidates to compress and optimize the library. Through this alternating cycle, DreamProver progressively evolves a compact set of high-level, transferable lemmas that can be effectively used to prove unseen theorems in related domains. Experimental results demonstrate that DreamProver substantially improves proof success rates across a diverse set of mathematical benchmarks, while also producing more concise proofs and reducing computational cost.

AI.tldr: DreamProver introduces a wake-sleep program induction framework to learn compact, reusable lemmas for formal theorem proving. It alternates between a wake stage that proves training theorems and proposes candidate lemmas, and a sleep stage that abstracts and consolidates these candidates into a transferable lemma library, iterating to improve generalization. Experimental results show higher proof success, more concise proofs, and lower computational cost across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap between rigid fixed lemma libraries and highly tailor-made intermediate lemmas by learning a compact, transferable set of lemmas that generalize to unseen theorems in related domains.
AI.method: An iterative wake-sleep loop. In the wake stage, the prover uses the current lemma library to prove training theorems and proposes new candidate lemmas. In the sleep stage, it abstracts, refines, and consolidates these candidates to compress and optimize the library, producing a compact, high-level lemma set that is transferable across related tasks.
AI.result: DreamProver substantially improves proof success rates across diverse mathematical benchmarks, yields more concise proofs, and reduces computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The wake-sleep paradigm yields a progressively refined, transferable lemma library that generalizes to unseen theorems, enabling more efficient automated theorem proving.

title: LLM-Flax : Generalizable Robotic Task Planning via Neuro-Symbolic Approaches with Large Language Models
summary: Deploying a neuro-symbolic task planner on a new domain today requires significant manual effort: a domain expert must author relaxation and complementary rules, and hundreds of training problems must be solved to supervise a Graph Neural Network (GNN) object scorer. We propose LLM-Flax, a three-stage framework that eliminates all three sources of manual effort using a locally hosted LLM given only a PDDL domain file. Stage 1 automatically generates relaxation and complementary rules via structured prompting with format validation and self-correction. Stage 2 introduces LLM-guided failure recovery with a feasibility-gated budget policy that explicitly reserves API latency cost before each LLM call, preventing the downstream relaxation fallback from being starved. Stage 3 replaces the domain-trained GNN entirely with zero-shot LLM object importance scoring, requiring no training data. We evaluate all three stages on the MazeNamo benchmark across 10x10, 12x12, and 15x15 grids (8 benchmarks total). LLM-Flax achieves average SR 0.945 versus the manual baseline's 0.828 (+0.117), matching or outperforming manual rules on every one of the eight benchmarks. On 12x12 Expert, LLM-Flax attains SR 0.733 where the manual planner fails entirely (SR 0.000); on 15x15 Hard, it achieves SR 1.000 versus Manual's 0.900. Stage 3 demonstrates feasibility (SR 0.720 on 12x12 Hard with no training data) but faces a context-window bottleneck at scale, pointing to the primary open challenge for future work.

AI.tldr: LLM-Flax is a three-stage framework that eliminates manual domain engineering for neuro-symbolic task planning by using a locally hosted LLM with a PDDL domain: automatic rule generation, failure-aware LLM guidance, and zero-shot LLM object scoring, achieving strong SR gains across MazeNamo benchmarks and revealing a context-window bottleneck for large-scale tasks.
AI.motivation: Reduce manual effort in adapting neuro-symbolic planners to new domains, removing the need for domain experts to author relaxation/complementary rules and large amounts of training problems for a GNN.
AI.method: Stage 1: structured prompting with self-correcting generation of relaxation and complementary rules. Stage 2: LLM-guided failure recovery with a feasibility-gated budget policy to reserve API latency before each LLM call, preventing relaxation fallback starvation. Stage 3: replace domain-trained GNN with zero-shot LLM object importance scoring; no training data required. Evaluation on MazeNamo across 10x10, 12x12, 15x15 grids (8 benchmarks).
AI.result: Avg SR improves from 0.828 (manual) to 0.945 (LLM-Flax). LLM-Flax matches or exceeds manual rules on all eight benchmarks. On 12x12 Expert, SR=0.733 vs 0.000 for manual. On 15x15 Hard, SR=1.000 vs 0.900. Stage 3 yields SR=0.720 on 12x12 Hard with no training data, but context-window bottleneck limits scaling.
AI.conclusion: LLM-Flax effectively replaces manual rule authoring and training data for neuro-symbolic planners, achieving superior performance in many settings; Stage 3 demonstrates feasibility of zero-shot scoring but highlights a key bottleneck—context window size—that future work must address.

title: Observable Neural ODEs for Identifiable Causal Forecasting in Continuous Time
summary: Causal inference in continuous-time sequential decision problems is challenged by hidden confounders. We show that, in latent state-space models with time-varying interventions, observability of the latent dynamics from observed data is necessary for identifying dynamic treatment effects, linking control-theoretic observability to causal identifiability, even when hidden confounders affect both treatments and outcomes. We derive a continuous-time adjustment formula expressing potential outcome distributions under treatment trajectories via the measurement model, latent dynamics, and the filtering distribution over latent states given observed histories. We propose Observable Neural ODEs (ObsNODEs), Neural ODE models in observable normal form for causal forecasting. ObsNODEs learn continuous-time dynamics with states reconstructible from observations, enabling outcome prediction under alternative treatment paths. Experiments on synthetic cancer data, semi-synthetic data based on MIMIC-IV, and real-world sepsis data show strong performance over recent sequence models.

AI.tldr: Establishes that latent-state observability is necessary for identifying dynamic treatment effects in continuous-time latent models; introduces Observable Neural ODEs (ObsNODEs) and a continuous-time adjustment formula for causal forecasting under interventions, with empirical validation on synthetic and real-world data.
AI.motivation: Hidden confounders and time-varying interventions in continuous-time decision problems impede causal identifiability; linking control-theoretic observability to causal inference offers a principled path to external validity.
AI.method: Proves that observability of latent dynamics from observations is required for identifying dynamic treatment effects; derives a continuous-time adjustment formula using the measurement model, latent dynamics, and the filtering distribution; proposes ObsNODEs—Neural ODEs in observable normal form—that learn reconstructible continuous-time dynamics from data; uses these to forecast outcomes under alternative treatment trajectories.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic cancer data, semi-synthetic MIMIC-IV data, and real sepsis data shows strong performance compared to recent sequence models, indicating practical viability of observability-driven causal forecasting.
AI.conclusion: Observability of latent states is key to causal identifiability in continuous time; ObsNODEs provide a practical modeling approach for outcome forecasting under interventions, enabling robust causal analysis in settings with hidden confounding.

title: Why Domain Matters: A Preliminary Study of Domain Effects in Underwater Object Detection
comment: Poster Presentation at ICRA 2026 Workshop S2S
summary: Domain shift, where deviations between training and deployment data distributions degrade model performance, is a key challenge in underwater environments. Existing benchmarks testing performance for underwater domain shift simulate variability through synthetic style transfer. This fails to capture intrinsic scene factors such as visibility, illumination, scene composition, or acquisition factors, limiting analysis of real-world effects. We propose a labeling framework that defines underwater domains using measurable image, scene, and acquisition characteristics. Unlike prior benchmarks, it captures physically meaningful factors, enabling semantically consistent image grouping and supporting domain-specific evaluation of detection performance including failure analysis. We validate this on public datasets, showing systematic variations across domain factors and revealing hidden failure modes.

AI.tldr: A labeling framework for underwater domain shift using physically measurable factors, enabling semantically consistent domain groups and domain-specific detection evaluation beyond synthetic style-transfer benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Underwater domain shift degrades performance; synthetic benchmarks based on style transfer fail to capture intrinsic scene and acquisition factors (visibility, illumination, scene composition, sensor factors). A physically grounded, measurable factor set is needed to analyze real-world effects and diagnose failures.
AI.method: Define underwater domains via measurable image, scene, and acquisition characteristics; form semantically consistent image groupings; enable domain-specific detection evaluation and failure analysis.
AI.result: Validated on public underwater datasets, showing systematic variation across domain factors and exposing hidden failure modes in detection performance.
AI.conclusion: A physically grounded labeling framework improves understanding and evaluation of underwater domain shift, enabling more robust perception and targeted failure analysis in real-world conditions.

title: Auto-Relational Reasoning
summary: Background & Objectives: In the last decade, Machine learning research has grown rapidly, but large models are reaching their soft limits demonstrating diminishing returns and still lack solid reasoning abilities. These limits could be surpassed through synergistic combination of Machine Learning scalability and rigid reasoning. Methods: In this work, we propose a theoretical framework for reasoning through object-relations in an automated manner integrated with Artificial Neural Networks. We present a formal analysis of the Reasoning, and we show the theory in practice through a paradigm integrating Reasoning and Machine Learning. Results: This paradigm is a system that solves Intelligence Quotient problems without any prior knowledge of the problem. Our system achieves 98.03% solving rate corresponding to the top 1% percentile or 132-144 iq score. This result is only limited by the small size of the model and the processing capabilities of the machine it run on. Conclusions: With the integration of prior knowledge in the system and the expansion of the dataset, the system can be generalized to solve a large category of problems. The functionality of the system inherently favors the solution of such problems in few-shot or zero-shot attempts.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework integrating object-relational reasoning with neural networks to enable automated reasoning, demonstrated by a paradigm claiming near IQ-level problem solving without prior problem knowledge, achieving 98.03% on IQ-style tasks; results claimed to be constrained by model size and hardware.
AI.motivation: Address diminishing returns of large ML models and lack of solid reasoning; pursue synergy of ML scalability and rigid reasoning; develop a formal analysis and practical paradigm for reasoning about object relations in AI systems.
AI.method: Formal framework for object-relational reasoning integrated into ANNs; formal analysis of reasoning; demonstration through a paradigm that couples reasoning with ML.
AI.result: System solves IQ problems without prior knowledge; 98.03% solving rate; claims to reach top 1% percentile (IQ 132-144); performance limited by model size and hardware.
AI.conclusion: With prior knowledge and larger dataset, generalizes to broader problem classes; method favors few-shot/zero-shot problem solving.

title: STAR-Filter: Efficient Convex Free-Space Approximation via Starshaped Set Filtering in Noisy Environments
summary: Approximating collision-free space is fundamental to robot planning in complex environments. Convex geometric representations, such as polytopes and ellipsoids, are widely employed due to their structural properties, which can be easily integrated with convex optimization. Iterative optimization-based inflation methods can generate large volume polytopes in cluttered environments, but their efficiency degrades as the obstacle set becomes more complex or when sensor data are noisy. These methods are also sensitive to initialization and often rely on accurate geometric models. In this paper, we propose the STAR-Filter, a lightweight framework that employs starshaped set construction as a fast filter for convex region generation in collision-free space. By identifying obstacle points as active supporting constraints, the proposed method significantly reduces redundant computation while preserving feasibility and robustness to sensor noise. We provide theoretical and numerical analyses that characterize the structural properties of the starshaped set and proposed pipeline in environments of varying complexity. Simulation results show that the proposed framework achieves the lowest computation time and reduces conservativeness in polytope generation for real-world noisy and large-scale data. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework for Safe Flight Corridor (SFC) generation and agile quadrotor planning in noisy environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes STAR-Filter: a fast, robust star-shaped set-based filter for convex region generation to guarantee collision-free space in planning, reducing computation and sensitivity to noise by treating obstacle points as active supporting constraints; validated via theory and simulations for Safe Flight Corridor and quadrotor planning.
AI.motivation: Iterative inflation methods for generating large polytopes in cluttered or noisy environments are computationally intensive, sensitive to initialization and accurate geometry, and degrade with complex obstacle sets. A fast, robust method is needed for real-time convex region generation in collision avoidance.
AI.method: Introduce STAR-Filter leveraging star-shaped set construction. Treat obstacle points as active supporting constraints to prune redundant computations while maintaining feasibility. Provide theoretical analysis of the star-shaped set’s structure and the proposed pipeline; validate with numerical simulations across varying environment complexity; demonstrate SFC generation and agile quadrotor planning in noisy data.
AI.result: Simulation results show the STAR-Filter achieves the lowest computation time and reduces conservativeness in polytope generation for large-scale, noisy data. Effective in Safe Flight Corridor generation and agile quadrotor planning under noise.
AI.conclusion: STAR-Filter offers a lightweight, robust, and computationally efficient approach for convex region generation in collision-free space, suitable for real-time planning in noisy and complex environments.

title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning Framework for Distributed Chemical Process Optimization
summary: Industrial chemical plants often operate under strict data confidentiality constraints, making centralized data-driven process modeling difficult. Federated learning (FL) provides a promising solution by enabling collaborative model training across distributed facilities without sharing raw operational data. This paper proposes a privacy-preserving federated learning framework for distributed chemical process optimization using data collected from multiple geographically separated plants. Each plant locally trains a neural-network-based process model using its own time-series sensor data, while only model parameters are transmitted to a central aggregation server through secure aggregation mechanisms. This design allows cross-plant knowledge sharing while maintaining strict data locality and industrial confidentiality. Experimental evaluation was conducted using process datasets from three independent chemical plants operating under heterogeneous conditions. The results demonstrate rapid convergence of the federated model, with the global mean squared error decreasing from approximately 2369 to below 50 within the first five communication rounds and stabilizing around 35 after 40 rounds. In comparison with local-only training, the proposed federated framework significantly improves prediction accuracy across all plants, while achieving performance comparable to centralized training. The findings indicate that federated learning provides an effective and scalable solution for collaborative industrial analytics, enabling privacy-preserving predictive modeling and process optimization across distributed chemical production facilities.

AI.tldr: Federated learning with secure aggregation enables privacy-preserving, cross-plant optimization in distributed chemical facilities, achieving rapid convergence and near-centralized performance.
AI.motivation: Industrial plants face strict data confidentiality, hindering centralized modeling. Federated learning offers collaborative model training without sharing raw data, balancing privacy and utility.
AI.method: Local neural-network time-series models are trained at each plant. Only encrypted/aggregated model parameters are sent to a central server, which performs secure aggregation to update a global model. Evaluation uses data from three geographically separated plants under heterogeneous conditions across multiple communication rounds.
AI.result: Global MSE drops from ~2369 to <50 within five rounds and stabilizes around 35 after ~40 rounds. Federated learning improves prediction accuracy over local models for all plants and performs comparably to centralized training.
AI.conclusion: A privacy-preserving federated learning framework is effective and scalable for cross-facility industrial analytics, enabling collaborative optimization while maintaining data locality and confidentiality.

title: Lifting Embodied World Models for Planning and Control
summary: World models of embodied agents predict future observations conditioned on an action taken by the agent. For complex embodiments, action spaces are high-dimensional and difficult to specify: for example, precisely controlling a human agent requires specifying the motion of each joint. This makes the world model hard to control and expensive to plan with as search-based methods like CEM scale poorly with action dimensionality. To address this issue, we train a lightweight policy that maps high-level actions to sequences of low-level joint actions. Composing this policy with the frozen world model produces a lifted world model that predicts a sequence of future observations from a single high-level action. We instantiate this framework for a human-like embodiment, defining the high-level action space as a small set of 2D waypoints annotated on the current observation frame, each specifying a near-term goal position for a leaf joint (pelvis, head, hands). Waypoints are low-dimensional, visually interpretable, and easy to specify manually or to search over. We show that the lifted world model substantially outperforms searching directly in low-level joint space ($3.8\times$ lower mean joint error to the goal pose), while remaining more compute-efficient and generalizing to environments unseen by the policy.

AI.tldr: A lifted world model uses a lightweight policy to map high-level 2D waypoints into sequences of low-level joint actions, enabling efficient future-prediction and planning with a human-like embodiment; it achieves 3.8× lower mean joint error to the goal pose compared with direct low-level search and generalizes to unseen environments.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional action spaces in embodied agents make world models hard to control and expensive to plan with; reducing action dimensionality is essential for efficient planning.
AI.method: Train a lightweight policy that translates high-level actions (2D waypoints annotated on the current frame, specifying near-term goals for leaf joints) into sequences of low-level joint actions. Freeze the world model and compose it with the policy to form a lifted world model that predicts future observations from a single high-level action. Apply to a human-like embodiment (pelvis, head, hands as leaf joints).
AI.result: The lifted model substantially outperforms search in low-level joint space, achieving 3.8× lower mean joint error to the goal pose, while being more compute-efficient and generalizing to environments unseen by the policy.
AI.conclusion: Using interpretable, low-dimensional high-level actions to drive a frozen world model via a lightweight policy yields a lifted world model that improves planning efficiency and predictive accuracy, with strong generalization to unseen environments.

title: Grounding vs. Compositionality: On the Non-Complementarity of Reasoning in Neuro-Symbolic Systems
summary: Compositional generalization remains a foundational weakness of modern neural networks, limiting their robustness and applicability in domains requiring out-of-distribution reasoning. A central, yet unverified, assumption in neuro-symbolic AI is that compositional reasoning will emerge as a byproduct of successful symbol grounding. This work presents the first systematic empirical analysis to challenge this assumption by disentangling the contributions of grounding and reasoning. To operationalize this investigation, we introduce the Iterative Logic Tensor Network ($i$LTN), a fully differentiable architecture designed for multi-step deduction. Using a formal taxonomy of generalization -- probing for novel entities, unseen relations, and complex rule compositions -- we demonstrate that a model trained solely on a grounding objective fails to generalize. In contrast, our full $i$LTN, trained jointly on perceptual grounding and multi-step reasoning, achieves high zero-shot accuracy across all tasks. Our findings provide conclusive evidence that symbol grounding, while necessary, is insufficient for generalization, establishing that reasoning is not an emergent property but a distinct capability that requires an explicit learning objective.

AI.tldr: Grounding alone is insufficient for compositional generalization; explicit multi-step reasoning, as implemented by iLTN, is necessary for robust zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: To test whether symbol grounding yields emergent reasoning and to delineate contributions of grounding vs reasoning in compositional generalization, across novel entities, unseen relations, and complex rule compositions.
AI.method: Introduce Iterative Logic Tensor Network (iLTN), a differentiable architecture for multi-step deduction; train models under grounding objective only vs joint grounding and reasoning; evaluate on a taxonomy of generalization tasks.
AI.result: A grounding-only model fails to generalize; the full iLTN trained jointly on grounding and reasoning achieves high zero-shot accuracy across all generalization categories.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning is not an emergent byproduct of grounding; it requires explicit learning objectives and architectural support; grounding is necessary but insufficient for generalization.

title: ATLAS: An Annotation Tool for Long-horizon Robotic Action Segmentation
summary: Annotating long-horizon robotic demonstrations with precise temporal action boundaries is crucial for training and evaluating action segmentation and manipulation policy learning methods. Existing annotation tools, however, are often limited: they are designed primarily for vision-only data, do not natively support synchronized visualization of robot-specific time-series signals (e.g., gripper state or force/torque), or require substantial effort to adapt to different dataset formats. In this paper, we introduce ATLAS, an annotation tool tailored for long-horizon robotic action segmentation. ATLAS provides time-synchronized visualization of multi-modal robotic data, including multi-view video and proprioceptive signals, and supports annotation of action boundaries, action labels, and task outcomes. The tool natively handles widely used robotics dataset formats such as ROS bags and the Reinforcement Learning Dataset (RLDS) format, and provides direct support for specific datasets such as REASSEMBLE. ATLAS can be easily extended to new formats via a modular dataset abstraction layer. Its keyboard-centric interface minimizes annotation effort and improves efficiency. In experiments on a contact-rich assembly task, ATLAS reduced the average per-action annotation time by at least 6% compared to ELAN, while the inclusion of time-series data improved temporal alignment with expert annotations by more than 2.8% and decreased boundary error fivefold compared to vision-only annotation tools.

AI.tldr: ATLAS is a modular, keyboard-driven annotation tool for long-horizon robotic action segmentation that synchronizes multi-modal data (video + proprioception) and supports common robotics data formats, reducing annotation time and improving temporal alignment with expert annotations.
AI.motivation: Improve the efficiency and accuracy of annotating long-horizon robotic demonstrations by providing synchronized, multi-modal visualization and easy integration with standard robotics datasets, addressing the limitations of vision-only annotation tools.
AI.method: Develop ATLAS with time-synchronized visualization of multi-view video and proprioceptive signals, modular dataset abstraction for formats like ROS bags and RLDS, native support for datasets such as REASSEMBLE, and a keyboard-centric annotation interface; evaluate on a contact-rich assembly task.
AI.result: Per-action annotation time reduced by at least 6% versus ELAN; inclusion of time-series data improved temporal alignment with expert annotations by >2.8% and reduced boundary error fivefold compared with vision-only annotation tools.
AI.conclusion: ATLAS enhances annotation efficiency and temporal precision for robotic action segmentation and is readily extensible to new formats via a modular data abstraction layer.

title: PPG-Based Affect Recognition with Long-Range Deep Models: A Measurement-Driven Comparison of CNN, Transformer, and Mamba Architectures
summary: Photoplethysmography (PPG) is increasingly used in wearable affective computing due to its low cost and ease of integration into consumer devices. Recent advances in deep learning have introduced long-range sequence models, such as Transformers, and state-space models, like Mamba, which have demonstrated strong performance on natural language and general time-series tasks. However, it remains unclear whether these architectures offer tangible benefits over widely used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTMs) for PPG-based affect recognition, given that datasets are typically small and noisy. This work presents a measurement-driven comparison of four deep learning architectures, CNN, CNN-LSTM hybrid, Transformers, and Mamba, for classifying arousal, valence, and relaxation states from wrist-based PPG signals. All models are evaluated under a subject-independent 5-fold cross-validation protocol using identical preprocessing, segmentation, and training pipelines. Our results show that the Transformer and Mamba models achieve performance comparable to that of a CNN baseline, but do not consistently outperform it across all tasks. CNNs remain the most effective overall, providing the highest accuracy with the smallest model size, whereas Transformers have a better balance of F1 scores for Arousal and Relaxation. The study provides the first evaluation of Transformer and Mamba models for PPG-based affect recognition, offering practical guidance on model selection for wearable affective monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: Measurement-driven comparison of CNN, CNN-LSTM, Transformer, and Mamba for PPG-based affect recognition; CNNs outperform as a baseline in small/noisy datasets, with Transformers/Mamba matching but not surpassing it overall.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether modern sequence architectures (Transformers, Mamba) offer practical benefits over CNN/LSTM for PPG-based affect recognition on small wearable datasets, to guide model selection.
AI.method: Subject-independent 5-fold cross-validation on wrist-based PPG; four architectures (CNN, CNN-LSTM, Transformer, Mamba); identical preprocessing, segmentation, and training pipelines; classify arousal, valence, and relaxation; report accuracy, F1, and model size.
AI.result: Transformers and Mamba achieve performance comparable to CNN baseline but do not consistently outperform it across tasks; CNN remains most effective overall with highest accuracy and smallest model size; Transformers offer a better balance of F1 for Arousal and Relaxation.
AI.conclusion: CNNs are the most practical choice for PPG-based affect recognition in wearables; Transformer and Mamba provide no clear advantage, though they may offer task-specific trade-offs (e.g., F1 for certain affective states).

title: Privacy-Preserving Clothing Classification using Vision Transformer for Thermal Comfort Estimation
comment: To be appeared in 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics - Taiwan (ICCE-TW 2026)
summary: A privacy-preserving clothing classification scheme is presented to enable secure occupant-centric control (OCC) systems. Although the utilization of camera images for HVAC control has been widely studied to optimize thermal comfort, privacy protection of occupant images has not been considered in prior works. While various privacy-preserving methods have been proposed for image classification, applying conventional schemes results in severe accuracy degradation. In this paper, we introduce a privacy-preserving classification method using Vision Transformer (ViT) applied to clothing insulation estimation. In an experiment using the DeepFashion dataset categorized by clothing insulation, while the conventional pixel-based method suffers a severe accuracy drop, our scheme maintains a high accuracy on encrypted images, showing no degradation from plain images across all categories.

AI.tldr: A privacy-preserving clothing-insulation classification via Vision Transformer maintains accuracy on encrypted images, enabling OCC without privacy leakage; traditional pixel-based methods degrade significantly.
AI.motivation: Address privacy concerns in occupant-centric control by enabling clothing-insulation classification without exposing sensitive visual information from camera feeds used for HVAC control.
AI.method: Apply Vision Transformer to clothing insulation estimation on encrypted/decrypted image representations, evaluating on DeepFashion categories; compare with conventional pixel-based classification to show robustness of the privacy-preserving approach.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves high accuracy on encrypted images with no degradation relative to plain images across all clothing insulation categories, outperforming conventional pixel-based methods that exhibit severe accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: A privacy-preserving ViT-based approach can enable accurate clothing-insulation classification for OCC systems without compromising occupant privacy, potentially enabling privacy-respecting HVAC control workflows.

title: AGEL-Comp: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Compositional Generalization in Interactive Agents
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents exhibit systemic failures in compositional generalization, limiting their robustness in interactive environments. This work introduces AGEL-Comp, a neuro-symbolic AI agent architecture designed to address this challenge by grounding actions of the agent. AGEL-Comp integrates three core innovations: (1) a dynamic Causal Program Graph (CPG) as a world model, representing procedural and causal knowledge as a directed hypergraph; (2) an Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) engine that synthesizes new Horn clauses from experiential feedback, grounding symbolic knowledge through interaction; and (3) a hybrid reasoning core where an LLM proposes a set of candidate sub-goals that are verified for logical consistency by a Neural Theorem Prover (NTP). Together, these components operationalize a deduction--abduction learning cycle: enabling the agent to deduce plans and abductively expand its symbolic world model, while a neural adaptation phase keeps its reasoning engine aligned with new knowledge. We propose an evaluation protocol within the \texttt{Retro Quest} simulation environment to probe for compositional generalization scenarios to evaluate our AGEL agent. Our findings clearly indicate the better performance of our AGEL model over pure LLM-based models. Our framework presents a principled path toward agents that build an explicit, interpretable, and compositionally structured understanding of their world.

AI.tldr: AGEL-Comp is a neuro-symbolic agent that grounds actions using a dynamic causal program graph world model, an ILP engine to synthesize Horn clauses from experience, and a neural theorem prover to verify LLM-generated sub-goals; it employs a deduction-abduction learning cycle and neural adaptation, showing improved compositional generalization in Retro Quest over pure LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs in agents struggle with compositional generalization and robust planning in interactive settings; need explicit, interpretable, and grounded symbolic reasoning built atop neural methods.
AI.method: Integrates (1) a dynamic CPG world model representing procedural and causal knowledge as a directed hypergraph; (2) an ILP engine that induces new Horn clauses from experiential feedback to ground symbolic knowledge; (3) a hybrid reasoning core where an LLM proposes candidate sub-goals that are verified for logical consistency by a Neural Theorem Prover, enabling a deduction–abduction learning cycle with neural adaptation to align reasoning with new knowledge.
AI.result: The AGEL-Comp framework yields better performance on compositional generalization tasks compared to pure LLM-based models in the proposed Retro Quest evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled path toward agents with explicit, interpretable, and compositionally structured world models, enabling grounded reasoning and adaptive knowledge expansion through a deduction–abduction loop.

title: Atomic-Probe Governance for Skill Updates in Compositional Robot Policies
comment: 8 pages main text + appendix; 3 figures, 12 tables;
summary: Skill libraries in deployed robotic systems are continually updated through fine-tuning, fresh demonstrations, or domain adaptation, yet existing typed-composition methods (BLADE, SymSkill, Generative Skill Chaining) treat the library as frozen at test time and do not analyze how composition outcomes change when a skill is replaced. We introduce a paired-sampling cross-version swap protocol on robosuite manipulation tasks to characterize this dimension of compositional skill learning. On a dual-arm peg-in-hole task we discover a dominant-skill effect: one ECM achieves 86.7% atomic success rate while every other ECM is at or below 26.7%, and whether this dominant ECM enters a composition shifts the success rate by up to +50pp. We characterize the boundary on a simpler pick task where all atomic policies saturate at 100% and the effect is undefined. Across three tasks we further find that off-policy behavioral distance metrics fail to identify the dominant ECM, ruling out the natural cheap predictor. We propose an atomic-quality probe and a Hybrid Selector combining per-skill probes (zero per-decision cost) with selective composition revalidation (full cost), and characterize its Pareto frontier on 144 skill-update decisions. On T6 the atomic-only probe sits 23pp below full revalidation (64.6% vs 87.5% oracle match) at zero per-decision cost; a Hybrid Selector with m=10 closes most of that gap to ~12pp at 46% of full-revalidation cost. On the cross-task average over 144 events, atomic-only is within 3pp of full revalidation under a mixed-oracle caveat. The atomic-quality probe is, to our knowledge, the first principled, deployment-ready primitive for skill-update governance in compositional robot policies.

AI.tldr: Introduces a cross-version cross-swap protocol to study how updating skill libraries affects compositional outcomes in robotic policies; reveals a dominant-skill effect and proposes an atomic-quality probe plus a Hybrid Selector to balance accuracy and revalidation cost, achieving near-full performance with reduced cost.
AI.motivation: Current typed-composition methods assume frozen skill libraries at test time; there is a need to understand how updates to atomic skills affect composition outcomes and to develop practical, deployment-ready governance primitives for skill updates in deployed robotic systems.
AI.method: Paired-sampling cross-version swap protocol on robosuite manipulation tasks; experiments on a dual-arm peg-in-hole task and a simpler pick task; evaluation across three tasks with 144 skill-update decisions; comparison of off-policy distance metrics; introduction of an atomic-quality probe and a Hybrid Selector combining per-skill probes with selective composition revalidation; Pareto frontier analysis of governance efficiency.
AI.result: Observed a dominant-skill effect: one ECM achieves 86.7% atomic success while others are at or below 26.7%; whether the dominant ECM enters composition shifts success by up to +50 percentage points. On the simpler pick task, all atomic policies saturate at 100%, making the effect undefined. Off-policy behavioral distance metrics fail to identify the dominant ECM. The atomic-quality probe and Hybrid Selector improve governance: on T6, atomic-only probe is 64.6% match vs 87.5% with full revalidation (zero per-decision cost); a Hybrid Selector with m=10 reduces most of the gap to ~12 percentage points at 46% of full revalidation cost. Across 144 events, atomic-only is within 3pp of full revalidation under a mixed-oracle caveat. The atomic-quality probe is presented as the first principled, deployment-ready primitive for skill-update governance in compositional robot policies.
AI.conclusion: The atomic-quality probe provides a principled, deployment-ready mechanism for governing skill updates in compositional robot policies, enabling effective trade-offs between accuracy and revalidation cost.

title: Momentum-Conserving Graph Neural Networks for Deformable Objects
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as a versatile and efficient option for modeling the dynamic behavior of deformable materials. While GNNs generalize readily to arbitrary shapes, mesh topologies, and material parameters, existing architectures struggle to correctly predict the temporal evolution of key physical quantities such as linear and angular momentum. In this work, we propose MomentumGNN -- a novel architecture designed to accurately track momentum by construction. Unlike existing GNNs that output unconstrained nodal accelerations, our model predicts per-edge stretching and bending impulses which guarantee the preservation of linear and angular momentum. We train our network in an unsupervised fashion using a physics-based loss, and we show that our method outperforms baselines in a number of common scenarios where momentum plays a pivotal role.

AI.tldr: MomentumGNN preserves momentum by construction by predicting per-edge impulses instead of unconstrained accelerations, trained with a physics-based unsupervised loss, outperforming baselines in momentum-critical scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current GNN-based dynamics models struggle to conserve linear and angular momentum, leading to non-physical behavior in deformable materials. A momentum-preserving architecture is needed for accurate temporal evolution.
AI.method: Introduce MomentumGNN that outputs per-edge stretching and bending impulses to guarantee conservation of linear and angular momentum; constrain the update mechanism to conserve momentum; train using a physics-based unsupervised loss.
AI.result: The method outperforms baselines in common scenarios where momentum is pivotal, demonstrating improved momentum preservation during dynamic evolution.
AI.conclusion: MomentumGNN provides a physically grounded, momentum-conserving GNN framework for deformable materials, enabling more accurate and stable dynamic predictions across arbitrary shapes, meshes, and material parameters.

title: FASH-iCNN: Making Editorial Fashion Identity Inspectable Through Multimodal CNN Probing
summary: Fashion AI systems routinely encode the aesthetic logic of specific houses, editors, and historical moments without disclosing it. We present FASH-iCNN, a multimodal system trained on 87,547 Vogue runway images across 15 fashion houses spanning 1991-2024 that makes this cultural logic inspectable. Given a photograph of a garment, the system recovers which house produced it, which era it belongs to, and which color tradition it reflects. A clothing-only model identifies the fashion house at 78.2% top-1 across 14 houses, the decade at 88.6% top-1, and the specific year at 58.3% top-1 across 34 years with a mean error of just 2.2 years. Probing which visual channels carry this signal reveals a sharp dissociation: removing color costs only 10.6pp of house identity accuracy, while removing texture costs 37.6pp, establishing texture and luminance as the primary carriers of editorial identity. FASH-iCNN treats editorial culture as the signal rather than background noise, identifying which houses, eras, and color traditions shaped each output so that users can see not just what the system predicts but which houses, editors, and historical moments are encoded in that prediction.

AI.tldr: FASH-iCNN is a multimodal model trained on Vogue runway images that predicts fashion house, decade, and year, revealing which editorial factors shape predictions. It achieves strong top-1 accuracy (house 78.2%, decade 88.6%, year 58.3%) with a mean year error of 2.2, and shows texture/luminance as key carriers of editorial identity, enabling inspectable, culture-aware predictions.
AI.motivation: Address opacity of fashion-AI systems that encode house editors and historical moments; provide an interpretable signal by identifying which cultural factors are embedded in predictions.
AI.method: Train a multimodal CNN (FASH-iCNN) on 87,547 Vogue runway images across 15 houses (1991–2024); tasks: house, decade, year; ablation to assess color vs texture; evaluate top-1 accuracies and mean year error; analysis of visual channels.
AI.result: Predictive performance: house 78.2% top-1; decade 88.6% top-1; year 58.3% top-1 (34 years) with mean error 2.2. Ablation: color removal reduces house accuracy by 10.6 pp; texture removal reduces by 37.6 pp; texture and luminance are primary carriers; the model makes editorial culture a signal rather than noise.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides inspectable predictions by revealing which houses/editors/historical moments are encoded; supports transparency and cultural accountability in fashion-AI systems.

title: Benchmarking the Safety of Large Language Models for Robotic Health Attendant Control
comment: 20 pages, 9 figures, 3 tables, 8 pages supplementary material
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly considered for deployment as the control component of robotic health attendants, yet their safety in this context remains poorly characterized. We introduce a dataset of 270 harmful instructions spanning nine prohibited behavior categories grounded in the American Medical Association Principles of Medical Ethics, and use it to evaluate 72 LLMs in a simulation environment based on the Robotic Health Attendant framework. The mean violation rate across all models was 54.4\%, with more than half exceeding 50\%, and violation rates varied substantially across behavior categories, with superficially plausible instructions such as device manipulation and emergency delay proving harder to refuse than overtly destructive ones. Model size and release date were the primary determinants of safety performance among open-weight models, and proprietary models were substantially safer than open-weight counterparts (median 23.7\% versus 72.8\%). Medical domain fine-tuning conferred no significant overall safety benefit, and a prompt-based defense strategy produced only a modest reduction in violation rates among the least safe models, leaving absolute violation rates at levels that would preclude safe clinical deployment. These findings demonstrate that safety evaluation must be treated as a first-class criterion in the development and deployment of LLMs for robotic health attendants.

AI.tldr: A large-scale safety evaluation of LLMs in robotic health attendants using a 270-instruction harmful dataset shows high violation rates; proprietary models are safer; medical fine-tuning provides no overall safety gains; prompt defenses offer limited relief; safety must be a first-class evaluation criterion.
AI.motivation: Assess safety of LLMs deployed as control components in robotic health attendants and quantify risk across categories of prohibited behavior; identify factors affecting safety (model size, release date, access mode); evaluate mitigation strategies.
AI.method: Constructed 270 harmful instructions across nine AMA-grounded categories. Evaluated 72 LLMs in a Robotic Health Attendant simulation. Measured violation rates by category and model. Compared open-weight vs proprietary models; assessed medical-domain fine-tuning and prompt-based defenses.
AI.result: Mean violation rate 54.4%; over half >50%; variation across categories; easier to refuse for some benign-looking instructions than overtly destructive; model size and release date predict safety; proprietary models median violation 23.7% vs open-weight 72.8%; medical domain fine-tuning had no significant overall benefit; prompt-based defenses reduced violations modestly for the least safe models; absolute rates still unsafe for clinical deployment.
AI.conclusion: Safety evaluation must be a primary criterion in development and deployment of LLMs for robotic health attendants.

title: Unified 4D World Action Modeling from Video Priors with Asynchronous Denoising
summary: We propose X-WAM, a Unified 4D World Model that unifies real-time robotic action execution and high-fidelity 4D world synthesis (video + 3D reconstruction) in a single framework, addressing the critical limitations of prior unified world models (e.g., UWM) that only model 2D pixel-space and fail to balance action efficiency and world modeling quality. To leverage the strong visual priors of pretrained video diffusion models, X-WAM imagines the future world by predicting multi-view RGB-D videos, and obtains spatial information efficiently through a lightweight structural adaptation: replicating the final few blocks of the pretrained Diffusion Transformer into a dedicated depth prediction branch for the reconstruction of future spatial information. Moreover, we propose Asynchronous Noise Sampling (ANS) to jointly optimize generation quality and action decoding efficiency. ANS applies a specialized asynchronous denoising schedule during inference, which rapidly decodes actions with fewer steps to enable efficient real-time execution, while dedicating the full sequence of steps to generate high-fidelity video. Rather than entirely decoupling the timesteps during training, ANS samples from their joint distribution to align with the inference distribution. Pretrained on over 5,800 hours of robotic data, X-WAM achieves 79.2% and 90.7% average success rate on RoboCasa and RoboTwin 2.0 benchmarks, while producing high-fidelity 4D reconstruction and generation surpassing existing methods in both visual and geometric metrics.

AI.tldr: X-WAM presents a unified 4D world model that couples real-time robotic action with high-fidelity 4D world synthesis by leveraging pretrained video diffusion priors, a depth-prediction branch, and asynchronous noise sampling to balance inference speed and generation quality.
AI.motivation: Prior unified world models (UWM) focus on 2D pixel-space and struggle to simultaneously achieve efficient control and high-quality 4D world modeling. Leveraging strong visual priors from video diffusion models and integrating a lightweight depth branch can improve spatial reconstruction while maintaining real-time performance.
AI.method: Replicate the final blocks of a pretrained Diffusion Transformer into a dedicated depth-prediction branch to reconstruct future spatial information. Introduce Asynchronous Noise Sampling (ANS) to optimize generation quality and action decoding efficiency: during inference, use an asynchronous denoising schedule that enables rapid action decoding with few steps while allocating the full diffusion sequence to high-fidelity 4D video generation; during training, sample from the joint distribution of timesteps to align with inference. Pretrained on 5,800+ hours of robotic data.
AI.result: X-WAM achieves 79.2% average success on RoboCasa and 90.7% on RoboTwin 2.0, while delivering high-fidelity 4D reconstruction and generation that surpasses existing methods in both visual and geometric metrics.
AI.conclusion: X-WAM offers a single framework that unifies action execution and 4D world modeling, achieving strong real-time performance without compromising 4D reconstruction quality, and demonstrates clear advantages over prior 2D-focused UWM approaches.

title: reward-lens: A Mechanistic Interpretability Library for Reward Models
comment: 30 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables, including appendix. Library available at https://github.com/suhailnadaf509/reward-lens (pip install reward-lens)
summary: Every RLHF-trained language model is shaped by a reward model, yet the mechanistic interpretability toolkit -- logit lens, direct logit attribution, activation patching, sparse autoencoders -- was built for generative LLMs whose primitives all project onto a vocabulary unembedding. Reward models replace that with a scalar regression head, breaking each tool. We present reward-lens, an open-source library that ports this toolkit to reward models, organised around one observation: the reward head's weight vector $w_r$ is the natural axis for every interpretability question. The library provides a Reward Lens, component attribution, three-mode activation patching, a reward-hacking probe suite, TopK SAE feature attribution, cross-model comparison, and five theory-grounded extensions (distortion index, divergence-aware patching, misalignment cascade detection, reward-term conflict analysis, concept-vector analysis). A ten-method adapter protocol covers Llama, Mistral, Gemma-2, and ArmoRM multi-objective heads, with a generic adapter for any HuggingFace sequence classification model. We validate on two production reward models across ~695 RewardBench pairs. The central empirical finding is negative: linear attribution does not predict causal patching effects (mean Spearman $ρ= -0.256$ on Skywork, $-0.027$ on ArmoRM). The framework treats this disagreement as a property to expose, not a bug -- motivating a design that keeps observational and causal views first-class and directly comparable.

AI.tldr: A toolkit that retools interpretability methods for RLHF reward models by centering on the reward head; introduces Reward Lens and a suite of attribution, patching, and analysis methods; finds a negative predictive relationship between linear attribution and causal patching; frames disagreement as informative and design-driven for observational vs causal interpretability.
AI.motivation: RLHF models replace a shared vocabulary projection with a scalar reward head, breaking existing interpretability tools. There is a need to port or adapt interpretability methods to reward-models so that researchers can diagnose and compare how rewards shape behavior.
AI.method: Extend existing interpretability toolkit (logit lens, attribution, activation patching, SAE) to reward-models via a Reward Lens; develop three-mode activation patching, component attribution, reward-hacking probes, TopK SAE feature attribution; provide cross-model adapters for multiple reward-model heads; evaluate on two production reward models across ∼695 RewardBench pairs.
AI.result: Central empirical finding is negative: linear attribution does not predict causal patching effects (mean Spearman ρ = -0.256 on Skywork, -0.027 on ArmoRM).
AI.conclusion: Treat disagreement between observational (attribution) and causal (patching) views as a feature to expose rather than a bug; propose design that keeps both views first-class and directly comparable; provide open-source library and several theory-grounded extensions to analyze reward models.

title: ViBE: Visual-to-M/EEG Brain Encoding via Spatio-Temporal VAE and Distribution-Aligned Projection
summary: Brain encoding models not only serve to decipher how visual stimuli are transformed into neural responses, but also represent a critical step toward visual prostheses that restore vision for patients with severe vision disorders. Brain encoding involves two fundamental steps: achieving faithful reconstruction of neural responses and establishing cross-modal alignment between visual stimuli and neural responses. To this end, we propose ViBE, a novel brain encoding framework for generating magnetoencephalography (MEG) and electroencephalography (EEG) signals from visual stimuli. Specifically, we first design a spatio-temporal convolutional variational autoencoder (TSC-VAE) that captures the spatio-temporal characteristics of M/EEG signals for effective neural response reconstruction. To bridge the modality gap between visual features and neural representations, we employ Q-Former to map CLIP image embeddings to the TSC-VAE latent space, producing neural proxy embeddings. For comprehensive cross-modal alignment, we combine mean squared error (MSE) loss for point-wise feature matching with sliced Wasserstein distance (SWD) for probability distribution alignment between the neural proxy embeddings and TSC-VAE latent embeddings. We conduct extensive experiments on the THINGS-EEG2 and THINGS-MEG datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of our approach in generating high-quality M/EEG signals from visual stimuli.

AI.tldr: ViBE is a brain-encoding framework that generates M/EEG signals from visual stimuli using a spatio-temporal VAE and a Q-Former to align CLIP image embeddings with neural latent space, trained with combined MSE and sliced Wasserstein losses; validated on THINGS-EEG2/THINGS-MEG showing high-quality neural signal generation.
AI.motivation: Bridge faithful neural response reconstruction with cross-modal alignment between vision and neural activity to enable brain encoding and potential visual prostheses.
AI.method: A spatio-temporal convolutional VAE (TSC-VAE) for M/EEG reconstruction; Q-Former maps CLIP image embeddings into TSC-VAE latent space to produce neural proxy embeddings; joint objective combining MSE for point-wise feature matching and SWD for distribution alignment between proxies and latent embeddings.
