AI.result: Extensive experiments on THINGS-EEG2 and THINGS-MEG demonstrate the method generates high-quality M/EEG signals from visual stimuli, validating cross-modal alignment and reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: ViBE offers a principled framework for visual-to-neural signal generation, advancing brain encoding and potential prosthetic applications by unifying faithful reconstruction with cross-modal alignment.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Benchmarking of Heterogeneous LLMs for Automated Competency Assessment in Secondary Level Mathematics
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to 2AI-2026-Applied AI Conference
summary:
As Competency-Based Education (CBE) is gaining traction around the world, the shift from marks-based assessment to qualitative competency mapping is a manual challenge for educators. This paper tackles the bottleneck issue by suggesting a "Human-in-the-Loop" benchmarking framework to assess the effectiveness of multiple LLMs in automating secondary-level mathematics assessment. Based on the Grade 10 Optional Mathematics curriculum in Nepal, we created a multi-dimensional rubric for four topics and four cross-cutting competencies: Comprehension, Knowledge, Operational Fluency, and Behavior and Correlation.
  The multi-provider ensemble, consisted of open-weight models -- Eagle (Llama 3.1-8B) and Orion (Llama 3.3-70B) -- and proprietary frontier models Nova (Gemini 2.5 Flash) and Lyra (Gemini 3 Pro), was benchmarked against a ground truth defined by two senior mathematics faculty members (kappa_w = 0.8652). The findings show a marked "Architecture-compatibility gap". Although the Gemini-based Mixture-of-Experts (Sparse MoE) models achieved "Fair Agreement" (kappa_w ~ 0.38), the larger Orion (70B) model exhibited "No Agreement" (kappa_w = -0.0261), suggesting that architectural compliance with instruction constraints outweighs the scale of raw parameters in rubric-constrained tasks. We conclude that while LLMs are not yet suitable for autonomous certification, they provide high-value assistive support for preliminary evidence extraction within a "Human-in-the-Loop" framework.

AI.tldr: Human-in-the-Loop benchmarking framework for LLMs in secondary math assessment; architecture alignment drives performance; limited autonomous suitability.
AI.motivation: Bridge gap in manual competency-based assessment (CBE) by evaluating LLMs against rubric-based evaluation on Nepal Grade 10 math; examine how model architecture interacts with rubric constraints.
AI.method: Constructed multi-dimensional rubric for four topics and four cross-cutting competencies; evaluated four models (Eagle 8B, Orion 70B, Nova 2.5, Lyra 3 Pro) on Grade 10 Optional Math; used two senior faculty to define ground truth; computed weighted Cohen's kappa; compared performance; analyzed architecture compatibility and MoE models vs standard large models.
AI.result: Gemini MoE models reached fair agreement (kappa_w ~0.38); Orion 70B showed no agreement (kappa_w ~ -0.026); ground truth inter-rater reliability kappa_w = 0.8652; architecture compliance with instruction constraints more critical than parameter count; LLMs currently provide assistive evidence extraction rather than autonomous certification.
AI.conclusion: LLMs not yet fit for autonomous certification in rubric-constrained education but valuable for prelim evidence extraction within a Human-in-the-Loop framework; suggests focus on instruction alignment and rubric-constrained prompting; future work could explore better architecture-informed prompting, improved MoE alignment, larger datasets, and human-in-the-loop workflow optimization.

title: Rule-based High-Level Coaching for Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning in Search-and-Rescue UAV Missions Under Limited-Simulation Training
summary: This paper presents a hierarchical decision-making framework for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) missions motivated by search-and-rescue (SAR) scenarios under limited simulation training. The framework combines a fixed rule-based high-level advisor with an online goal-conditioned low-level reinforcement learning (RL) controller. To stress-test early adaptation, we also consider a strict no-pretraining deployment regime. The high-level advisor is defined offline from a structured task specification and compiled into deterministic rules. It provides interpretable mission- and safety-aware guidance through recommended actions, avoided actions, and regime-dependent arbitration weights. The low-level controller learns online from task-defined dense rewards and reuses experience through a mode-aware prioritized replay mechanism augmented with rule-derived metadata. We evaluate the framework on two tasks: battery-aware multi-goal delivery and moving-target delivery in obstacle-rich environments. Across both tasks, the proposed method improves early safety and sample efficiency primarily by reducing collision terminations, while preserving the ability to adapt online to scenario-specific dynamics.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical rule-guided, online RL framework for UAV missions that boosts safety and data efficiency in SAR-like tasks, even with no pretraining.
AI.motivation: Overcome limited simulation data and the need for safety in UAV SAR missions; provide interpretable high-level guidance while enabling data-efficient, adaptive control.
AI.method: A deterministic, offline high-level advisor derived from structured task specifications; a goal-conditioned low-level RL controller trained online with dense rewards; mode-aware prioritized replay incorporating rule-derived metadata; evaluated on two obstacle-rich tasks: battery-aware multi-goal delivery and moving-target delivery.
AI.result: In both tasks, the method improves early safety and sample efficiency by reducing collision terminations, while preserving the ability to adapt online to scenario-specific dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid framework successfully merges offline rule-based guidance with online RL to achieve safer, more data-efficient UAV missions under limited training, with maintained adaptability.

title: Spatially-constrained clustering of geospatial features for heat vulnerability assessment of favelas in Rio de Janeiro
summary: Informal settlements face disproportionate exposure to climate-related health hazards. However, existing methodologies lack systematic approaches to link diverse settlement characteristics with environmental health outcomes. We develop a data-driven framework to assess heat vulnerability in Rio de Janeiro's favelas by combining spatially-constrained clustering with land surface temperature (LST) analysis. Using remote sensing and geospatial features, we identify two distinct favela typologies: recent, well-connected settlements on flat terrain (Cluster 0) and historical, poorly-connected communities on vegetated slopes (Cluster 1). Analysis of 16 extreme heat events reveals systematic temperature differences of 2--3$^\circ$C between clusters, with flat-terrain favelas experiencing significantly higher heat exposure. Our findings demonstrate that settlement morphology critically influences heat vulnerability, providing a replicable framework for targeted urban planning and public health interventions in informal settlements globally.

AI.tldr: Data-driven framework using spatially-constrained clustering and land surface temperature (LST) analysis to classify Rio de Janeiro's favelas into two heat-exposure typologies, revealing 2–3°C higher exposure in flat, recent settlements versus vegetated slopes; provides a replicable approach for targeted urban health interventions.
AI.motivation: Informal settlements experience disproportionate climate-related health risks, yet existing methods poorly link settlement morphology to environmental health outcomes. The work aims to develop a replicable, data-driven framework to quantify heat vulnerability in favelas.
AI.method: Combine remote-sensing LST with geospatial features within a spatially-constrained clustering framework to identify favela typologies. Validate using 16 extreme heat events to compare exposure across clusters.
AI.result: Two distinct typologies emerged: Cluster 0 — recent, well-connected settlements on flat terrain; Cluster 1 — historical, poorly-connected communities on vegetated slopes. Across 16 extreme heat events, clusters show a 2–3°C difference in temperature, with flat-terrain favelas experiencing higher exposure.
AI.conclusion: Settlement morphology significantly shapes heat vulnerability. The framework is replicable and adaptable for targeted urban planning and public health interventions in informal settlements globally.

title: Seeking Consensus: Geometric-Semantic On-the-Fly Recalibration for Open-Vocabulary Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
summary: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) in remote sensing images is a promising task that employs textual descriptions for identifying undefined land cover categories. Despite notable advances, existing methods typically employ a static inference paradigm, overlooking the distinct distribution of each scene, resulting in semantic ambiguity in diverse land covers and incomplete foreground activation. Motivated by this, we propose Seeking Consensus, termed SeeCo, a plug-and-play framework to boost the performance of training-free OVSS models in remote sensing images, which recalibrates arbitrary OVSS models on-the-fly by seeking dual consensus: geometric consensus learning (GCL) through multi-view consistent observations and semantic consensus learning (SCL) via textual description adaptive calibration, which assists collaborative recalibration of visual and textual semantics. The two consensus are injected via an online consensus injector (OCI), effectively alleviating the under-activation and semantic bias. SeeCo requires no specific training process, yet recalibrates semantic-geometric alignment for each unique scene during inference. Extensive experiments on eight remote sensing OVSS benchmarks show consistent gains, proving its effectiveness and universality.

AI.tldr: SeeCo is a training-free plug-and-play framework for open-vocabulary semantic segmentation in remote sensing that recalibrates geometric and semantic alignment on-the-fly via an online consensus injector, achieving consistent gains across eight benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Static inference in existing OVSS methods ignores scene-specific distributions, leading to semantic ambiguity and under-activation. A per-scene recalibration without retraining is desirable.
AI.method: SeeCo introduces dual consensus learning: geometric consensus learning (GCL) via multi-view consistent observations and semantic consensus learning (SCL) via textual-description adaptive calibration, integrated by an online consensus injector (OCI). No training is required; inference-time recalibration aligns visual and textual semantics for each scene.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on eight remote-sensing OVSS benchmarks show consistent performance gains, validating SeeCo's effectiveness and universality.
AI.conclusion: SeeCo provides a universal, training-free approach to boost OVSS in remote sensing by dynamically aligning semantic and geometric cues per scene through online consensus during inference.

title: When to Vote, When to Rewrite: Disagreement-Guided Strategy Routing for Test-Time Scaling
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) achieve strong performance on mathematical reasoning tasks but remain unreliable on challenging instances. Existing test-time scaling methods, such as repeated sampling, self-correction, and tree search, improve performance at the cost of increased computation, yet often exhibit diminishing returns on hard problems. We observe that output disagreement is strongly correlated with instance difficulty and prediction correctness, providing a useful signal for guiding instance-level strategy selection at test time. Based on this insight, we propose a training-free framework that formulates test-time scaling as an instance-level routing problem, rather than allocating more computation within a single strategy, dynamically selecting among different scaling strategies based on output disagreement. The framework applies lightweight resolution for consistent cases, majority voting for moderate disagreement, and rewriting-based reformulation for highly ambiguous instances. Experiments on seven mathematical benchmarks and three models show that our method improves accuracy by 3% - 7% while reducing sampling cost compared to existing approaches.

AI.tldr: A training-free, instance-level routing framework for test-time scaling of Large Reasoning Models that uses output disagreement to route instances to lightweight resolution, majority voting, or rewriting-based reformulation, yielding 3–7% accuracy gains and lower sampling costs on math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) are powerful for mathematical reasoning but unreliable on hard instances. Existing test-time scaling methods improve accuracy at high compute cost and show diminishing returns on difficult problems. There is a need for an efficient, instance-aware strategy to allocate effort where it matters most.
AI.method: Formulate test-time scaling as an instance-level routing problem guided by output disagreement. No additional training required. Route decisions: consistent cases use lightweight resolution, moderate disagreement uses majority voting, highly ambiguous cases use rewriting-based reformulation. Evaluated across seven mathematical benchmarks and three models.
AI.result: The method yields 3%–7% improvements in accuracy while reducing sampling cost compared with existing scaling approaches.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, disagreement-guided routing framework can effectively balance accuracy and computation for LRMs on mathematical tasks, with potential for broader applicability and extension to other domains.

title: Walk With Me: Long-Horizon Social Navigation for Human-Centric Outdoor Assistance
summary: Assisting humans in open-world outdoor environments requires robots to translate high-level natural-language intentions into safe, long-horizon, and socially compliant navigation behavior. Existing map-based methods rely on costly pre-built HD maps, while learning-based policies are mostly limited to indoor and short-horizon settings. To bridge this gap, we propose Walk with Me, a map-free framework for long-horizon social navigation from high-level human instructions. Walk with Me leverages GPS context and lightweight candidate points-of-interest from a public map API for semantic destination grounding and waypoint proposal. A High-Level Vision-Language Model grounds abstract instructions into concrete destinations and plans coarse waypoint sequences. During execution, an observation-aware routing mechanism determines whether the Low-Level Vision-Language-Action policy can handle the current situation or whether explicit safety reasoning from the High-Level VLM is needed. Routine segments are executed by the Low-Level VLA, while complex situations such as crowded crossings trigger high-level reasoning and stop-and-wait behavior when unsafe. By combining semantic intent grounding, map-free long-horizon planning, safety-aware reasoning, and low-level action generation, Walk with Me enables practical outdoor social navigation for human-centric assistance.

AI.tldr: Walk with Me is a map-free, long-horizon social navigation framework that grounds high-level human instructions into outdoor destinations using GPS context and public map POIs, with a High-Level Vision-Language Model for coarse waypoint planning and an observation-aware routing mechanism to trigger safety-aware, high-level reasoning when needed.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between map-based outdoor navigation (requiring expensive HD maps) and learning-based indoor/short-horizon policies by enabling open-world, socially compliant navigation without pre-built maps.
AI.method: Key components include: (1) semantic grounding using GPS context and public map API POIs for destination grounding; (2) a High-Level Vision-Language Model that grounds abstract instructions into concrete destinations and plans coarse waypoint sequences; (3) an observation-aware routing policy that decides whether the Low-Level Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy suffices or whether explicit safety reasoning from the High-Level VLM is required; (4) routine segments executed by Low-Level VLA and complex situations (e.g., crowded crossings) triggering high-level reasoning and stop-and-wait behavior.
AI.result: The abstract presents a framework without reported empirical results or quantitative validation; claims practicality and potential for outdoor social navigation but lacks experimental evidence within the abstract.
AI.conclusion: By integrating semantic intent grounding, map-free long-horizon planning, safety-aware reasoning, and low-level action generation, Walk with Me aims to deliver practical outdoor social navigation for human-centric assistance.

title: Budget-Constrained Causal Bandits: Bridging Uplift Modeling and Sequential Decision-Making
summary: Treatment allocation under budget constraints is a central challenge in digital advertising: advertisers must decide which users to show ads to while spending a limited budget wisely. The standard approach follows a two-stage offline pipeline - first collect historical data to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE), then solve a constrained optimization to allocate the budget. This works well with abundant data, but fails in cold-start settings such as new campaigns, new markets, or new customer segments where little historical data exists. We propose Budget-Constrained Causal Bandits (BCCB), an online framework that learns which users respond to ads while simultaneously spending the budget, making treatment decisions one user at a time. BCCB unifies three components into a single sequential process: learning individual-level ad effectiveness, exploring users whose response is uncertain, and pacing the budget over time. We evaluated on the Criteo Uplift dataset, a large-scale advertising dataset from a real randomized controlled trial. Our key finding is a data-efficiency crossover: offline methods require approximately 10,000 historical observations to produce reliable results, while BCCB operates effectively from the very first user. Furthermore, BCCB exhibits 3-5x lower performance variance between runs, making it more practical for real campaign planning. Among purely online methods, BCCB consistently outperforms standard Thompson Sampling, budgeted Thompson Sampling, and greedy HTE estimation across all budget levels tested.

AI.tldr: BCCB is an online framework that learns ad effectiveness while spending within a budget, integrating learning, exploration, and pacing in a single sequential process; it shows data efficiency and stability advantages over both offline uplift-based methods and standard online baselines.
AI.motivation: Budget-constrained digital advertising typically relies on a two-stage offline pipeline (estimate HTE from historical data, then optimize allocation). This approach falters in cold-start scenarios with scarce data, prompting the need for an online method that learns causally while controlling spend.
AI.method: Introduce Budget-Constrained Causal Bandits (BCCB): a sequential, single-process framework that (i) learns individual-level ad effectiveness (HTE), (ii) explores uncertain users to reduce regret, and (iii) paces the budget over time. Empirical evaluation on the Criteo Uplift dataset (RCT data) compares against offline uplift + optimization and pure online methods.
AI.result: BCCB delivers data-efficiency crossover: offline methods need about 10,000 historical observations to be reliable, while BCCB performs effectively from the first user. It also achieves 3–5x lower variance across runs and outperforms Thompson Sampling, budgeted TS, and greedy HTE estimation across budget levels.
AI.conclusion: Online, joint optimization of learning, exploration, and budgeting is practically superior in cold-start campaigns, offering faster usable performance and more stable outcomes. The approach is data-efficient and scalable to real campaigns, with potential extensions to non-stationary settings and broader ad-optimization contexts.

title: HOI-aware Adaptive Network for Weakly-supervised Action Segmentation
summary: In this paper, we propose an HOI-aware adaptive network named AdaAct for weakly-supervised action segmentation. Most existing methods learn a fixed network to predict the action of each frame with the neighboring frames. However, this would result in ambiguity when estimating similar actions, such as pouring juice and pouring coffee. To address this, we aim to exploit temporally global but spatially local human-object interactions (HOI) as video-level prior knowledge for action segmentation. The long-term HOI sequence provides crucial contextual information to distinguish ambiguous actions, where our network dynamically adapts to the given HOI sequence at test time. More specifically, we first design a video HOI encoder that extracts, selects, and integrates the most representative HOI throughout the video. Then, we propose a two-branch HyperNetwork to learn an adaptive temporal encoder, which automatically adjusts the parameters based on the HOI information of various videos on the fly. Extensive experiments on two widely-used datasets including Breakfast and 50Salads demonstrate the effectiveness of our method under different evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: AdaAct proposes an HOI-aware adaptive network for weakly-supervised action segmentation, using a video-level HOI prior via a video HOI encoder and a two-branch HyperNetwork that adapts the temporal encoder at test time; evaluated on Breakfast and 50Salads with positive results.
AI.motivation: Ambiguity in frame-wise action predictions arises when similar actions occur; long-term, temporally global but spatially local human-object interactions (HOI) provide contextual priors to disambiguate actions.
AI.method: A video HOI encoder extracts, selects, and integrates representative HOIs across the video. A two-branch HyperNetwork learns an adaptive temporal encoder that adjusts its parameters on the fly based on the HOI information from different videos, enabling test-time customization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Breakfast and 50Salads show the method's effectiveness under various evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating HOI context with an adaptive, test-time network improves weakly-supervised action segmentation and generalizes across datasets.

title: SciHorizon-DataEVA: An Agentic System for AI-Readiness Evaluation of Heterogeneous Scientific Data
summary: AI-for-Science (AI4Science) is increasingly transforming scientific discovery by embedding machine learning models into prediction, simulation, and hypothesis generation workflows across domains. However, the effectiveness of these models is fundamentally constrained by the AI-readiness of scientific data, for which no scalable and systematic evaluation mechanism currently exists. In this work, we propose SciHorizon-DataEVA, a novel agentic system to scalable AI-readiness evaluation of heterogeneous scientific data. At the evaluation-criteria level, we introduce the Sci-TQA2 principles, which organize AI-readiness into four complementary dimensions: Governance Trustworthiness, Data Quality, AI Compatibility, and Scientific Adaptability. Each dimension is decomposed into measurable atomic elements that enable fine-grained and executable assessment. To operationalize these principles at scale, we develop Sci-TQA2-Eval, a hierarchical multi-agent evaluation approach orchestrated through a directed, cyclic workflow. Our Sci-TQA2-Eval dynamically constructs dataset-aware evaluation specifications by combining lightweight dataset profiling, applicability-aware metric activation, and knowledge-augmented planning grounded in domain constraints and dataset-paper signals. These specifications are executed through an adaptive, tool-centric evaluation mechanism with built-in verification and self-correction, enabling scalable and reliable assessment across heterogeneous scientific data. Extensive experiments on scientific datasets spanning multiple domains demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of SciHorizon-DataEVA for principled AI-readiness evaluation.

AI.tldr: Proposes SciHorizon-DataEVA for scalable AI-readiness evaluation of heterogeneous scientific data, with the Sci-TQA2 framework (Governance Trustworthiness, Data Quality, AI Compatibility, Scientific Adaptability) and a hierarchical multi-agent pipeline Sci-TQA2-Eval; validated across domains.
AI.motivation: AI for science is constrained by the readiness of scientific data to support ML models; there is a lack of scalable, principled evaluation mechanisms to measure data AI-readiness.
AI.method: Introduce Sci-TQA2 principles decomposed into measurable elements; develop Sci-TQA2-Eval, a hierarchical multi-agent, device- and dataset-aware evaluation workflow with profiling, adaptive metric activation, and knowledge-augmented planning guided by domain constraints and dataset-paper signals; employ a tool-centric evaluation with verification and self-correction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on scientific datasets from multiple domains demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of SciHorizon-DataEVA for principled AI-readiness evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a scalable, principled framework to assess AI-readiness of heterogeneous scientific data, enabling reliable integration of ML into prediction, simulation, and hypothesis-generation workflows.

title: STARRY: Spatial-Temporal Action-Centric World Modeling for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Robotic manipulation critically requires reasoning about future spatial-temporal interactions, yet existing VLA policies and world-model-enhanced policies do not fully model action-relevant spatial-temporal interaction structure. We propose STARRY, a world-model-enhanced action-generation policy that aligns spatial-temporal prediction with action generation. STARRY jointly denoises future spatial-temporal latents and action sequences, and introduces Geometry-Aware Selective Attention Modulation to convert predicted depth and end-effector geometry into token-aligned weights for selective action-attention modulation. On RoboTwin 2.0, STARRY achieves 93.82% / 93.30% average success under Clean and Randomized settings. Real-world experiments further improve average success from 42.5% to 70.8% over $π_{0.5}$, demonstrating the effectiveness of action-centric spatial-temporal world modeling for spatial-temporally demanding robotic action generation.

AI.tldr: STARRY is a world-model-enhanced action-generation policy that aligns spatial-temporal prediction with action generation for robotic manipulation, using joint denoising of future latents and geometry-aware selective attention to map depth and end-effector geometry into token-aligned action modulation.
AI.motivation: Existing visual-language-action (VLA) policies and world-model-enhanced policies inadequately model action-relevant spatial-temporal interaction structure, limiting performance in spatially-temporally demanding manipulation tasks.
AI.method: STARRY jointly denoises future spatial-temporal latents and action sequences, and introduces Geometry-Aware Selective Attention Modulation to convert predicted depth and end-effector geometry into token-aligned weights for selective action-attention modulation.
AI.result: In RoboTwin 2.0, STARRY achieves 93.82% and 93.30% average success under Clean and Randomized settings, respectively. Real-world experiments show a substantial improvement from 42.5% to 70.8% average success over π0.5, demonstrating effective action-centric spatial-temporal world modeling for robotic action generation.
AI.conclusion: Action-centric spatial-temporal world modeling enables more effective generation of spatial-temporally demanding robotic actions, with strong gains in both simulation and real-world evaluations.

title: Entropy Centroids as Intrinsic Rewards for Test-Time Scaling
summary: An effective way to scale up test-time compute of large language models is to sample multiple responses and then select the best one, as in Grok Heavy and Gemini Deep Think. Existing selection methods often rely on external reward models, which requires training a strong reward model and introduces additional computation overhead. As an alternative, previous approaches have explored intrinsic signals, such as confidence and entropy, but these signals are noisy with naive aggregation. In this work, we observe that high-entropy tokens tend to cluster into consecutive groups during inference, providing a more stable notion of model uncertainty than individual tokens. Together, these clusters reveal temporal patterns of model uncertainty throughout the inference process. Motivated by this observation, we propose to use the temporal structure of uncertainty as an intrinsic reward. To this end, we first formalize the basic unit of segment-level uncertainty as the High Entropy Phase (HEP), a variable-length segment that begins at a high-entropy token and ends when consecutive low-entropy tokens appear. We then define the Entropy Centroid, inspired by the concept of the center of mass in physics, as the weighted average position of all HEPs along the trajectory. Intuitively, a lower centroid indicates early exploration followed by confident generation, which we find often corresponds to higher response quality. Based on this insight, we propose the Lowest Centroid method, which selects the response with the lowest entropy centroid among multiple candidates. Experiments on mathematics, code generation, logical reasoning, and agentic tasks, across model scales ranging from 14B to 480B, show that Lowest Centroid consistently outperforms existing baselines and delivers stable gains as model size increases. Code is available at https://github.com/hkust-nlp/entropy-centroid.

AI.tldr: Introduces Lowest Centroid, an intrinsic reward for selecting among multiple LLM responses by exploiting the temporal structure of high-entropy phases during generation. Defines High Entropy Phase segments and the Entropy Centroid (center of mass of HEP positions); selects the candidate with the lowest centroid, achieving robust gains across tasks and model scales without external reward models.
AI.motivation: To avoid training external reward models and the noise of token-level uncertainty by leveraging intrinsic, temporally structured uncertainty signals during inference.
AI.method: Define High Entropy Phase (HEP) as a variable-length segment starting at a high-entropy token and ending before consecutive low-entropy tokens. Compute Entropy Centroid as the weighted average position of all HEPs along a generation trajectory. Use the candidate with the lowest centroid (Lowest Centroid) for final selection among multiple samples. Validate across mathematics, code, reasoning, and agentic tasks with model scales from 14B to 480B.
AI.result: Lowest Centroid consistently outperforms existing baselines and yields stable gains as model size increases across diverse tasks. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Temporal, cluster-level uncertainty signals provide a stable intrinsic reward for multi-sample selection, enabling scalable, calibration-friendly performance improvements without external reward models.

title: DepthPilot: From Controllability to Interpretability in Colonoscopy Video Generation
summary: Controllable medical video generation has achieved remarkable progress, but it still lacks interpretability, which requires the alignment of generated contents with physical priors and faithful clinical manifestations. To push the boundaries from mere controllability to interpretability, we propose DepthPilot, the first interpretable framework for colonoscopy video generation. This work takes a step toward trustworthy generation through two synergistic paradigms. To achieve explicit geometric grounding, DepthPilot devises a prior distribution alignment strategy, injecting depth constraints into the diffusion backbone via parameter-efficient fine-tuning to ensure anatomical fidelity. To enhance intrinsic nonlinear modeling under these geometric constraints, DepthPilot employs an adaptive spline denoising module, replacing fixed linear weights with learnable spline functions to capture complex spatio-temporal dynamics. Extensive evaluations across three public datasets and in-house clinical data confirm DepthPilot's robust ability to produce physically consistent videos. It achieves FID scores below 15 across all benchmarks and ranks first in clinician assessments, bridging the gap between "visually realistic" and "clinically interpretable". Moreover, DepthPilot-generated videos are expected to enable reliable 3D reconstruction, facilitating surgical navigation and blind region identification, and serve as a foundation toward the colorectal world model.

AI.tldr: DepthPilot introduces an interpretable diffusion-based colonoscopy video generation framework that grounds synthesis in depth priors and adaptive spline denoising, achieving strong quantitative/clinical performance and enabling downstream tasks like 3D reconstruction and navigation.
AI.motivation: Current controllable medical video generation often lacks interpretability and explicit geometric grounding. For colonoscopy, faithful depth geometry and clinically faithful manifestations are crucial to trustworthiness, navigation, and potentially a colorectal world model.
AI.method: Two synergistic components: (1) prior distribution alignment that injects depth constraints into the diffusion backbone via parameter-efficient fine-tuning to preserve anatomical fidelity; (2) adaptive spline denoising that replaces fixed linear weights with learnable spline functions to model complex spatio-temporal dynamics under geometric constraints.
AI.result: Evaluations on three public datasets and in-house clinical data show depth-grounded videos with robust physical consistency. Reported FID scores are below 15 across benchmarks, and clinicians rank DepthPilot first in assessments, suggesting good interpretability alongside realism. The approach also supports 3D reconstruction, surgical navigation, blind region identification, and serves as groundwork for a colorectal world model.
AI.conclusion: DepthPilot advances from mere controllability to interpretable, geometry-grounded medical video generation. It reconciles visual realism with clinical interpretability and promises downstream benefits in navigation and modeling, while laying the foundation for trustworthy, depth-consistent colorectal video synthesis.

title: FutureWorld: A Live Environment for Training Predictive Agents with Real-World Outcome Rewards
comment: Our experiments are ongoing, and we will release the code in the near future. We release a subset of our historical data on Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/datasets/PredictingFuture/FutureWorld
summary: Live future prediction refers to the task of making predictions about real-world events before they unfold. This task is increasingly studied using large language model-based agent systems, and it is important for building agents that can continually learn from real-world. Just as interactive environments have often driven progress in agents, advancing live future prediction naturally motivates viewing it as a learning environment. Prior works have explored future prediction from several different parts, but have generally not framed it as a unified learning environment. This task is appealing for learning because it can provide a large number of prediction questions grounded in diverse real-world events, while preventing answer leakage. To leverage the advantages of live future prediction, we present FutureWorld, a live agentic reinforcement learning environment that closes the training loop between prediction, outcome realization, and parameters update. In our environment, we take three open-source base models and train them for consecutive days. The results show that training is effective. Furthermore, we build a daily benchmark based on the environment and evaluate several frontier agents on it to establish performance baselines for current agent systems.

AI.tldr: FutureWorld is a unified live future-prediction RL environment that closes the loop between prediction, outcome, and learning, demonstrating effective training of three base models and establishing a daily benchmark for frontier agents.
AI.motivation: To unify fragmented live future prediction tasks into a single learning environment that supports continual real-world learning, leveraging LLM-based agents while preventing answer leakage and providing grounded, diverse prediction questions.
AI.method: Construct FutureWorld as a live agentic RL environment; train three open-source base models over consecutive days; create a daily benchmark; evaluate frontier agents to establish performance baselines.
AI.result: Training shows effectiveness; a daily benchmark is established; frontier agents provide performance baselines for current agent systems.
AI.conclusion: FutureWorld presents a viable, scalable framework for live future prediction, enabling closed-loop learning and standardized evaluation to advance agent systems in real-world prediction tasks.

title: Stochastic Entanglement of Deterministic Origami Tentacles For Universal Robotic Gripping
summary: Origami-inspired robotic grippers have shown promising potential for object manipulation tasks due to their compact volume and mechanical flexibility. However, robust capture of objects with random shapes in dynamic working environments often comes at the cost of additional actuation channels and control complexity. Here, we introduce a tendon-driven origami tentacle gripper capable of universal object gripping by exploiting a synergy between local, deterministic deformation programming and global, stochastic entanglements. Each origami tentacle is made by cutting thin Mylar sheets; It features carefully placed holes for routing an actuation tendon, origami creases for controlling the deformation, and a tapered shape. By tailoring these design features, one can prescribe the shrinking, bending, and twisting deformation, eventually creating deterministic coiling with a simple tendon pull. Then, when multiple coiling tentacles are placed in proximity, stochastic entanglement emerges, allowing the tentacles to braid, knot, and grip objects with random shapes. We derived a simulation model by integrating origami mechanics with Cosserat rods to correlate origami design, tendon deformation, and their collective gripping performance. Then, we experimentally tested how these coiling and entangling origami tentacles can grasp objects under gravity and in water. A stow-and-release deployment mechanism was also tested to simulate in-orbit grasping. Overall, the entertaining origami tentacle gripper presents a new strategy for robust object grasping with simple design and actuation.

AI.tldr: A tendon-driven origami tentacle gripper uses deterministic local deformation and global stochastic entanglement to robustly grasp objects of random shapes with simple actuation, supported by Cosserat-rod–origami modeling and experiments in gravity, water, and stow-release deployment.
AI.motivation: Enable reliable manipulation of objects with arbitrary shapes in dynamic environments without complex actuation, by leveraging the natural entanglement of multiple tentacles to achieve universal gripping.
AI.method: Design origami tentacles from Mylar with routing holes, creases, and tapering; prescribe deformation via tendon pull to induce shrinking, bending, and twisting, enabling deterministic coiling. Place multiple tentacles in proximity to allow stochastic entanglement (braiding/knotting). Develop a simulation model by integrating origami mechanics with Cosserat rods to connect design, tendon actuation, and gripping performance. Experimental validation under gravity and in water, plus a stow-and-release deployment test for in-orbit-like grasping.
AI.result: Demonstrated universal gripping of objects with random geometries through a combination of deterministic coiling and stochastic entanglement, with a predictive model aligning origami design and tendon actuation to gripping performance. Successful grasping tests under gravity and in aquatic environments, and a deployable stow-release mechanism relevant to in-space manipulation.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a simple-actuation yet robust grasping strategy by merging programmable local deformation with emergent global entanglement, enabling reliable object manipulation across varied shapes and environments and showing potential for space applications.

title: SWAN: World-Aware Adaptive Multimodal Networks for Runtime Variations
summary: Multimodal deep neural networks deployed in realistic environments must contend with runtime variations: changes in modality quality, overall input complexity, and available platform resources. Current networks struggle with such fluctuations -- adaptive networks cannot adhere to a strict compute budget, controller-based networks neglect to consider input complexity, and statically provisioned networks fail at all the above. Consequently, they do not extract maximum utility from the expended computational resources. We present SWAN (Sample and World-Aware Multimodal Network), the first adaptive multimodal network that accomplishes all three goals. SWAN employs a quality-aware controller to assign resources among modalities according to a variable user-specified maximum budget. Within this budget, an adaptive gating module further optimizes efficiency by scaling layer utilization according to sample complexity. For further gains, SWAN also employs a token dropping module that masks semantically irrelevant multimodal features before performing detections. We evaluate SWAN in the domain of autonomous driving with complex multi-object 3D detection, reducing FLOPs by up to 49% with minimal degradation.

AI.tldr: SWAN is an adaptive multimodal network that jointly handles budget constraints, input complexity, and platform resources by a quality-aware controller, adaptive gating, and token dropping, achieving up to 49% FLOP reduction with minimal performance loss in 3D autonomous driving detection.
AI.motivation: The abstract addresses runtime variability in multimodal networks, including varying modality quality, input complexity, and resource availability, which can prevent static models from efficient operation and exploiting resources.
AI.method: SWAN integrates three components: (1) a quality-aware controller that allocates resources across modalities under a user-specified budget, (2) an adaptive gating module that scales layer usage per sample complexity, and (3) a token dropping module that masks semantically irrelevant multimodal features before detection.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation in autonomous driving with complex 3D object detection shows up to 49% reduction in FLOPs with minimal degradation in performance.
AI.conclusion: SWAN demonstrates that an end-to-end adaptive multimodal network can meet budget, input complexity, and resource constraints simultaneously, improving computational efficiency while preserving task performance.

title: EnerGS: Energy-Based Gaussian Splatting with Partial Geometric Priors
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has been widely adopted for scene reconstruction, where training inherently constitutes a highly coupled and non-convex optimization problem. Recent works commonly incorporate geometric priors, such as LiDAR measurements, either for initialization or as training constraints, with the goal of improving photometric reconstruction quality. However, in large-scale outdoor scenarios, such geometric supervision is often spatially incomplete and uneven, which limits its effectiveness as a reliable prior and can even be detrimental to the final reconstruction. To address this challenge, we model partially observable geometry as a continuous energy field induced by geometric evidence and propose EnerGS. Rather than enforcing geometry as a hard constraint, EnerGS provides a soft geometric guidance for the optimization of Gaussian primitives, allowing geometric information to steer the optimization process without directly restricting the solution space. Extensive experiments on large-scale outdoor scenes demonstrate that, under both sparse multi-view and monocular settings, EnerGS consistently improves photometric quality and geometric stability, while effectively mitigating overfitting during 3DGS training.

AI.tldr: EnerGS adds soft geometric guidance to 3D Gaussian Splatting by modeling missing geometry as a continuous energy field, improving photometric quality and stability in large outdoor scenes.
AI.motivation: In large-scale outdoor scenes, geometric priors (e.g., LiDAR) are often incomplete and uneven, limiting their effectiveness as hard constraints or initializations for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). A soft, continuous guidance can leverage partial geometry without over-constraining the solution.
AI.method: Introduce EnerGS, which treats partially observable geometry as a continuous energy field induced by geometric evidence and uses it to softly guide the optimization of Gaussian primitives in 3DGS rather than enforcing a hard geometric constraint.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on large-scale outdoor scenes show that EnerGS consistently improves photometric quality and geometric stability under both sparse multi-view and monocular settings, and mitigates overfitting during 3DGS training.
AI.conclusion: Soft geometric guidance via EnerGS provides robust 3DGS optimization in the presence of incomplete geometry, outperforming hard-constraint approaches and enhancing performance in real-world outdoor scenarios.

title: Bian Que: An Agentic Framework with Flexible Skill Arrangement for Online System Operations
summary: Operating and maintaining (O&M) large-scale online engine systems (search, recommendation, advertising) demands substantial human effort for release monitoring, alert response, and root cause analysis. While LLM-based agents are a natural fit for these tasks, the deployment bottleneck is not reasoning capability but orchestration: selecting, for each operational event, the relevant data (metrics, logs, change events) and the applicable operational knowledge (handbook rules and practitioner experience). Feeding all signals indiscriminately causes dilution and hallucination, while manually curating the event-to-(data, knowledge) mapping is intractable under dozens of daily releases. We present Bian Que, an agentic framework with three contributions: (i) a \emph{unified operational paradigm} abstracting day-to-day O&M into three canonical patterns: release interception, proactive inspection, and alert root cause analysis; (ii) \emph{Flexible Skill Arrangement}, where each Skill specifies which data and knowledge to retrieve for a given business-module context and can be automatically generated and updated by LLMs or iteratively refined through natural-language instructions from on-call engineers; (iii) a \emph{unified self-evolving mechanism} in which one correction signal drives two parallel pathways, case-memory-to-knowledge distillation and targeted Skill refinement. Deployed on the e-commerce search engine of KuaiShou, the major short-video platform in China, Bian Que reduces alert volume by 75%, achieves 80% root-cause analysis accuracy, and cuts mean time to resolution by over 50%. Our framework achieves 99.0% pass rate on offline evaluations. Our code is available at https://github.com/benchen4395/BianQue_Assistant.

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
AI.method: Method extraction failed
AI.result: Result analysis unavailable
AI.conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

title: Bi-Level Optimization for Contact and Motion Planning in Rope-Assisted Legged Robots
summary: This paper presents a planning pipeline framework for locomotion in rope-assisted robots climbing vertical surfaces. The proposed framework is formulated as a bi-level optimization scheme that addresses a mixed-integer problem: selecting feasible terrain regions for landing while simultaneously optimizing the control inputs, namely rope tensions and leg forces, and landing location. The outer level of the optimization is solved using the Cross-Entropy Method, while the inner level relies on gradient-based nonlinear optimization to compute dynamically feasible motions. The approach is validated on a novel climbing robot platform, ALPINE, across a variety of challenging terrain configurations.

AI.tldr: Bi-level optimization-based planning pipeline for rope-assisted rope climbing on vertical surfaces; outer Cross-Entropy Method selects feasible landing regions, inner gradient-based optimization computes rope tensions, leg forces, and landing location for dynamically feasible motions; validated on the ALPINE robot across diverse terrains.
AI.motivation: Address the mixed-integer planning problem in rope-assisted vertical climbing by jointly selecting landing regions and optimizing continuous control inputs.
AI.method: Bi-level optimization: outer level solved with the Cross-Entropy Method to choose feasible terrain landing regions; inner level uses gradient-based nonlinear optimization to compute rope tensions, leg forces, and landing location ensuring dynamic feasibility.
AI.result: Validation on a novel climbing robot platform (ALPINE) across a variety of challenging terrain configurations, demonstrating the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed bi-level planning pipeline effectively handles discrete region selection and continuous control optimization for rope-assisted climbing, with demonstrated robustness across diverse terrains on ALPINE.

title: Efficient and Interpretable Transformer for Counterfactual Fairness
summary: The growing reliance of machine learning models in high-stakes, highly regulated domains such as finance and insurance has created a growing tension between predictive performance, interpretability, and regulatory fairness requirements. In these settings, models are expected not only to deliver reliable predictions but also to provide transparent decision rationales and comply with strict fairness requirements. Attention-based transformers offer powerful mechanisms for modeling complex data relationships as demonstrated in various language tasks, yet their attention mechanisms alone do not ensure counterfactually fair predictions, even when combined with fairness-aware techniques. To address these limitations, we propose the Feature Correlation Transformer (FCorrTransformer), an attention-light architecture tailored for tabular data. In this design, the attention matrix admits a direct statistical interpretation as pairwise feature dependencies, enhancing both interpretability and efficiency. Leveraging this structure, we introduce Counterfactual Attention Regularization (CAR), a framework that enforces group-invariant fair representations of sensitive features at the attention level, promoting counterfactually fair predictions without relying on explicit causal assumptions. Empirical evaluations on imbalanced classification and regression benchmarks demonstrate that FCorrTransformer combined with CAR achieves strong counterfactual fairness while maintaining competitive predictive performance and substantially reducing model complexity compared with standard transformer-based baselines. Overall, this work bridges a critical gap between fairness theory and machine learning models, offering a practical framework for responsible AI in regulatory-sensitive domains.

AI.tldr: Proposes FCorrTransformer, an interpretable, attention-light transformer for tabular data, with Counterfactual Attention Regularization (CAR) to achieve counterfactual fairness; demonstrates strong fairness with competitive accuracy and lower model complexity than standard transformers.
AI.motivation: There is a tension among predictive performance, interpretability, and regulatory fairness in high-stakes domains. Existing attention-based transformers do not guarantee counterfactual fairness, and practical fairness in tabular data remains challenging, especially without explicit causal modeling.
AI.method: Introduce FCorrTransformer, an attention-light architecture where the attention matrix is interpretable as pairwise feature dependencies. Propose Counterfactual Attention Regularization (CAR) to enforce group-invariant representations of sensitive features at the attention level, aiming for counterfactually fair predictions without requiring explicit causal assumptions. Evaluate on imbalanced classification and regression benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical results show that FCorrTransformer with CAR achieves strong counterfactual fairness while maintaining competitive predictive performance and substantially reducing model complexity compared with standard transformer baselines.
AI.conclusion: Bridges fairness theory and practical ML in regulatory-sensitive domains by providing a practical, interpretable, and efficient framework for counterfactually fair, responsible AI.

title: Camera-RFID Fusion for Robust Asset Tracking in Forested Environments
comment: 11 Pages, 10 Figures, Submitted and awaiting acceptance at IEEE RFID
summary: Passive RFID tags offer a cost-effective and scalable solution for tracking numerous deployed assets. However, in forested environments, signal attenuation and multipath effects generally limit RFID spatial accuracy to the meter level. Conversely, while cameras employing stereo vision can achieve centimeter-level precision, relying solely on computer vision fails to resolve issues arising from spatial association ambiguity and partial occlusions in dense settings. Fusing these modalities allows systems to harness the high-accuracy benefits of vision while retaining the robust, non-line-of-sight identification advantages of RFID. Yet, a primary challenge in achieving this, which is the central focus of this paper, lies in accurately associating the disparate trajectories generated by these two sensors. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a novel camera--RFID fusion framework that integrates depth and object information with advanced trajectory-matching algorithms. By successfully bridging the meter-to-centimeter accuracy gap, the proposed approach helps achieve reliable tag localization even when assets temporarily leave the camera's field of view. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first application of camera--RFID fusion for asset tracking in natural forested environments.

AI.tldr: A camera–RFID fusion framework achieves centimeter-level asset localization in forested environments by aligning depth-enabled vision with RFID trajectories, enabling robust tracking when items leave the camera FOV.
AI.motivation: Forest RFID signals are attenuated and distorted; vision offers high accuracy but suffers from association ambiguity and occlusions. Fusion aims to combine strengths of both modalities for reliable, scalable asset tracking in natural forests.
AI.method: Fuse depth and object information from camera data with advanced trajectory-matching algorithms to link RFID tag trajectories with visual tracks, enabling meter-to-centimeter localization and continuity when the tag is temporarily out of the camera view.
AI.result: Claims to be the first camera–RFID fusion application for asset tracking in natural forested environments; demonstrates centimeter-level localization and improved tracking continuity across camera occlusions.
AI.conclusion: The approach bridges the accuracy gap between RFID and visual sensing, enhancing robust asset tracking in challenging forested settings and enabling scalable deployment with low-cost RFID and vision sensors.

title: Unsupervised Graph Modeling for Anomaly Detection in Accounting Subject Relationships
summary: This paper addresses the problem of anomaly detection in accounting subject association structures, proposing a structured modeling and unsupervised discriminant framework based on graph neural networks. This framework is used to mine stable correspondences between subjects and identify structural deviations from general ledger details and voucher entries. The method first abstracts accounting subjects as graph nodes, and the co-occurrence and debit/credit correspondence of subjects in the same business record are abstracted as weighted edges. The edge weights are characterized by statistical measures such as co-occurrence frequency or amount aggregation, thus forming a period-level accounting subject association graph. In the representation learning stage, a message passing mechanism is used to fuse the node's own attributes and neighborhood context to obtain node embeddings containing structural information. In the anomaly detection stage, the rationality of subject pair connections is estimated through a relation reconstruction decoder, and edge-level anomaly scores are defined based on the degree of deviation in reconstruction probabilities. These scores are then aggregated to obtain node-level risk ranking and local anomaly localization. This framework can simultaneously capture local substructure anomalies and cross-community anomaly connections without relying on anomaly labeling, outputting traceable subject pair risk clues. Comparative experiments demonstrate more stable comprehensive discriminant capabilities and higher top-ranking accuracy.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised graph neural network framework for anomaly detection in accounting subject associations, using edge reconstruction to identify structural deviations in ledger/voucher data, yielding node-level risk rankings without labeled data.
AI.motivation: Address the need for unsupervised anomaly detection in accounting subject associations where labeled anomalies are scarce, by leveraging graph structure to uncover both local substructure anomalies and cross-community deviations.
AI.method: Construct a period-level accounting subject association graph with nodes as accounting subjects and edges weighted by co-occurrence and debit/credit correspondence; apply a message passing GNN to learn node embeddings; use a relation reconstruction decoder to assess the rationality of subject-pair connections; compute edge-level anomaly scores from reconstruction deviations and aggregate to node-level risk rankings for local anomaly localization, all without requiring anomaly labels; output traceable risk clues.
AI.result: Comparative experiments show the approach achieves more stable comprehensive discriminant capabilities and higher top-ranking accuracy compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively detects both local and cross-community anomalies in accounting data in an unsupervised manner, providing traceable subject-pair risk clues and robust node-level risk rankings.

title: MetaSR: Content-Adaptive Metadata Orchestration for Generative Super-Resolution
summary: We study generative super-resolution (SR) in real-world scenarios where content and degradations vary across domains, genres, and segments. For example, images and videos may alternate between text overlays, fast motion, smooth cartoons, and low-light faces, each benefiting from different forms of side information. Existing metadata-guided SR methods typically use a fixed conditioning design, which is suboptimal when useful cues are content dependent and transmission budgets are limited. We propose MetaSR, a Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based framework that selects and injects task-relevant metadata to guide SR under resource constraints. Specifically, we use the DiT's own VAE and transformer backbone to fuse heterogeneous metadata, and adopt an efficient distillation strategy that enables one-step diffusion inference. Experiments across diverse content buckets and degradation regimes show that MetaSR outperforms reference solutions by up to 1.0~dB PSNR while achieving up to 50\% transmission bitrate saving at matched quality. We assess these gains under a rate--distortion optimization (RDO) framework that jointly accounts for sender-side bitrate and receiver/display quality metrics (e.g., PSNR and SSIM).

AI.tldr: MetaSR uses a Diffusion Transformer with content-dependent metadata conditioning and distillation for efficient SR under bitrate constraints, achieving strong rate–distortion gains.
AI.motivation: In real-world SR, content and degradations vary across domains; fixed conditioning is suboptimal when cues are content-dependent and bandwidth is limited.
AI.method: Employs Diffusion Transformer (DiT) with its VAE and transformer to fuse heterogeneous metadata; selects task-relevant cues and applies an efficient distillation strategy enabling one-step diffusion inference; evaluated within a rate–distortion optimization framework.
AI.result: Outperforms reference methods by up to 1.0 dB PSNR and achieves up to 50% bitrate savings at matched quality across diverse content and degradation regimes.
AI.conclusion: Content-adaptive metadata conditioning with a DiT-based SR framework yields substantial rate–distortion gains in real-world generative SR tasks.

title: DORA: A Scalable Asynchronous Reinforcement Learning System for Language Model Training
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a critical paradigm for LLM post-training, yet the rollout phase -- accounting for 50--80% of total step time -- is bottlenecked by skewed generation: long-tailed trajectories indispensable for model performance block the entire training pipeline. Asynchronous training offers a natural remedy by overlapping generation with training, but introduces a fundamental tension between efficiency and algorithmic correctness. We identify three constraints in asynchronous training to preserve convergence: intra-trajectory policy consistency, data integrity, and bounded staleness. Existing approaches fail to intrinsically address the long-tailed trajectory problem, which is further exacerbated by the imbalance characteristic of Mix-of-Experts models, or deviate from the standard RL training formulation, thereby hindering model convergence. Therefore, we propose DORA (Dynamic ORchestration for Asynchronous Rollout), which addresses this challenge through algorithm-system co-design. DORA introduces multi-version streaming rollout, a novel asynchronous paradigm that maintains multiple policy versions concurrently -- simultaneously achieving full bubble elimination without compromising algorithmic constraints. Experimental results demonstrate that our DORA system achieves substantial improvements in throughput -- up to 2--3 times higher than state-of-the-art systems on open-source benchmarks -- without compromising convergence. Furthermore, in large-scale industrial applications with tens of thousands of accelerators, DORA accelerates RL training by 2--4 times compared to synchronous training across various scenarios. The resultant open-source models, LongCat-Flash-Thinking, exhibit competitive performance on complex reasoning benchmarks, matching the capability of most advanced LLMs.

AI.tldr: DORA introduces multi-version streaming rollout to accelerate RL-based LLM post-training by overlapping rollout with training while preserving convergence; achieves substantial throughput gains without sacrificing convergence.
AI.motivation: Rollout is the main bottleneck; asynchronous rollout can improve efficiency but risks convergence; long-tailed trajectories and mixture-of-experts worsen; need to address intra-trajectory consistency, data integrity, and bounded staleness.
AI.method: Algorithm-system co-design; Dynamic ORchestration for Asynchronous Rollout (DORA); multi-version streaming rollout maintaining multiple policy versions concurrently; aligns generation with training while preserving convergence constraints.
AI.result: Throughput up to 2–3x on open benchmarks vs state of the art; 2–4x speedups in large-scale industrial settings; LongCat-Flash-Thinking open-source models achieve competitive reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: DORA demonstrates that asynchronous rollout can be effectively integrated with RL for LLM post-training, delivering major speedups without compromising convergence; enables scalable training pipelines with open-source models.

title: Beyond Shortcuts: Mitigating Visual Illusions in Frozen VLMs via Qualitative Reasoning
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures, and 1 table. This is a methodology paper for the DataCV 2026 Challenge (CVPR Workshops), Task 1, where our method ranked 2nd
summary: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved state-of-the-art performance in general visual tasks, their perceptual robustness remains remarkably brittle when confronted with optical illusions. These failures are often attributed to shortcut heuristics, where models prioritize linguistic priors and memorized prototypes over direct visual evidence. In this work, we propose Structured Qualitative Inference (SQI), a training-free, data-centric framework designed to fortify visual grounding in frozen VLMs. SQI addresses perceptual anomalies through three systematic modules: (1) Axiomatic Constraint Injection, which suppresses erroneous metric estimations and quantitative hallucinations; (2) Hierarchical Scene Decomposition, which decouples target visual manifolds from complex background distractors; and (3) Counterfactual Self-Verification, an adversarial reasoning step that mitigates confirmation bias. By orchestrating these qualitative constraints at inference time, SQI effectively aligns high-level linguistic reasoning with low-level visual perception. Our framework was evaluated on the DataCV 2026 Challenge (Task I: Classic Illusion Understanding), where it ranked 2nd place overall. Experimental results demonstrate that SQI not only significantly enhances accuracy across diverse illusion categories but also provides superior diagnostic interpretability without any model fine-tuning. Our success underscores the potential of structured qualitative grounding as a robust paradigm for developing next-generation, illusion-resistant vision-language systems.

AI.tldr: SQI is a training-free, data-centric framework that strengthens visual grounding in frozen Vision-Language Models against optical illusions via three modules, achieving 2nd place in DataCV 2026 Task I and improving interpretability without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: VLMs show perceptual brittleness to optical illusions due to shortcut priors and reliance on linguistic/memorized cues. There is a need to align high-level linguistic reasoning with low-level visual evidence by injecting qualitative constraints at inference time.
AI.method: Introduce three modules operating at inference: (1) Axiomatic Constraint Injection to suppress erroneous metric estimates and quantify hallucinations; (2) Hierarchical Scene Decomposition to separate target visuals from background distractors; (3) Counterfactual Self-Verification, an adversarial reasoning step to reduce confirmation bias. All constraints are applied without training, making the approach training-free and data-centric.
AI.result: In the DataCV 2026 Challenge (Task I: Classic Illusion Understanding), SQI ranked 2nd overall. Experiments show significant accuracy gains across diverse illusion categories and improved diagnostic interpretability without any model fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Structured Qualitative Grounding (SQI) is a promising paradigm for robust, illusion-resistant vision-language systems, effectively aligning high-level language reasoning with low-level visual perception without altering pre-trained models.

title: NeuroPlastic: A Plasticity-Modulated Optimizer for Biologically Inspired Learning Dynamics
summary: Optimization algorithms are fundamental to modern deep learning, yet most widely used methods rely on update rules based primarily on local gradient statistics. We introduce NeuroPlastic, a plasticity-modulated optimizer that augments gradient-based updates with an adaptive multi-signal modulation mechanism inspired by multi-factor synaptic plasticity, a concept from neurobiology. NeuroPlastic dynamically scales gradient updates using interacting components that capture gradient, activity-like, and memory-like statistics, forming a lightweight modulation layer compatible with standard deep learning training pipelines. Across image classification benchmarks, NeuroPlastic consistently improves over a controlled gradient-only ablation, with more pronounced gains on the Fashion-MNIST benchmark and in reduced-data regimes. In transfer experiments on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18, the method remains stable and competitive without retuning. These results suggest that multi-signal plasticity-inspired modulation can provide a useful extension to conventional gradient-driven optimization, particularly when learning signals are limited or noisy, and offer a promising direction for gradient-based methods in deep learning.

AI.tldr: A gradient-based optimizer (NeuroPlastic) augments updates with a lightweight, plasticity-inspired multi-signal modulation that combines gradient, activity-like, and memory-like statistics to improve training, especially in low-data or noisy settings.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of purely gradient-based updates by incorporating non-gradient signals inspired by synaptic plasticity, enabling more robust and data-efficient learning.
AI.method: Introduce NeuroPlastic, a modulation layer that dynamically scales gradient updates using interacting gradient-, activity-, and memory-like components. It is lightweight and compatible with standard training pipelines, augmenting existing optimizers rather than replacing them.
AI.result: Across image classification benchmarks, NeuroPlastic consistently surpasses gradient-only ablations, with larger gains on Fashion-MNIST and in reduced-data regimes; in transfer experiments on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18, performance remains stable and competitive without retuning.
AI.conclusion: Multi-signal plasticity-inspired modulation can enhance gradient-based optimization, particularly when learning signals are limited or noisy, representing a promising direction for improving deep learning optimizers.

title: Multi-Stage Bi-Atrial Segmentation Framework from 3D Late Gadolinium-Enhanced MRI using V-Net Family Models
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, technical report for participating the MBAS2024 challenge hosted on the MICCAI2024 conference
summary: We report our multi-stage framework designed for the problem of multi-class bi-atrial segmentation from 3D late gadolinium-enhanced (LGE) MRI of the human heart. The pipeline consists of a preprocessing step using multidimensional contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (MCLAHE); coarse region segmentation from MCLAHE-enhanced and down-sampled MRI using a V-Net family model; and fine segmentation from the coarse region using another V-Net model. Asymmetric loss is adopted to optimize the model weights.

AI.tldr: A multi-stage V-Net based framework for multi-class bi-atrial segmentation in 3D LGE MRI, featuring MCLAHE preprocessing and a coarse-to-fine segmentation with asymmetric loss.
AI.motivation: Accurate segmentation of left and right atria in 3D LGE MRI is challenging due to variability in anatomy and image contrast; a robust pipeline combining image enhancement, coarse localization, and precise fine segmentation aims to improve accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: 1) Preprocess images with multidimensional contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (MCLAHE). 2) Generate a coarse region by down-sampling the enhanced image and performing segmentation with a V-Net family model. 3) Perform fine segmentation within the coarse region using a second V-Net model. 4) Train with an asymmetric loss to balance class performances and optimize weights.
AI.result: Not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Not specified in the abstract.

title: Edge AI for Automotive Vulnerable Road User Safety: Deployable Detection via Knowledge Distillation
summary: Deploying accurate object detection for Vulnerable Road User (VRU) safety on edge hardware requires balancing model capacity against computational constraints. Large models achieve high accuracy but fail under INT8 quantization required for edge deployment, while small models sacrifice detection performance. This paper presents a knowledge distillation (KD) framework that trains a compact YOLOv8-S student (11.2M parameters) to mimic a YOLOv8-L teacher (43.7M parameters), achieving 3.9x compression while preserving quantization robustness. We evaluate on full-scale BDD100K (70K training images) with Post-Training Quantization to INT8. The teacher suffers catastrophic degradation under INT8 (-23% mAP), while the KD student retains accuracy (-5.6% mAP). Analysis reveals that KD transfers precision calibration rather than raw detection capacity: the KD student achieves 0.748 precision versus 0.653 for direct training at INT8, a 14.5% gain at equivalent recall, reducing false alarms by 44% versus the collapsed teacher. At INT8, the KD student exceeds the teacher's FP32 precision (0.748 vs. 0.718) in a model 3.9x smaller. These findings establish knowledge distillation as a requirement for deploying accurate, safety-critical VRU detection on edge hardware.

AI.tldr: Knowledge distillation enables a compact YOLOv8-S to retain INT8 quantization robustness when deployed on edge hardware by mimicking a larger YOLOv8-L teacher; achieves 3.9x compression with less accuracy drop than direct quantization, due to transferring precision calibration rather than raw capacity.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate vulnerable road user (VRU) detection on edge devices under INT8 constraints; large models degrade under quantization; need compact models that preserve precision and reduce false alarms.
AI.method: Train a YOLOv8-S student (11.2M) to imitate a YOLOv8-L teacher (43.7M) using a knowledge distillation framework. Evaluate on full-scale BDD100K (70K train) with Post-Training Quantization to INT8. Compare against direct INT8 training and FP32 teacher. Analyze calibration transfer vs raw capacity and measure mAP and precision; report false-alarm reductions.
AI.result: 3.9x model compression (11.2M vs 43.7M). Teacher degrades under INT8 with -23% mAP; KD student preserves accuracy with -5.6% mAP. KD transfers precision calibration: KD student precision 0.748 vs 0.653 for directly trained INT8 at similar recall (14.5% gain). At INT8, KD student outperforms FP32 teacher precision (0.748 vs 0.718). False alarms reduced by 44% relative to collapsed teacher.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge distillation is essential for deploying accurate, safety-critical VRU detection on edge hardware; KD transfers calibration signals rather than raw capacity, enabling robust INT8 inference with high precision while significantly compressing the model.

title: Cheeger--Hodge Contrastive Learning for Structurally Robust Graph Representation Learning
summary: Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) has emerged as a prominent framework for unsupervised graph representation learning. However, relying on augmentation design alone to define the invariances learned by GCL can be brittle under structural perturbations. To address this issue, we propose Cheeger--Hodge Contrastive Learning (CHCL), a framework that aligns a perturbation-stable Cheeger--Hodge joint signature across augmented views for robust graph representation learning. The proposed signature combines a Cheeger-inspired connectivity signature derived from the algebraic connectivity \(λ_2\) with the low-frequency spectrum of the 1-Hodge Laplacian, thereby capturing both global connectivity and higher-order structural information. By aligning encoder representations with the proposed Cheeger--Hodge joint signature across augmented views, CHCL learns graph embeddings that are robust to local structural perturbations. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks, transfer settings demonstrate that CHCL consistently improves performance, robustness, and generalization.

AI.tldr: CHCL introduces a perturbation-stable Cheeger–Hodge joint signature for graph contrastive learning, aligning encoder representations across augmented views to improve robustness and transferability.
AI.motivation: Augmentation-based invariances in GCL can be brittle under structural perturbations; there is a need for perturbation-stable, topology-aware representations.
AI.method: Construct a Cheeger-inspired connectivity signature from algebraic connectivity λ2 and the low-frequency spectrum of the 1-Hodge Laplacian; enforce cross-view alignment of encoder representations with this joint signature via contrastive learning.
AI.result: Empirical results on standard benchmarks and transfer tasks show improved performance, robustness to perturbations, and generalization over baselines.
AI.conclusion: A perturbation-stable, topology-aware embedding framework, CHCL, improves GCL robustness and generalization by jointly leveraging global connectivity and higher-order topology through Cheeger–Hodge signatures.

title: OmniTrend: Content-Context Modeling for Scalable Social Popularity Prediction
summary: Predicting social media popularity requires understanding both the intrinsic appeal of content and the external context that determines how it is exposed to users. Existing methods focus on content signals but do not separate them from exposure-related patterns, which causes the learned representations to absorb platform-specific visibility effects and weakens both interpretability and cross-platform transfer. This paper introduces OmniTrend, a unified framework that models popularity as the joint outcome of content attractiveness and contextual exposure. The content module learns cross-modal representations from visual, audio, and textual cues to quantify intrinsic appeal, while the context module estimates exposure from exogenous signals such as posting time, author activity, topical trends, and retrieval-based neighborhood statistics. OmniTrend learns separate predictors for content attractiveness and contextual exposure and integrates them in the final popularity estimate, which makes the role of each factor explicit and supports robust transfer across image and video platforms.

AI.tldr: OmniTrend disentangles popularity into content attractiveness and contextual exposure via a two-branch model, enabling explicit interpretability and cross-platform transfer.
AI.motivation: Current approaches conflate intrinsic content appeal with exposure patterns, reducing interpretability and hindering cross-platform generalization.
AI.method: A content module learns cross-modal representations from visual, audio, and textual cues to quantify intrinsic appeal; a context module estimates exposure from exogenous signals (posting time, author activity, topical trends, neighborhood statistics). Separate predictors for attractiveness and exposure are learned and then integrated to predict popularity.
AI.result: The abstract claims explicit separation improves interpretability and cross-platform transfer; it does not report quantitative results.
