AI.conclusion: Disentangling content attractiveness from contextual exposure yields a unified, transfer-friendly framework for popularity prediction across image and video platforms.

title: Three-Step Nav: A Hierarchical Global-Local Planner for Zero-Shot Vision-and-Language Navigation
summary: Breakthrough progress in vision-based navigation through unknown environments has been achieved by using multimodal large language models (MLLMs). These models can plan a sequence of motions by evaluating the current view at each time step against the task and goal given to the agent. However, current zero-shot Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) agents powered by MLLMs still tend to drift off course, halt prematurely, and achieve low overall success rates. We propose Three-Step Nav to counteract these failures with a three-view protocol: First, "look forward" to extract global landmarks and sketch a coarse plan. Then, "look now" to align the current visual observation with the next sub-goal for fine-grained guidance. Finally, "look backward" audits the entire trajectory to correct accumulated drift before stopping. Requiring no gradient updates or task-specific fine-tuning, our planner drops into existing VLN pipelines with minimal overhead. Three-Step Nav achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on the R2R-CE and RxR-CE dataset. Our code is available at https://github.com/ZoeyZheng0/3-step-Nav.

AI.tldr: Introduces Three-Step Nav, a three-view, no-training planner for zero-shot Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) using multimodal LLMs. It uses a three-stage process—look forward, look now, look backward—to generate a robust plan, align sub-goals, and audit the trajectory. No gradient updates; plug-in to existing VLN pipelines. Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on R2R-CE and RxR-CE; code released.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot VLN agents powered by MLLMs still suffer from drifting off course, premature halting, and low success. A lightweight, non-fine-tuned planner that can be dropped into existing VLN systems is needed to stabilize navigation and improve success rates.
AI.method: Three-step protocol integrated into VLN pipelines: (1) look forward: extract global landmarks and sketch a coarse plan; (2) look now: align the current visual observation with the next sub-goal for fine-grained guidance; (3) look backward: audit the entire trajectory to correct accumulated drift before stopping. No gradient updates or task-specific fine-tuning; minimal overhead; compatible with existing VLN pipelines.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on the R2R-CE and RxR-CE datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed three-step visual planning approach provides a simple, effective, plug-in solution for improving robustness and success in zero-shot VLN with MLLMs without requiring fine-tuning, and offers a foundation for future enhancements.

title: Addressing Performance Saturation for LLM RL via Precise Entropy Curve Control
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has unlocked complex reasoning abilities in large language models (LLMs). However, most RL algorithms suffer from performance saturation, preventing further gains as RL training scales. This problem can be characterized by the collapse of entropy, a key diagnostic for exploration in RL. Existing attempts have tried to prevent entropy collapse through regularization or clipping, but their resulting entropy curves often exhibit instability in the long term, which hinders performance gains. In this paper, we introduce Entrocraft, a simple rejection-sampling approach that realizes any user-customized entropy schedule by biasing the advantage distributions. Entrocraft requires no objective regularization and is advantage-estimator-agnostic. Theoretically, we relate per-step entropy change to the advantage distribution under minimal assumptions, which explains the behavior of existing RL and entropy-preserving methods. Entrocraft also enables a systematic study of entropy schedules, where we find that linear annealing, which starts high and decays to a slightly lower target, performs best. Empirically, Entrocraft addresses performance saturation, significantly improving generalization, output diversity, and long-term training. It enables a 4B model to outperform an 8B baseline, sustains improvement for up to 4x longer before plateauing, and raises pass@K by 50% over the baseline.

AI.tldr: Entrocraft introduces a rejection-sampling method to realize customizable entropy schedules in RL for LLMs, addressing entropy collapse and saturation. Linear annealing (high-to-lower target) performs best; yields substantial gains: a 4B model surpasses an 8B baseline, training remains productive longer, and pass@K improves ~50% over baseline.
AI.motivation: Entropy collapse and long-term instability hinder exploration and scaling in RL for LLMs. Regularization or clipping methods can destabilize training. A simple, objective-regularization-free approach that is agnostic to the advantage estimator is sought to control entropy and study its schedules.
AI.method: Entrocraft uses a rejection-sampling scheme to bias the policy's advantage distribution to match a user-defined entropy schedule. It requires no objective regularization and is agnostic to the advantage estimator. The paper also provides theoretical links between per-step entropy change and the advantage distribution under minimal assumptions, enabling systematic study of entropy schedules.
AI.result: Empirically, Entrocraft mitigates performance saturation, improving generalization, output diversity, and long-term training stability. A 4B model with Entrocraft outperforms an 8B baseline, improvements persist up to ~4x longer before plateauing, and pass@K increases by about 50% over the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Entrocraft offers a simple, effective mechanism to realize customizable entropy schedules without regularization, enabling sustained improvements in RL-driven LLMs. Linear annealing (high-to-lower target) emerges as a particularly effective schedule, and the approach facilitates systematic analysis of entropy dynamics across training.

title: GaitKD: A Universal Decoupled Distillation Framework for Efficient Gait Recognition
summary: Gait recognition is an attractive biometric modality for long-range and contact-free identification, but high-performing gait models often rely on deep and computationally expensive architectures that are difficult to deploy in practice. Knowledge distillation (KD) offers a natural way to transfer knowledge from a powerful teacher to an efficient student; however, standard KD is often less effective for part-structured gait models, where supervision is formed from both part-wise classification logits and part-wise retrieval embeddings. In this paper, we propose GaitKD, a distillation framework that decouples gait knowledge transfer into two complementary components: decision-level distillation and boundary-level distillation. Specifically, GaitKD aligns the teacher and student through part-calibrated logit distillation to transfer inter-class decision relations, while preserving the teacher-induced partitioning of the embedding space through an activation-boundary objective instead of direct feature regression. With a simple aligned part-wise design, GaitKD supports heterogeneous teacher-student gait models without introducing additional inference cost. Experimental results across multiple gait recognition benchmarks and teacher-student configurations show consistent improvements over strong gait baselines. Our study demonstrates that the two transfer components are complementary, and boundary-preserving distillation provides more stable performance than direct feature regression. Source code is available at https://github.com/liyiersan/GaitKD/

AI.tldr: GaitKD is a two-component knowledge distillation framework for gait recognition that decouples decision-level and boundary-level distillation, enabling heterogeneous teacher-student models with no extra inference cost and improving performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Gait recognition benefits from powerful models but is hindered by heavy architectures; standard KD is less effective for part-structured gait models that combine part-wise logits and part-wise embeddings. A distillation method tailored to preserve both decision relations and embedding space partitioning is needed.
AI.method: GaitKD decouples knowledge transfer into two components: (1) part-calibrated logit distillation to transfer inter-class decision relations (decision-level); (2) an activation-boundary objective to preserve the teacher-induced partitioning of the embedding space without direct feature regression (boundary-level). The framework aligns teacher and student with a simple aligned part-wise design, supporting heterogeneous teacher-student models without increasing inference cost.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple gait recognition benchmarks and various teacher-student configurations show consistent improvements over strong baselines. The two transfer components are complementary, and boundary-preserving distillation yields more stable performance than direct feature regression.
AI.conclusion: GaitKD provides an effective, architecture-agnostic distillation framework for gait recognition, balancing decision-level and boundary-level transfer to achieve robust performance. The approach preserves embedding space structure while improving decision relations, and requires no extra inference cost. Code is available at the provided repository.

title: AlphaJet: Automated Conceptual Aircraft Synthesis via Disentangled Generative Priors and Topology-Preserving Evolutionary Search
summary: Conceptual aircraft design is traditionally an expert-mediated iterative process in which a human designer proposes a configuration, runs low-order physics, inspects the result, and re-proposes. We present AlphaJet, an end-to-end automated synthesis pipeline that closes this loop. From a textual mission specification (mass, range, cruise speed, hard size envelope, engine count, areal density) AlphaJet evolves a feasible 3D aircraft in real time, scored by a transparent multi-disciplinary fitness function covering aerodynamics, structures, weights, stability, packaging, and geometric mount consistency. Three contributions distinguish our approach: (i) an Anatomically-Disentangled Variational Autoencoder (AD-VAE) whose first 25 latent dimensions are supervised to align with named anatomical parameters, providing an interpretable shape prior; (ii) a topology-elitist genetic algorithm that protects the best individual from each of five tail topologies and triggers stagnation restarts, preventing premature collapse to a single configuration; and (iii) mount-aware geometric scoring that computes signed penetration between engines and other structural parts, eliminating the redundant artifacts common in generative aircraft models. The full loop runs interactively on a CPU and streams every generation to a browser viewer, making it a practical real-world automation tool for early-phase design-space exploration.

AI.tldr: End-to-end automated conceptual aircraft design pipeline (AlphaJet) that, from a textual mission spec, synthesizes feasible 3D aircraft in real time using an interpretable AD-VAE, a topology-elitist GA, and mount-aware scoring, with interactive CPU-based streaming to a browser viewer.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on expert-mediated, iterative design loops by providing an interpretable shape prior, robust multi-topology search that guards against premature convergence, and geometry-aware scoring to prevent non-physical artifacts, enabling rapid early-phase design-space exploration.
AI.method: Anatomically-Disentangled Variational Autoencoder (AD-VAE) with first 25 latent dimensions supervised to align with named anatomical parameters for interpretable shape priors; a topology-elitist genetic algorithm that preserves the best individual from each of five tail topologies and triggers stagnation restarts to prevent collapse to a single configuration; mount-aware geometric scoring computing signed penetration between engines and other structures to ensure feasible packaging; a CPU-based end-to-end loop that streams every generation to a browser viewer for real-time inspection and interaction.
AI.result: The system can generate feasible 3D aircraft designs from mission specifications in real time, scoring via a multidisciplinary fitness function (aerodynamics, structures, weights, stability, packaging, and mount consistency) and providing transparent feedback, with an interactive browser visualization and practical applicability for design-space exploration.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a practical, interpretable, and robust automated design tool for early-phase conceptual aircraft design, combining an interpretable generative prior, topology-aware search, and geometry-aware scoring to streamline exploration and reduce reliance on expert mediation.

title: Multiple Consistent 2D-3D Mappings for Robust Zero-Shot 3D Visual Grounding
summary:
Zero-shot 3D Visual Grounding (3DVG) is a critical capability for open-world embodied AI. However, existing methods are fundamentally bottlenecked by the poor quality of open-vocabulary 3D proposals, suffering from inaccurate categories and imprecise geometries, as well as the spatial redundancy of exhaustive multi-view reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose MCM-VG, a novel framework that achieves robust zero-shot 3DVG by explicitly establishing Multiple Consistent 2D-3D Mappings. Instead of passively relying on noisy 3D segments, MCM-VG enforces 2D-3D consistency across three fundamental dimensions to achieve precise target localization and reliable reasoning. First, a Semantic Alignment module corrects category mismatches via LLM-driven query parsing and coarse-to-fine 2D-3D matching. Second, an Instance Rectification module leverages VLM-guided 2D segmentations to reconstruct missing targets, back-projecting these reliable visual priors to establish accurate 3D geometries. Finally, to eliminate spatial redundancy, a Viewpoint Distillation module clusters 3D camera directions to extract optimal frames. By pairing these optimal RGB frames with Bird's Eye View maps into concise visual prompt sets, we formulate the final target disambiguation as a multiple-choice reasoning task for Vision-Language Models.
  Extensive evaluations on ScanRefer and Nr3D benchmarks demonstrate that MCM-VG sets a new state-of-the-art for zero-shot 3D visual grounding. Remarkably, it achieves 62.0\% and 53.6\% in Acc@0.25 and Acc@0.5 on ScanRefer, outperforming previous baselines by substantial margins of 6.4\% and 4.0\%.

AI.tldr: MCM-VG introduces a multi-2D-3D consistency framework for zero-shot 3D visual grounding, combining Semantic Alignment, Instance Rectification, and Viewpoint Distillation to overcome noisy 3D proposals and view redundancy, achieving state-of-the-art on ScanRefer and Nr3D.
AI.motivation: To overcome bottlenecks in open-vocabulary 3D proposals—category misalignment, imprecise geometry, and redundant multi-view reasoning—by enforcing cross-modal and cross-dimensional consistency.
AI.method: Three modules: Semantic Alignment with LLM-driven query parsing and coarse-to-fine 2D-3D matching; Instance Rectification using VLM-guided 2D segmentation to reconstruct missing targets and back-project to 3D; Viewpoint Distillation clustering camera directions to select optimal RGB frames, pairing with BEV maps to form concise visual prompts and cast disambiguation as a multi-choice VLM reasoning task.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA in zero-shot 3DVG on ScanRefer and Nr3D; on ScanRefer, Acc@0.25 = 62.0% and Acc@0.5 = 53.6%, exceeding baselines by 6.4% and 4.0%.
AI.conclusion: By enforcing multiple consistent 2D-3D mappings across semantic, geometric, and viewpoint dimensions, MCM-VG robustly localizes targets in zero-shot 3D groundings, reduces reliance on noisy proposals, and improves reasoning with compact visual prompts.

title: Adaptive and Fine-grained Module-wise Expert Pruning for Efficient LoRA-MoE Fine-Tuning
summary: LoRA-MoE has emerged as an effective paradigm for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, combining the low training cost of LoRA with the increased adaptation capacity of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE). However, existing LoRA-MoE frameworks typically adopt a fixed and uniform expert configuration across heterogeneous Transformer modules (\eg, attention query/key projections and MLP gating networks), ignoring their distinct functional roles and capacity requirements. This design leads to localized over-provisioning, redundant trainable parameters, and unnecessary optimizer-state overhead. Moreover, prior methods enforce load balancing among experts throughout training. Although beneficial in the early stage, this constraint becomes restrictive once routing patterns stabilize, limiting expert specialization on downstream tasks. In this paper, we propose DMEP, a novel LoRA-MoE fine-tuning framework based on Dynamic Module-wise Expert Pruning. DMEP tracks expert utilization during training and physically removes low-utility experts on a per-module basis, yielding a more compact expert structure tailored to different modules. The pruned model then continues training without the load-balancing constraint, freeing the remaining experts to focus entirely on the downstream task and develop specialized expertise. By jointly adapting module-wise expert capacity and eliminating unnecessary balancing, DMEP improves both parameter efficiency and training efficiency. Extensive experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks show that DMEP reduces trainable parameters by 35\%--43\% and improves training throughput by about 10\%, while maintaining or surpassing the downstream reasoning accuracy of uniform LoRA-MoE baselines.

AI.tldr: DMEP is a dynamic module-wise pruning approach for LoRA-MoE fine-tuning that removes low-utility experts per module, eliminating fixed load-balancing constraints and enhancing efficiency while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address inefficiencies of fixed, uniform expert configurations in LoRA-MoE across heterogeneous Transformer modules and the restrictive load-balancing constraint that hampers specialization.
AI.method: Introduce DMEP: during training, monitor expert utilization for each module, prune low-utility experts on a per-module basis, and continue training without the load-balancing constraint; the framework jointly adapts module-wise expert capacity and prunes unnecessary components to yield a compact, specialized expert structure.
AI.result: On reasoning benchmarks, DMEP reduces trainable parameters by 35–43% and increases training throughput by ~10%, while achieving downstream reasoning accuracy comparable to or better than uniform LoRA-MoE baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, module-aware pruning creates compact, specialized expert configurations per module, improving parameter and training efficiency without sacrificing performance.

title: Semantic Foam: Unifying Spatial and Semantic Scene Decomposition
comment: 15 pages, 10 figures, Accepted to CVPR 2026 (Highlight) , Project page: http://semanticfoam.github.io/
summary: Modern scene reconstruction methods, such as 3D Gaussian Splatting, enable photo-realistic novel view synthesis at real-time speeds. However, their adoption in interactive graphics applications remains limited due to the difficulty of interacting with these representations compared to traditional, human-authored 3D assets. While prior work has attempted to impose semantic decomposition on these models, significant challenges remain in segmentation quality and cross-view consistency.To address these limitations, we introduce Semantic Foam, which extends the recently proposed Radiant Foam representation to semantic decomposition tasks. Our approach leverages the inherent spatial structure of Radiant Foam's volumetric Voronoi mesh and augments it with an explicit semantic feature field defined at the cell level. This design enables direct spatial regularization, improving consistency across views and mitigating artifacts caused by occlusion and inconsistent supervision, which are common issues in point-based representations.Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves superior object-level segmentation performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches such as Gaussian Grouping and SAGA.Project page: http://semanticfoam.github.io/

AI.tldr: Semantic Foam extends Radiant Foam with a cell-level semantic field on its volumetric Voronoi mesh, enabling direct spatial regularization to improve cross-view consistency and object-level segmentation, outperforming Gaussian Grouping and SAGA.
AI.motivation: Current neural scene representations, while capable of real-time novel view synthesis, struggle with robust, consistent semantic decomposition and intuitive interaction in interactive graphics due to segmentation quality, cross-view inconsistencies, occlusion, and uneven supervision in point-based methods.
AI.method: Introduce an explicit semantic feature field defined at the Radiant Foam cell level within its volumetric Voronoi mesh, enabling spatial regularization and improved cross-view consistency for semantic segmentation.
AI.result: Experimental results show superior object-level segmentation performance compared with state-of-the-art approaches such as Gaussian Grouping and SAGA; a project page is provided.
AI.conclusion: Semantic Foam delivers improved, view-consistent semantic decomposition for neural scene representations, enhancing interactivity and addressing occlusion/supervision artifacts in point-based methods.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Reward Discounting for Mitigating Reward Hacking
summary:
Reinforcement learning (RL) systems typically optimize scalar reward functions that assume precise and reliable evaluation of outcomes. However, real-world objectives--especially those derived from human preferences--are often uncertain, context-dependent, and internally inconsistent. This mismatch can lead to alignment failures such as reward hacking, over-optimization, and overconfident behavior.
  We introduce a dual-source uncertainty-aware reward framework that explicitly models both epistemic uncertainty in value estimation and uncertainty in human preferences. Model uncertainty is captured via ensemble disagreement over value predictions, while preference uncertainty is derived from variability in reward annotations. We combine these signals through a confidence-adjusted Reliability Filter that adaptively modulates action selection, encouraging a balance between exploitation and caution.
  Empirical results across multiple discrete grid configurations (6x6, 8x8, 10x10) and high-dimensional continuous control environments (Hopper-v4, Walker2d-v4) demonstrate that our approach yields more stable training dynamics and reduces exploitative behaviors under reward ambiguity, achieving a 93.7% reduction in reward-hacking behavior as measured by trap visitation frequency. We demonstrate statistical significance of these improvements and robustness under up to 30% supervisory noise, albeit with a trade-off in peak observed reward compared to unconstrained baselines.
  By treating uncertainty as a first-class component of the reward signal, this work offers a principled approach toward more reliable and aligned reinforcement learning systems.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware reward framework combining epistemic value uncertainty (ensembles) and human-preference uncertainty (annotation variability) via a confidence-adjusted Reliability Filter to balance exploitation and caution, reducing reward hacking and improving stability under noise but with some loss in peak rewards.
AI.motivation: Real-world objectives, especially human-derived rewards, are uncertain and conflicting, causing alignment failures such as reward hacking and over-optimization when scalar rewards are assumed to be precise.
AI.method: Model uncertainty is captured with ensemble disagreement over value estimates; preference uncertainty is derived from variability in reward annotations. These signals feed into a confidence-adjusted Reliability Filter that modulates action selection to balance exploitation and caution.
AI.result: Tested on discrete grids (6x6, 8x8, 10x10) and continuous tasks (Hopper-v4, Walker2d-v4). Showed more stable training and reduced exploitative behavior under reward ambiguity, including a 93.7% reduction in reward-hacking trap visits, statistical significance, and robustness to up to 30% supervisory noise, with a trade-off of lower peak reward relative to unconstrained baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating uncertainty as an integral part of the reward signal yields more reliable and aligned RL systems.

title: High-Dimensional Noise to Low-Dimensional Manifolds: A Manifold-Space Diffusion Framework for Degraded Hyperspectral Image Classification
summary: Recently, Hyperspectral Image (HSI) classification has attracted increasing attention in remote sensing. However, HSI data are inherently high-dimensional but low-rank, with discriminative information concentrated on a low-dimensional latent manifold. In real-world remote sensing scenarios, the superposition of multiple degradation factors disrupts this intrinsic manifold structure, driving samples away from their original low-dimensional distribution and introducing substantial redundant and non-discriminative variations. To better handle this challenge, this paper proposes a manifold-space diffusion framework (MSDiff) for robust hyperspectral classification under complex degradation conditions. Specifically, the proposed method first maps high-dimensional, degradation-affected HSI data into a compact low-dimensional manifold through a discriminative spectral-spatial reconstruction task, preserving class semantics and reducing redundant variations. A diffusion-based generative model is then applied to regularize the spectral-spatial distribution within the manifold, enabling progressive refinement and stabilization of latent features against residual degradations. The key advantage of the proposed framework lies in performing diffusion-based distribution modeling directly on the low-dimensional manifold, effectively decoupling degradation-induced disturbances from intrinsic discriminative structures and enhancing representation stability under complex degradations. Experimental results on multiple hyperspectral benchmarks demonstrate consistent performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods under diverse composite degradation settings. The code will be available at https://github.com/yangboxiang1207/MSDiff

AI.tldr: MSDiff: a manifold-space diffusion framework for robust HSI classification under complex degradations, which maps degraded high-dimensional HSIs into a discriminative low-dimensional manifold via spectral–spatial reconstruction and then applies diffusion to refine representations on the manifold, improving robustness and accuracy across benchmarks; code available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Hyperspectral data are high-dimensional yet low-rank, with discriminative information concentrated on a latent manifold. Real-world degradations distort this manifold, causing redundant and non-discriminative variations. A robust classifier should decouple degradation from intrinsic structure.
AI.method: 1) Learn a discriminative spectral-spatial reconstruction to map data into a compact low-dimensional manifold while preserving class semantics. 2) Apply a diffusion-based generative model on the manifold to regularize the distribution, enabling progressive refinement and stabilization of latent features against remaining degradations.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple hyperspectral benchmarks show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods under diverse composite degradations.
AI.conclusion: Operating diffusion directly on the low-dimensional manifold decouples degradation-induced disturbances from intrinsic discriminative structures, yielding more stable representations and improved classification robustness under complex degradations. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

title: CoQuant: Joint Weight-Activation Subspace Projection for Mixed-Precision LLMs
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) has become an important technique for reducing the inference cost of Large Language Models (LLMs). While recent mixed-precision methods improve ultra-low bit quantization by preserving critical subspaces in high precision, they typically construct these subspaces relying solely on activation statistics. This ignores the fundamental nature of linear operations, where the output perturbation is jointly driven by both activation and weight quantization noise. In this paper, we propose CoQuant, a joint weight-activation subspace projection method. By theoretically modeling the expected output error, CoQuant formulates a closed-form weighted PCA solution that balances activation and weight covariances to select the optimal high-precision subspace. Extensive experiments on Llama-3.2 and Qwen2.5 models show that CoQuant consistently outperforms strong PTQ baselines in both WikiText perplexity and zero-shot common-sense reasoning accuracy. These results demonstrate that joint weight-activation subspace modeling provides a principled and effective direction for low-bit LLM quantization. The source code is available at https://github.com/Zachary5895/CoQuant.

AI.tldr: CoQuant introduces a joint weight-activation subspace projection for post-training quantization that uses a closed-form weighted PCA to balance activation and weight noise, yielding consistent gains over baselines on LLMs (Llama-3.2, Qwen2.5) in perplexity and zero-shot reasoning.
AI.motivation: PTQ for LLMs often achieves ultra-low bit quantization by preserving critical subspaces based on activation statistics alone. This neglects the joint impact of weight quantization noise, which also perturbs outputs in linear layers.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical model of expected output error under joint weight-activation quantization. Derive a closed-form weighted PCA solution that balances activation and weight covariances to select the optimal high-precision subspace for quantization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Llama-3.2 and Qwen2.5 shows CoQuant consistently outperforms strong PTQ baselines in WikiText perplexity and zero-shot common-sense reasoning accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Joint weight-activation subspace modeling provides a principled and effective direction for low-bit LLM quantization; source code is provided for reproducibility.

title: MedSynapse-V: Bridging Visual Perception and Clinical Intuition via Latent Memory Evolution
summary: High-precision medical diagnosis relies not only on static imaging features but also on the implicit diagnostic memory experts instantly invoke during image interpretation. We pinpoint a fundamental cognitive misalignment in medical VLMs caused by discrete tokenization, leading to quantization loss, long-range information dissipation, and missing case-adaptive expertise. To bridge this gap, we propose ours, a framework for latent diagnostic memory evolution that simulates the experiential invocation of clinicians by dynamically synthesizing implicit diagnostic memories within the model's hidden stream. Specifically, it begins with a Meta Query for Prior Memorization mechanism, where learnable probes retrieve structured priors from an anatomical prior encoder to generate condensed implicit memories. To ensure clinical fidelity, we introduce Causal Counterfactual Refinement (CCR), which leverages reinforcement learning and counterfactual rewards derived from region-level feature masking to quantify the causal contribution of each memory, thereby pruning redundancies and aligning latent representations with diagnostic logic. This evolutionary process culminates in Intrinsic Memory Transition (IMT), a privileged-autonomous dual-branch paradigm that internalizes teacher-branch diagnostic patterns into the student-branch via full-vocabulary divergence alignment. Comprehensive empirical evaluations across multiple datasets demonstrate that ours, by transferring external expertise into endogenous parameters, significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly chain-of-thought paradigms, in diagnostic accuracy.

AI.tldr: Proposes a latent diagnostic memory evolution framework for medical VLMs to overcome tokenization-induced information loss, using a three-stage pipeline (Meta Query for Prior Memorization, Causal Counterfactual Refinement, Intrinsic Memory Transition) that transfers external expertise into endogenous parameters and outperforms chain-of-thought baselines in diagnostic accuracy.
AI.motivation: Identify cognitive misalignment in medical VLMs caused by discrete tokenization leading to quantization loss and long-range information dissipation, resulting in missing case-adaptive expertise during image interpretation.
AI.method: A three-stage framework: (1) Meta Query for Prior Memorization retrieves structured priors from an anatomical prior encoder to synthesize condensed implicit memories; (2) Causal Counterfactual Refinement uses reinforcement learning with counterfactual rewards from region-level feature masking to measure each memory's causal contribution and prune redundancy; (3) Intrinsic Memory Transition is a privileged-autonomous dual-branch architecture that aligns student-branch latent representations with teacher-branch diagnostic patterns via full-vocabulary divergence alignment.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on multiple medical datasets show that the proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly chain-of-thought paradigms, in diagnostic accuracy by transferring external expertise into endogenous model parameters.
AI.conclusion: Latent diagnostic memory evolution effectively embeds clinical reasoning into model internals, mitigating tokenization-related losses and improving diagnostic performance without relying on external reasoning modules; the approach emphasizes causal contribution pruning and teacher-student latent alignment to capture diagnostic logic.

title: Unifying Runtime Monitoring Approaches for Safety-Critical Machine Learning: Application to Vision-Based Landing
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables, submitted to ICPR 2026
summary: Runtime monitoring is essential to ensure the safety of ML applications in safety-critical domains. However, current research is fragmented, with independent methods emerging from different communities. In this paper, we propose a unified framework categorising runtime monitoring approaches into three distinct types: Operational Design Domain (ODD) monitoring, which ensures compliance with expected operating conditions; Out-of-Distribution (OOD) monitoring, which rejects inputs that deviate from the training data; and Out-of-Model-Scope (OMS) monitoring, which detects anomalous model behaviour based its internal states or outputs. We demonstrate the benefits of this categorization with a dedicated experiment on an aeronautical safety-critical application: runway detection during landing. This framework facilitates design of monitoring activities, with complementary categories of monitors, and enables evaluation and comparison of different monitors using common, safety-oriented metrics.

AI.tldr: Proposes a unified three-type framework (ODD, OOD, OMS) for runtime ML monitoring and demonstrates its value via a runway-detection experiment in safety-critical aviation.
AI.motivation: Current runtime monitoring research is fragmented across communities; a unified taxonomy and evaluation framework is needed to design, compare, and improve monitors in safety-critical ML applications.
AI.method: Define three monitoring categories: Operational Design Domain (ODD) monitoring, Out-of-Distribution (OOD) monitoring, and Out-of-Model-Scope (OMS) monitoring. Propose a framework for designing complementary monitors and evaluating them using common safety-oriented metrics. Validate the approach with an aeronautical case study on runway detection during landing.
AI.result: Clarifies the benefits of categorization, enabling systematic design of monitors and enabling evaluation and comparison across monitors using shared safety-focused metrics.
AI.conclusion: A unified monitoring framework structures monitor design and evaluation for safety-critical ML systems, facilitating cross-community collaboration and standardized assessment.

title: Event-based Liveness Detection using Temporal Ocular Dynamics: An Exploratory Approach
summary: Face liveness detection has been extensively studied using RGB cameras, achieving strong performance under controlled conditions but often failing to generalize across sensors and attack scenarios. In this work, we explore event cameras as an alternative sensing modality for liveness detection based on temporal ocular dynamics. Event cameras capture sparse, asynchronous changes in brightness with microsecond resolution, enabling precise analysis of fast eye movements such as saccades. Replay attacks cannot faithfully reproduce these dynamics due to temporal resampling and display artifacts, leading to distinctive spatio-temporal patterns in the event domain. We design a data collection protocol to extend RGBE-Gaze with replay-attack recordings, yielding an event-based fake counterpart for liveness detection. We analyze event-driven temporal features from eye regions and evaluate their effectiveness for ocular motion segmentation and liveness classification. Our results show that event-based representations enable reliable discrimination between genuine and replayed sequences, achieving up to 95.37% top-1 accuracy with a spiking convolutional neural network. These preliminary findings highlight the potential of event-based sensing for robust and low-latency liveness detection.

AI.tldr: Event-based eye motion patterns enable robust liveness detection against replay attacks, achieving high accuracy with a spiking CNN.
AI.motivation: RGB-based liveness detection generalizes poorly across sensors and attack scenarios; event cameras offer high temporal resolution to capture fast ocular dynamics and reveal artifacts in replay attacks.
AI.method: Extend RGB-E-Gaze with an event-camera data collection protocol, extracting event-driven temporal features from eye regions; perform ocular motion segmentation; train and evaluate a spiking convolutional neural network on the event representations for liveness classification.
AI.result: The approach discriminates genuine vs replayed sequences with up to 95.37% top-1 accuracy using a spiking CNN, demonstrating strong discriminative power of event-based representations for liveness detection and low-latency potential.
AI.conclusion: Event-based sensing is a promising modality for robust liveness detection, leveraging precise capture of fast eye movements to improve resistance to replay artifacts and potentially enable low-latency solutions.

title: STLGT: A Scalable Trace-Based Linear Graph Transformer for Tail Latency Prediction in Microservices
summary: Accurate end-to-end tail-latency forecasting is critical for proactive SLO management in microservice systems. However, modeling long-range dependency propagation and non-stationary, bursty workloads while maintaining inference efficiency at scale remains challenging. We present STLGT (Scalable Trace-based Linear Graph Transformer), a per-API predictor that encodes traces as span graphs for multi-step p95 tail-latency forecasting. STLGT uses a structure-aware linear graph Transformer to propagate cross-service dependencies with inference time linear in span graph size, and a decoupled temporal module to capture workload dynamics. Across a personalized education microservice application, DeathStarBench, and Alibaba traces, STLGT improves forecasting accuracy over PERT-GNN by 8.5% MAPE on average and achieves up to 12x faster CPU inference at N=32, matching the maximum span graph size after preprocessing the Alibaba traces. Ablation studies further demonstrate the effectiveness of each component, especially under bursty traffic.

AI.tldr: STLGT is a scalable per-API tail-latency predictor that encodes traces as span graphs and uses a structure-aware linear graph Transformer with a decoupled temporal module to forecast multi-step p95 latency; it achieves linear inference time in graph size and outperforms PERT-GNN on DeathStarBench and Alibaba traces, with up to 12x faster CPU inference.
AI.motivation: Accurately forecasting end-to-end tail latency in microservice systems is hard due to long-range dependency propagation, non-stationary bursty workloads, and the need for scalable, real-time inference.
AI.method: Encode traces as span graphs per API; apply a structure-aware linear graph Transformer to propagate cross-service dependencies with linear-time inference; incorporate a decoupled temporal module to capture workload dynamics; evaluate on real microservice traces and compare to PERT-GNN; conduct ablations.
AI.result: STLGT improves forecasting accuracy over PERT-GNN by 8.5% MAPE on average; achieves up to 12x faster CPU inference at N=32; scales to maximum span graph size after Alibaba trace preprocessing; ablations show component effectiveness, especially under bursty traffic.
AI.conclusion: STLGT provides a scalable, accurate approach for end-to-end tail-latency forecasting in microservices, with efficient inference suitable for large-scale deployments and robust performance under bursty workloads; future work could explore broader workloads, online adaptation, and multi-SLO optimization.

title: CheXthought: A global multimodal dataset of clinical chain-of-thought reasoning and visual attention for chest X-ray interpretation
summary: Chest X-ray interpretation is one of the most frequently performed diagnostic tasks in medicine and a primary target for AI development, yet current vision--language models are primarily trained on datasets of paired images and reports, not the cognitive processes and visual attention that underlie clinical reasoning. Here, we present CheXthought, a global, multimodal resource containing 103,592 chain-of-thought reasoning traces and 6,609,082 synchronized visual attention annotations across 50,312 multi-read chest X-rays from 501 radiologists in 71 countries. Our analysis reveals clinical reasoning patterns in how experts deploy distinct visual search strategies, integrate clinical context, and communicate uncertainty. We demonstrate the clinical utility of CheXthought across four dimensions. First, CheXthought reasoning significantly outperforms state--of--the--art vision--language model chain-of-thought in factual accuracy and spatial grounding. Second, visual attention data used as an inference--time hint recovers missed findings and significantly reduces hallucinations. Third, models trained on CheXthought data achieve significantly stronger pathology classification, visual faithfulness, temporal reasoning and uncertainty communication. Fourth, leveraging CheXthought's multi-reader annotations, we predict both human--human and human--AI disagreement directly from an image, enabling transparent communication of case difficulty, uncertainty and model reliability. These findings establish CheXthought as a resource for advancing multimodal clinical reasoning and the development of more transparent, interpretable vision--language models.

AI.tldr: CheXthought is a large global multimodal resource of 103k chain-of-thought traces and 6.6M synchronized visual attention annotations for 50k chest X-rays, enabling analysis of expert clinical reasoning and improving vision–language models.
AI.motivation: Current vision–language models for medical imaging are trained on image-report pairs and lack access to the cognitive processes and visual search patterns underlying radiologists' reasoning, hindering interpretability and robustness.
AI.method: Compilation of 103,592 chain-of-thought traces and 6,609,082 synchronized visual attention annotations from 501 radiologists across 71 countries for 50,312 multi-read chest X-rays. The study analyzes reasoning patterns, then evaluates models on multiple dimensions of clinical reasoning and interpretability.
AI.result: CheXthought reasoning outperforms state-of-the-art V–L model chain-of-thought in factual accuracy and spatial grounding; use of visual-attention hints reduces missed findings and hallucinations; models trained on CheXthought show stronger pathology classification, visual faithfulness, temporal reasoning, and uncertainty communication; multi-reader annotations enable predicting human–human and human–AI disagreement from image, aiding transparency.
AI.conclusion: CheXthought constitutes a valuable resource to advance multimodal clinical reasoning and to develop more transparent, interpretable vision–language models in medical imaging.

title: Layer-wise Lipschitz-Product Control for Deep Kolmogorov--Arnold Network Representations of Compositionally Structured Functions
comment: 15 pages, theoretical note on layer-wise Lipschitz control for deep KANs
summary: We prove that any continuous function f from [0,1]^n to R representable by a finite computation tree with N internal nodes and compositional sparsity s = O(1) admits a deep Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) representation. Each internal node is realised by a primitive KAN block with controlled block depth and Lipschitz product. The layer-wise Lipschitz product satisfies the primary domain-sensitive bound independent of the input dimension n. It simplifies to P(KAN_f) <= max(C*,1)^L_f with L_f <= c_max * N. For the standard operations {+,-,x,sin,cos} with x nodes on [0,1]-bounded inputs we obtain P(KAN) <= 1. Layer widths satisfy n_l <= n + 2 w_max * N. The uniform approximation error is bounded by N * max(C*,1)^d(f) * epsilon_Op (simplifies when C* <=1). For f in C^m we obtain optimal B-spline rates. Range bounds are also derived (B_f <= N+1 for additive trees). This addresses the gap on Lipschitz control in deep KAN stacks noted by Liu et al. (2024). Experiments confirm P(KAN)=1.0 for several compositionally structured functions.

AI.tldr: Deep Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) can represent any continuous function computed by a finite, compositional-sparse computation tree with Lipschitz guarantees that are independent of input dimension; they exhibit strong width bounds and provable approximation/error properties, with experiments showing P(KAN)=1 for structured functions.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in Lipschitz control for deep KAN stacks and establish dimension-free Lipschitz bounds, width/approximation properties, and range control for compositionally structured functions.
AI.method: Construct primitive KAN blocks for each internal node of a finite computation tree with sparsity s=O(1). Establish a layer-wise Lipschitz product bound and accumulate it across depth to bound P(KAN_f) by max(C*,1)^L_f with L_f ≤ c_max N. Prove P(KAN) ≤ 1 for standard ops on bounded inputs. Derive layer width bounds n_l ≤ n + 2 w_max N. Derive uniform approximation error bound: error ≤ N * max(C*,1)^d(f) * epsilon_Op. For C^m functions, derive optimal B-spline rates. Derive range bounds B_f ≤ N+1 for additive trees.
AI.result: P(KAN_f) is bounded by P(KAN_f) ≤ max(C*,1)^L_f with L_f ≤ c_max N. In the standard ops case and x-bounded inputs, P(KAN) ≤ 1. Layer widths satisfy n_l ≤ n + 2 w_max N. Uniform error bound scales as N * max(C*,1)^d(f) * epsilon_Op. For C^m, optimal B-spline rates are obtained. Range bounds B_f ≤ N+1 for additive trees. Empirical tests yield P(KAN)=1.0 for several compositions.
AI.conclusion: The work provides dimension-robust Lipschitz control and quantitative bounds for deep KAN stacks, addressing previous gaps and validating the theory with experiments on compositionally structured functions.

title: The Unseen Adversaries: Robust and Generalized Defense Against Adversarial Patches
summary: The vulnerabilities of deep neural networks against singularities have raised serious concerns regarding their deployment in the physical world. One of the most prominent and impactful physical-world adversarial perturbations is the attachment of patches to clean images, known as an adversarial patch attack. Similarly, natural noises such as Gaussian and Salt\&Pepper are highly prevalent in the real world. The current research need arises from the above vulnerabilities and the lack of efforts to tackle these two singularities independently and, especially, in combination. In this research, we have, for the first time, combined these two prominent singularities and proposed a novel dataset. Using this dataset, we have conducted a benchmark study of singularity data-point detection using features from several convolutional neural networks. For classification, rather than the popular neural network-based parameter tuning, we have used traditional yet effective machine learning classifiers. The extensive experiments across various in- and out-of-distribution (OOD) singularities reveal several interesting findings about the effectiveness of classifiers and show that it is hard to defend against adversaries when they are treated independently, and inefficient classifiers are selected.

AI.tldr: Dataset combining adversarial patch attacks with natural noise; benchmarks singularity data-point detection using CNN features and traditional ML classifiers; finds defense hard when singularities treated independently and classifier choice matters.
AI.motivation: Address real-world vulnerabilities of deep neural networks to singularities by jointly studying adversarial patches and natural noises; fills gap where these two perturbations were treated separately and creates a dataset for benchmarking.
AI.method: Create a novel dataset that blends patch-based adversarial perturbations with Gaussian and Salt&Pepper noise. Benchmark singularity data-point detection using features from multiple CNNs. Use traditional machine learning classifiers for classification instead of neural network-based tuning. Conduct in- and out-of-distribution (OOD) experiments across various singularities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments reveal that classifier effectiveness varies across settings; defending against adversaries is hard when singularities are treated independently; inefficient (poor) classifiers degrade performance.
AI.conclusion: Joint singularities pose substantial defense challenges; relying on independent treatment yields weak defenses; classifier selection is crucial for detection/robustness; results suggest a need for improved integrative defense strategies and benchmarks.

title: Near-Optimal Cryptographic Hardness of Learning With Homogeneous Halfspaces Under Gaussian Marginals
summary: We study three problems that involve identifying homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian distributions: agnostic learning, one-sided reliable learning, and fairness auditing. In each of these problems, we are given labeled examples $(\mathbf{x}, \mathrm{y})$ drawn from an unknown distribution on $\mathbb{R}^d\times\{-1, +1\}$, whose marginal distribution on $\mathbf{x}$ is standard Gaussian and on $\mathrm{y}$ is arbitrary. The goal of each problem is to output a homogeneous halfspace that approaches the best-fitting homogeneous halfspace in terms of its corresponding loss measure. We prove near-optimal computational hardness results for these problems under the widely believed hardness assumption of the Learning With Errors (LWE) problem. Prior hardness results for these problems were mostly established for general halfspaces; our findings extend some of these hardness results to homogeneous halfspaces. Remarkably, our lower bound strictly generalizes over prior works and narrows the gap between the upper and lower bounds for agnostically learning homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian marginals.

AI.tldr: Proves near-optimal hardness for agnostic learning, one-sided reliable learning, and fairness auditing of homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian data, under LWE; generalizes prior hardness from general to homogeneous halfspaces and tightens bounds for agnostic learning with Gaussian marginals.
AI.motivation: Understand computational limits for three related learning tasks (agnostic learning, one-sided reliable learning, fairness auditing) when the target is a homogeneous halfspace with Gaussian marginals. Extend hardness results from general halfspaces to homogeneous ones and tighten the gap between upper and lower bounds.
AI.method: Provide LWE-based reductions to the three problems under standard Gaussian marginal for x and arbitrary label distribution. Show that achieving near-optimal learning performance would imply breaking LWE, thus establishing computational hardness. Generalize prior hardness results and unify the hardness framework across the three tasks.
AI.result: Near-optimal computational hardness results are established for agnostic learning, one-sided reliable learning, and fairness auditing of homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian marginals. The lower bounds extend to homogeneous halfspaces and strictly generalize prior work, narrowing the gap in bounds for agnostic learning under Gaussian marginals.
AI.conclusion: The work clarifies computational limits for learning homogeneous halfspaces with Gaussian inputs across three related tasks, showing that, under LWE, no efficient algorithm can closely match the best-fitting homogeneous halfspace. It also broadens the scope of prior hardness results and tightens the bound gap for agnostic learning.

title: Point Cloud Registration via Probabilistic Self-Update Local Correspondence and Line Vector Sets
summary: Point cloud registration (PCR) is a fundamental task for integrating 3D observations in remote sensing applications. This paper proposes a fast and effective PCR algorithm utilizing probabilistic self-updating local correspondence and line vector sets. Our dual RANSAC interaction model comprises a global RANSAC evaluating the global correspondence set and a local RANSAC operating on dynamically updated local sets. Initially, these local sets are constructed using angle histogram statistics and line vector length preservation techniques. To improve accuracy, a probabilistic self-updating strategy refines the local sets after each interaction round. To reduce runtime, we introduce a global early termination condition that optimally balances accuracy and efficiency. Finally, a weighted singular value decomposition estimates the registration solution. Evaluations on public datasets demonstrate our algorithm achieves superior time efficiency and at least a 10% root mean square error improvement over state-of-the-art methods. The C++ source code is publicly available at https://github.com/ivpml84079/Probabilistic-Self-Update-Line-Vector-Set-Based-Point-Cloud-Registration.

AI.tldr: A fast, robust point cloud registration method using dual RANSAC with probabilistic self-updating local line-vector sets, guided by angle histograms and line-length preservation, followed by weighted SVD for the final transformation, achieving improved runtime and RMSE over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Efficient and accurate PCR is critical for integrating 3D data in remote sensing. Existing robust registration methods often trade speed for accuracy or struggle with maintaining reliable local correspondences under noise and partial overlap. The work introduces probabilistic self-updating local correspondences and line-vector sets to enhance both robustness and efficiency.
AI.method: Utilizes a dual RANSAC framework: a global RANSAC evaluating a global correspondence set, and a local RANSAC operating on dynamically updated local sets. Local sets are initialized using angle histogram statistics and line-vector length preservation. A probabilistic self-updating strategy refines the local sets after each interaction round. A global early termination condition balances accuracy and efficiency. The final registration is computed via a weighted singular value decomposition.
AI.result: Evaluations on public datasets show the method achieves superior time efficiency and at least 10% RMSE improvement over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed probabilistic self-update line-vector-set-based PCR framework provides faster and more accurate registration, with a practical early-termination mechanism and publicly available code for reproducibility.

title: Hierarchical adaptive control for real-time dynamic inference at the edge
comment: Accepted as paper at 5th Real-time And intelliGent Edge computing (RAGE 2026) workshop
summary: Industrial systems increasingly depend on Machine Learning (ML), and operate on heterogeneous nodes that must satisfy tight latency, energy, and memory constraints. Dynamic ML models, which reconfigure their computational footprint at runtime, promise high energy efficiency and lower average latency for modest accuracy tradeoffs; however, their deployment is complex due to the additional hyperparameters they rely on. These hyperparameters, controlling the accuracy versus average latency tradeoff, are often tuned on a calibration dataset that must match the test time distribution, an assumption that rarely holds in real-world scenarios, leading to suboptimal operational conditions, possibly below static models. We propose a two-tier adaptive architecture that co-optimizes model and system decisions. At the global level, a scheduler configures and deploys, for each edge node, a cascade of classifiers composed of lightweight specialized models and a generalist fallback, satisfying latency and memory constraints. At the node level, a local controller tracks data drifts and hardware resources, enabling or disabling specialized predictors (SP) to preserve high energy efficiency and avoid latency-constraint violations under varying conditions. This design allows longer operating times without forcing a global redeployment step, and enables efficient execution in case of an unreachable remote global controller. We evaluate the approach on two datasets under controlled distribution mismatch scenarios, showing average per-inference reductions of latency up to 2.45x and energy up to 2.86x, with less than 4% accuracy drop compared to static baselines. Our contributions are:(1) a budgeted SP-cascade formulation that preserves worst-case latency constraints;(2) a hierarchical controller that maintains efficiency under data and resource changes; and (3) an experimental evaluation on embedded hardware.

AI.tldr: Two-tier adaptive architecture jointly optimizes model and system decisions for dynamic ML on edge, using a global SP-cascade scheduler and a local drift/resource-aware controller; achieves substantial latency and energy improvements under data distribution shifts with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Dynamic ML models offer energy efficiency and lower latency but rely on calibration-tuned hyperparameters that fail under distribution shifts; need a co-optimized architecture that adapts both model complexity and resource use without frequent redeployments.
AI.method: Global level: scheduler deploys per-edge-node cascades of lightweight specialized models plus a generalist fallback to meet latency/memory budgets. Node level: local controller monitors data drift and hardware resources, enabling/disabling specialized predictors to preserve efficiency; budgeted SP-cascade formulation; hierarchical control; operation without constant remote controller.
AI.result: evaluated on two datasets with distribution mismatch; latency reductions up to 2.45x; energy reductions up to 2.86x; accuracy loss under 4% relative to static baselines; embedded hardware experiments.
AI.conclusion: A two-tier adaptive framework with a budgeted SP-cascade and hierarchical controller effectively maintains efficiency under changing data and resources, delivering substantial latency/energy gains with minimal accuracy loss and robustness when remote control is unavailable.

title: Motion-Driven Multi-Object Tracking of Model Organisms in Space Science Experiments
comment: 2026 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW)
summary: Automated animal behavior analysis relies on long-term, interpretable individual trajectories; however, multi-animal tracking in space science experimental videos remains highly challenging due to weak appearance cues, low-quality imaging, complex maneuvering behaviors, and frequent interactions. To address this problem, we first construct the SpaceAnimal-MOT dataset to characterize the motion complexity and long-term identity preservation challenges in biological videos acquired under microgravity conditions. We then propose ART-Track (Adaptive Robust Tracking), a motion-driven tracking framework tailored to this setting. Specifically, multi-model motion estimation is introduced to handle abrupt maneuvers and nonlinear motion, motion-state-driven association is designed to reduce identity switches under dense interactions and temporary mismatch, and uncertainty-adaptive fusion is used to dynamically balance spatial and motion cues when prediction reliability varies. Experimental results show that ART-Track significantly reduces identity switches on zebrafish and fruitfly sequences, while maintaining more stable association under occlusion, deformation, and high-density interactions, thereby providing a more reliable tracking foundation for downstream quantitative behavior analysis. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/yyy7777777/ART_TRACK/tree/main.

AI.tldr: Introduces SpaceAnimal-MOT dataset for microgravity animal tracking and ART-Track, a motion-driven tracking framework with multi-model motion estimation, motion-state-driven association, and uncertainty-adaptive fusion to handle abrupt maneuvers, dense interactions, and occlusions, achieving reduced identity switches in zebrafish and fruit flies; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of long-term, interpretable multi-animal tracking in space science videos with weak appearance cues, low quality imagery, and frequent interactions under microgravity.
AI.method: (1) SpaceAnimal-MOT dataset creation to characterize motion complexity and identity preservation; (2) ART-Track: multi-model motion estimation for abrupt/nonlinear motion; motion-state-driven association to reduce identity switches during dense interactions; and uncertainty-adaptive fusion balancing spatial and motion cues based on prediction reliability.
AI.result: ART-Track significantly reduces identity switches on zebrafish and fruitfly sequences and maintains stable associations under occlusion, deformation, and high-density interactions compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: ART-Track provides a more reliable tracking foundation for downstream quantitative behavior analysis in challenging microgravity videos, and the public code enables reuse and evaluation.

title: Understanding DNNs in Feature Interaction Models: A Dimensional Collapse Perspective
summary: DNNs have gained widespread adoption in feature interaction recommendation models. However, there has been a longstanding debate on their roles. On one hand, some works claim that DNNs possess the ability to implicitly capture high-order feature interactions. Conversely, recent studies have highlighted the limitations of DNNs in effectively learning dot products, specifically second-order interactions, let alone higher-order interactions. In this paper, we present a novel perspective to understand the effectiveness of DNNs: their impact on the dimensional robustness of the representations. In particular, we conduct extensive experiments involving both parallel DNNs and stacked DNNs. Our evaluation encompasses an overall study of complete DNN on two feature interaction models, alongside a fine-grained ablation analysis of components within DNNs. Experimental results demonstrate that both parallel and stacked DNNs can effectively mitigate the dimensional collapse of embeddings. Furthermore, a gradient-based theoretical analysis, supported by empirical evidence, uncovers the underlying mechanisms of dimensional collapse.

AI.tldr: DNNs help prevent dimensional collapse in embedding representations for feature interaction models, across parallel and stacked architectures, with gradient-based analysis explaining the mechanism.
AI.motivation: Resolve mixed evidence on DNNs' ability to model high-order interactions and understand their effect on the dimensional robustness of embeddings in feature interaction recommendations.
AI.method: Empirical study with parallel and stacked DNNs applied to two feature-interaction models, comprehensive ablations, and gradient-based theoretical analysis, supported by empirical results.
AI.result: Both parallel and stacked DNNs mitigate dimensional collapse of embeddings; DNNs contribute to dimensional robustness; analysis reveals mechanisms behind dimensional collapse.
AI.conclusion: DNNs play a beneficial role in stabilizing embedding dimensionality in feature-interaction models, offering a plausible explanation for their effectiveness beyond explicit high-order interaction modeling.

title: Federated Medical Image Classification under Class and Domain Imbalance exploiting Synthetic Sample Generation
comment: Accepted at ICPR 2026, 13 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables
summary: Exploiting deep learning in medical imaging faces critical challenges, including strict privacy constraints, heterogeneous imaging devices with varying acquisition properties, and class imbalance due to the uneven prevalence of pathologies. In this work, we propose FedSSG, a novel Federated Learning framework that addresses domain shifts caused by diverse imaging devices while mitigating the under-representation of rare pathologies. The key contribution is a strategy for generating synthetic samples and distributing them across clients to improve coverage of both underrepresented pathologies and imaging devices. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances model performance and generalization across heterogeneous institutions, with minimal computational overhead at the client side.

AI.tldr: FedSSG: a Federated Learning framework for medical imaging that uses synthetic sample generation and distribution across clients to address domain shifts from diverse imaging devices and underrepresented pathologies, improving performance and generalization with low client-side overhead.
AI.motivation: Address privacy constraints in FL, cope with heterogeneous imaging devices causing domain shifts, and mitigate class imbalance due to rare pathologies by ensuring coverage of underrepresented categories through synthetic data distribution.
AI.method: Propose FedSSG, a strategy to generate synthetic samples and distribute them across clients to enhance coverage of underrepresented pathologies and imaging devices in federated learning, aiming to reduce domain shift and imbalance without imposing heavy computation on clients.
AI.result: Experimental results show significant improvements in model performance and generalization across heterogeneous institutions, with minimal computational overhead at the client side.
AI.conclusion: FedSSG effectively mitigates device-induced domain shifts and data under-representation in medical imaging FL, yielding better cross-institution generalization with efficient client-side requirements.

title: Do Larger Models Really Win in Drug Discovery? A Benchmark Assessment of Model Scaling in AI-Driven Molecular Property and Activity Prediction
summary: The rapid growth of molecular foundation models and general-purpose large language models has encouraged a scale-centric view of artificial intelligence in drug discovery, in which larger pretrained models are expected to supersede compact cheminformatics models and task-specific graph neural networks (GNNs). We test this assumption on 22 molecular property and activity endpoints, including public ADMET and Tox21 benchmarks and two internal anti-infective activity datasets. Across 167,056 held-out task--molecule evaluations under structure-similarity-separated five-fold cross-validation (37,756 ADMET, 77,946 Tox21, 49,266 anti-TB and 2,088 antimalaria), classical machine-learning (ML) models such as RF(ECFP4) and ExtraTrees(RDKit descriptors) win ten primary-metric tasks, GNNs such as GIN and Ligandformer win nine, and pretrained molecular sequence models such as MoLFormer and ChemBERTa2 win three. Rule-based SAR reasoning baselines, represented by GPT5.5-SAR and Opus4.7-SAR, do not win under the prespecified primary metrics, although train-fold-derived SAR knowledge provides measurable but uneven gains for SAR reasoning and interpretation. These results indicate that compact, specialized models remain highly effective for molecular property and activity prediction. The performance differences among classical ML, GNN and pretrained sequence models are often modest and endpoint-dependent, whereas larger or more general models do not provide a universal predictive advantage. Large models may still add value for zero-shot reasoning, SAR interpretation and hypothesis generation, but the results suggest that predictive performance depends on the alignment among molecular representation, inductive bias, data regime, endpoint biology and validation protocol.

AI.tldr: Large molecular models do not universally outperform compact, task-specific models for molecular property prediction; performance is endpoint-dependent and depends on representation-inductive bias-data alignment, with compact models often winning or matching large models on many tasks.
AI.motivation: Test the prevailing assumption that larger pretrained models (foundation models/LLMs) will surpass compact cheminformatics models for molecular property and activity prediction across diverse endpoints.
AI.method: Evaluation on 22 molecular endpoints (public ADMET, Tox21, and two internal anti-infective datasets) using 167,056 held-out task–molecule evaluations under structure-similarity-separated five-fold cross-validation. Compared models include classical ML (RF with ECFP4, ExtraTrees with RDKit descriptors), graph neural networks (GIN, Ligandformer), pretrained molecular sequence models (MoLFormer, ChemBERTa2), and rule-based SAR baselines (GPT5.5-SAR, Opus4.7-SAR).
AI.result: Classical ML and compact models won more primary-metric tasks (10) than GNNs (9) or pretrained sequence models (3). SAR baselines did not win primary metrics, though SAR knowledge from training folds offered some gains for reasoning/interpretation. Overall, performance differences among model families are modest and endpoint-dependent. Larger models may add value in zero-shot reasoning, SAR interpretation, and hypothesis generation, but predictive performance strongly depends on alignment among molecular representation, inductive bias, data regime, endpoint biology, and validation protocol.
AI.conclusion: Compact, specialized models remain highly effective for molecular property and activity prediction. There is no universal predictive advantage for larger, more general models. Model choice should consider representation-inductive bias alignment with data and tasks; larger models may offer niche benefits (zero-shot reasoning, SAR interpretation) rather than broad performance gains.

title: SpatialFusion: Endowing Unified Image Generation with Intrinsic 3D Geometric Awareness
summary: Recent unified image generation models have achieved remarkable success by employing MLLMs for semantic understanding and diffusion backbones for image generation. However, these models remain fundamentally limited in spatially-aware tasks due to a lack of intrinsic spatial understanding and the absence of explicit geometric guidance during generation. In this paper, we propose SpatialFusion, a novel framework that internalizes 3D geometric awareness into unified image generation models. Specifically, we first employ a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture to augment the MLLM with a parallel spatial transformer to enhance 3D geometric modeling capability. By sharing self-attention with the MLLM, the spatial transformer learns to derive metric-depth maps of target images from rich semantic contexts. These explicit geometric scaffolds are then injected into the diffusion backbone through a specialized depth adapter, providing precise spatial constraints for spatially-coherent image generation. Through a progressive two-stage training strategy, SpatialFusion significantly enhances performance on spatially-aware benchmarks, notably outperforming leading models such as GPT-4o. Additionally, it achieves generalized performance gains across both text-to-image generation and image editing scenarios, all while maintaining negligible inference overhead.

AI.tldr: SpatialFusion adds explicit 3D geometry to unified image generation by (1) a Mixture-of-Transformers with a parallel spatial transformer that shares self-attention with the MLLM to infer metric-depth maps, and (2) a depth adapter that injects these cues into the diffusion backbone. Trained in two stages, it improves spatially-aware generation and editing, outperforming GPT-4o with negligible inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Current unified image generation models lack intrinsic spatial understanding and explicit geometric guidance, limiting spatial coherence and 3D awareness. SpatialFusion aims to internalize 3D geometry to enable spatially-aware generation and editing.
AI.method: Introduce a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture combining a parallel spatial transformer that shares self-attention with the MLLM to learn metric-depth maps from semantic context. Use a depth adapter to inject explicit geometric scaffolds into the diffusion backbone. Employ a progressive two-stage training strategy.
AI.result: Significant gains on spatially-aware benchmarks, outperforming leading models such as GPT-4o. Generalizes across text-to-image generation and image editing with negligible inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: SpatialFusion successfully internalizes 3D geometric awareness into unified image generation models, enabling spatially-coherent generation and editing with efficient training and inference.

title: Progressive Semantic Communication for Efficient Edge-Cloud Vision-Language Models
comment: Under review. Extended version with additional figures and appendices
summary: Deploying Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on edge devices remains challenging due to their substantial computational and memory demands, which exceed the capabilities of resource-constrained embedded platforms. Conversely, fully offloading inference to the cloud is often impractical in bandwidth-limited environments, where transmitting raw visual data introduces substantial latency overhead. While recent edge-cloud collaborative architectures attempt to partition VLM workloads across devices, they typically rely on transmitting fixed-size representations, lacking adaptability to dynamic network conditions and failing to fully exploit semantic redundancy. In this paper, we propose a progressive semantic communication framework for edge-cloud VLM inference, using a Meta AutoEncoder that compresses visual tokens into adaptive, progressively refinable representations, enabling plug-and-play deployment with off-the-shelf VLMs without additional fine-tuning. This design allows flexible transmission at different information levels, providing a controllable trade-off between communication cost and semantic fidelity. We implement a full end-to-end edge-cloud system comprising an embedded NXP i.MX95 platform and a GPU server, communicating over bandwidth-constrained networks. Experimental results show that, at 1 Mbps uplink, the proposed progressive scheme significantly reduces network latency compared to full-edge and full-cloud solutions, while maintaining high semantic consistency even under high compression. The implementation code will be released upon publication at https://github.com/open-ep/ProSemComVLM.

AI.tldr: A progressive semantic communication framework for edge-cloud VLM inference using a Meta AutoEncoder to compress visual tokens into adaptive, progressively refinable representations, enabling plug-and-play deployment with off-the-shelf VLMs without fine-tuning, achieving flexible trade-offs between bandwidth and semantic fidelity.
AI.motivation: Edge devices face limited compute/memory; sending raw visual data or fixed-size representations over bandwidth-constrained links incurs latency and wasteful energy. Existing edge-cloud schemes use fixed-size, non-adaptive representations and fail to exploit semantic redundancies.
AI.method: Introduce a progressive semantic communication framework with a Meta AutoEncoder that yields adaptive, progressively refinable visual token representations. Enables plug-and-play use with existing VLMs without additional fine-tuning. Supports transmission at different information levels to trade off cost and fidelity. Demonstrated via an end-to-end system on an embedded NXP i.MX95 platform and a GPU server over bandwidth-limited networks.
AI.result: At 1 Mbps uplink, the progressive scheme reduces network latency compared with full-edge and full-cloud baselines while preserving high semantic consistency under high compression.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables flexible, efficient edge-cloud VLM inference without fine-tuning, accommodating varying network conditions and leveraging semantic redundancy to reduce communication costs while maintaining fidelity; code will be released upon publication.

title: Which Face and Whose Identity? Solving the Dual Challenge of Deepfake Proactive Forensics in Multi-Face Scenarios
summary: Unlike single-face forgeries, deepfakes in complex multi-person interaction scenarios (such as group photos and multi-person meetings) more closely reflect real-world threats. Although existing proactive forensics solutions demonstrate good performance, they heavily rely on a "single-face" setting, making it difficult to effectively address the problems of deepfake localization and source tracing in complex multi-person environments. To address this challenge, we propose the Deep Attributable Watermarking Framework (DAWF). This framework adopts a novel multi-face encoder-decoder architecture that bypasses the cumbersome offline pre-processing steps of traditional forensics, facilitating efficient in-network parallel watermark embedding and cross-face collaborative processing. Crucially, we propose a selective regional supervision loss. This innovative mechanism guides the decoder to focus exclusively on the facial regions tampered with by deepfakes. Leveraging this mechanism alongside the embedded identity payloads, DAWF realizes the "which + who" goal, answering the dual questions of which facial region was forged and who was forged. Extensive experiments on challenging multi-face datasets show that DAWF achieves excellent deepfake localization and traceability in complex multi-person scenes.

AI.tldr: DAWF introduces a multi-face encoder-decoder watermarking framework with selective regional supervision to enable localization and traceability of deepfakes in multi-person scenes, achieving 'which+who' claims via embedded identity payloads and cross-face collaboration.
