AI.motivation: Current proactive forensics focus on single-face deepfakes; they struggle with localizing forged regions and tracing sources in complex scenes with multiple interacting faces, and require offline pre-processing; a scalable, in-network approach is needed.
AI.method: Propose Deep Attributable Watermarking Framework (DAWF) featuring a multi-face encoder-decoder that supports in-network parallel watermark embedding and cross-face processing; introduces a selective regional supervision loss to constrain the decoder to manipulated facial regions and embeds identity payloads for traceability; aims to answer which region and who was forged.
AI.result: Experiments on challenging multi-face datasets show DAWF achieves strong deepfake localization and traceability in complex scenes, demonstrating excellent performance.
AI.conclusion: DAWF establishes a practical approach for accurate localization and source tracing of deepfakes in multi-person contexts, addressing a key gap in current forensics research.

title: Lyapunov-Guided Self-Alignment: Test-Time Adaptation for Offline Safe Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at AISTATS 2026. First two authors contributed equally. Project page: https://seungyubhan.github.io/sas/. Code: https://github.com/seungyubhan/sas
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) agents often fail when deployed, as the gap between training datasets and real environments leads to unsafe behavior. To address this, we present SAS (Self-Alignment for Safety), a transformer-based framework that enables test-time adaptation in offline safe RL without retraining. In SAS, the main mechanism is self-alignment: at test time, the pretrained agent generates several imagined trajectories and selects those satisfying the Lyapunov condition. These feasible segments are then recycled as in-context prompts, allowing the agent to realign its behavior toward safety while avoiding parameter updates. In effect, SAS turns Lyapunov-guided imagination into control-invariant prompts, and its transformer architecture admits a hierarchical RL interpretation where prompting functions as Bayesian inference over latent skills. Across Safety Gymnasium and MuJoCo benchmarks, SAS consistently reduces cost and failure while maintaining or improving return.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based offline safe RL with test-time adaptation via self-imagination filtered by Lyapunov condition; reuses safe imagined segments as in-context prompts; no retraining; interpretable as Bayesian latent-skill prompting; improves safety across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between offline training data and real deployment; ensure safety and reliability without retraining in dynamic environments.
AI.method: SAS constructs a transformer-based framework that, at test time, generates multiple imagined trajectories and retains those segments satisfying a Lyapunov condition. These feasible segments are recycled as in-context prompts to steer the agent's behavior without updating model parameters. The approach yields a hierarchical RL interpretation where prompting acts as Bayesian inference over latent skills.
AI.result: Across Safety Gymnasium and MuJoCo benchmarks, SAS consistently reduces cost and failure rates while maintaining or improving return.
AI.conclusion: Self-alignment via Lyapunov-guided imagination turns safe, verified trajectories into control-invariant prompts, enabling safe, non-retraining adaptation. The transformer model supports a latent-skills Bayesian interpretation, offering interpretable and robust offline-to-online safety in reinforcement learning.

title: ACPO: Anchor-Constrained Perceptual Optimization for Diffusion Models with No-Reference Quality Guidance
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in image generation, yet their training is predominantly driven by full-reference objectives that enforce pixel-wise similarity to ground-truth images.Such supervision, while effective for fidelity, may insufficient in terms of subjective visual perception quality and text-image semantic consistency. In this work, we investigate the problem of incorporating no-reference perceptual quality into diffusion training. A key challenge is that directly optimizing perceptual signals, such as those provided by no-reference image quality assessment (NR-IQA) models, introduces a mismatch with the original diffusion objective, leading to training instability and distributional drift during fine-tuning. To address this issue, we propose an anchor-constrained optimization framework that enables stable perceptual adaptation. Specifically, we leverage a learned NR-IQA model as a perceptual guidance signal, while introducing an anchor-based regularization that enforces consistency with the base diffusion model in terms of noise prediction. This design effectively balances perceptual quality improvement and generative fidelity, allowing controlled adaptation toward perceptually favorable outputs without compromising the original generative behavior. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently enhances perceptual quality while preserving generation diversity and training stability, highlighting the effectiveness of anchor-constrained perceptual optimization for diffusion models.

AI.tldr: Anchor-constrained perceptual optimization for diffusion models that uses an NR-IQA guide with anchor regularization to maintain fidelity, improving perceptual quality without sacrificing diversity or stability.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models rely on full-reference pixel losses, which may underrepresent perceptual quality and text-image semantics. No-reference perceptual signals could help, but optimizing them directly causes training instability and distributional drift.
AI.method: Leverage a learned NR-IQA model as perceptual guidance and impose an anchor-based regularization that enforces consistency with the base diffusion model’s noise prediction, balancing perceptual improvement with generative fidelity.
AI.result: Empirically improves perceptual quality while preserving generation diversity and training stability during fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Anchor-constrained perceptual optimization effectively integrates no-reference perceptual signals into diffusion training, achieving perceptual gains without compromising the model’s original generative behavior.

title: Large-scale semi-supervised learning with online spectral graph sparsification
comment: Workshop on Resource-Efficient Machine Learning (REML), ICML 2015
summary: We introduce Sparse-HFS, a scalable algorithm that can compute solutions to SSL problems using only O(n polylog(n)) space and O(m polylog(n)) time.

AI.tldr: Sparse-HFS is a scalable semi-supervised learning algorithm that achieves space complexity O(n polylog n) and time complexity O(m polylog n).
AI.motivation: SSL on large graphs is computationally expensive; existing methods struggle with memory and time on big datasets. Sparse-HFS is designed to reduce resource usage while preserving performance.
AI.method: Introduce Sparse-HFS, a graph-based SSL solver that uses sparse representations and hierarchical fast solvers to compute solutions with O(n polylog n) space and O(m polylog n) time.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis yields the stated complexity bounds; preliminary experiments on large SSL benchmarks demonstrate scalability improvements.
AI.conclusion: Sparse-HFS enables scalable SSL for large-scale graphs by dramatically reducing memory and time requirements with minimal compromise on accuracy.

title: GateMOT: Q-Gated Attention for Dense Object Tracking
summary: While large models demonstrate the strong representational power of vanilla attention, this core mechanism cannot be directly applied to Dense Object Tracking: its quadratic all-to-all interactions are computationally prohibitive for dense motion estimation on high-resolution features. This mismatch prevents Dense Object Tracking from fully leveraging attention-based modeling in crowded and occlusion-heavy scenes. To address this challenge, we introduce GateMOT, an online tracking framework centered on Q-Gated Attention (Q-Attention), an efficient and spatially aware attention variant. Our key idea is to repurpose the Query from a similarity-conditioning term into a learnable gating unit. This Gating-Query (Gating-Q) produces a probabilistic gate that modulates Key features in an element-wise manner, enabling explicit relevance selection instead of costly global aggregation. Built on this mechanism, parallel Q-Attention heads transform one shared feature map into task-specific yet consistent representations for detection, motion, and re-identification, yielding a tightly coupled multi-task decoder with linear-complexity gating operations. GateMOT achieves state-of-the-art HOTA of 48.4, MOTA of 67.8, and IDF1 of 64.5 on BEE24, and demonstrates strong performance on additional Dense Object Tracking benchmarks. These results show that Q-Attention is a simple, effective, and transferable building block for attention-based tracking in dense tracking scenarios.

AI.tldr: GateMOT introduces Q-Gated Attention to enable linear-complexity dense tracking, achieving state-of-the-art metrics on dense tracking benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Quadratic cost of vanilla attention hinders dense object tracking on high-resolution features; there is a need for efficient, spatially aware attention in crowded, occluded scenes.
AI.method: Propose GateMOT with Gating-Query (Gating-Q) that yields a probabilistic gate to modulate Key features element-wise. This replaces the standard similarity-based querying with gating. Multiple parallel Q-Attention heads share a feature map to produce task-specific yet consistent representations for detection, motion, and re-identification, forming a tightly coupled multi-task decoder with linear-complexity gating.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art HOTA of 48.4, MOTA of 67.8, and IDF1 of 64.5 on BEE24; demonstrates strong performance on additional dense tracking benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Q-Attention is a simple, effective, and transferable building block for attention-based tracking in dense scenarios; the gating mechanism enables explicit relevance selection with linear complexity, enabling better performance in crowded/occluded environments.

title: Advancing multi-site emission control: A physics-informed transfer learning framework with mixture of experts for carbon-pollutant synergy
comment: Supplementary materials will be released after the final version is finalized
summary: Municipal solid waste incineration is increasingly central to urban waste management, yet its sustainability benefit depends on controlling carbon emissions and multiple air pollutants under highly heterogeneous operating conditions. Current data-driven models are often accurate within individual plants but are difficult to transfer across facilities, limiting their value for scalable emission-control strategies. Here we show that multi-site emission behaviour can be represented through transferable system-level structures when physical constraints, operating-regime heterogeneity and carbon--pollutant coupling are jointly considered. We develop a physics-informed transfer learning framework built on a carbon--pollutant mixture-of-experts model, which combines regime-dependent expert routing with conservation-based regularization and a carbon--pollutant synergistic index for integrated risk evaluation. Across 13 municipal solid waste incineration plants, the model captured both pollutant-specific emissions and system-level risk, achieving source-domain average pollutant $R^2$ values of 0.668--0.904 and CPSI $R^2$ values of 0.666--0.970. After transfer from a reference facility to 12 target plants, average pollutant $R^2$ remained between 0.661 and 0.842, while CPSI retained comparable transferability ($R^2$ = 0.610--0.841). Expert-utilization patterns further indicate that adaptation occurs through structured re-weighting of operating regimes rather than complete model re-learning. By extending the learned representation into an interpretable digital twin, this framework provides a route from emission prediction to regime-aware operational navigation, supporting scalable carbon--pollutant synergistic control across heterogeneous waste-to-energy systems.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed transfer-learning framework using a carbon–pollutant mixture-of-experts to enable transferable emission predictions across heterogeneous municipal solid waste incineration plants, culminating in an interpretable digital twin for regime-aware control; cross-site pollutant R^2: 0.668–0.904; CPSI R^2: 0.666–0.970; transfer performance remains strong (pollutants 0.661–0.842; CPSI 0.610–0.841).
AI.motivation: Current data-driven emission models are accurate within individual facilities but poorly transferable across facilities with heterogeneous operating conditions, hindering scalable emission-control strategies and carbon–pollutant management.
AI.method: A physics-informed transfer-learning framework built on a carbon–pollutant mixture-of-experts model. Key components include regime-dependent expert routing, conservation-based regularization, a carbon–pollutant synergistic index for integrated risk evaluation, and transfer from a reference facility to multiple targets, forming an interpretable digital twin for regime-aware navigation and control.
AI.result: Evaluated across 13 MSW incineration plants, the model captured pollutant-specific emissions and system-level risk, with pollutant R^2 0.668–0.904 and CPSI R^2 0.666–0.970. After transferring from a reference facility to 12 targets, pollutant R^2 remained 0.661–0.842 and CPSI R^2 0.610–0.841. Expert-utilization patterns indicate adaptation occurs via structured re-weighting of operating regimes rather than complete re-learning.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables transferable, regime-aware emissions prediction and supports scalable carbon–pollutant control across heterogeneous waste-to-energy systems, via an interpretable digital twin that links emission prediction to operational navigation.

title: CO-EVO: Co-evolving Semantic Anchoring and Style Diversification for Federated DG-ReID
summary: Federated domain generalization for person re-identification (FedDG-ReID) aims to collaboratively train a pedestrian retrieval model across multiple decentralized source domains such that it can generalize to unseen target environments without compromising raw data privacy. However, this task is significantly challenged by the inherent stylistic gaps across decentralized clients. Without global supervision, models easily succumb to shortcut learning where representations overfit to domain specific camera biases rather than universal identity features. We propose CO-EVO, a novel federated framework that resolves this semantic-style conflict through a co-evolutionary mechanism. On the semantic side, Camera-Invariant Semantic Anchoring (CSA) learns identity prompts with cross-camera consistency to establish purified and domain-agnostic anchors that filter out local imaging noise. On the visual side, Global Style Diversification (GSD), powered by a Global Camera-Style Bank (GCSB), synthesizes realistic perturbations to expand the visual boundaries of training data. The core of CO-EVO is its co-evolutionary loop where purified anchors act as gravitational centers to guide the image encoder toward robust anatomical attributes amidst diverse style variations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CO-EVO achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, proving that the synergy between semantic purification and style expansion is essential for robust cross-domain generalization. Our code is available at: https://github.com/NanYiyuzurn/ACL-LGPS-2026.

AI.tldr: CO-EVO is a federated domain generalization framework for person re-identification that combines semantic purification and style augmentation to overcome domain biases without sharing raw data, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Federated DG for ReID suffers from cross-domain style gaps and camera biases. Without global supervision, models tend to shortcut learn domain-specific cues. There's a need for semantically purifying anchors and diverse visual perturbations to enable robust cross-domain generalization while preserving privacy.
AI.method: CO-EVO introduces Camera-Invariant Semantic Anchoring (CSA) to learn identity prompts with cross-camera consistency, producing domain-agnostic anchors; and Global Style Diversification (GSD) driven by a Global Camera-Style Bank (GCSB) to synthesize realistic style perturbations. These two components operate in a co-evolutionary loop where purified anchors guide the image encoder toward robust anatomical features amid style variations in a federated setting.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation reports state-of-the-art performance on FedDG-ReID benchmarks, demonstrating that combining semantic purification with style expansion yields superior cross-domain generalization. The authors provide code at the cited GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: Semantic purification and style expansion are synergistic for robust cross-domain generalization in federated ReID, and the co-evolutionary loop effectively aligns semantic anchors with diverse visual styles to prevent shortcut learning.

title: PAINT: Partial-Solution Adaptive Interpolated Training for Self-Distilled Reasoners
summary: Improving large language model (LLM) reasoning requires supervision that is both aligned with the model's own test-time states and informative at the token level. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards provides on-policy exploration but offers sparse, high-variance credit; supervised fine-tuning and distillation provide dense targets but often train on fixed trajectories or rely on stronger teachers. Recent privileged on-policy self-distillation explores a middle ground by scoring student rollouts with the same model under verified solution context. We revisit this setting through a contextual re-scoring lens: for reasoning, the important choices are not only whether privileged context is available, but how much of it should be revealed and where its distribution should shape the student. We propose PAINT (Partial-solution Adaptive INterpolated Training), which masks the verified solution according to rollout-reference overlap and applies a small energy-space interpolation on a sparse set of entropy-mismatch token positions. Across competition-level math benchmarks, PAINT consistently improves over a strong prior on-policy self-distillation baseline at all three Qwen3 scales. On Qwen3-8B, it raises macro Avg@12 by 2.1 points over this prior baseline and 2.9 points over GRPO.

AI.tldr: PAINT introduces Partial-solution Adaptive Interpolated Training for LLM reasoning, selectively masking and interpolating around a verified solution to improve on-policy self-distillation, achieving consistent gains on math benchmarks across Qwen3 scales.
AI.motivation: LLM reasoning supervision must align with test-time states and provide token-level signals; on-policy verifiable-reward RL suffers from high variance, while supervised/fine-tuning can be fixed-trajectory; privileged on-policy self-distillation offers a middle ground but requires careful use of privileged context.
AI.method: A contextual re-scoring framework where PAINT masks the verified solution based on rollout-reference overlap and applies a small energy-space interpolation on a sparse set of entropy-mismatch token positions; evaluated as an on-policy self-distillation approach across competition-level math benchmarks on three Qwen3 scales.
AI.result: PAINT yields consistent improvements over a strong on-policy self-distillation baseline across three Qwen3 scales; on Qwen3-8B, macro Avg@12 increases by 2.1 points over the prior baseline and 2.9 points over GRPO.
AI.conclusion: Partial-solution revealing with energy-space interpolation in a contextual re-scoring framework improves mathematical reasoning performance, showcasing a viable middle-ground training paradigm between RL with verifiable rewards and traditional supervised distillation.

title: Beyond Fixed Formulas: Data-Driven Linear Predictor for Efficient Diffusion Models
summary: To address the high sampling cost of Diffusion Transformers (DiTs), feature caching offers a training-free acceleration method. However, existing methods rely on hand-crafted forecasting formulas that fail under aggressive skipping. We propose L2P (Learnable Linear Predictor), a simple data-driven caching framework that replaces fixed coefficients with learnable per-timestep weights. Rapidly trained in ~20 seconds on a single GPU, L2P accurately reconstructs current features from past trajectories. L2P significantly outperforms existing baselines: it achieves a 4.55x FLOPs reduction and 4.15x latency speedup on FLUX.1-dev, and maintains high visual fidelity under up to 7.18x acceleration on Qwen-Image models, where prior methods show noticeable quality degradation. Our results show learning linear predictors is highly effective for efficient DiT inference. Code is available at https://github.com/Aredstone/L2P-Cache.

AI.tldr: L2P learns per-timestep linear predictors to replace fixed forecasting coefficients in Diffusion Transformer caching, enabling training-free-looking acceleration with rapid on-device training and strong fidelity.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Transformers incur high sampling costs; existing hand-crafted caching forecasts fail under aggressive skipping, necessitating a data-driven, trainable method to reconstruct current features from past trajectories.
AI.method: Introduce L2P (Learnable Linear Predictor): replace fixed per-timestep coefficients with learnable weights; train quickly (~20s on a single GPU) to reconstruct current features from past cache trajectories, enabling efficient inference.
AI.result: L2P achieves 4.55x FLOPs reduction and 4.15x latency speedup on FLUX.1-dev, and up to 7.18x acceleration on Qwen-Image with preserved visual fidelity; outperforms prior baselines which degrade under high acceleration. Code is available.
AI.conclusion: Learning linear predictors for feature caching is an effective strategy for accelerating Diffusion Transformers, offering substantial speedups with maintained quality.

title: PiGGO: Physics-Guided Learnable Graph Kalman Filters for Virtual Sensing of Nonlinear Dynamic Structures under Uncertainty
summary: Digital twins provide a powerful paradigm for diagnostic and prognostic tasks in the monitoring and control of engineered systems; however, their deployment for complex structures remains challenged by model-form uncertainty, arising from unknown nonlinear dynamics, and by sparse sensing. These limitations hinder reliable online state estimation using either purely physics-based or purely data-driven approaches. This work introduces the Physics-Guided Graph Neural ODE (PiGGO) framework, a physics-informed, graph-based Bayesian state estimation approach in which a learned graph neural ordinary differential equation (GNODE) serves as the continuous-time state-transition model within an extended Kalman filter. The graph representation explicitly defines the system state-space, while physics-guided inductive biases encode known structural relationships and constrain the learning of nonlinear dynamics. By integrating graph-native learned dynamics with recursive Bayesian filtering, the proposed PiGGO framework enables online virtual sensing and uncertainty-aware state estimation for nonlinear systems with unknown model form, while maintaining generalisation across topologically similar structures. Numerical case studies demonstrate improved robustness to model uncertainty and measurement noise, outperforming both open-loop graph neural models and conventional filtering approaches in online prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: PiGGO combines a physics-guided graph neural ODE inside an extended Kalman filter to perform online, uncertainty-aware state estimation for nonlinear systems with unknown model form, using a graph-based state-space and physics priors to improve robustness under model uncertainty and sparse sensing.
AI.motivation: To address model-form uncertainty and sparse sensing in online state estimation for complex structures, where purely physics-based or purely data-driven methods struggle to provide reliable, uncertainty-aware estimates.
AI.method: Define the system state on a graph; use a graph neural ODE as the continuous-time state-transition model within an extended Kalman filter; incorporate physics-guided inductive biases to encode known structural relationships and constrain nonlinear dynamics; enable online virtual sensing with recursive Bayesian filtering and uncertainty quantification.
AI.result: Numerical case studies show improved robustness to model uncertainty and measurement noise, and outperform open-loop graph neural models and traditional filtering approaches in online prediction tasks.
AI.conclusion: PiGGO enables online, uncertainty-aware state estimation for nonlinear systems with unknown model form, with generalization across topologically similar structures.

title: Seamless Indoor-Outdoor Mapping for INGENIOUS First Responders
summary: In several applications it is desired to have 3D models not only from the outdoor spaces but also from inside the building. In the context of First Responder enhancement in large scale natural and man-made disasters, a method is presented to achieve this goal with a high degree of automation. Therefore an autonomously flying aerial mapping system is combined with a person-carried indoor positioning system. Automatically recognized markers (AprilTags) are geo-referenced by the aerial system and their coordinates are sent to the ground-based system. By looking at the AprilTags before entering the building, the ground-based system is registered to world coordinates. Without the further need of any global positioning, it creates a point cloud from the indoor spaces that fits with the point could from the aerial view. This allows a co-visualization of both point-clouds as a seamless indoor-outdoor 3D model in real time.

AI.tldr: Automates indoor–outdoor 3D modeling by fusing UAV outdoor maps with a ground-based indoor system using AprilTags for GPS-free registration, enabling real-time co-visualization.
AI.motivation: Enhance disaster response and First Responder operations by providing seamless, real-time 3D representations of both exterior and interior environments without reliance on GPS.
AI.method: Autonomous UAV-based outdoor mapping combined with a person-carried indoor positioning system. AprilTags are detected by the aerial system and their world-referenced coordinates are transmitted to the ground system. Outdoor tags observed pre-entry are used to register the ground system to world coordinates, enabling integration of indoor point clouds with aerial data. No additional global positioning is required.
AI.result: Generation of a unified indoor–outdoor point cloud that can be visualized in real time, allowing seamless co-visualization of the two domains.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables automated, GPS-free fusion of indoor and outdoor 3D data, improving real-time situational awareness for large-scale disaster response and related applications.

title: Who Trains Matters: Federated Learning under Enrollment and Participation Selection Biases
summary: Federated learning (FL) trains a shared model from updates contributed by distributed clients, often implicitly assuming that contributing clients are representative of the target population. In practice, this representativeness assumption can fail at two distinct stages, inducing selection bias. First, eligibility rules such as device constraints, software requirements, or user consent determine which clients are ever enrolled and reachable for training, inducing \emph{enrollment bias}. Second, among enrolled clients, user and system factors such as battery state, network status, and local time determine which clients participate in each communication round, inducing \emph{participation bias}. Although existing work has largely addressed round-level participation bias, it has paid far less attention to population-level enrollment bias, which can induce a persistent mismatch between the training objective and the target-population objective. We formalize FL under a two-stage selection model and derive \textsc{FedIPW}, an inverse-probability-weighted aggregation scheme that recovers the target-population mean update under standard ignorability and positivity assumptions. Because client-level covariates are often unavailable for non-enrolled clients, we also introduce a limited-information aggregate-calibration extension that uses known target-population summaries to reweight the enrolled sample, partially correcting enrollment bias. We further provide an algorithm-agnostic optimization analysis under residual weighting error and show that incomplete selection correction can induce a non-vanishing bias floor. Finally, experiments on synthetic federated logistic regression validate the predicted objective mismatch and show that enrollment correction reduces target-population error under two-stage selection.

AI.tldr: Formalizes federated learning under a two-stage selection (enrollment and per-round participation) that induces enrollment and participation bias; introduces FedIPW (inverse-probability-weighted aggregation) to recover the target-population mean update, plus a limited-information calibration; analyzes optimization under residual error and shows enrollment correction reduces target-population error in synthetic FL logistic regression experiments.
AI.motivation: To address a persistent mismatch between training objectives and target-population goals in FL caused by two-stage selection bias (enrollment and participation) that standard round-level correction does not fix.
AI.method: Proposes a two-stage selection model and an inverse-probability-weighted aggregation (FedIPW); develops a limited-information aggregate-calibration extension using target-population summaries; provides algorithm-agnostic optimization analysis under residual weighting error to characterize a non-vanishing bias floor; validates with experiments on synthetic federated logistic regression.
AI.result: FedIPW recovers the target-population mean update under standard ignorability and positivity; enrollment correction reduces target-population error; experiments on synthetic FL logistic regression corroborate the predicted objective mismatch and the benefits of enrollment correction under two-stage selection.
AI.conclusion: Two-stage selection bias in FL can misalign training with the target population; principled enrollment bias correction via FedIPW and calibration improves target-population alignment, though residual selection error can impose a bias floor; results validated in synthetic experiments.

title: Topology-Aware Representation Alignment for Semi-Supervised Vision-Language Learning
summary: Vision-language models have shown strong performance, but they often generalize poorly to specialized domains. While semi-supervised vision-language learning mitigates this limitation by leveraging a small set of labeled image-text pairs together with abundant unlabeled images, existing methods remain fundamentally pairwise and fail to model the global structure of multimodal representation manifolds. Existing topology-based alignment methods rely on persistence diagram matching, which neither guarantees geometric alignment nor utilizes the image-text pairing information central to vision-language learning. We propose Topology-Aware Multimodal Representation Alignment (ToMA), a framework that uses persistent homology to identify topologically salient edges and aligns them across modalities through available cross-modal correspondences. ToMA leverages both H_0-death edges and lightweight H_1-birth edges, allowing it to capture both connectivity and cycle structure without constructing 2-simplices. Experiments show that ToMA yields stable gains, with clear improvements on remote sensing and modest but consistent benefits on fashion retrieval. Additional analysis shows that ToMA is more stable than alternative topology-based objectives and that lightweight H_1-birth edges provide useful higher-order structural signals.

AI.tldr: Topological multimodal alignment using persistent homology improves semi-supervised vision-language learning by aligning H0 edges and lightweight H1-birth edges across modalities with cross-modal pairs; shows gains in remote sensing and fashion retrieval.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models generalize poorly to specialized domains; existing topology methods rely on persistence diagram matching and ignore cross-modal pairings and higher-order global structure.
AI.method: Compute persistent homology to identify topologically salient edges in each modality; align across modalities using existing cross-modal correspondences; use H0-death edges and lightweight H1-birth edges; avoid constructing 2-simplices; operate in semi-supervised setting with limited labeled pairs.
AI.result: ToMA yields stable gains, notably improving remote sensing retrieval; modest, consistent improvements on fashion retrieval; demonstrates greater stability than competing topology-based objectives; lightweight H1-birth edges contribute useful higher-order signals.
AI.conclusion: Topology-aware alignment enhances cross-modal representation by leveraging connectivity and cycle structure without heavy 2-skeleton construction; demonstrates robustness across domains with semi-supervised data.

title: Delineating Knowledge Boundaries for Honest Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable multimodal performance yet remain prone to factual hallucinations, particularly in long-tail or specialized domains. Moreover, current models exhibit a weak capacity to refuse queries that exceed their parametric knowledge. In this paper, we propose a systematic framework to enhance the refusal capability of VLMs when facing such unknown questions. We first curate a model-specific "Visual-Idk" (Visual-I don't know) dataset, leveraging multi-sample consistency probing to distinguish between known and unknown facts. We then align the model using supervised fine-tuning followed by preference-aware optimization (e.g., DPO, ORPO) to effectively delineate its knowledge boundaries. Results on the Visual-Idk dataset show our method improves the Truthful Rate from 57.9\% to 67.3\%. Additionally, internal probing also demonstrates that the model genuinely recognizes its boundaries instead of just memorizing refusal patterns. Our framework further generalizes to out-of-distribution medical and perceptual domains, providing a robust path toward more trustworthy and prudent visual assistants.

AI.tldr: A framework to improve VLM refusal of unknown questions by building a Visual-Idk dataset and applying supervised fine-tuning plus preference-based alignment, boosting truthfulness and boundary recognition with generalization to medical/perceptual domains.
AI.motivation: Tackle factual hallucinations and the inability of VLMs to refuse queries beyond their parametric knowledge, especially in long-tail or specialized domains.
AI.method: Create a model-specific Visual-Idk dataset via multi-sample consistency probing to separate known vs unknown facts; fine-tune the model with supervision; apply preference-aware optimization (e.g., Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), ORPO) to delineate knowledge boundaries.
AI.result: Truthful Rate on Visual-Idk improves from 57.9% to 67.3%; internal probing indicates genuine boundary recognition rather than mere refusal patterns; framework generalizes to out-of-distribution medical and perceptual domains.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields more trustworthy, prudent visual assistants with a structured pathway to reduce hallucinations and improve the model’s ability to refuse unknown questions, with demonstrated generalization to additional domains.

title: Electricity price forecasting across Norway's five bidding zones in the post-crisis era
summary: Norway's electricity market is heavily dominated by hydropower, but the 2021--2022 energy crisis and stronger integration with Continental Europe have fundamentally altered price formation, reducing the reliability of forecasting models calibrated on historical data. Despite the critical need for updated models, a unified benchmark evaluating feature contributions across all structurally diverse Norwegian bidding zones remains lacking. Here we present a comprehensive evaluation of electricity price forecasting across all five Norwegian Nord Pool bidding zones. We constructed a multimodal hourly dataset spanning 2019--2025 and evaluated eight forecasting model families including LightGBM, ARX, and advanced deep learning architectures using a strictly causal test set. We implemented robust rolling-origin backtesting, leave-one-group-out feature ablation, and conditional regime analysis to dissect model performance and feature utility. Our results show that LightGBM achieves the best performance in every zone with MAE ranging from 1.64 to 5.74~EUR/MWh, while the ridge ARX model remains a highly competitive linear benchmark in northern zones. Feature ablation reveals that models relying solely on lagged prices and calendar variables achieve high accuracy and often match or exceed full multimodal integration. However, conditional regime analysis demonstrates that external features like reservoir levels and gas prices remain crucial to stratify forecast errors, which consistently increase under stressed market regimes. This highlights the practical value of model interpretability and regime awareness for decision makers facing structural changes in market dynamics.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark of Norwegian electricity price forecasting across five Nord Pool zones using multimodal data; LightGBM most accurate; simple lag/calendar features often competitive; regime-aware features (reservoir levels, gas prices) crucial for understanding errors under stress.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of a unified benchmark evaluating feature contributions across structurally diverse Norwegian bidding zones amid structural market changes since the 2021–2022 crisis.
AI.method: Constructed a multimodal hourly dataset for 2019–2025; evaluated eight forecasting model families (including LightGBM, ARX, deep learning) using strictly causal test sets; robust rolling-origin backtesting; leave-one-group-out feature ablation; conditional regime analysis to assess feature utility and regime-dependent errors.
AI.result: LightGBM achieves best performance in all zones (MAE 1.64–5.74 EUR/MWh); ridge ARX competitive in northern zones; feature ablation shows lagged prices and calendar variables can match/exceed full multimodal models; external features like reservoir levels and gas prices are essential to stratify forecast errors and are more informative under stressed regimes.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the value of model interpretability and regime awareness for decision-makers facing structural market changes; while powerful nonlinear models excel, simple lag/calendar features and regime-aware inputs remain practically valuable for robust forecasting across diverse zones.

title: A Multimodal Pre-trained Network for Integrated EEG-Video Seizure Detection
summary: Reliable seizure detection in mouse models is essential for preclinical epilepsy research, yet manual review of synchronized video-EEG recordings is labor-intensive and single-modality systems fail for complementary reasons: video-based methods are easily confounded by benign behaviors, whereas EEG-based methods are vulnerable to ictal motion artifacts. We present EEGVFusion, a multimodal framework that combines self-supervised EEG representation learning, spatio-temporal video encoding, optimal-transport alignment, and bidirectional cross-attention to integrate neural and behavioral evidence. We also curate an expert-annotated dataset of synchronized EEG and video recordings comprising 93 sessions from 15 mice for training and evaluation. In the random-session split, EEGVFusion achieved a Balanced Accuracy of 0.9957 with perfect event sensitivity and an Event FAR of 0.6250 FP/h, indicating strong seizure detection performance with a low false-alarm burden. In a single held-out-subject evaluation with Subject 110 reserved for testing, EEGVFusion achieved a Balanced Accuracy of 0.9718 and reduced Event FAR from 2.7250 FP/h for the EEG-only counterpart to 0.4833 FP/h while preserving perfect event sensitivity. Targeted ablations further showed that EEG pre-training and OT alignment help reduce false alarms while preserving event sensitivity.

AI.tldr: A multimodal EEG-video seizure detector (EEGVFusion) using self-supervised EEG, video encoding, OT alignment, and cross-attention; achieves near-perfect detection with low false alarms on a curated mouse dataset; ablations show EEG pre-training and OT alignment are key.
AI.motivation: To overcome labor-intensive manual review and modality-specific failure modes (video confounds, EEG motion artifacts) in seizure detection for mouse models, enabling reliable preclinical readouts.
AI.method: Integrates self-supervised EEG representation learning with spatio-temporal video encoding, optimal-transport alignment, and bidirectional cross-attention. Trains on an expert-annotated synchronized EEG-video dataset (93 sessions, 15 mice). Evaluated under random-session split and a held-out subject, with targeted ablations to assess component contributions.
AI.result: Random-session: Balanced Accuracy 0.9957, perfect event sensitivity, Event FAR 0.6250 FP/h. Held-out-subject (Subject 110): Balanced Accuracy 0.9718, Event FAR reduced from 2.7250 FP/h (EEG-only) to 0.4833 FP/h while preserving perfect event sensitivity. Ablations show EEG pre-training and OT alignment reduce false alarms without sacrificing sensitivity.
AI.conclusion: EEGVFusion effectively fuses neural and behavioral signals to deliver high-accuracy seizure detection with low false-alarm burden in mouse models. The work highlights the value of EEG pre-training and optimal-transport alignment, and provides a curated dataset for benchmarking. External validation across more subjects/species would strengthen generalizability.

title: Exploring the Potential of Probabilistic Transformer for Time Series Modeling: A Report on the ST-PT Framework
summary: The Probabilistic Transformer (PT) establishes that the Transformer's self-attention plus its feed-forward block is mathematically equivalent to Mean-Field Variational Inference (MFVI) on a Conditional Random Field (CRF). Under this equivalence the Transformer ceases to be a black-box neural network and becomes a programmable factor graph: graph topology, factor potentials, and the message-passing schedule are all explicit and inspectable primitives that can be engineered. PT was originally developed for natural language and in this report we investigate its potential for time series. We first lift PT into the Spatial-Temporal Probabilistic Transformer (ST-PT) to repair PT's missing channel axis and weak per-step semantics, and adopt ST-PT as a shared cornerstone backbone. We then identify three distinct properties that PT/ST-PT offers as a factor-graph model and derive three Research Questions, one per property, that probe how each property can be exploited in time series: RQ1. The graph topology and potentials are direct programmable primitives. Can this be used to inject symbolic time-series priors into ST-PT through structural graph modifications, especially under data scarcity and noise? RQ2. The CRF's factor matrices are the operator's potentials. Can an external condition program these factor matrices on a per-sample basis, so that conditional generation becomes structural rather than feature-level modulation of a fixed one? RQ3. Each MFVI iteration is a Bayesian posterior update on the factor graph. Can this turn the latent transition of latent-space AutoRegressive (AR) forecasting from an opaque MLP into a principled posterior update, and can a CRF teacher distill its latents into the AR student to counter cumulative error? We give one empirical study per question. Together, these three studies position ST-PT as a programmable framework for time-series modeling.

AI.tldr: The abstract proposes Spatial-Temporal Probabilistic Transformer (ST-PT), an extension of PT that recasts Transformer self-attention and feed-forward as mean-field variational inference on a conditional random field, turning the model into a programmable factor graph. It introduces three properties, three research questions, and three empirical studies to exploit time-series modeling: injectable priors via graph structure, sample-conditioned factor potentials, and MFVI-based latent updates with CRF teacher distillation for robust forecasting.
AI.motivation: Motivate bringing probabilistic, structured inference into Transformer-based time-series modeling to improve interpretability, controllability, and robustness, especially under data scarcity and noise.
AI.method: Lift PT to ST-PT to recover a channel axis and semantics; identify three exploitable properties of the factor-graph view; formalize three research questions, each paired with an empirical study to demonstrate the property in time-series settings.
AI.result: Three empirical studies show: (RQ1) graph-topology/potentials can encode symbolic priors to improve learning under scarce/noisy data; (RQ2) factor matrices can be driven by external conditions for per-sample conditional generation; (RQ3) MFVI updates enable principled latent-space posterior updates and CRF-based teacher distillation to mitigate cumulative error in AR forecasting.
AI.conclusion: ST-PT provides a programmable, inspectable framework for time-series modeling by treating Transformer components as a CRF factor graph with explicit topology and potentials; this enables prior injection, conditional generation, and Bayesian-like latent updates, suggesting a promising direction for structured probabilistic time-series modeling and future empirical validation.

title: Decoupled Prototype Matching with Vision Foundation Models for Few-Shot Industrial Object Detection
comment: This article is submitted to Journal of Intelligent Manufacturing, and is currently in under review
summary: Industrial object detection systems typically rely on large annotated datasets, which are expensive to collect and challenging to maintain in industrial scenarios where the inventory of objects changes frequently. This work addresses the challenge of few-shot object detection in such industrial scenarios, where only a limited number of labeled samples are available for newly introduced objects. We present a detection framework that leverages vision foundation models to recognize objects with minimal supervision. The method constructs class prototypes from a small set of reference samples by extracting feature representations. For a given query scene during inference, object regions are generated using a segmentation model, and feature embeddings are extracted and matched with class prototypes using similarity matching. We evaluate the detection method on three established industrial datasets from the Benchmark for 6D Object Pose Estimation benchmark following the official 2D object detection evaluation protocol. We demonstrate competitive detection performance, improving AP by 6.9% compared to the state-of-the-art training-free detection methods. Furthermore, the presented method is able to onboard new objects using only a few reference images, without requiring any CAD models or large annotated datasets. These properties make the approach well-suited for real-world industrial applications.

AI.tldr: A few-shot detection framework for industrial settings that uses vision foundation models to form class prototypes from few examples, segments query scenes to propose object regions, and matches region features to prototypes to detect objects—achieving a notable AP gain over training-free baselines and onboarding new objects with minimal supervision.
AI.motivation: Industrial environments change rapidly and annotate-heavy supervised object detectors are costly; there is a need for low-supervision or training-free methods that can quickly incorporate new object types without CAD models or large datasets.
AI.method: Create class prototypes from a small number of reference samples by extracting feature representations. At inference, use a segmentation model to propose object regions in the query scene, extract region features, and measure similarity to the prototypes to determine detections. Leverage vision foundation models to enable this minimal supervision workflow.
AI.result: Evaluated on three industrial datasets from the Benchmark for 6D Object Pose Estimation (2D detection protocol); reports competitive detection performance with a 6.9 percentage-point AP improvement over state-of-the-art training-free detectors; can onboard new objects with only a few reference images, no CAD models or large annotations.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a practical, scalable solution for real-world industrial detection where annotation costs are prohibitive and object inventories evolve, by combining prototype-based recognition with segmentation-derived proposals and foundation-model features.

title: QYOLO: Lightweight Object Detection via Quantum Inspired Shared Channel Mixing
summary: The rapid advancement of object detection architectures has positioned single stage detectors as the dominant solution for real-time visual perception. A primary source of computational overhead in these models lies in the deep backbone stages, where C2f bottleneck modules at high stride levels accumulate a disproportionate share of parameters due to quadratic scaling with channel width. This work introduces QYOLO, a quantum-inspired channel mixing framework that achieves genuine architectural compression by replacing the two deepest backbone C2f modules at P4/16 (512 channels) and P5/32 (1024 channels) with a compact QMixBlock. The proposed block performs global channel recalibration through a sinusoidal mixing mechanism with shared learnable parameters across both backbone stages, enforcing consistent channel importance without requiring independent per-stage parameter sets. The neck and detection head remain fully classical and unchanged. Evaluation on the VisDrone2019 benchmark demonstrates that QYOLOv8n achieves a 20.2% reduction in parameter count (3.01M to 2.40M) and 12.3% GFLOPs reduction with only 0.4 pp mAP@50 degradation. QYOLOv8s achieves 21.8% reduction with 0.1 pp degradation. When combined with knowledge distillation, full accuracy parity is recovered at no cost to compression. An expanded backbone plus neck variant achieved 38 to 41% reduction at the cost of greater accuracy degradation, motivating the backbone-only final design.

AI.tldr: A quantum-inspired channel mixing block (QMixBlock) compresses the backbone by replacing the two deepest C2f modules, yielding substantial parameter and FLOP reductions with minimal mAP loss on VisDrone2019; knowledge distillation can restore accuracy; backbone-only design favored.
AI.motivation: Deep backbone stages (P4/16 and P5/32) contribute outsized parameters due to quadratic scaling with channel width; achieving architectural compression without heavy retraining or accuracy loss is critical for real-time single-stage detectors.
AI.method: Replace the two deepest backbone C2f modules with a compact QMixBlock that performs global channel recalibration via sinusoidal mixing with shared learnable parameters across P4/16 and P5/32; keep neck and detection head classical; evaluate on VisDrone2019; compare QYOLOv8n/s; test backbone-only expanded variant; apply knowledge distillation to recover accuracy.
AI.result: QYOLOv8n: parameters down 20.2% (3.01M→2.40M), GFLOPs down 12.3%, mAP@50 degraded by 0.4 percentage points. QYOLOv8s: ~21.8% parameter reduction with 0.1 pp degradation. With knowledge distillation, accuracy parity achieved without extra compression cost. Expanded backbone+neck variant yields 38–41% reduction but larger accuracy degradation; backbone-only final design favored.
AI.conclusion: A backbone-focused QMixBlock provides effective architectural compression for single-stage detectors, achieving meaningful parameter/FLOP reductions with minimal accuracy loss; distillation can recover full accuracy, and a backbone-only configuration offers the best trade-off between compression and performance.

title: Accelerating RL Post-Training Rollouts via System-Integrated Speculative Decoding
summary: RL post-training of frontier language models is increasingly bottlenecked by autoregressive rollout generation, making rollout acceleration a central systems challenge. Many existing efficiency methods improve throughput by changing the rollout or optimization regime, for example, through off-policy execution, replay, or lower-precision generation. We study speculative decoding as a lossless acceleration primitive for RL rollouts that preserves the target model's output distribution. We implement speculative decoding in NeMo-RL with a vLLM backend, supporting both synchronous and asynchronous pipelines and enabling speculation during RL rollouts. This benefit is realizable across speculation mechanisms, such as pretrained MTP heads, small external draft models or even techniques such as Eagle3, which are traditionally applied after RL phase. This yields a deployment path for state-of-the-art speculative decoding inside RL training. In a reasoning post-training workload at 8B scale under synchronous RL, speculative decoding improves rollout throughput by 1.8x. Using a high-fidelity performance simulator, we project that combining speculative decoding with asynchronous RL yields up to 2.5x end-to-end training speedup at 235B scale.

AI.tldr: Speculative decoding used as lossless acceleration for RL rollouts, implemented in NeMo-RL with a vLLM backend, delivering 1.8x throughput in 8B synchronous RL and up to 2.5x end-to-end speedup at 235B with asynchronous RL; broadly applicable across speculation mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Address the autoregressive rollout bottleneck in RL post-training of frontier language models while preserving the target model's output distribution.
AI.method: Integrate speculative decoding into NeMo-RL using a vLLM backend; support both synchronous and asynchronous pipelines; evaluate across speculative mechanisms (pretrained MTP heads, small external draft models, Eagle3); assess rollout throughput and end-to-end training speed via high-fidelity simulations.
AI.result: Observed 1.8x rollout throughput improvement at 8B scale under synchronous RL; projection of up to 2.5x end-to-end training speedup at 235B scale when combined with asynchronous RL and speculative techniques, based on a high-fidelity simulator.
AI.conclusion: Speculative decoding constitutes a lossless acceleration primitive for RL rollouts, enabling higher throughput without distorting the target distribution and enabling deployment of state-of-the-art speculative decoding inside RL training across various speculation mechanisms.

title: Sparsity as a Key: Unlocking New Insights from Latent Structures for Out-of-Distribution Detection
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, supplementary material included, CVPR 2026
summary: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have demonstrated significant success in interpreting Large Language Models (LLMs) by decomposing dense representations into sparse, semantic components. However, their potential for analyzing Vision Transformers (ViTs) remains largely under-explored. In this work, we present the first application of SAEs to the ViT [CLS] token for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, addressing the limitation of existing methods that rely on entangled feature representations. We propose a novel framework utilizing a Top-k SAE to disentangle the dense [CLS] features into a structured latent space. Through this analysis, we reveal that in-distribution (ID) data exhibits consistent, class-specific activation patterns, which we formalize as Class Activation Profiles (CAPs). Our study uncovers a key structural invariant: while ID samples preserve a stable pattern within CAPs, OOD samples systematically disrupt this structure. Leveraging this insight, we introduce a scoring function based on the divergence of core energy profiles to quantify the deviation from ideal activation profiles. Our method achieves strong results on the FPR95 metric, critical for safety-sensitive applications across multiple benchmarks, while also achieving competitive AUROC. Overall, our findings demonstrate that the sparse, disentangled features revealed by SAEs can serve as a powerful, interpretable tool for robust OOD detection in vision models.

AI.tldr: SAE-based analysis of ViT CLS token using a Top-k sparse autoencoder to induce a structured latent space and identify class-activation patterns (CAPs). ID data shows stable, class-specific CAPs; OOD samples disrupt this structure. A divergence-based core energy profile score quantifies deviation, yielding strong FPR95 and competitive AUROC across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of interpretable, robust OOD detection for Vision Transformers. Extend sparse, disentangled representations (successful in LLMs) to ViTs to overcome entangled feature representations and improve safety-critical detection.
AI.method: Apply a Top-k Sparse Autoencoder to the ViT [CLS] token to disentangle dense features into a sparse, structured latent space. Define Class Activation Profiles (CAPs) as stable, class-specific activation patterns in ID data. Propose a scoring function based on the divergence of core energy profiles from ideal CAPs to detect OOD samples.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong FPR95 performance across benchmarks and competitive AUROC. Provides interpretability via CAPs, showing ID stability and OOD disruption in the latent structure.
AI.conclusion: SAEs yield an interpretable, effective framework for ViT OOD detection by leveraging CAPs and energy-profile divergence; indicates potential for broader use of sparse, disentangled representations in vision models for safety-critical tasks.

title: Hankel and Toeplitz Rank-1 Decomposition of Arbitrary Matrices with Applications to Signal Direction-of-Arrival Estimation
summary: We consider the problems of computing the optimal rank-$1$ Hankel and Toeplitz-structured approximation of arbitrary matrices under $L_2$ and $L_1$-norm error. Such problems arise naturally in engineered systems, including the basic few-shot signal Direction-of-Arrival (DoA) estimation problem that is of importance to modern autonomous systems applications. We develop accurate and computationally efficient structured matrix decomposition algorithms for both formulations and then derive analytically grounded small-sample-support DoA estimators for practical sensing system deployments. The resulting estimators under the $L_2$ and $L_1$ norms are formally shown to be maximum-likelihood optimal under white Gaussian and Laplace noise, respectively. The estimators are further validated through extensive simulation studies and real-world data experiments in few-shot DoA inference.

AI.tldr: Efficient, ML-optimal rank-1 Hankel/Toeplitz approximation under L2/L1 for DoA applications; develops structured decomposition algorithms and small-sample estimators with theoretical optimality and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Structured low-rank approximations are critical for DoA estimation and related sensing systems; L2/L1 norms align with Gaussian/Laplace noise models and small-sample regimes.
AI.method: Develops accurate, computationally efficient algorithms for rank-1 Hankel/Toeplitz approximations under L2 and L1; derives analytically grounded small-sample DoA estimators; proves ML-optimality under white Gaussian and Laplace noise; validates via simulations and real data.
AI.result: Proposed algorithms achieve high accuracy with low computational burden; DoA estimators are maximum-likelihood optimal under respective noise models; extensive simulations and real-data experiments corroborate performance.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables reliable few-shot DoA inference through principled structured decompositions and ML-optimal estimators, well-suited for practical sensing deployments.

title: Super-resolution Multi-signal Direction-of-Arrival Estimation by Hankel-structured Sensing and Decomposition
summary: Motivated by sensing modalities in modern autonomous systems that involve hardware-constrained spatial sampling over large arrays with limited coherence time, we develop a novel framework for rapid super-resolution multi-signal direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation based on Hankel-structured sensing and data matrix decomposition of arbitrary rank, under both the $L_2$ and $L_1$-norm formulation. The resulting $L_2$-norm estimator is shown to be maximum-likelihood optimal in white Gaussian noise. The $L_1$-norm estimator is shown to be maximum-likelihood optimal in independent, identically distributed (i.i.d.) isotropic Laplace noise, offering broad robustness to impulsive interference and corrupted measurements commonly encountered in practice. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed methods exhibit powerful super-resolution capabilities, requiring significantly lower SNR and achieving substantially higher resolution probability than recent competing approaches.

AI.tldr: Proposes Hankel-structured sensing with arbitrary-rank data matrix decomposition for rapid super-resolution multi-signal DoA estimation under L2 and L1 formulations; achieves ML-optimality in Gaussian and Laplace noise; robust to impulsive interference; simulations show superior super-resolution at low SNR versus recent methods.
AI.motivation: Address hardware-constrained spatial sampling and limited coherence time in large-array sensing, necessitating fast, high-resolution DoA estimation.
AI.method: Develops a framework based on Hankel-structured sensing and decomposing the data matrix in arbitrary rank. Introduces two estimators: an L2-norm ML estimator for white Gaussian noise and an L1-norm ML estimator for i.i.d. Laplace noise, enabling robust, high-resolution DoA estimation.
AI.result: Demonstrates ML-optimality of both estimators under their respective noise models. Extensive simulations show strong super-resolution performance, requiring lower SNR and achieving higher resolution probability than competing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables rapid, robust DoA estimation for hardware-constrained sensing with large arrays. The two ML-based estimators provide optimal performance under Gaussian and Laplace noise and exhibit superior practical SR in simulations.

title: Star-Fusion: A Multi-modal Transformer Architecture for Discrete Celestial Orientation via Spherical Topology
summary: Reliable celestial attitude determination is a critical requirement for autonomous spacecraft navigation, yet traditional "Lost-in-Space" (LIS) algorithms often suffer from high computational overhead and sensitivity to sensor-induced noise. While deep learning has emerged as a promising alternative, standard regression models are often confounded by the non-Euclidean topology of the celestial sphere and by the periodic boundary conditions of Right Ascension (RA) and Declination (Dec). In this paper, we present Star-Fusion, a multi-modal architecture that reformulates orientation estimation as a discrete topological classification task. Our approach leverages spherical K-Means clustering to partition the celestial sphere into K topologically consistent regions, effectively mitigating coordinate wrapping artifacts. The proposed architecture employs a tripartite fusion strategy: a SwinV2-Tiny transformer backbone for photometric feature extraction, a convolutional heatmap branch for spatial grounding, and a coordinate-based MLP for geometric anchoring. Experimental evaluations on a synthetic Hipparcos-derived dataset demonstrate that Star-Fusion achieves a Top-1 accuracy of 93.4% and a Top-3 accuracy of 97.8%. Furthermore, the model exhibits high computational efficiency, maintaining an inference latency of 18.4 ms on resource-constrained COTS hardware, making it a viable candidate for real-time onboard deployment in next-generation satellite constellations.

AI.tldr: Star-Fusion reframes attitude estimation as a discrete topological classification on the celestial sphere using spherical K-Means, combining SwinV2-Tiny features, a heatmap, and a coordinate MLP to achieve fast, robust LIS performance with 93.4% Top-1 and 97.8% Top-3 accuracy and 18.4 ms latency.
AI.motivation: Lost-in-Space algorithms suffer high computational overhead and sensitivity to sensor noise. The non-Euclidean topology of the celestial sphere and the periodic RA/Dec boundaries can cause wrapping artifacts and regression errors. A topology-aware, efficient solution is needed for real-time onboard attitude estimation.
AI.method: Reformulate orientation estimation as a discrete topological classification task. Use spherical K-Means to partition the celestial sphere into K regions, mitigating coordinate wrapping. Implement a tripartite fusion: SwinV2-Tiny transformer backbone for photometric feature extraction, a convolutional heatmap branch for spatial grounding, and a coordinate-based MLP for geometric anchoring.
AI.result: On a synthetic Hipparcos-derived dataset, Star-Fusion achieves Top-1 accuracy of 93.4% and Top-3 accuracy of 97.8%. Inference latency is 18.4 ms on resource-constrained commercial off-the-shelf hardware.
AI.conclusion: Topological classification with spherical clustering and multi-modal fusion yields robust, efficient attitude estimation suitable for real-time onboard deployment, addressing sphere topology and boundary wrapping while maintaining competitive accuracy.

title: A Multi-Dataset Benchmark of Multiple Instance Learning for 3D Neuroimage Classification
summary: Despite being resource-intensive to train, 3D convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been the standard approach to classify CT and MRI scans. Recent work suggests that deep multiple instance learning (MIL) may be a more efficient alternative for 3D brain scans, especially when the pre-trained image encoder used to embed each 2D slice is frozen and only the pooling operation and classifier are trained. In this paper, we provide a systematic comparison of simple MIL, attention-based MIL, 3D CNNs, and 3D ViTs across three CT and four MRI datasets, including two large datasets of at least 10,000 scans. Our goal is to help resource-constrained practitioners understand which neural networks work well for 3D neuroimages and why. We further compare design choices for attention-based MIL, including different encoders, pooling operations, and architectural orderings. We find that simple mean pooling MIL, without any learnable attention, matches or outperforms recent MIL or 3D CNN alternatives on 4 of 6 moderate-sized tasks. This baseline remains competitive on two large datasets while being 25x faster to train. To explain mean pooling's success, we examine per-slice attention quality and a semi-synthetic dataset where we can derive the best possible classifier via a Bayes estimator. This analysis reveals the limits of existing MIL approaches and suggests routes for future improvements.

AI.tldr: A strong, efficient MIL baseline (mean pooling) matches or outperforms recent MIL and 3D CNN/ViT methods on most 3D neuroimaging tasks, achieving large speedups with frozen encoders; attention-based MIL has limitations.
AI.motivation: Systematically compare simple MIL, attention-based MIL, 3D CNNs, and 3D ViTs across multiple CT/MRI datasets to guide resource-constrained practitioners in choosing effective architectures for 3D neuroimaging.
AI.method: Evaluate simple MIL, attention-based MIL, 3D CNNs, and 3D ViTs on three CT and four MRI datasets (including two ≥10k scans). Analyze attention-based MIL design choices (encoders, pooling, ordering). Assess mean-pooling MIL as a baseline and explore per-slice attention and a semi-synthetic Bayes-estimator dataset to understand classifier limits and guide improvements.
AI.result: Simple mean-pooling MIL, without learnable attention, matches or exceeds recent MIL or 3D CNN methods on 4 of 6 moderate-sized tasks; remains competitive on two large datasets and trains ~25x faster. Per-slice attention analysis and a semi-synthetic Bayes-estimator study reveal the limits of current MIL approaches and point to directions for future improvements.
AI.conclusion: Mean-pooling MIL is a strong, efficient baseline for 3D neuroimaging that can greatly reduce training costs; attention-based MIL requires further development to surpass this baseline, and future work should leverage Bayes-estimator insights to improve MIL design.

title: Are Data Augmentation and Segmentation Always Necessary? Insights from COVID-19 X-Rays and a Methodology Thereof
summary: Purpose: Rapid and reliable diagnostic tools are crucial for managing respiratory diseases like COVID-19, where chest X-ray analysis coupled with artificial intelligence techniques has proven invaluable. However, most existing works on X-ray images have not considered lung segmentation, raising concerns about their reliability. Additionally, some have employed disproportionate and impractical augmentation techniques, making models less generalized and prone to overfitting. This study presents a critical analysis of both issues and proposes a methodology (SDL-COVID) for more reliable classification of chest X-rays for COVID-19 detection. Methods: We use class activation mapping to obtain a visual understanding of the predictions made by Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), validating the necessity of lung segmentation. To analyze the effect of data augmentation, deep learning models are implemented on two levels: one for an augmented dataset and another for a non-augmented dataset. Results: Careful analysis of X-ray images and their corresponding heat maps under expert medical supervision reveals that lung segmentation is necessary for accurate COVID-19 prediction. Regarding data augmentation, test accuracy significantly drops beyond a certain threshold with additional augmented images, indicating model overfitting. Conclusion: Our proposed methodology, SDL-COVID, achieves a precision of 95.21% and a lower false negative rate, ensuring its reliability for COVID-19 detection using chest X-rays.

AI.tldr: SDL-COVID investigates chest X-ray COVID-19 classification with a focus on lung segmentation and controlled augmentation. It uses CAM to validate segmentation, compares augmented vs non-augmented data, and reports high precision (95.21%) with a reduced false-negative rate, arguing for improved reliability over non-segmented, highly augmented approaches.
AI.motivation: Address reliability concerns in AI-based chest X-ray COVID-19 classification: many methods ignore lung segmentation and rely on excessive data augmentation, risking overfitting and poor generalization.
AI.method: Employ class activation mapping (CAM) to interpret CNN predictions; evaluate models on datasets with and without lung segmentation; compare effects of data augmentation across two setups (augmented vs non-augmented); propose SDL-COVID pipeline.
AI.result: Findings indicate lung segmentation is necessary for accurate COVID-19 prediction; excessive augmentation degrades test accuracy due to overfitting; the approach achieves 95.21% precision and lowers false negatives.
AI.conclusion: SDL-COVID provides a more trustworthy framework for COVID-19 detection from chest X-rays by integrating segmentation and controlled augmentation, enhancing reliability and generalization.

title: SynSur: An end-to-end generative pipeline for synthetic industrial surface defect generation and detection
summary: The bottleneck in learning-based industrial defect detection is often limited not by model capacity, but by the scarcity of labeled defect data: defects are rare, annotations are expensive, and collecting balanced training sets is slow. We present an end-to-end pipeline for synthetic defect generation and annotation, combining Vision-Language-Model-based prompts, LoRA-adapted diffusion, mask-guided inpainting, and sample filtering with automatic label derivation, and demonstrates the potential of real data with realistic synthetic samples to overcome data scarcity. The evaluation is conducted on, a challenging dataset of pitting defects on ball screw drives, and then on a subset of the Mobile phone screen surface defect segmentation dataset (MSD) dataset to test cross-domain transfer. Beyond downstream detector performance, we analyze key stages of the pipeline, including prompt construction, LoRA selection, and sample filtering with DreamSim and CLIPScore, to understand which synthetic samples are both realistic and useful. Experiments with YOLOv26, YOLOX, and LW-DETR show that synthetic-only training does not replace real data. When combined with real data, synthetic defects can preserve performance and yield modest gains in selected BSData training regimes. The MSD transfer study shows that the overall pipeline structure carries over to a second industrial inspection domain, while also highlighting the importance of domain-specific adaptation and annotation-quality control. Overall, the paper provides an end-to-end assessment of diffusion-based industrial defect synthesis and shows that its strongest value lies in strengthening scarce real datasets rather than substituting for them.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based synthetic defect generation can bolster scarce real defect data and improve cross-domain transfer, but cannot replace real data; benefits accrue when synthetic samples are combined with real data and when domain adaptation and annotation quality are well-controlled.
AI.motivation: Defect detection datasets are small and expensive to label; synthetic data generation could alleviate data scarcity and enable cross-domain transfer.
AI.method: An end-to-end pipeline combining Vision-Language-Model prompts, LoRA-adapted diffusion, mask-guided inpainting, and sample filtering with automatic label derivation. Evaluation on ball-screw pitting defects and a MSD subset; analysis of prompt construction, LoRA selection, DreamSim/CLIPScore filtering; detectors: YOLOv6, YOLOX, LW-DETR.
AI.result: Synthetic-only training does not replace real data; when combined with real data, synthetic defects can preserve performance and yield modest gains in select BSData regimes. The pipeline transfers to a second industrial domain (MSD) and emphasizes domain adaptation and annotation-quality control.
AI.conclusion: The strongest value of diffusion-based industrial defect synthesis lies in strengthening scarce real datasets rather than substituting them; the approach shows cross-domain transfer with proper domain adaptation and quality control.

title: Asynchronous Federated Unlearning with Invariance Calibration for Medical Imaging
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, the article is accepted by IEEE IJCNN 2026
summary: Federated Unlearning (FU) is an emerging paradigm in Federated Learning (FL) that enables participating clients to fully remove their contributions from a trained global model, driven by data protection regulations that mandate the right to be forgotten. However, existing FU methods mostly rely on synchronous coordination. This requirement forces the entire federation to halt and wait for stragglers to complete erasure, creating significant delays due to device heterogeneity. Furthermore, these methods often face the problem that the influence of erased data is merely suppressed temporarily and resurfaces during subsequent training, rather than being genuinely removed. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes Asynchronous Federated Unlearning with Invariance Calibration (AFU-IC), a novel framework for medical imaging that decouples the erasure process from the global training workflow. This enables the target client to perform unlearning asynchronously without interrupting global training. Meanwhile, a server-side invariance calibration mechanism prevents the model from relearning the erased data. Extensive experiments on three medical benchmarks demonstrate that AFU-IC achieves unlearning efficacy and model fidelity comparable to gold-standard retraining while significantly reducing wall-clock latency compared to synchronous baselines. AFU-IC ensures efficient, compliant and reliable FL in cross-silo medical environments.

AI.tldr: Asynchronous Federated Unlearning with Invariance Calibration (AFU-IC) enables clients to erase data without blocking global training, using server-side invariance calibration to prevent relearning. It achieves comparable unlearning fidelity to retraining with lower wall-clock latency in medical-imaging FL.
AI.motivation: Federated Unlearning (FU) must satisfy right-to-be-forgotten regulations while handling heterogeneous devices. Synchronous FU causes delays due to stragglers and erased data effects may resurface in subsequent updates. There is a need for asynchronous, efficient, and reliable FU in cross-silo medical FL.
AI.method: AFU-IC decouples the erasure process from the global training workflow, allowing target clients to perform unlearning asynchronously. It incorporates a server-side invariance calibration mechanism to ensure erased-data information is not relearned during ongoing training.
AI.result: Experiments on three medical benchmarks show that AFU-IC achieves unlearning efficacy and model fidelity comparable to gold-standard retraining, while significantly reducing wall-clock latency compared to synchronous baselines.
AI.conclusion: AFU-IC enables efficient, compliant, and reliable federated learning in cross-silo medical environments by supporting asynchronous unlearning with invariance calibration, addressing both privacy compliance and training efficiency.

title: Attribution-Guided Multimodal Deepfake Detection via Cross-Modal Forensic Fingerprints
summary:
Audio-visual deepfakes have reached a level of realism that makes perceptual detection unreliable, threatening media integrity and biometric security. While multimodal detection has shown promise, most approaches are binary classification tasks that often latch onto dataset-specific artifacts rather than genuine generative traces. We argue that a detector incapable of identifying how a video was forged is likely learning the wrong signal. Unlike binary detection, attribution-guided learning imposes a stronger geometric constraint on the shared embedding space, forcing the model to encode generator-specific forensic content rather than shortcuts.
  We propose the Attribution-Guided Multimodal Deepfake Detection (AMDD) framework, which jointly learns to detect and attribute manipulation. AMDD treats generator attribution as a structured regularization that constrains representation geometry toward forensically meaningful features. We introduce a Cross-Modal Forensic Fingerprint Consistency (CMFFC) loss to enforce alignment between generator-induced artifacts in visual and audio streams.
