This exploits the fact that coherent manipulation leaves correlated traces across modalities, grounded in the physical coupling between speech and facial articulation that synthetic pipelines routinely disrupt.
  Architecturally, we pair a ResNet50 with temporal attention for visual encoding against a pretrained ResNet18 for mel spectrograms, closing the encoder capacity gap found in prior models. On FakeAVCeleb, AMDD achieves 99.7% balanced accuracy and 99.8% AUC with 95.9% attribution accuracy. Cross-dataset evaluation on DeepfakeTIMIT, DFDM, and LAV-DF confirms that real video detection generalizes robustly, while fake detection on unseen generators remains an open challenge that we analyze in depth.

AI.tldr: AMDD introduces attribution-guided multimodal deepfake detection with cross-modal forensic fingerprint consistency, improving attribution and detection robustness on seen data, but unseen generators remain challenging.
AI.motivation: Binary deepfake detectors risk exploiting dataset-specific artifacts and may not capture genuine generative traces. Attribution-guided learning enforces a geometry that encodes generator-specific forensic content, promoting generalizable detection.
AI.method: Joint detection and attribution via structured regularization; Cross-Modal Forensic Fingerprint Consistency (CMFFC) loss aligns generator-induced artifacts across visual (ResNet50 + temporal attention) and audio (mel-spectrogram ResNet18) streams; evaluated on FakeAVCeleb with high metrics; cross-dataset tests on DeepfakeTIMIT, DFDM, LAV-DF.
AI.result: On FakeAVCeleb: 99.7% balanced accuracy, 99.8% AUC, 95.9% attribution accuracy. Cross-dataset results show robust real-video detection but remaining challenges for fake detection from unseen generators.
AI.conclusion: Attribution-guided multimodal detection with cross-modal fingerprint consistency yields strong performance and interpretable generator attribution; generalization to unseen generators remains an open area for further work.

title: Semi-supervised learning with max-margin graph cuts
comment: Published at AISTATS 2010 (13th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics)
summary: This paper proposes a novel algorithm for semisupervised learning. This algorithm learns graph cuts that maximize the margin with respect to the labels induced by the harmonic function solution. We motivate the approach, compare it to existing work, and prove a bound on its generalization error. The quality of our solutions is evaluated on a synthetic problem and three UCI ML repository datasets. In most cases, we outperform manifold regularization of support vector machines, which is a state-of-the-art approach to semi-supervised max-margin learning.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised learning method that learns graph cuts to maximize margin with respect to harmonic-function-based labels, with a theoretical generalization bound and empirical gains over manifold regularized SVMs on synthetic and UCI data.
AI.motivation: Improve semi-supervised max-margin learning by integrating graph-based label propagation (harmonic function) with margin optimization, aiming for better generalization.
AI.method: Proposes a novel algorithm that optimizes graph cuts to maximize the margin against the labels produced by the harmonic function solution; compares to existing work and derives a generalization error bound.
AI.result: Derives a bound on generalization error; empirical evaluation on synthetic data and three UCI datasets; in most cases, outperforms manifold regularization of SVMs.
AI.conclusion: Offers a principled semi-supervised max-margin framework that blends harmonic-function labels with margin optimization and provides theoretical guarantees plus empirical improvements over a strong baseline.

title: Last-Layer-Centric Feature Recombination: Unleashing 3D Geometric Knowledge in DINOv3 for Monocular Depth Estimation
summary: Monocular depth estimation (MDE) is a fundamental yet inherently ill-posed task. Recent vision foundation models (VFMs), particularly DINO-based transformers, have significantly improved accuracy and generalization for dense prediction. Prior works generally follow a unified paradigm: sampling a fixed set of intermediate transformer layers at uniform intervals to build multi-scale features. This common practice implicitly assumes that geometric information is uniformly distributed across layers, which may underutilize the structural 3D cues encoded in VFMs. In this study, we present a systematic layer-wise analysis of DINOv3, revealing that 3D information is distributed non-uniformly: deeper layers exhibit stronger depth predictability and better capture inter-sample geometric variation. Motivated by this, we introduce a Last-Layer-Centric Feature Recombination (LFR) module to enhance geometric expressiveness. LFR treats the final layer as a geometric anchor and adaptively selects complementary intermediate layers according to a minimal-similarity criterion. Selected features are fused with the last-layer representation via compact linear adapters.Extensive experiments show that LFR module consistently improves MDE accuracy and achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our analysis sheds light on how geometric knowledge is organized within VFMs and offers an efficient strategy for unlocking their potential in dense 3D tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes DINOv3 representations for monocular depth estimation (MDE), discovers non-uniform distribution of 3D information across layers, and introduces a Last-Layer-Centric Feature Recombination (LFR) module that adaptively selects and fuses intermediate layers with the last layer to enhance geometric expressiveness, yielding state-of-the-art MDE performance.
AI.motivation: Uniformly sampling a fixed set of transformer layers for multi-scale features assumes equal geometric information across layers. The authors hypothesize and demonstrate that deeper layers contain stronger depth cues and inter-sample geometric variation, motivating a targeted, layer-wise fusion strategy.
AI.method: Perform a systematic layer-wise analysis of DINOv3 to map depth-related cues across layers. Propose LFR: treat the final layer as a geometric anchor and adaptively select complementary intermediate layers using a minimal-similarity criterion. Fuse selected features with the last-layer representation via compact linear adapters to form the final depth predictor.
AI.result: LFR consistently improves MDE accuracy and achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks. The analysis provides insight into how geometric knowledge is organized within VFMs and demonstrates an efficient approach to leverage it for dense 3D tasks.
AI.conclusion: Geometric information in vision foundation models is non-uniformly distributed across layers; focusing on the last layer and selectively incorporating informative intermediate layers via LFR offers an effective and efficient strategy to enhance dense 3D tasks like monocular depth estimation.

title: Random Cloud: Finding Minimal Neural Architectures Without Training
summary: I propose the \emph{Random Cloud} method, a training-free approach to neural architecture search that discovers minimal feedforward network topologies through stochastic exploration and progressive structural reduction. Unlike post-training pruning methods that require a full train-prune-retrain cycle, this method evaluates randomly initialized networks without backpropagation, progressively reduces their topology, and only trains the best minimal candidate at the end. I evaluate on 7 classification benchmarks against magnitude pruning and random pruning baselines. The Random Cloud matches or outperforms both baselines in 6 of 7 datasets, achieving statistically significant improvements on Sonar ($+4.9$pp accuracy, $p{=}0.017$ vs magnitude pruning) with 87\% parameter reduction. Crucially, the method is faster than both pruning baselines in 4 of 5 datasets (0.67--0.94$\times$ the cost of full training), since it avoids training the full-size network entirely.

AI.tldr: Introduces Random Cloud, a training-free NAS method that discovers minimal network topologies via random exploration and progressive reduction, training only the final smallest candidate; it frequently outperforms pruning baselines and is faster than full training on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce NAS and post-training pruning costs by avoiding training of large networks and eliminating iterative prune–retrain cycles.
AI.method: Stochastic exploration of randomly initialized networks without backpropagation, progressively reduces their topology, and only trains the best minimal candidate at the end.
AI.result: On 7 classification benchmarks, Random Cloud matches or surpasses magnitude and random pruning baselines in 6/7 datasets; notable improvement on Sonar (+4.9 percentage points accuracy, p=0.017 vs magnitude pruning) with 87% parameter reduction; faster than pruning baselines in 4/5 datasets (0.67–0.94× the cost of full training).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable, training-free NAS approach that can achieve competitive accuracy with substantial training cost savings; promising for scalable NAS, though broader validation and analysis of search dynamics are warranted.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Predictive Safety Filters for Probabilistic Neural Network Dynamics
summary: Predictive safety filters (PSFs) leverage model predictive control to enforce constraint satisfaction during deep reinforcement learning (RL) exploration, yet their reliance on first-principles models or Gaussian processes limits scalability and broader applicability. Meanwhile, model-based RL (MBRL) methods routinely employ probabilistic ensemble (PE) neural networks to capture complex, high-dimensional dynamics from data with minimal prior knowledge. However, existing attempts to integrate PEs into PSFs lack rigorous uncertainty quantification. We introduce the Uncertainty-Aware Predictive Safety Filter (UPSi), a PSF that provides rigorous safety predictions using PE dynamics models by formulating future outcomes as reachable sets. UPSi introduces an explicit certainty constraint that prevents model exploitation and integrates seamlessly into common MBRL frameworks. We evaluate UPSi within Dyna-style MBRL on standard safe RL benchmarks and report substantial improvements in exploration safety over prior neural network PSFs while maintaining performance on par with standard MBRL. UPSi bridges the gap between the scalability and generality of modern MBRL and the safety guarantees of predictive safety filters.

AI.tldr: An uncertainty-aware predictive safety filter (UPSi) uses probabilistic ensemble dynamics to provide rigorous safety predictions via reachable sets, adding a certainty constraint to prevent model exploitation, integrated into Dyna-style MBRL; it improves exploration safety over prior neural-network PSFs while preserving standard MBRL performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome scalability and applicability limits of traditional predictive safety filters that rely on first-principles models or Gaussian processes, and to achieve rigorous uncertainty quantification when integrating probabilistic ensemble dynamics into safety filtering for RL.
AI.method: Formulate future outcomes as reachable sets using probabilistic ensemble (PE) dynamics models; introduce an explicit certainty constraint to prevent exploitation of uncertain predictions; integrate UPSi into common MBRL workflows (e.g., Dyna-style planning/learning); evaluate on standard safe RL benchmarks.
AI.result: Substantial improvements in exploration safety compared with prior neural-network PSFs, while maintaining performance on par with standard MBRL methods.
AI.conclusion: UPSi combines the scalability and generality of modern MBRL with the safety guarantees of predictive safety filters, enabling uncertainty-aware, safe exploration in data-driven control.

title: A Multistage Extraction Pipeline for Long Scanned Financial Documents: An Empirical Study in Industrial KYC Workflows
summary: Structured information extraction from long, multilingual scanned financial documents is a core requirement in industrial KYC and compliance workflows. These documents are typically non machine readable, noisy, and visually heterogeneous. They usually span dozens of pages while containing only sparse task relevant information. Although recent vision-language models achieve strong benchmark performance, directly applying them end to end to full financial reports often leads to unreliable extraction under real world conditions. We present a multistage extraction framework that integrates image preprocessing, multilingual OCR, hybrid page-level retrieval, and compact VLM-based structured extraction. The design separates page localization from multimodal reasoning, enabling more accurate extraction from complex multipage documents. We evaluated the framework on 120 production KYC documents comprising about 3000 multilingual scanned pages. Across multiple OCR-VLM combinations, the proposed pipeline consistently outperforms direct PDF-to-VLM baselines, improving field-level accuracy by up to 31.9 percentage points. The best configuration, PaddleOCR with MiniCPM2.6, achieves 87.27 percent accuracy. Ablation studies show that page-level retrieval is the dominant factor in performance improvements, particularly for complex financial statements and non-English documents.

AI.tldr: A multistage pipeline improves structured extraction from long multilingual scanned financial documents by separating page localization from multimodal reasoning; achieves up to 87.27% accuracy with PaddleOCR+MiniCPM2.6, outperforming end-to-end PDF-to-VLM baselines, with page-level retrieval driving gains.
AI.motivation: Industrial KYC/compliance requires reliable extraction from noisy, non-machine-readable, multi-page, multilingual financial reports; end-to-end vision-language models struggle under real-world conditions.
AI.method: Image preprocessing; multilingual OCR; hybrid page-level retrieval to select relevant pages; compact vision-language model (VLM) for structured extraction; decouples localization from reasoning; evaluated on 120 production KYC documents (~3,000 pages).
AI.result: Outperforms direct PDF-to-VLM baselines; up to 31.9 percentage-point improvement in field-level accuracy; best configuration PaddleOCR with MiniCPM2.6 achieving 87.27% accuracy; ablations show page-level retrieval is the dominant factor, especially for complex statements and non-English documents.
AI.conclusion: Modular architecture with explicit page localization and retrieval substantially boosts field-level extraction accuracy for challenging multilingual, multipage financial documents, making it practical for real-world KYC workflows.

title: Unifying Sparse Attention with Hierarchical Memory for Scalable Long-Context LLM Serving
summary:
Long-context LLM serving is bottlenecked by the cost of attending over ever-growing KV caches. Dynamic sparse attention promises relief by accessing only a small, query-dependent subset of the KV state per decoding step and extending the KV storage to CPU memory. In practice, however, these algorithmic savings rarely translate into end-to-end system-level gains because sparse methods typically operate at different granularities and thus rely on ad hoc, per-algorithm implementations. At the same time, hierarchical KV storage introduces a new systems bottleneck: retrieving fine-grained, irregular KV subsets across the GPU-CPU boundary can easily erase the benefits of sparsity.
  We present SPIN, a sparse-attention-aware inference framework that co-designs the execution pipeline with hierarchical KV storage through three techniques: (1) a unified partition abstraction that maps different sparsity granularities onto a shared page-based KV substrate; (2) a locality-aware KV cache manager that dynamically sizes per-request HBM budgets and uses a GPU-friendly bucketed LRU policy to cut PCIe round-trips; and (3) a two-level hierarchical metadata layout sized to the active working set rather than the worst-case address space. Built on vLLM with three representative sparse attention algorithms, SPIN delivers 1.66-5.66x higher end-to-end throughput and 7-9x lower TTFT than vLLM, and reduces TPOT by up to 58% over the original sparse-attention implementations.

AI.tldr: Co-designed sparse-attention inference framework SPIN improves long-context LLM serving by co-optimizing execution with hierarchical KV storage, achieving substantial system-level gains.
AI.motivation: Dynamic sparse attention saves computation but often fails to yield end-to-end gains due to granularity mismatches and GPU-CPU data transfer bottlenecks in hierarchical KV stores.
AI.method: Three techniques: (1) unified partition abstraction mapping varying sparsity granularities onto a shared page-based KV substrate; (2) locality-aware KV cache manager that dynamically budgets HBM per request and uses a bucketed LRU policy to reduce PCIe round-trips; (3) a two-level hierarchical metadata layout tailored to the active working set. Implemented on vLLM with three representative sparse attention algorithms.
AI.result: SPIN delivers 1.66–5.66x higher end-to-end throughput and 7–9x lower TTFT than vLLM, and reduces TPOT by up to 58% over the original sparse-attention implementations.
AI.conclusion: Co-design of sparse attention with hierarchical KV storage enables meaningful system-level benefits in long-context LLM serving, suggesting a viable path for deploying practical sparse-attention in production.

title: Cross-Domain Transfer of Hyperspectral Foundation Models
summary: Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) semantic segmentation typically relies on in-domain training, but limited data availability often restricts model performance in real-world applications. Current approaches to leverage foundation models in proximal sensing use cross-modality techniques, bridging RGB and HSI to exploit vision foundation models. However, these methods either discard spectral information or introduce architectural complexity. We propose cross-domain transfer as an alternative, reusing HSI foundation models - originally trained in remote sensing - for proximal sensing applications. By eliminating the need to bridge modality gaps, our approach preserves spectral information while maintaining a simple architecture. Using the HS3-Bench benchmark, we systematically evaluate and compare conventional in-domain, in-modality training, cross-modality transfer and cross-domain transfer strategies. Our results demonstrate that cross-domain transfer achieves large performance improvements over in-domain, in-modality training, reduces the performance gap to cross-modality approaches and maintains strong performance in limited data settings. Thus, this work advances more effective HSI semantic segmentation in diverse applications.

AI.tldr: Cross-domain transfer of hyperspectral foundation models (trained in remote sensing) to proximal sensing preserves spectral information and avoids RGB–HSI bridging, yielding strong segmentation with limited data and narrowing the gap to cross-modality approaches.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity and limited generalization in HSI semantic segmentation for proximal sensing; existing RGB–HSI cross-modality methods lose spectral richness or add architectural complexity. A cross-domain transfer using HSI foundation models aims to leverage rich remote-sensing representations without modality gaps.
AI.method: Reuse HSI foundation models trained in remote sensing for proximal sensing tasks (cross-domain transfer). Avoid bridging modalities. Evaluate on HS3-Bench and compare against in-domain training, in-modality training, and cross-modality transfer.
AI.result: Cross-domain transfer delivers large performance gains over in-domain training, reduces the gap to cross-modality approaches, and remains strong under limited data regimes.
AI.conclusion: Cross-domain transfer is an effective, simpler strategy for HSI semantic segmentation in proximal sensing, preserving spectral information and expanding applicability where labeled data are scarce.

title: MemOVCD: Training-Free Open-Vocabulary Change Detection via Cross-Temporal Memory Reasoning and Global-Local Adaptive Rectification
summary: Open-vocabulary change detection aims to identify semantic changes in bi-temporal remote sensing images without predefined categories. Recent methods combine foundation models such as SAM, DINO and CLIP, but typically process each timestamp independently or interact only at the final comparison stage. Such paradigms suffer from insufficient temporal coupling during semantic reasoning, which limits their ability to distinguish genuine semantic changes from non-semantic appearance discrepancies. In addition, patch-dominant inference on high-resolution images often weakens global semantic continuity and produces fragmented change regions. To address these issues, we propose MemOVCD, a training-free open-vocabulary change detection framework based on cross-temporal memory reasoning and global-local adaptive rectification. Specifically, we reformulate bi-temporal change detection as a two-frame tracking problem and introduce weighted bidirectional propagation to aggregate semantic evidence from both temporal directions. To stabilize memory propagation across large temporal gaps, we construct histogram-aligned transition frames to smooth abrupt appearance changes. Moreover, a global-local adaptive rectification strategy adaptively fuses local and global-view predictions, improving spatial consistency while preserving fine-grained details. Experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate that MemOVCD achieves favorable performance on two change detection tasks, validating its effectiveness and generalization under diverse open-vocabulary settings.

AI.tldr: MemOVCD reframes bi-temporal change detection as two-frame tracking, leveraging cross-temporal memory propagation and histogram-aligned transitions, with global-local adaptive rectification, to enable training-free open-vocabulary change detection and improve semantic coupling and spatial consistency.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary CD suffers from weak temporal coupling when timestamps are processed independently and from patch-dominant inference causing fragmented changes. A cross-temporal, memory-based approach aims to robustly reason about semantic changes across time without predefined categories.
AI.method: Recast bi-temporal CD as a two-frame tracking problem. Use weighted bidirectional memory propagation to aggregate semantic evidence from both temporal directions. Introduce histogram-aligned transition frames to smooth abrupt appearance changes over large temporal gaps. Apply a global-local adaptive rectification to fuse local and global-view predictions, enhancing spatial consistency while preserving details.
AI.result: Evaluations on five benchmarks show MemOVCD achieves favorable performance on two change-detection tasks, demonstrating effectiveness and generalization under diverse open-vocabulary settings.
AI.conclusion: A training-free framework that couples semantics across time with memory-based propagation and adaptive fusion yields improved open-vocabulary CD, offering robust generalization and improved spatial coherence across varied datasets.

title: Language Diffusion Models are Associative Memories Capable of Retrieving Unseen Data
comment: Also see arXiv:2505.21777 for a related work
summary: When do language diffusion models memorize their training data, and how to quantitatively assess their true generative regime? We address these questions by showing that Uniform-based Discrete Diffusion Models (UDDMs) fundamentally behave as Associative Memories (AMs) $\textit{with emergent creative capabilities}$. The core idea of an AM is to reliably recover stored data points as $\textit{memories}$ by establishing distinct basins of attraction around them. Historically, models like Hopfield networks use an explicit energy function to guarantee these stable attractors. We broaden this perspective by leveraging the observation that energy is not strictly necessary, as basins of attraction can also be formed via conditional likelihood maximization. By evaluating token recovery of $\textit{training}$ and $\textit{test}$ examples, we identify in UDDMs a sharp memorization-to-generalization transition governed by the size of the training dataset: as it increases, basins around training examples shrink and basins around unseen test examples expand, until both later converge to the same level. Crucially, we can detect this transition using only the conditional entropy of predicted token sequences: memorization is characterized by vanishing conditional entropy, while in the generalization regime the conditional entropy of most tokens remains finite. Thus, conditional entropy offers a practical probe for the memorization-to-generalization transition in deployed models.

AI.tldr: UDDMs function as associative memories with emergent creativity; memorization-to-generalization is governed by training set size; conditional entropy of token predictions serves as a practical diagnostic of regime.
AI.motivation: Address when diffusion models memorize training data and how to quantify their true generative regime; unify associative memory concepts with diffusion models via basins of attraction formed by conditional likelihood.
AI.method: Assess token recovery for training vs. test examples; analyze basins of attraction via conditional likelihood maximization; track memorization-generalization transition as dataset size grows; use conditional entropy of predicted tokens as a diagnostic.
AI.result: Observes a sharp memorization-to-generalization transition: training basins shrink while test basins expand, converging to a common level; memorization corresponds to vanishing conditional entropy, generalization to finite entropy for most tokens.
AI.conclusion: Conditional entropy provides a practical probe of memorization-to-generalization in deployed models; energy is not strictly necessary to form attractor basins; UDDMs can exhibit emergent creative capabilities.

title: Featurising Pixels from Dynamic 3D Scenes with Linear In-Context Learners
comment: To appear at CVPR 2026 (oral). Project website: https://lila-pixels.github.io
summary: One of the most exciting applications of vision models involve pixel-level reasoning. Despite the abundance of vision foundation models, we still lack representations that effectively embed spatio-temporal properties of visual scenes at the pixel level. Existing frameworks either train on image-based pretext tasks, which do not account for dynamic elements, or on video sequences for action-level reasoning, which does not scale to dense pixel-level prediction. We present a framework that learns pixel-accurate feature descriptors from videos, LILA. The core element of our training framework is linear in-context learning. LILA leverages spatio-temporal cue maps -- depth and motion -- estimated with off-the-shelf networks. Despite the noisy nature of those cues, LILA trains effectively on uncurated video datasets, embedding semantic and geometric properties in a temporally consistent manner. We demonstrate compelling empirical benefits of the learned representation across a diverse suite of vision tasks: video object segmentation, surface normal estimation and semantic segmentation.

AI.tldr: LILA trains pixel-accurate descriptors from uncurated video using linear in-context learning with depth and motion cue maps, enabling dense pixel-level reasoning across tasks.
AI.motivation: Need scalable, pixel-level representations that capture spatio-temporal information; existing image-based pretext or action-focused video methods fail to provide dense, temporally coherent, pixel-wise descriptors.
AI.method: A framework (LILA) employing linear in-context learning on videos, using noisy depth and motion cue maps estimated by off-the-shelf networks to produce pixel-accurate feature descriptors that remain temporally consistent, trained on uncurated video data.
AI.result: Demonstrates improvements across tasks requiring dense predictions: video object segmentation, surface normal estimation, and semantic segmentation.
AI.conclusion: LILA shows that pixel-accurate, temporally coherent representations can be learned from uncurated videos by leveraging simple, robust cues within a linear in-context learning framework, benefiting multiple dense prediction tasks.

title: ViCrop-Det: Spatial Attention Entropy Guided Cropping for Training-Free Small-Object Detection
summary: Transformer-based architectures have established a dominant paradigm in global semantic perception; however, they remain fundamentally constrained by the profound spatial heterogeneity inherent in natural images. Specifically, the imposition of a uniform global receptive field across regions of varying information density inevitably leads to local feature degradation, particularly in dense conflict zones populated by microscopic targets. To address this mechanistic limitation, we propose ViCrop-Det, a training-free inference framework that introduces adaptive spatial trust region shrinkage. Inspired by the use of attention entropy in anomaly segmentation, ViCrop-Det leverages the detection decoder's cross-attention distribution as an endogenous probe. By utilizing Spatial Attention Entropy (SAE) to heuristically evaluate local spatial ambiguity, the framework executes dynamic spatial routing, allocating a fixed computational budget exclusively to regions exhibiting both high target saliency and high cognitive uncertainty. By shrinking the spatial trust region and injecting high-frequency localized observations, ViCrop-Det actively resolves spatial ambiguity and recovers fine-grained features without requiring architectural modifications. Extensive evaluations on VisDrone and DOTA-v1.5 demonstrate that ViCrop-Det yields competitive performance enhancements, consistently adding +1-3 mAP@50 to RT-DETR-R50 and Deformable DETR with a marginal 20-23\% latency overhead. On MS COCO, $AP_{S}$ improves while $AP_{M}/AP_{L}$ remains stable, indicating precise fine-scale refinement without compromising the global spatial prior. Under compute-matched settings, our adaptive routing strategy comprehensively surpasses uniform slicing baselines, achieving a highly optimized accuracy-speed trade-off.

AI.tldr: A training-free adaptive inference framework (ViCrop-Det) uses Spatial Attention Entropy to identify high-saliency, high-uncertainty regions and allocates computing budget via dynamic spatial routing by shrinking the spatial trust region, improving small-object refinement with modest latency overhead.
AI.motivation: Transformers struggle with spatial heterogeneity in natural scenes; a uniform global receptive field degrades dense, small targets. Existing attention-based anomaly cues inspire a lightweight, data-free mechanism to focus computation where needed without changing architecture.
AI.method: During inference, ViCrop-Det computes Spatial Attention Entropy (SAE) from the cross-attention distribution of the detection decoder to estimate local spatial ambiguity. It then perform adaptive spatial routing by shrinking the spatial trust region and injecting high-frequency localized observations only in regions with both high target saliency and high cognitive uncertainty, all without architectural changes or extra training.
AI.result: On VisDrone and DOTA-v1.5, ViCrop-Det adds about +1–3 mAP@50 to RT-DETR-R50 and Deformable DETR with 20–23% latency overhead. On COCO, AP_S improves while AP_M/AP_L stay stable. Under compute-matched settings, it surpasses uniform slicing baselines and achieves favorable accuracy-speed trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: ViCrop-Det demonstrates that a training-free, entropy-guided adaptive routing approach can recover fine-grained features and mitigate spatial ambiguity in dense regions while preserving global priors, offering a practical improvement over uniform inference schemes.

title: KAYRA: A Microservice Architecture for AI-Assisted Karyotyping with Cloud and On-Premise Deployment
summary: We present KAYRA, an end-to-end karyotyping system that operates inside the operational constraints of a clinical cytogenetic laboratory. KAYRA is architected as a containerized microservice pipeline whose ML stack combines an EfficientNet-B5 + U-Net semantic segmenter, a Mask R-CNN (ResNet-50 + FPN) instance detector, and a ResNet-18 classifier, orchestrated through a cascaded ROI-narrowing strategy that focuses each downstream model on the chromosome-bearing region. The same container images are deployed both as a cloud service and as an on-premise installation, supporting clinical environments where patient-data egress is not permitted as well as those where it is. A pilot clinical evaluation against two commercial reference karyotyping systems on 459 chromosomes from 10 metaphase spreads shows segmentation accuracy of 98.91 % (vs. 78.21 % / 40.52 %), classification accuracy of 89.1 % (vs. 86.9 % / 54.5 %), and rotation accuracy of 89.76 % (vs. 94.55 % / 78.43 %). KAYRA improves over the older density-thresholding reference on all three axes (p < 0.0001 for segmentation and classification by Fisher's exact test on chromosome-level counts), and on segmentation also against the modern AI- supported reference (p < 0.0001); on classification the difference vs. the modern AI reference is not statistically significant at the present test-set size (p = 0.34). The system reaches TRL 6 maturity and integrates the human-in-the-loop expert-review workflow that diagnostic cytogenetic practice requires. The thesis of this paper is that a multi-model cytogenetic AI service can be packaged as a microservice architecture supporting flexible deployment - cloud-hosted or on-premise - while delivering strong empirical performance on a pilot clinical evaluation.

AI.tldr: KAYRA is a containerized, end-to-end karyotyping AI pipeline combining segmentation, instance detection, and classification with a cascaded ROI-narrowing strategy, deployable in cloud or on-premise, achieving strong segmentation and competitive classification against reference methods in a pilot clinical evaluation.
AI.motivation: To automate clinical cytogenetics with high accuracy while complying with data governance constraints, enabling flexible deployment in real-world lab environments.
AI.method: A multi-model cascade: EfficientNet-B5 + U-Net for segmentation; Mask R-CNN (ResNet-50 + FPN) for chromosome instance detection; ResNet-18 classifier; ROI narrowing to focus downstream models on chromosome-bearing regions; containerized microservices deployed in cloud and on-premise; human-in-the-loop workflow integration.
AI.result: Pilot on 459 chromosomes from 10 metaphase spreads. Segmentation 98.91% vs 78.21% and 40.52%; classification 89.1% vs 86.9% and 54.5%; rotation 89.76% vs 94.55% and 78.43%. Significant improvements over density-thresholding (p<0.0001 for segmentation and classification) and segmentation vs modern AI (p<0.0001); classification vs modern AI not significantly different (p=0.34). TRL 6; supports lab diagnostic workflow with human review.
AI.conclusion: KAYRA demonstrates that a multi-model cytogenetic AI service packaged as a microservice architecture can deliver strong empirical performance and deployment flexibility (cloud or on-prem), with a credible path to clinical adoption given pilot results and human-in-the-loop integration.

title: Robust Alignment: Harmonizing Clean Accuracy and Adversarial Robustness in Adversarial Training
comment: 2026 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition - Findings Track (CVPR'26 Findings)
summary: Adversarial Training (AT) is one of the most effective methods for developing robust deep neural networks (DNNs). However, AT faces a trade-off problem between clean accuracy and adversarial robustness. In this work, we reveal a surprising phenomenon for the first time: Varying input perturbation intensities for training samples near decision boundaries in AT have minimal impact on model robustness. This finding directly exposes the inconsistency between accuracy and robustness score fluctuations, leading us to identify the misalignment between input and latent spaces as a critical driver of the robustness-accuracy trade-off. To mitigate this misalignment for harmonizing accuracy and robustness, we define Robust Alignment as a new AT target, encouraging the model perception to change with input perturbations provided the final label prediction remains unchanged, which can be achieved via two novel ideas. First, we suggest a reduced and fixed perturbation intensity for those boundary samples, which facilitates the model to utilize the perturbations as learnable patterns, instead of noises that complicate decision boundaries meaninglessly. Second, we propose a Domain Interpolation Consistency Adversarial Regularization (DICAR), based on rigorous theoretical derivations, which explicitly introduces semantic alignment between input and latent spaces into AT. Based on these two ideas, we end up with a new Robust Alignment Adversarial Training (RAAT) method, effectively harmonizing accuracy and robustness. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet with ResNet-18, PreActResNet-18, and WideResNet-28-10 demonstrate the effectiveness of RAAT in improving the trade-off beyond four common baselines and a total of 14 related state-of-the-art (SOTA) works.

AI.tldr: RAAT harmonizes accuracy and robustness in adversarial training by aligning input and latent spaces via fixed perturbations on boundary samples and DICAR regularization, outperforming multiple baselines and SOTA.
AI.motivation: The paper reveals a misalignment between input perturbations near decision boundaries and latent representations, causing instability in robustness vs. accuracy. This misalignment drives the robustness–accuracy trade-off in standard adversarial training.
AI.method: Define Robust Alignment as the AT target. Two components: (1) apply reduced/fixed perturbation intensity for boundary samples so perturbations become learnable patterns rather than noise; (2) Domain Interpolation Consistency Adversarial Regularization (DICAR) to inject semantic alignment between input and latent spaces. Combine these into Robust Alignment Adversarial Training (RAAT).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet using ResNet-18, PreActResNet-18, and WideResNet-28-10 shows RAAT improves the accuracy–robustness trade-off beyond four baselines and 14 state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: RAAT effectively harmonizes accuracy and robustness by aligning input and latent representations under adversarial perturbations, with strong cross-dataset and cross-architecture performance.

title: Multiple Additive Neural Networks for Structured and Unstructured Data
comment: Accepted author manuscript; page layout differs from the published Springer version
summary: This paper extends and explains the Multiple Additive Neural Networks (MANN) methodology, an enhancement to the traditional Gradient Boosting framework, utilizing nearly shallow neural networks instead of decision trees as base learners. This innovative approach leverages neural network architectures, notably Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Capsule Neural Networks, to extend its application to both structured data and unstructured data such as images and audio. For structured data the advantages of capsule neural networks as feature extractors are used and combined with MANN as a classifier. MANN's unique architecture promotes continuous learning and integrates advanced heuristics to combat overfitting, ensuring robustness and reducing sensitivity to hyperparameter settings like learning rate and iterations. Our empirical studies reveal that MANN surpasses traditional methods such as Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB) in accuracy across well-known datasets. This research demonstrates MANN's superior precision and generalizability, making it a versatile tool for diverse data types and complex learning environments.

AI.tldr: MANN extends gradient boosting by using shallow neural networks as base learners (instead of trees), enabling CNNs and Capsule Nets for both structured and unstructured data, with continuous learning and anti-overfitting heuristics; reported to outperform XGBoost in accuracy on standard datasets.
AI.motivation: Improve predictive accuracy and robustness beyond traditional tree-based boosting, leveraging neural representations and feature extraction (especially capsules) to handle diverse data types, while reducing sensitivity to hyperparameters.
AI.method: Incorporates nearly shallow neural networks as boosting base learners within a boosting-like framework; employs CNNs and capsule networks as feature extractors; uses capsule-based features for structured data; includes mechanisms for continuous learning and overfitting control.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations claim MANN achieves higher accuracy than XGBoost on well-known datasets, indicating better precision and generalizability across data types.
AI.conclusion: MANN is a versatile, robust boosting framework that integrates neural networks to extend applicability to structured and unstructured data with improved performance and reduced hyperparameter sensitivity.

title: Delta Score Matters! Spatial Adaptive Multi Guidance in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in synthesizing complex static and temporal visuals, a breakthrough largely driven by Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG). However, despite its pivotal role in aligning generated content with textual prompts, standard CFG relies on a globally uniform scalar. This homogeneous amplification traps models in a well-documented "detail-artifact dilemma": low guidance scales fail to inject intricate semantics, while high scales inevitably cause structural degradation, color over-saturation, and temporal inconsistencies in videos. In this paper, we expose the physical root of this flaw through the lens of differential geometry. By analyzing Tweedie's Formula, we reveal that CFG intrinsically performs a tangential linear extrapolation. Because the natural data manifold is highly curved, this uniform linear step introduces a severe orthogonal deviation. To keep the generation trajectory safely bounded, we formulate a theoretical upper bound for spatial and adaptive guidance. Based on these geometric insights, we propose Spatial Adaptive Multi Guidance (SAMG), a training-free and virtually zero-cost sampling algorithm. SAMG dynamically computes point-wise conditional guidance energy, applying a conservative minimum scale to high-energy boundary regions to preserve delicate micro-textures, while deploying an aggressive maximum scale in low-energy regions to maximize semantic injection. Extensive experiments across diverse image (SD 1.5, SDXL, SD3.5 Medium) and video (CogVideoX, ModelScope) architectures demonstrate that SAMG effectively resolves the detail-artifact dilemma, achieving superior semantic alignment, structural integrity, and temporal smoothness without any computational overhead.

AI.tldr: SAMG introduces spatially adaptive, training-free guidance to replace a globally uniform CFG in diffusion models, addressing the detail-artifact dilemma in images and videos by bounding and modulating guidance energy based on local geometry.
AI.motivation: Uniform Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) with a global scalar causes misalignment between semantic strength and local structure on curved data manifolds, producing degraded textures, colors, and temporal artifacts in videos. A geometry-informed bound on guidance is needed to safely inject semantics without breaking structure or continuity.
AI.method: Geometric analysis via Tweedie’s formula reveals CFG acts as tangential linear extrapolation on a curved data manifold. The authors derive an upper bound for spatial/adaptive guidance and propose Spatial Adaptive Multi Guidance (SAMG): at sampling time, compute point-wise guidance energy and apply conservative minimum scales in high-energy boundary regions to preserve micro-textures and aggressive maximum scales in low-energy regions to maximize semantic injection. The approach is training-free and incurs virtually zero cost.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on image models (SD 1.5, SDXL, SD3.5 Medium) and video models (CogVideoX, ModelScope) show SAMG resolves the detail-artifact dilemma, achieving better semantic alignment, structural fidelity, and temporal smoothness without additional computation.
AI.conclusion: Geometric, sampling-time adaptive guidance effectively balances semantic injection with preservation of fine texture and temporal coherence; SAMG is a practical, low-cost solution that generalizes across image and video diffusion systems.

title: Causal Learning with Neural Assemblies
summary: Can Neural Assemblies -- groups of neurons that fire together and strengthen through co-activation -- learn the direction of causal influence between variables? While established as a computationally general substrate for classification, parsing, and planning, neural assemblies have not yet been shown to internalize causal directionality. We demonstrate that the inherent operations of neural assemblies -- projection, local plasticity control, and sparse winner selection -- are sufficient for directional learning. We introduce DIRECT (DIRectional Edge Coupling/Training), a mechanism that co-activates source and target assemblies under an adaptive gain schedule to internalize directed relations. Unlike backpropagation-based methods, DIRECT relies solely on local plasticity, making the resulting causal claims auditable at the mechanism level. Our findings are verified through a dual-readout validation strategy: (i) synaptic-strength asymmetry, measuring the emergent weight gap between forward and reverse links, and (ii) functional propagation overlap, quantifying the reliability of directional signal flow. Across multiple domains, the framework achieves perfect structural recovery under a supervised, known-structure setting. These results establish neural assemblies as an auditable bridge between biologically plausible dynamics and formal causal models, offering an "explainable by design" framework where causal claims are traceable to specific neural winners and synaptic asymmetries.

AI.tldr: Neural assemblies can learn causal directionality using a local-plasticity, winner-take-all mechanism (DIRECT) with co-activation of source and target assemblies, enabling auditable, non-backpropagation directional learning and perfect structural recovery under supervised settings.
AI.motivation: To determine whether biologically plausible neural assemblies can internalize the direction of causal influence between variables and provide auditable causal claims grounded in neural mechanisms, addressing a gap beyond classification and planning.
AI.method: Introduce DIRECT: co-activate source and target assemblies under an adaptive gain schedule, leveraging projection, local plasticity, and sparse winner selection. Validation uses dual-readout: (i) synaptic-strength asymmetry (forward vs. reverse weight gap), and (ii) functional propagation overlap (reliability of directional signal flow). Evaluate across multiple domains with supervised-known structure.
AI.result: DIRECT enables robust directional learning with emergent forward/backward asymmetry and reliable directional propagation, achieving perfect structural recovery in supervised-known-structure settings; synaptic and functional readouts confirm directionality.
AI.conclusion: Neural assemblies can serve as an auditable bridge between biologically plausible dynamics and formal causal models, delivering explainable-by-design causal claims traceable to winning neurons and synaptic asymmetries.

title: MTCurv: Deep learning for direct microtubule curvature mapping in noisy fluorescence microscopy images
comment: Accepted for presentation at the International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) 2026
summary: Accurate quantification of the geometry of curvilinear biological structures is essential for understanding cellular mechanics and disease-related morphological alterations. Microtubule curvature is a key descriptor of filament rigidity and mechanical perturbations. However, reliable curvature extraction from fluorescence microscopy images remains challenging due to noise, low contrast, and partial filament visibility. Existing approaches rely on segmentation pipelines with pre or post-processing, which are highly sensitive to segmentation errors and often fail under adverse imaging conditions. In this work, we propose MTCurv, a deep learning framework for direct, segmenta-tion-free regression of microtubule curvature maps from noisy microscopy images. Leveraging a synthetic dataset with pixel-wise curvature annotations, we reformulated curvature estimation as a regression problem and adapted an attention-based residual U-Net. To reduce hallucinations and enforce spatial coherence, we introduced a gradient-aware loss combining Mean Squared Error with a gradient consistency term. Beyond model and loss design, we evaluated commonly used regression and image quality metrics, revealing that many perceptual and blind metrics are poorly suited for curvature estimation. Correlation-based metrics, particularly Spearman correlation, emerged as more reliable indicators of curvature prediction quality. Experiments on two datasets of increasing difficulty demonstrated that MTCurv accurately recovers local microtubule curvatures, even in the presence of background fluorescence. Ablation studies highlighted the contribution of both residual encoding and attention-based decoding. Overall, this work provides a practical tool for filament curvature analysis and methodological insights for geometry-aware regression in biomedical imaging. Datasets and code are made available.

AI.tldr: Segmentation-free regression of microtubule curvature using an attention-based residual U-Net with a gradient-aware loss, robust to noisy fluorescence and background, outperforming segmentation-based pipelines.
AI.motivation: Accurate microtubule curvature is essential for understanding filament mechanics, but noisy imaging and partial visibility hinder curvature extraction; segmentation-based methods are fragile to errors.
AI.method: Propose MTCurv, an attention-based residual U-Net trained on synthetic data with pixel-wise curvature annotations to perform direct regression of curvature maps. Introduces a gradient-aware loss combining MSE with a gradient-consistency term to enforce spatial coherence and reduce hallucinations. Evaluates multiple regression and image-quality metrics, finding correlation-based metrics (notably Spearman) to be more reliable for curvature estimation.
AI.result: MTCurv accurately recovers local microtubule curvatures even with background fluorescence. Ablation shows the importance of residual encoding and attention in decoding. Perceptual metrics are less suitable for curvature; correlation-based metrics correlate better with true curvature. Datasets and code are released.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, segmentation-free approach for filament curvature analysis and offers methodological insights for geometry-aware regression in biomedical imaging; datasets and code are made available.

title: On the Learning Curves of Revenue Maximization
comment: To appear in the 58th ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC 2026)
summary:
Learning curves are a fundamental primitive in supervised learning, describing how an algorithm's performance improves with more data and providing a quantitative measure of its generalization ability. Formally, a learning curve plots the decay of an algorithm's error for a fixed underlying distribution as a function of the number of training samples. Prior work on revenue-maximizing learning algorithms, starting with the seminal work of Cole and Roughgarden [STOC, 2014], adopts a distribution-free perspective, which parallels the PAC learning framework in learning theory. This approach evaluates performance against the hardest possible sequence of valuation distributions, one for each sample size, effectively defining the upper envelope of learning curves over all possible distributions, thus leading to error bounds that do not capture the shape of the learning curves.
  In this work we initiate the study of learning curves for revenue maximization and provide a near-complete characterization of their rate of decay in the basic setting of a single item and a single buyer. In the absence of any restriction on the valuation distribution, we show that there exists a Bayes-consistent algorithm, meaning that its learning curve converges to zero for any arbitrary valuation distribution as the number of samples $n \to \infty$. However, this convergence must be arbitrarily slow, even if the optimal revenue is finite. In contrast, if the optimal revenue is achieved by a finite price, then the optimal rate of decay is roughly $1/\sqrt{n}$. Finally, for distributions supported on discrete sets of values, we show that learning curves decay almost exponentially fast, a rate unattainable under the PAC framework.

AI.tldr: This work initiates the study of learning curves for revenue maximization and provides a near-complete rate characterization in the single-item, single-buyer setting. It shows Bayes-consistency with arbitrarily slow convergence without distribution restrictions, a 1/√n rate when the optimum revenue is achieved by a finite price, and almost-exponential decay for discrete-valued valuation distributions, highlighting rates beyond the PAC upper envelope.
AI.motivation: To move beyond worst-case, distribution-free bounds (which consider the hardest sequence of distributions) and understand the actual decay shapes of learning curves for revenue-maximizing mechanisms, clarifying how the revenue objective interacts with sampling.
AI.method: The authors analyze the learning-curve decay rates for revenue maximization in the basic single-item, single-buyer setting under different distributional assumptions: unrestricted valuations, finite-price optimal revenue, and discrete-valued valuation supports, deriving (i) existence of a Bayes-consistent algorithm with zero asymptotic error, (ii) a 1/√n rate when a finite price suffices for optimal revenue, and (iii) near-exponential decay for discrete supports.
AI.result: Key findings are: (i) Bayes-consistent learning with error converging to zero for any distribution, but potentially arbitrarily slow; (ii) when optimal revenue is achieved at a finite price, the optimal decay rate is ~1/√n; (iii) with discrete-valued valuations, the decay is nearly exponential, a rate unattainable under PAC bounds.
AI.conclusion: The paper shows that revenue-learning curves can exhibit significantly faster or slower decay than PAC-based worst-case analyses, depending on valuation structure, and provides a near-complete rate characterization in the canonical setting, guiding future work on more complex auction settings.

title: GIFGuard: Proactive Forensics against Deepfakes in Facial GIFs via Spatiotemporal Watermarking
summary: The rapid evolution of deepfake technology poses an unprecedented threat to the authenticity of Graphics Interchange Format (GIF) imagery, which serves as a representative of short-loop temporal media in social networks. However, existing proactive forensics works are designed for static images, which limits their applicability to animated GIFs. To bridge this gap, we propose GIFGuard, the first spatiotemporal watermarking framework tailored for deepfake proactive forensics in GIFs. In the embedding stage, we propose the Spatiotemporal Adaptive Residual Encoder (STARE) to ensure robustness against high-level semantic tampering. It employs a 3D convolutional backbone with adaptive channel recalibration to capture globally coherent temporal dependencies. In the extraction stage, we design the Deep Integrity Restoration Decoder (DIRD). It utilizes a spatiotemporal hourglass architecture equipped with 3D attention to restore latent features, allowing for the accurate extraction of watermark signals even under severe facial manipulation. Furthermore, we construct GIFfaces, the first large-scale benchmark dataset curated for GIF proactive forensics to facilitate research in this domain. Extensive results show that GIFGuard achieves high-fidelity visual quality and remarkable robustness performance against deepfakes. Related code and dataset will be released.

AI.tldr: GIFGuard introduces a first spatiotemporal watermarking framework for deepfake proactive forensics in animated GIFs, featuring STARE embedding and DIRD extraction, plus GIFfaces dataset; demonstrates high fidelity and robustness against deepfakes.
AI.motivation: Deepfake threats extend to animated GIFs; static-image forensic methods fail to protect short-loop temporal media. A spatiotemporal watermarking approach is needed to ensure integrity of GIFs used on social networks.
AI.method: Embedding stage uses Spatiotemporal Adaptive Residual Encoder (STARE) with a 3D CNN backbone and adaptive channel recalibration to capture global temporal dependencies. Extraction stage uses Deep Integrity Restoration Decoder (DIRD), a spatiotemporal hourglass with 3D attention to restore latent features and reliably extract watermark signals under severe manipulation. A new GIFfaces benchmark dataset for GIF proactive forensics is introduced.
AI.result: Demonstrates high visual quality and strong robustness of the watermark against deepfake manipulation; watermark can be extracted reliably even after significant facial manipulation.
AI.conclusion: GIFGuard represents a novel, end-to-end spatiotemporal watermarking framework for GIFs, with a dedicated dataset to advance GIF proactive forensics; code and dataset are planned for release.

title: A Note on How to Remove the $\ln\ln T$ Term from the Squint Bound
summary: In Orabona and Pál [2016], we introduced the shifted KT potentials, to remove the $\ln \ln T$ factor in the parameter-free learning with expert bound. In this short technical note, I show that this is equivalent to changing the prior in the Krichevsky--Trofimov algorithm. Then, I show how to use the same idea to remove the $\ln \ln T$ factor in the data-independent bound for the Squint algorithm.

AI.tldr: Shifting KT potentials is equivalent to a prior change in the Krichevsky–Trofimov algorithm, and this idea can remove the log-log T factor from both parameter-free regret bounds with expert advice and from the data-independent bound of the Squint algorithm.
AI.motivation: Remove the log-log T (ln ln T) factor in key regret bounds for parameter-free and data-independent settings, and unify the analysis under a prior-shift view of potential-based algorithms.
AI.method: Introduce shifted KT potentials; demonstrate that the shift is equivalent to reweighting the KT prior. Apply the same idea to the Squint bound to eliminate the ln ln T term.
AI.result: Establishes an equivalence between potential shifts and prior changes; removes the ln ln T factor in the parameter-free expert-bound setting and in Squint’s data-independent bound, yielding tighter regret guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Shifting potentials provides a unifying, simple mechanism to cancel log-log factors via prior selection, with potential applicability to other algorithms beyond KT/Squint.

title: 3D-LENS: A 3D Lifting-based Elevated Novel-view Synthesis method for Single-View Aerial-Ground Re-Identification
summary: Aerial-Ground Re-Identification (AG-ReID) is constrained by the viewpoint-domain gap, as drastic viewpoint disparities occlude or distort discriminative features, making cross-viewpoint image retrieval challenging. While existing methods rely on paired cross-view annotations, real-world deployments, such as wilderness search-and-rescue (SAR), often lack target-domain data, requiring retrieval from ground-level references alone. To our knowledge, we are the first to address this challenge by formalizing the Single-View AG-ReID (SV AG-ReID) setting, where models trained on a single real viewpoint must generalize to an unseen viewpoint. We propose 3D Lifting-based Elevated Novel-view Synthesis (3D-LENS), a unified framework combining geometrically-consistent novel view synthesis that leverages large-scale 3D mesh reconstruction, with a robust representation learning scheme to mitigate synthetic-to-real bias. Unlike 2D generative baselines that suffer from geometric inconsistencies or prior 3D methods that are restricted to class-specific templates, our approach ensures view-consistent synthesis across diverse categories without predefined templates that fail to capture fine-grained details, such as carried objects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on SV AG-ReID scenarios. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/TurtleSmoke/3D-LENS.

AI.tldr: Single-view AG-ReID under significant viewpoint gaps; 3D-LENS synthesizes view-consistent novel views via 3D mesh lifting and robust representation learning to generalize to unseen viewpoints, achieving state-of-the-art results without requiring templates.
AI.motivation: AG-ReID suffers from a viewpoint-domain gap where drastic viewpoint changes degrade discriminative features; real-world deployments (e.g., SAR) often lack target-domain data for cross-view matching. There is a need to generalize from a single real viewpoint to unseen views without paired cross-view annotations or target-domain data.
AI.method: 3D Lifting-based Elevated Novel-view Synthesis (3D-LENS) combines geometrically-consistent novel view synthesis via large-scale 3D mesh reconstruction with a robust representation learning strategy to mitigate synthetic-to-real bias. Unlike 2D generative baselines (which have geometric inconsistencies) and template-restricted 3D methods, it provides view-consistent synthesis across diverse categories without predefined templates, capable of capturing fine-grained details (e.g., carried objects).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance on the SV AG-ReID setting; code and data will be released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: The proposed 3D-LENS offers a unified framework that jointly addresses view-consistent novel-view synthesis and robust representation learning, enabling effective cross-viewpoint re-identification from a single real-view training regime.

title: Hyper Input Convex Neural Networks for Shape Constrained Learning and Optimal Transport
comment: 65 pages, 13 figures, the first two authors contributed equally
summary: We introduce Hyper Input Convex Neural Networks (HyCNNs), a novel neural network architecture designed for learning convex functions. HyCNNs combine the principles of Maxout networks with input convex neural networks (ICNNs) to create a neural network that is always convex in the input, theoretically capable of leveraging depth, and performs reliable when trained at scale compared to ICNNs. Concretely, we prove that HyCNNs require exponentially fewer parameters than ICNNs to approximate quadratic functions up to a given precision. Throughout a series of synthetic experiments, we demonstrate that HyCNNs outperform existing ICNNs and MLPs in terms of predictive performance for convex regression and interpolation tasks. We further apply HyCNNs to learn high-dimensional optimal transport maps for synthetic examples and for single-cell RNA sequencing data, where they oftentimes outperform ICNN-based neural optimal transport methods and other baselines across a wide range of settings.

AI.tldr: HyCNNs introduce a convex neural network architecture that fuses Maxout networks with input-convex neural networks (ICNNs) to ensure convexity in the input, enabling depth benefits and scalable training. They achieve exponential parameter efficiency over ICNNs for approximating quadratics and show superior performance on convex regression/interpolation and high-dimensional optimal transport tasks, including single-cell RNA sequencing data.
AI.motivation: To create expressive neural models that are provably convex in the input, overcoming ICNNs' potential parameter inefficiency and limitation in depth, while remaining scalable to large datasets and applicable to tasks like convex regression and optimal transport.
AI.method: Propose Hyper Input Convex Neural Networks (HyCNNs) by integrating Maxout units with ICNN principles to preserve input convexity while allowing increased depth. Provide theoretical analysis showing exponential parameter efficiency relative to ICNNs for approximating quadratic functions. Validate via synthetic experiments on convex regression/interpolation and apply to high-dimensional optimal transport maps for synthetic data and single-cell RNA-seq.
AI.result: HyCNNs outperform competitive baselines (ICNNs and MLPs) in convex regression/interpolation tasks. In high-dimensional optimal transport problems, HyCNNs often surpass ICNN-based OT methods and other baselines across varied settings, including applications to single-cell RNA sequencing data.
AI.conclusion: HyCNNs deliver improved parameter efficiency and predictive performance for convex learning tasks and neural OT mapping, while maintaining convexity in the input and benefiting from depth and scalable training.

title: DenseStep2M: A Scalable, Training-Free Pipeline for Dense Instructional Video Annotation
summary:
Long-term video understanding requires interpreting complex temporal events and reasoning over procedural activities. While instructional video corpora, like HowTo100M, offer rich resources for model training, they present significant challenges, including noisy ASR transcripts and inconsistent temporal alignments between narration and visual content. In this work, we introduce an automated, training-free pipeline to extract high-quality procedural annotations from in-the-wild instructional videos. Our approach segments videos into coherent shots, filters poorly aligned content, and leverages state-of-the-art multimodal and large language models (Qwen2.5-VL and DeepSeek-R1) to generate structured, temporally grounded procedural steps.
  This pipeline yields DenseStep2M, a large-scale dataset comprising approximately 100K videos and 2M detailed instructional steps, designed to support comprehensive long-form video understanding. To rigorously evaluate our pipeline, we curate DenseCaption100, a benchmark of high-quality, human-written captions. Evaluations demonstrate strong alignment between our auto-generated steps and human annotations. Furthermore, we validate the utility of DenseStep2M across three core downstream tasks: dense video captioning, procedural step grounding, and cross-modal retrieval. Models fine-tuned on DenseStep2M achieve substantial gains in captioning quality and temporal localization, while exhibiting robust zero-shot generalization across egocentric, exocentric, and mixed-perspective domains. These results underscore the effectiveness of DenseStep2M in facilitating advanced multimodal alignment and long-term activity reasoning. Our dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/mingjige/DenseStep2M.

AI.tldr: Introduces DenseStep2M, a training-free pipeline that extracts high-quality procedural annotations from in-the-wild instructional videos, producing a 100K-video, 2M-step dataset and a DenseCaption100 benchmark; demonstrates improved deep learning tasks in dense captioning, step grounding, and cross-modal retrieval with strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Long-term video understanding demands temporal reasoning of procedural activities; instructional video corpora suffer from noisy transcripts and misalignment; need automated, high-quality, temporally grounded procedural annotations.
AI.method: Segment videos into coherent shots; filter poorly aligned content; use multimodal LLMs Qwen2.5-VL and DeepSeek-R1 to generate structured, temporally grounded steps; assemble DenseStep2M; create DenseCaption100 as a human-written caption benchmark; evaluate on dense video captioning, procedural step grounding, cross-modal retrieval; assess zero-shot generalization across egocentric, exocentric, mixed.
AI.result: DenseStep2M includes ~100K videos and ~2M steps; dense alignment between auto steps and human annotations; models trained on DenseStep2M show substantial gains in captioning quality and temporal localization; robust zero-shot generalization across domains.
AI.conclusion: DenseStep2M enables advanced multimodal alignment and long-term activity reasoning; dataset is publicly available at HuggingFace.

title: AirZoo: A Unified Large-Scale Dataset for Grounding Aerial Geometric 3D Vision
summary: Despite the rapid progress in data-driven 3D vision, aerial geometric 3D vision remains a formidable challenge due to the severe scarcity of large-scale, high-fidelity training data. Existing benchmarks, predominantly biased toward ground-level or object-centric views, do not account for complex viewpoint transformations and diverse environmental conditions in UAV-based sensing. To bridge this critical gap, we propose AirZoo, a unified large-scale dataset and benchmark for grounding aerial geometric 3D vision. AirZoo possesses three appealing properties: 1) Scalable Generation Pipeline: Leveraging freely available, world-scale photogrammetric 3D meshes, it renders vast outdoor environments with customizable UAV flight trajectories and configurable weather/illumination. 2) Comprehensive Scene Diversity: It provides the most extensive coverage of region types to date (spanning 378 regions across 22 countries), systematically encompassing both highly structured urban landscapes and complex unstructured natural environments. 3) Rich Geometric Annotations: Each frame provides synchronized, pixel-level metric depth and precise 6-DoF geo-referenced poses, essential for geometry-aware learning. Through three rigorous evaluation tracks -- aerial image retrieval, cross-view matching, and multi-view 3D reconstruction -- we demonstrate that AirZoo serves as a powerful pre-training engine. Extensive experiments on both public and newly collected real-world benchmarks reveal that fine-tuning on AirZoo yields substantial performance gains for SoTA models (e.g., MegaLoc, RoMa, VGGT, and Depth Anything 3), establishing a new performance upper bound for aerial spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: AirZoo provides a large-scale synthetic aerial 3D vision dataset and benchmarks with scalable generation, diverse scenes across 378 regions in 22 countries, and per-frame depth plus 6-DoF poses; evaluated on aerial image retrieval, cross-view matching, and multi-view reconstruction; enables pre-training that improves SOTA models.
AI.motivation: To address the scarcity of large-scale, high-fidelity aerial 3D data and biases in ground-level datasets; enable robust learning under varied viewpoints and environmental conditions for UAV sensing.
AI.method: Leverage world-scale photogrammetric 3D meshes to render outdoor environments with configurable UAV trajectories, weather and illumination; provide synchronized per-frame pixel-level depth and geo-referenced 6-DoF poses; cover diverse region types; evaluate across aerial image retrieval, cross-view matching, and multi-view 3D reconstruction; serve as a pre-training engine for SOTA models.
AI.result: Fine-tuning on AirZoo yields substantial gains for state-of-the-art models (MegaLoc, RoMa, VGGT, Depth Anything 3) on real benchmarks, establishing a new upper bound for aerial spatial intelligence.
AI.conclusion: AirZoo is a unified, scalable dataset and benchmark that enables synthetic-to-real aerial 3D learning with rich geometric annotations, supporting pre-training and pushing performance of leading aerial vision systems.

title: FunFace: Feature Utility and Norm Estimation for Face Recognition
summary: Face Recognition (FR) is used in a variety of application domains, from entertainment and banking to security and surveillance. Such applications rely on the FR model to be robust and perform well in a variety of settings. To achieve this, state-of-the-art FR models typically use expressive adaptive margin loss functions, which tie the feature norm to concepts related to sample quality, such as recognizability and perceptual image quality. Recently, through the development of Face Image Quality Assessment (FIQA) techniques, biometric utility has become the preferred measure of face-image quality and has been shown to be a better predictor of the usefulness of samples for face recognition compared to more human-centric aspects, such as resolution, blur, and lighting, tied to general image quality. While image quality expressed through feature norms exhibits a certain level of correlation with biometric utility, it does not fully encapsulate all aspects of utility. To address this point, we propose a new adaptive margin loss, FunFace (Face Recognition Through Utility and Norm Estimation), which incorporates biometric utility, estimated by the Certainty Ratio, into the adaptive margin, taking inspiration from AdaFace. We show that FunFace (when used to train a face recognition model) achieves competitive results to other state-of-the-art FR models on benchmarks containing high-quality samples, while surpassing them on low quality benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes FunFace: an adaptive-margin loss for face recognition that injects biometric utility (via Certainty Ratio) into the margin, improving performance on low-quality samples while maintaining high-quality accuracy.
AI.motivation: Motivated by limitations of feature-norm-based margins and evidence from FIQA that biometric utility correlates with recognition usefulness; aim to align optimization objective with real-world utility.
AI.method: Introduce FunFace by incorporating Certainty Ratio into adaptive margin; build on AdaFace architecture; train FR models with the modified margin; utilize biometric utility estimates to scale margins.
AI.result: Competitive with state-of-the-art on high-quality benchmarks; superior on low-quality benchmarks; shows benefit of utility-informed margins.
AI.conclusion: FunFace demonstrates that integrating biometric utility into the margin enhances robustness to sample quality; promising direction for utility-aware FR; potential for further refinements and broader utility metrics.

title: State Beyond Appearance: Diagnosing and Improving State Consistency in Dial-Based Measurement Reading
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved impressive progress on general multimodal tasks, yet they remain brittle on dial-based measurement reading. In this paper, we study this problem through controlled benchmarks and feature-space probing, and show that current MLLMs not only achieve unsatisfactory accuracy on dial-based readout, but also suffer sharp performance drops under viewpoint and illumination changes even when the underlying dial state remains fixed. Our probing analysis further reveals that same-state samples under appearance variation are not consistently clustered, while neighboring states fail to preserve the local structure implied by continuous dial values. These findings suggest that existing MLLMs largely ignore the intrinsic state geometry of dial measurement tasks and instead rely on superficial appearance cues. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose TriSCA, a tri-level state-consistent alignment framework for dial-based measurement reading. Specifically, TriSCA consists of state-distance-aware representation alignment, metadata-grounded observation-to-state supervision, and state-aware objective alignment. Extensive ablation studies and evaluation experiments on controlled clock and gauge benchmarks, together with evaluation on an external real-world benchmark, demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: TriSCA introduces a tri-level state-consistent alignment framework to robustly read dial-based measurements with multimodal LLMs, addressing brittle appearance-based cues by enforcing state geometry coherence across representations, observations, and objectives; validated on controlled clock/gauge benchmarks and a real-world benchmark.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs falter on dial-based reading due to reliance on superficial appearance cues; same-state samples under appearance variation are inconsistently clustered and local structure across continuous dial values is not preserved, indicating a neglect of intrinsic state geometry.
AI.method: TriSCA comprises three components: (1) state-distance-aware representation alignment, (2) metadata-grounded observation-to-state supervision, and (3) state-aware objective alignment, collectively enforcing consistent state geometry across modalities and learning objectives.
AI.result: Extensive ablations and evaluations on controlled clock and gauge benchmarks, plus evaluation on an external real-world benchmark, show that TriSCA improves robustness to viewpoint and illumination changes and better preserves the intrinsic state geometry compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling and enforcing state geometry through tri-level alignment is effective for dial-based measurement reading in MLLMs and can generalize across benchmarks, reducing reliance on superficial cues.

title: SnapPose3D: Diffusion-Based Single-Frame 2D-to-3D Lifting of Human Poses
summary: Depth ambiguity and joint uncertainty are the two main obstacles in obtaining accurate human pose predictions by 2D-to-3D lifting methods proposed in the literature. In particular, these issues are caused by 2D joint locations that can be mapped to multiple 3D positions, inducing multiple possible final poses. Following these considerations, we propose leveraging diffusion-based models generation capability to predict multiple hypotheses and aggregate them in a final accurate pose. Therefore, we introduce SnapPose3D, a pose-lifting framework trained deterministically to denoise 3D poses conditioned on both visual context and 2D pose features. SnapPose3D adopts a probabilistic approach during inference, generating multiple hypotheses through random sampling from a unit Gaussian distribution. Unlike most previous methods that address pose ambiguity by processing temporal sequences, SnapPose3D uses single frames as input, avoiding tracking and limiting computational cost, data acquisition complexity, and the need for online, real-time applications. We extensively evaluate SnapPose3D on well-known benchmarks for the 3D human pose estimation task showing its ability to generate and aggregate accurate hypotheses that lead to state-of-the-art results.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based, deterministic denoising for multi-hypothesis 3D pose lifting from single frames, enabling probabilistic inference of multiple plausible poses and state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Depth ambiguity and joint uncertainty in 2D-to-3D lifting arise because fixed 2D joint locations can map to multiple 3D configurations. Generating and aggregating multiple plausible poses is essential to improve accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: Train a diffusion-based model that denoises 3D poses conditioned on visual context and 2D pose features; during inference, sample from a unit Gaussian to produce multiple pose hypotheses, aggregating them without relying on temporal sequences (single-frame input).
