AI.result: Extensive evaluation on standard 3D human pose benchmarks shows the method can generate and aggregate accurate hypotheses, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: SnapPose3D offers a single-frame, diffusion-based解 pose-lifting approach that addresses depth and joint uncertainty by producing multiple plausible poses and aggregating them to reach state-of-the-art performance while avoiding temporal tracking costs.

title: Hearing the Room Through the Shape of the Drum: Modal-Guided Sound Recovery from Multi-Point Surface Vibrations
comment: Oral presentation at The IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026
summary: Optical vibration sensing enables recovering the scene sound directly from the surface vibration of nearby objects, turning everyday objects into ``visual microphones''. However, most prior methods had focused on capturing the vibrations of specific objects with highly favorable vibration responses. These include objects where the surface vibrations are generated by the object itself (e.g., speaker membrane or guitar body) or objects consisting of a thin membrane which is highly reactive to sound (e.g., a chip bag or the leaf of a plant). In this paper, we tackle sound recovery for a more challenging class of solid objects whose vibration responses are poor or highly resonant. We simultaneously capture vibrations for multiple surface points on the object using a speckle-based vibrometry imaging system. Then, we derive a novel physics-guided vibration formation model that relates the scene sound source to the captured multi-point multi-axis vibrations via the object's vibrational modes. The model is then used to reverse the resonant transfer function of the vibrating object, fusing multiple vibration signals to estimate the original sound source in the scene. We evaluate our approach by recovering sound from a variety of everyday objects, demonstrating that it significantly outperforms traditional single-point speckle vibrometry in challenging scenarios and other signal-processing-based methods for multi-signal fusing.

AI.tldr: A speckle-based multi-point vibrometry system with a physics-guided model reverses resonant transfer functions to recover scene sound from objects with weak or highly resonant vibrations, outperforming single-point vibrometry and other fusion methods.
AI.motivation: Recovering sound from everyday solid objects is challenging when their vibrational responses are weak or dominated by resonances; existing methods target objects with favorable, often single-point responses.
AI.method: Capture vibrations at multiple surface points across an object using speckle vibrometry; derive a physics-guided vibration formation model that links the scene sound source to the observed multi-point multi-axis vibrations via the object's vibrational modes; invert the model to compensate for resonances and fuse multiple signals to estimate the original sound.
AI.result: The approach significantly outperforms traditional single-point speckle vibrometry and other multi-signal fusion methods in challenging scenarios, demonstrated on a variety of everyday objects.
AI.conclusion: Multi-point, physics-informed vibrometry enables robust sound recovery from challenging vibrating objects, expanding the applicability of visual microphone techniques.

title: CurEvo: Curriculum-Guided Self-Evolution for Video Understanding
summary: Recent advances in self-evolution video understanding frameworks have demonstrated the potential of autonomous learning without human annotations. However, existing methods often suffer from weakly controlled optimization and uncontrolled difficulty progression, as they lack structured guidance throughout the iterative learning process. To address these limitations, we propose CurEvo, a curriculum-guided self-evolution framework that introduces curriculum learning into self-evolution to achieve more structured and progressive model improvement. CurEvo dynamically regulates task difficulty, refines evaluation criteria, and balances data diversity according to model competence, forming a curriculum-guided feedback loop that aligns learning complexity with model capability. Built upon this principle, we develop a multi-dimensional adaptive QA framework that jointly evolves question generation and answer evaluation across perception, recognition, and understanding dimensions, ensuring coherent and measurable curriculum progression. Through this integration, CurEvo transforms weakly controlled self-evolution into a more structured learning process for autonomous video understanding. Across seven backbones, CurEvo consistently improves both benchmark accuracy and evaluator-based semantic score on four VideoQA benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of curriculum-guided self-evolution for video understanding.

AI.tldr: Introduces CurEvo, a curriculum-guided self-evolution framework for video understanding; integrates curriculum learning into self-evolution; includes multi-dimensional adaptive QA; reports improvements across seven backbones on four VideoQA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing self-evolution methods lack structured guidance, leading to weak optimization and uncontrolled progression of task difficulty. A mechanism to regulate task difficulty, evaluation criteria, and data diversity aligned with model competence is needed to stabilize and improve autonomous video understanding.
AI.method: CurEvo dynamically regulates task difficulty, refines evaluation criteria, and balances data diversity via a curriculum-guided feedback loop. It extends to a multi-dimensional adaptive QA framework that jointly evolves question generation and answer evaluation across perception, recognition, and understanding dimensions to ensure coherent curriculum progression.
AI.result: Across seven backbones, CurEvo consistently improves both benchmark accuracy and evaluator-based semantic score on four VideoQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Curriculum-guided self-evolution provides a structured, progressive learning process for autonomous video understanding, yielding robust improvements and demonstrating broad applicability across backbones and benchmarks.

title: Learning Sparse BRDF Measurement Samples from Image
summary: Accurate BRDF acquisition is important for realistic rendering, but dense gonioreflectometer measurements are slow and expensive. We study how to select a small number of BRDF measurements that are most useful for reconstructing material appearance under a learned reflectance prior. Our method combines a set encoder for sparse coordinate-value observations, a pretrained hypernetwork-based BRDF reconstructor, and a differentiable renderer. During sampler training, the reconstructor is kept fixed and gradients from BRDF-space and rendered-image losses are used to optimize measurement locations. This separates sample selection from prior fitting and encourages the sampler to choose directions that are informative under the learned material distribution. Experiments on the MERL dataset show that the proposed sampler improves low-budget reconstruction quality at 8 and 16 measurements compared with neural reconstruction baselines, while PCA-based methods remain strong at larger budgets. We further analyze the effect of image-space supervision, co-optimization, and image-only latent fitting for unseen materials.

AI.tldr: A sample-efficient BRDF measurement strategy using a learned prior to select informative directions, enabling accurate appearance reconstruction with few measurements.
AI.motivation: Dense BRDF acquisition is slow and expensive; there is a need to reduce measurements by exploiting a learned prior over materials to maintain reconstruction quality.
AI.method: Introduce a set encoder to handle sparse coordinate-value observations, a pretrained hypernetwork-based BRDF reconstructor, and a differentiable renderer. Train a sampler with the reconstructor fixed, using gradients from BRDF-space and rendered-image losses to optimize measurement locations, thereby decoupling sample selection from prior fitting and guiding sampling toward directions informative under the learned material distribution.
AI.result: On the MERL dataset, the proposed sampler improves low-budget BRDF reconstruction quality at 8 and 16 measurements compared with neural reconstruction baselines; PCA-based methods remain strong at larger budgets.
AI.conclusion: The sampler enhances low-budget BRDF reconstruction under a learned material distribution, with further analysis on image-space supervision, co-optimization, and image-only latent fitting for unseen materials.

title: GLM-5V-Turbo: Toward a Native Foundation Model for Multimodal Agents
summary: We present GLM-5V-Turbo, a step toward native foundation models for multimodal agents. As foundation models are increasingly deployed in real environments, agentic capability depends not only on language reasoning, but also on the ability to perceive, interpret, and act over heterogeneous contexts such as images, videos, webpages, documents, GUIs. GLM-5V-Turbo is built around this objective: multimodal perception is integrated as a core component of reasoning, planning, tool use, and execution, rather than as an auxiliary interface to a language model. This report summarizes the main improvements behind GLM-5V-Turbo across model design, multimodal training, reinforcement learning, toolchain expansion, and integration with agent frameworks. These developments lead to strong performance in multimodal coding, visual tool use, and framework-based agentic tasks, while preserving competitive text-only coding capability. More importantly, our development process offers practical insights for building multimodal agents, highlighting the central role of multimodal perception, hierarchical optimization, and reliable end-to-end verification.

AI.tldr: GLM-5V-Turbo advances native multimodal foundation models for agents by integrating perception into core reasoning, planning, and action, achieving strong multimodal coding, visual tool use, and agentic tasks while maintaining solid text-only capability.
AI.motivation: To enable agents to perceive, interpret, and act over heterogeneous contexts (images, videos, webpages, documents, GUIs) as an integral part of reasoning rather than as a separate multimodal interface.
AI.method: Iterative improvements across model design, multimodal training, reinforcement learning, toolchain expansion, and integration with agent frameworks; emphasizes multimodal perception as central to perception-action loops, hierarchical optimization, and end-to-end verification.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong performance in multimodal coding, visual tool use, and framework-based agentic tasks; maintains competitive text-only coding performance.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical insights for building multimodal agents, underscoring the central role of multimodal perception, hierarchical optimization, and reliable end-to-end verification in solidifying native multimodal foundation models for agents.

title: TAP into the Patch Tokens: Leveraging Vision Foundation Model Features for AI-Generated Image Detection
comment: This paper has been accepted at IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW), 2026
summary: Recent methods demonstrate that large-scale pretrained models, such as CLIP vision transformers, effectively detect AI-generated images (AIGIs) from unseen generative models when used as feature extractors. Many state-of-the-art methods for AI-generated image detection build upon the original CLIP-ViT to enhance this generalization. Since CLIP's release, numerous vision foundation models (VFMs) have emerged, incorporating architectural improvements and different training paradigms. Despite these advances, their potential for AIGI detection and AI image forensics remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present a comprehensive benchmark across multiple VFM families, covering diverse pretraining objectives, input resolutions, and model scales. We systematically evaluate their out-of-the-box performance for detecting fully-generated AI-images and AI-inpainted images, and discover that the best model outperforms the original CLIP by more than 12% in accuracy, beating established approaches in the process. To fully leverage the features of a modern VFM, we propose a simple redesign of the classifier head by utilizing tunable attention pooling (TAP), which aggregates output tokens into a refined global representation. Integrating TAP with the latest VFMs yields substantial performance gains across several AIGI detection benchmarks, establishing a new state-of-the-art on two challenging benchmarks for in-the-wild detection of AI-generated and -inpainted images.

AI.tldr: Benchmarks vision foundation models (VFMs) for AI-generated image detection and proposes Tunable Attention Pooling (TAP) to improve performance; newest VFMs outperform CLIP by >12% and TAP-based heads achieve state-of-the-art on two in-the-wild benchmarks for AI-generated and inpainted images.
AI.motivation: Assess whether modern VFMs beyond CLIP can improve AI-generated image detection/forensics, potentially surpassing CLIP-based baselines and enabling robust in-the-wild detection.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate out-of-the-box performance of diverse VFMs (different families, pretraining objectives, resolutions, scales) on fully-generated AI images and AI-inpainted images; propose TAP to replace standard classifier head to aggregate tokens into a refined global representation.
AI.result: The best VFM-based detector surpasses the original CLIP by over 12% accuracy; incorporating TAP yields substantial gains across multiple benchmarks, setting a new state-of-the-art on two challenging in-the-wild AI-generated/inpainted image benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: VFMs exhibit strong potential for AI image forensics. A simple classifier-head redesign (TAP) can unlock further gains, enabling SOTA performance on key benchmarks for AIGI detection and inpainting.

title: Virtual-reality based patient-specific simulation of spine surgical procedures: A fast, highly automated and high-fidelity system for surgical education and planning
summary: Surgical training involves didactic teaching, mentor-led learning, surgical skills laboratories, and direct exposure to surgery; however, increasing clinical pressures have limited operating room (OR) exposure. This work leverages virtual reality (VR) to provide a safe and immersive training environment. Existing VR training is often based on standardized scenarios not tailored to individual clinical cases. This study addresses this limitation using artificial intelligence (AI) based computer vision methods to generate patient-specific simulations from computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). This study focuses on patient-specific spinal decompression simulation for spinal stenosis in a virtual operating room. The objectives were (1) automatic creation of 3D anatomical models and (2) VR simulation of spinal decompression procedures including laminectomy, disc resection, and foraminotomy. Model construction required multimodal fusion (registration) of CT and MRI and segmentation of relevant structures. Segmentation was evaluated using the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC), and registration accuracy using Target Registration Error (TRE). Qualitative feedback was obtained from surgeons and trainees. High-fidelity patient-specific 3D models were generated efficiently (approximately 2.5 minutes per case, N = 15). Segmentation accuracy was high, with a DSC of 0.95 (+/- 0.03) for vertebral bone and 0.895 (+/- 0.02) for soft tissue structures. Registration accuracy showed a mean TRE of 1.73 (+/- 0.42) mm. Semi-structured interviews indicated improved spatial understanding, increased procedural confidence, and strong perceived educational value. This platform significantly reduced the time and costs of patient-specific modelling, thereby facilitating pre-operative planning, post-procedural assessments, and comprehensive surgical simulation.

AI.tldr: AI-driven patient-specific VR spinal decompression simulator using CT/MRI; automatic 3D model creation, segmentation, and multimodal registration; high accuracy, rapid generation, and positive educational feedback; potential for pre-operative planning and training.
AI.motivation: Address limited OR exposure and the lack of patient-specific VR training; enable realistic, patient-tailored simulations to improve spatial understanding, confidence, and planning while reducing time and cost.
AI.method: Multimodal fusion (CT-MRI) for registration and segmentation of relevant structures to create high-fidelity 3D anatomical models. Evaluation used Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) for segmentation and Target Registration Error (TRE) for registration. VR simulation of spinal decompression procedures (laminectomy, disc resection, foraminotomy) with N=15 cases. Segmentation accuracy and registration accuracy were quantified; qualitative feedback obtained via semi-structured interviews with surgeons and trainees; generation time ~2.5 minutes per case.
AI.result: Segmentation DSC: vertebral bone 0.95±0.03; soft tissue 0.895±0.02. Registration TRE: 1.73±0.42 mm. Generation time ~2.5 minutes per case; qualitative feedback indicated improved spatial understanding, increased procedural confidence, and strong educational value.
AI.conclusion: Platform substantially reduces time and cost of patient-specific modeling, enabling effective pre-operative planning, post-procedural assessment, and comprehensive surgical simulation; supports broader VR-based surgical training and patient-specific rehearsal, though further validation with larger samples is warranted.

title: MesonGS++: Post-training Compression of 3D Gaussian Splatting with Hyperparameter Searching
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieves high-quality novel view synthesis with real-time rendering, but its storage cost remains prohibitive for practical deployment. Existing post-training compression methods still rely on many coupled hyperparameters across pruning, transformation, quantization, and entropy coding, making it difficult to control the final compressed size and fully exploit the rate-distortion trade-off. We propose MesonGS++, a size-aware post-training codec for 3D Gaussian compression. On the codec side, MesonGS++ combines joint importance-based pruning, octree geometry coding, attribute transformation, selective vector quantization for higher-degree spherical harmonics, and group-wise mixed-precision quantization with entropy coding. On the configuration side, it treats the reserve ratio and bit-width allocation as the dominant rate-distortion knobs and jointly optimizes them under a target storage budget via discrete sampling and 0--1 integer linear programming. We further propose a linear size estimator and a CUDA parallel quantization operator to accelerate the hyperparameter searching process. Extensive experiments show that MesonGS++ achieves over 34$\times$ compression while preserving rendering fidelity, outperforming state-of-the-art post-training methods and accurately meeting target size budgets. Remarkably, without any training, MesonGS++ can even surpass the PSNR of vanilla 3DGS at a 20$\times$ compression rate on the Stump scene. Our code is available at https://github.com/mmlab-sigs/mesongs_plus

AI.tldr: MesonGS++ is a size-aware post-training codec for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that achieves ~34x compression while preserving rendering fidelity by combining pruning, octree coding, transformations, selective vector quantization, and mixed-precision quantization with entropy coding; it optimizes reserve ratio and bit-width under a storage budget via discrete sampling and 0-1 ILP, with speedups from a linear size estimator and CUDA quantization; it outperforms prior post-training methods and can surpass vanilla 3DGS PSNR at high compression on certain scenes.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting delivers high-quality novel view synthesis but suffers from prohibitive storage costs. Existing post-training compression methods rely on many coupled hyperparameters across pruning, transformation, quantization, and entropy coding, making rate-distortion control difficult. A size-aware, end-to-end post-training codec is needed to efficiently manage the rate-distortion trade-off.
AI.method: Develop MesonGS++: (1) codec side: joint importance-based pruning, octree geometry coding, attribute transformation, selective vector quantization for higher-degree spherical harmonics, and group-wise mixed-precision quantization with entropy coding. (2) configuration side: treat reserve ratio and bit-width allocation as primary rate-distortion knobs; optimize under a storage budget via discrete sampling and 0-1 integer linear programming. Add linear size estimator and CUDA parallel quantization to accelerate hyperparameter search.
AI.result: Achieves over 34× compression while preserving rendering fidelity; outperforms state-of-the-art post-training methods and accurately hits target size budgets. Notably, without training, can surpass PSNR of vanilla 3DGS at 20× compression on the Stump scene.
AI.conclusion: MesonGS++ provides an effective size-aware post-training compression framework for 3D Gaussian compression, enabling substantial storage reductions with controlled rate-distortion performance, practical speedups, and competitive rendering quality; code is publicly available.

title: Bridge: Basis-Driven Causal Inference Marries VFMs for Domain Generalization
summary: Detectors often suffer from degraded performance, primarily due to the distributional gap between the source and target domains. This issue is especially evident in single-source domains with limited data, as models tend to rely on confounders (e.g., illumination, co-occurrence, and style) from the source domain, leading to spurious correlations that hinder generalization. To this end, this paper proposes a novel Basis-driven framework for domain generalization, namely \textbf{\textit{Bridge}}, that incorporates causal inference into object detection. By learning the low-rank bases for front-door adjustment, \textbf{\textit{Bridge}} blocks confounders' effects to mitigate spurious correlations, while simultaneously refining representations by filtering redundant and task-irrelevant components. \textbf{\textit{Bridge}} can be seamlessly integrated with both discriminative (e.g., DINOv2/3, SAM) and generative (e.g., Stable Diffusion) Vision Foundation Models (VFMs). Extensive experiments across multiple domain generalization object detection datasets, i.e., Cross-Camera, Adverse Weather, Real-to-Artistic, Diverse Weather Datasets, and Diverse Weather DroneVehicle (our newly augmented real-world UAV-based benchmark), underscore the superiority of our proposed method over previous state-of-the-art approaches. The project page is available at: https://mingbohong.github.io/Bridge/.

AI.tldr: Bridge introduces a basis-driven, causality-aware front-door adjustment for domain generalization in object detection, blocking confounders and refining representations. It is a plug-in framework compatible with discriminative and generative vision foundation models, achieving strong generalization across diverse domain shifts and a new UAV-based dataset.
AI.motivation: Domain shifts cause spurious correlations by leveraging source-domain confounders (illumination, style, co-occurrence). In single-source, data is limited, making models brittle. A causal approach that blocks confounding effects while preserving task signals is needed to improve generalization.
AI.method: Learn low-rank bases to perform front-door adjustment, effectively blocking confounders from affecting the detector’s predictions. The framework also filters redundant and task-irrelevant components to refine representations. It is designed as a plug-in that works with discriminative VFMs (e.g., DINOv2/3, SAM) and generative VFMs (e.g., Stable Diffusion).
AI.result: Extensive experiments across Cross-Camera, Adverse Weather, Real-to-Artistic, Diverse Weather Datasets, and the new Diverse Weather DroneVehicle UAV benchmark show superior performance over prior state-of-the-art domain generalization methods. A project page is provided for reproducibility and implementation details.
AI.conclusion: A causal, basis-driven front-door adjustment framework (Bridge) is effective for domain generalization in object detection, offering a versatile, plug-in solution compatible with both discriminative and generative VFMs and supporting additional UAV-based benchmarks.

title: Breaking the Rigid Prior: Towards Articulated 3D Anomaly Detection
summary: Existing 3D anomaly detection methods are built on a rigid prior: normal geometry is pose-invariant and can be canonicalized through registration or alignment. This prior does not hold for articulated objects with hinge or sliding joints, where valid pose changes induce structured geometric variations that cannot be collapsed to a single canonical template, causing pose-induced deformations to be misidentified as anomalies while true structural defects are obscured. No existing benchmark addresses this challenge. We introduce ArtiAD, the first large-scale benchmark for articulated 3D anomaly detection, comprising 15,229 point clouds across 39 object categories with dense joint-angle variations and six structural anomaly types. Each sample is annotated with its joint configuration and part-level motion labels, enabling explicit disentanglement of pose-induced geometry from structural defects. ArtiAD also provides a seen/unseen articulation split to evaluate both interpolation and extrapolation to novel joint configurations. We propose Shape-Pose-Aware Signed Distance Field (SPA-SDF), a baseline that replaces the rigid prior with a continuous pose-conditioned implicit field, factorized into an articulation-independent structural prior and a Fourier-encoded joint embedding. At inference, the articulation state is recovered by minimizing reconstruction energy, and anomalies are identified as point-wise deviations from the learned manifold. SPA-SDF achieves 0.884 object-level AUROC on seen configurations and 0.874 on unseen configurations, substantially outperforming all rigid-based baselines. Our code and benchmark will be publicly released to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: Introduces ArtiAD, a large-scale benchmark for articulated 3D anomaly detection, and SPA-SDF, a pose-aware implicit model for separating pose geometry from defects; achieves strong AUROC (~0.88) on seen/unseen configurations.
AI.motivation: Rigid priors assume pose-invariant normal geometry, which fails for articulated objects with joints where pose changes induce structured geometric variations; a dedicated benchmark and pose-aware method are needed to disentangle pose from defects and evaluate generalization to novel configurations.
AI.method: Propose ArtiAD: a dataset of 15,229 point clouds across 39 categories with dense joint-angle variation, joint/config labels, and seen/unseen splits; introduce SPA-SDF: a Fourier-embedded, pose-conditioned implicit field factorizing into articulation-independent structural prior and joint embedding; inference via minimizing reconstruction energy to recover articulation state; anomalies detected as point-wise deviation from learned manifold.
AI.result: SPA-SDF achieves 0.884 object-level AUROC on seen configurations and 0.874 on unseen configurations, outperforming rigid-based baselines; code and benchmark will be released publicly.
AI.conclusion: First large-scale benchmark for articulated 3D anomaly detection; a pose-aware implicit model significantly improves detection and generalization; enables future research with public resources.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Pedestrian Attribute Recognition via Evidential Deep Learning
summary: We propose UAPAR, an Uncertainty-Aware Pedestrian Attribute Recognition framework. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first EDL-based uncertainty-aware framework for pedestrian attribute recognition (PAR). Unlike conventional deterministic methods, which fail to assess prediction reliability on low-quality samples, UAPAR effectively identifies unreliable predictions and thus enhances system robustness in complex real-world scenarios. To achieve this, UAPAR incorporates Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) into a CLIP-based architecture. Specifically, a Region-Aware Evidence Reasoning module employs cross-attention and spatial prior masks to capture fine-grained local features, which are further processed by an evidence head to estimate attribute-wise epistemic uncertainty. To further enhance training robustness, we develop an uncertainty-guided dual-stage curriculum learning strategy to alleviate the adverse effects of severe label noise during training. Extensive experiments on the PA100K, PETA, RAPv1, and RAPv2 datasets demonstrate that UAPAR achieves competitive or superior performance. Furthermore, qualitative results confirm that the proposed framework generates uncertainty estimates that are predictive of challenging or erroneous samples.

AI.tldr: Introduces UAPAR, an uncertainty-aware pedestrian attribute recognition framework that uses Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) within a CLIP-based architecture. It includes a Region-Aware Evidence Reasoning module with cross-attention and spatial prior masks, and an uncertainty-guided dual-stage curriculum to mitigate label noise. Demonstrates competitive results on multiple datasets and that uncertainty estimates predict challenging or erroneous samples.
AI.motivation: To enable reliability estimation in pedestrian attribute recognition (PAR), addressing the lack of uncertainty assessment in traditional deterministic methods especially on low-quality inputs and noisy labels, thereby improving robustness in real-world deployments.
AI.method: Integrates Evidential Deep Learning into a CLIP-based PAR framework. A Region-Aware Evidence Reasoning module uses cross-attention and spatial prior masks to extract local features, followed by an evidence head that estimates attribute-wise epistemic uncertainty. An uncertainty-guided dual-stage curriculum learning scheme combats label noise during training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on PA100K, PETA, RAPv1, and RAPv2 shows competitive or superior attribute recognition performance. Qualitative analyses indicate that predicted uncertainties correlate with difficult or erroneous samples.
AI.conclusion: UAPAR is the first EDL-based uncertainty-aware PAR framework, improving robustness to challenging inputs and enabling reliable uncertainty estimates, with strong empirical performance across multiple benchmarks.

title: SEAL: Semantic-aware Single-image Sticker Personalization with a Large-scale Sticker-tag Dataset
comment: The last two authors are co-corresponding authors. Please visit our project page at https://cmlab-korea.github.io/SEAL
summary: Synthesizing a target concept from a single reference image is challenging in diffusion-based personalized text-to-image generation, particularly for sticker personalization where prompts often require explicit attribute edits. With only one reference, test-time fine-tuning (TTF) methods tend to overfit, producing \textit{visual entanglement}, where background artifacts are absorbed into the learned concept, and \textit{structural rigidity}, where the model memorizes reference-specific spatial configurations and loses contextual controllability. To address these issues, we introduce \textbf{SE}mantic-aware single-image sticker person\textbf{AL}ization (\textbf{SEAL}), a plug-and-play, architecture-agnostic adaptation module that integrates into existing personalization pipelines without modifying their U-Net-based diffusion backbones. SEAL applies three components during embedding adaptation: (1) a Semantic-guided Spatial Attention Loss, (2) a Split-merge Token Strategy, and (3) Structure-aware Layer Restriction. To support sticker-domain personalization with attribute-level control, we present StickerBench, a large-scale sticker image dataset with structured tags under a six-attribute schema (Appearance, Emotion, Action, Camera Composition, Style, Background). These annotations provide a consistent interface for varying context while keeping target identity fixed, enabling systematic evaluation of identity disentanglement and contextual controllability. Experiments show that SEAL consistently improves identity preservation while maintaining contextual controllability, highlighting the importance of explicit spatial and structural constraints during test-time adaptation. The code, StickerBench, and project page will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: SEAL is a plug-and-play adaptation module that improves single-image sticker personalization in diffusion models by enforcing semantic spatial constraints, token handling, and structure-aware layer restrictions, along with a StickerBench dataset for evaluation and identity controllability.
AI.motivation: To overcome test-time fine-tuning overfitting (visual entanglement) and structural rigidity when modeling a target concept from a single reference in diffusion-based personalized generation, especially for stickers requiring attribute edits.
AI.method: Integrates into existing pipelines without changing U-Net backbones via three components: (1) Semantic-guided Spatial Attention Loss, (2) Split-merge Token Strategy, and (3) Structure-aware Layer Restriction. Also introduces StickerBench with six-attribute taxonomy (Appearance, Emotion, Action, Camera Composition, Style, Background) for systematic evaluation.
AI.result: Empirical results show SEAL enhances identity preservation while preserving contextual controllability, demonstrating that explicit spatial and structural constraints during test-time adaptation are crucial.
AI.conclusion: SEAL provides a robust, architecture-agnostic plug-in for semantic and structural constraints in single-image sticker personalization, enabling better identity disentanglement and controllable context; code, StickerBench, and project page to be released.

title: AnimateAnyMesh++: A Flexible 4D Foundation Model for High-Fidelity Text-Driven Mesh Animation
summary: Recent advances in 4D content generation have attracted increasing attention, yet creating high-quality animated 3D models remains challenging due to the complexity of modeling spatio-temporal distributions and the scarcity of 4D training data. We present AnimateAnyMesh++, a feed-forward framework for text-driven animation of arbitrary 3D meshes with substantial upgrades in data, architecture, and generative capability. First, we expand the DyMesh-XL dataset by mining dynamic content from Objaverse-XL, increasing the number of unique identities from 60K to 300K and substantially broadening category and motion diversity. Second, we redesign DyMeshVAE-Flex with power-law topology-aware attention and vertex-normal enhanced features, which significantly improves trajectory reconstruction, local geometry preservation, and mitigates trajectory-sticking artifacts. Third, we introduce architectural changes to both DyMeshVAE-Flex and the rectified-flow (RF) generator to support variable-length sequence training and generation, enabling longer animations while preserving reconstruction fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnimateAnyMesh++ generates semantically accurate and temporally coherent mesh animations within seconds, surpassing prior approaches in quality and efficiency. The enlarged DyMesh-XL, the upgraded DyMeshVAE-Flex, and variable-length RF together deliver consistent gains across benchmarks and in-the-wild meshes. We will release code, models, and the expanded DyMesh-XL upon acceptance of this manuscript to facilitate research in 4D content creation.

AI.tldr: AnimateAnyMesh++ is a feed-forward, text-driven animation framework for arbitrary 3D meshes, featuring an expanded dataset (DyMesh-XL), topology-aware DyMeshVAE-Flex, and variable-length rectified-flow (RF) to enable high-quality, temporally coherent animations efficiently.
AI.motivation: 4D content generation remains challenging due to complex spatio-temporal modeling and scarce 4D training data. This work aims to scale data, enhance architecture, and improve generative capability for 3D mesh animation.
AI.method: 1) Expand DyMesh-XL by mining dynamic content from Objaverse-XL to boost identities and motion diversity. 2) Redesign DyMeshVAE-Flex with power-law topology-aware attention and vertex-normal enhanced features to improve trajectory reconstruction and local geometry. 3) Introduce architectural changes to DyMeshVAE-Flex and the rectified-flow (RF) generator to support variable-length sequence training and generation for longer animations and preserved fidelity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show semantically accurate, temporally coherent mesh animations produced within seconds, outperforming prior approaches in quality and efficiency. Gains are consistent across enlarged dataset, upgraded models, and variable-length RF, both on benchmarks and in-the-wild meshes.
AI.conclusion: The enlarged DyMesh-XL, upgraded DyMeshVAE-Flex, and variable-length RF yield consistent performance gains; code, models, and the expanded dataset will be released upon manuscript acceptance to advance 4D content creation research.

title: Color-Encoded Illumination for High-Speed Volumetric Scene Reconstruction
comment: accepted to IEEE CVPR 2026 as a highlight
summary: The task of capturing and rendering 3D dynamic scenes from 2D images has become increasingly popular in recent years. However, most conventional cameras are bandwidth-limited to 30-60 FPS, restricting these methods to static or slowly evolving scenes. While overcoming bandwidth limitations is difficult for general scenes, recent years have seen a flurry of computational imaging methods that yield high-speed videos using conventional cameras for specific applications (e.g., motion capture and particle image velocimetry). However, most of these methods require modifications to a camera's optics or the addition of mechanically moving components, limiting them to a single-view high-speed capture. Consequently, these methods cannot be readily used to capture a 3D representation of rapid scene motion. In this paper, we propose a novel method to capture and reconstruct a volumetric representation of a high-speed scene using only unaugmented low-speed cameras. Instead of modifying the hardware or optics of each individual camera, we encode high-speed scene dynamics by illuminating the scene with a rapid, sequential color-coded sequence. This results in simultaneous multi-view capture of the scene, where high-speed temporal information is encoded in the spatial intensity and color variations of the captured images. To construct a high-speed volumetric representation of the dynamic scene, we develop a novel dynamic Gaussian Splatting-based approach that decodes the temporal information from the images. We evaluate our approach on simulated scenes and real-world experiments using a multi-camera imaging setup, showing first-of-a-kind high-speed volumetric scene reconstructions.

AI.tldr: A hardware-light approach to high-speed volumetric capture by color-coding illumination and decoding with dynamic Gaussian Splatting; achieves first high-speed volumetric reconstructions with unmodified low-speed cameras in multi-view setups.
AI.motivation: Overcome conventional cameras' bandwidth limits (30–60 FPS) that hinder dynamic 3D scene capture; avoid camera hardware modifications while enabling full 3D motion capture.
AI.method: Illuminate the scene with rapid sequential color-coded lighting; capture with standard low-speed cameras from multiple views; reconstruct a dynamic 3D volume via a dynamic Gaussian Splatting-based decoding that extracts temporal information from color/intensity variations.
AI.result: Demonstrated on simulated and real-world multi-camera experiments; presents first-of-a-kind high-speed volumetric reconstructions of dynamic scenes.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of high-speed volumetric capture without hardware changes; offers a new computational imaging pipeline; future work likely addresses calibration, synchronization, color-ambiguous artifacts, and computational efficiency.

title: World2VLM: Distilling World Model Imagination into VLMs for Dynamic Spatial Reasoning
comment: The code is available at https://github.com/WanyueZhang-ai/World2VLM. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/WanyueZhang/World2VLM
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown strong performance on static visual understanding, yet they still struggle with dynamic spatial reasoning that requires imagining how scenes evolve under egocentric motion. Recent efforts address this limitation either by scaling spatial supervision with synthetic data or by coupling VLMs with world models at inference time. However, the former often lacks explicit modeling of motion-conditioned state transitions, while the latter incurs substantial computational overhead. In this work, we propose World2VLM, a training framework that distills spatial imagination from a generative world model into a vision-language model. Given an initial observation and a parameterized camera trajectory, we use a view-consistent world model to synthesize geometrically aligned future views and derive structured supervision for both forward (action-to-outcome) and inverse (outcome-to-action) spatial reasoning. We post-train the VLM with a two-stage recipe on a compact dataset generated by this pipeline and evaluate it on multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks. World2VLM delivers consistent improvements over the base model across diverse benchmarks, including SAT-Real, SAT-Synthesized, VSI-Bench, and MindCube. It also outperforms the test-time world-model-coupled methods while eliminating the need for expensive inference-time generation. Our results suggest that world models can serve not only as inference-time tools, but also as effective training-time teachers, enabling VLMs to internalize spatial imagination in a scalable and efficient manner.

AI.tldr: World2VLM trains vision-language models to perform dynamic spatial reasoning by distilling imagination from a view-consistent world model into a VLM; it synthesizes future views along a camera trajectory for supervised learning, improving spatial reasoning without costly inference-time generation.
AI.motivation: Static VLMs struggle with dynamic, motion-conditioned spatial reasoning. Existing approaches either scale spatial supervision with synthetic data (lacking explicit motion-conditioned state transitions) or couple VLMs with world models at inference (high computational cost).
AI.method: Generate geometrically aligned future views with a view-consistent world model given an initial observation and a parameterized camera trajectory. Create structured supervision for forward (action-to-outcome) and inverse (outcome-to-action) reasoning. Post-train the VLM in two stages on a compact dataset produced by this pipeline.
AI.result: World2VLM yields consistent improvements over the base VLM across multiple benchmarks (SAT-Real, SAT-Synthesized, VSI-Bench, MindCube) and outperforms test-time world-model-coupled methods while eliminating the need for expensive inference-time generation.
AI.conclusion: World models can serve as effective training-time teachers, enabling VLMs to internalize spatial imagination in a scalable and efficient manner.

title: ProcFunc: Function-Oriented Abstractions for Procedural 3D Generation in Python
summary: We introduce ProcFunc, a library for Blender-based procedural 3D generation in Python. ProcFunc provides a library of easy-to-use Python functions, which streamline creating, combining, analyzing, and executing procedural generation code. ProcFunc makes it easy to create large-scale diverse training data, by combinatorial compositions of semantic components. VLMs can use ProcFunc to edit procedural material and geometry code and can create new procedural code with significantly fewer coding errors. Finally, as an example use case, we use ProcFunc to develop a new procedural generator of indoor rooms, which includes a collection of new compositional procedural materials. We demonstrate the detail, runtime efficiency, and diversity of this room generator, as well as its use for 3D synthetic data generation. Please visit https://github.com/princeton-vl/procfunc for source code.

AI.tldr: ProcFunc is a Blender-based Python library for procedural 3D generation that uses composable semantic components to enable scalable synthetic data creation and VLM-assisted editing, demonstrated by an indoor-room generator; source at GitHub.
AI.motivation: There is a need for simple, reusable tooling to generate large, diverse procedural assets and to reduce coding errors in procedural pipelines. Leveraging visual-language models (VLMs) to edit and generate procedural code can streamline the development of synthetic 3D data. The approach targets efficient, diverse data generation for ML applications.
AI.method: Provide a library of easy-to-use Python functions integrated with Blender to create, combine, analyze, and execute procedural generation workflows. Support combinatorial compositions of semantic components to yield diverse materials and geometry. Demonstrate with a new procedural indoor-room generator and a collection of new compositional procedural materials.
AI.result: Demonstrates detailed, runtime-efficient, and diverse indoor-room generation, suitable for 3D synthetic data generation; showcases the practical applicability of ProcFunc and its materials. Source code available at the specified GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: ProcFunc offers a practical, extensible framework for compositional procedural generation in Blender, enabling scalable data synthesis and potential for VLM-assisted code editing and generation.
